topics.title: Human Factors: Objectives and Approach. Systems Thinking: Human-Machine Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human Factors: Objectives and Approach. Systems Thinking: Human-Machine Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Human Factors Engineering (HFE) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)**, also called **Ergonomics** (Europe) or **Human Factors** (North America), is the *discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans and other elements of a system, and the profession that applies theory, principles, data, and methods to design in order to optimize human well-being and overall system performance.*

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PESFT748), Human Factors is treated as the **science of fitting the job/task to the worker**, rather than forcing the worker to adapt to the job. It draws upon **engineering, physiology, psychology, anthropometry, and industrial design**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms to remember for KTU exams:**
> - Human Factors Engineering (HFE)
> - Ergonomics
> - Human Engineering
> - Biotechnology of Work
> - Engineering Psychology

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you buy a brand-new **car**. The steering wheel is too small for your hands, the pedals are too far from the seat, and the dashboard indicators are hidden behind the steering column. You can technically drive the car, but you will be *uncomfortable, slower, and more prone to accidents*.

Now imagine a car designed by a Human Factors Engineer: the seat adjusts to your height, pedals are at the correct distance, the speedometer sits in your **direct line of sight**, and the horn is exactly where your thumb naturally falls. The car *fits YOU*.

> **Human Factors is the engineering discipline that makes machines fit humans, instead of forcing humans to fit machines.**

---

## 1.3 Objectives of Human Factors Engineering

The primary objectives, as laid out in standard KTU/HFE textbooks, are:

1. **Safety** — Minimizing accidents and injuries at the workplace and during equipment operation.
2. **Productivity** — Improving operator efficiency and overall system throughput.
3. **Comfort & Well-being** — Reducing physical and mental fatigue, stress, and discomfort.
4. **User Satisfaction** — Ensuring the human-machine interface (HMI) is intuitive and acceptable.
5. **Reliability** — Reducing human error, which is the leading cause of system failures in critical industries (aviation, nuclear, medical).
6. **Cost Reduction** — Lowering absenteeism, training costs, workers' compensation claims, and equipment damage.

---

## 1.4 Approach (Disciplinary Approach)

Human Factors adopts a **multi-disciplinary** approach because human interaction with a system cannot be understood from a single viewpoint. The KTU syllabus highlights the following converging disciplines:

| Discipline | Contribution to HFE |
|------------|---------------------|
| **Engineering** (Mechanical, Industrial, Electrical) | Designing the physical hardware, controls, displays |
| **Anatomy & Physiology** | Bodily limits — strength, reach, vision, hearing |
| **Psychology** | Perception, cognition, learning, motivation |
| **Anthropometry** | Body dimensions (static & dynamic) |
| **Biomechanics** | Movement, posture, force exertion |
| **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic and usability aspects |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two complementary approaches** exist:
> - **Anthropometric Approach** — Designing for the 5th–95th percentile of the user population.
> - **Systems Approach** — Designing the *entire* man-machine-environment system as one integrated whole.

---

## 1.5 Systems Thinking — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Systems Thinking** is a holistic way of analyzing a problem that focuses on the way a system's constituent parts interrelate and how systems work over time and within the context of larger systems.

In Human Factors, this means we never look at a single component (e.g., a button) in isolation. We look at:
- The **operator** (human),
- The **machine** (equipment/tools),
- The **environment** (light, noise, temperature),
- The **task** (goals, procedures),
- The **organisation** (rules, culture, training).

---

## 1.6 Human-Machine System — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Human-Machine System (HMS)** is any system in which a human operator performs a task using a machine, where information flows between them through displays (machine → human) and controls (human → machine).

**Real-world HMS examples:**
- Pilot flying an aircraft (cockpit)
- Surgeon performing laparoscopic surgery
- CNC operator programming a lathe
- Driver controlling an automobile
- Power plant operator monitoring a control room

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Systems Model (Classical KTU Framework)

A Human-Machine System is modelled as a **closed-loop** control system. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats it as:

$$\text{Human} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Display} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cognition} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Control} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Machine}$$

The two fundamental information channels are:
1. **Display Channel (Machine → Human):** The human receives feedback (gauges, screens, sounds, vibrations).
2. **Control Channel (Human → Machine):** The human issues commands (buttons, levers, steering).

---

## 2.2 Components of a Human-Machine System

### A. Human Subsystem
The human acts as a **controller**, **processor**, and **decision-maker**. The human is described by:

- **Sensory Input (S)** — Vision, hearing, touch, smell, taste.
- **Central Processing (P)** — Perception, memory, reasoning, decision-making.
- **Motor Output (M)** — Physical action (push, turn, pull).
- **Feedback (F)** — Internal proprioception + external environmental cues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classical model used in KTU exams is the **S–P–M Model** (Stimulus–Processor–Motor) often shown as a feedback loop with the human at the centre.

### B. Machine Subsystem
The machine consists of:

| Element | Function | Examples |
|---------|----------|----------|
| **Displays** | Present information to human | Speedometer, monitor, warning lights |
| **Controls** | Receive human input | Steering wheel, joystick, keyboard |
| **Workspace** | Physical environment | Seat, desk, cockpit |
| **Auxiliary** | Support tools | Footrest, harness |

### C. Environment
- Physical (light, temperature, noise, vibration)
- Organisational (shift work, supervision, workload)
- Social (team dynamics)

### D. Task
- Goals the operator must achieve
- Procedures and protocols
- Performance standards (time, accuracy)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although HFE is largely descriptive, several **quantitative models** appear in KTU exams.

### 2.3.1 System Performance Model

The classical **systems model** states that overall system performance is a function of three components:

$$P_{system} = f(H, M, E)$$

Where:
- $P_{system}$ = Overall system performance
- $H$ = Human performance (capability × motivation)
- $M$ = Machine performance (reliability, accuracy)
- $E$ = Environmental factors

### 2.3.2 Human Information Processing Model

Information throughput in bits per second (Channel Capacity) follows **Hick's Law**:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$$

Where:
- $T$ = Average reaction time (seconds)
- $n$ = Number of equally probable choices
- $a, b$ = Empirical constants ($a \approx 0.155$ s, $b \approx 0.150$ s for simple choice tasks)

> [!NOTE]
> Hick's Law is a **favourite KTU question** — it tells designers to *limit the number of choices* on a control panel so that reaction time does not blow up.

### 2.3.3 Fitts' Law (Motor Control)

Time to move a hand to a target is:

$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

Where:
- $MT$ = Movement time (seconds)
- $D$ = Distance to target
- $W$ = Width of target
- $a, b$ = Empirical constants

### 2.3.4 Signal Detection Theory

Detection probability is governed by:

$$d' = \frac{\mu_{signal} - \mu_{noise}}{\sigma_{noise}}$$

Where $d'$ = sensitivity index, used in alarm/display design.

### 2.3.5 Anthropometric Design Range

To accommodate 90% of the user population:

$$R_{design} = P_5 \;\text{to}\; P_{95}$$

| Boundary | Percentile | Use |
|----------|-----------|-----|
| **5th** ($P_5$) | Lower limit (smallest user) | Reach down, eye level min |
| **95th** ($P_{95}$) | Upper limit (largest user) | Reach up, clearance |

---

## 2.4 Why "Systems Thinking"?

A student often asks: *"Why don't we just design the machine perfectly and forget about the human?"*

The answer lies in a fundamental principle of HFE:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human, machine, and environment are not independent — they are coupled.** A change in one affects the others. Therefore, optimisation must be holistic, not local.

**Engineering Utility:** This approach underpins critical industries:
- **Aviation** — Glass cockpits designed using systems thinking reduced pilot error by ~40%.
- **Nuclear Power** — Three Mile Island and Chernobyl investigations cited poor HMI design rooted in *non-systems* thinking.
- **Medical Devices** — Modern ventilators and patient monitors use systems ergonomics to reduce alarm fatigue.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Closed-Loop Human-Machine Model

A Human-Machine System can be rigorously mapped onto the classical **control-theory feedback loop**. Let us walk through each transition step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define the Operator Goal

The operator (human) is assigned a **goal** $G(t)$ that varies with time. Example: keep an aircraft at altitude $h = 35{,}000$ ft.

$$G(t) = h_{ref}(t)$$

### Step 2 — Display Presents System State

The machine's sensors measure actual state $X(t)$ and present it on a display:

$$D(t) = f_{display}(X(t))$$

For an altimeter: $D(t)$ = needle position representing altitude.

### Step 3 — Human Senses the Display

The human's sensory system encodes the display:

$$S(t) = K_s \cdot D(t) + \nu_s(t)$$

Where $K_s$ = sensory gain and $\nu_s$ = sensory noise.

### Step 4 — Cognitive Comparison

The brain compares perceived state with goal:

$$E(t) = G(t) - S(t)$$

This is the **error signal** — the fundamental driver of corrective action.

### Step 5 — Decision / Control Selection

Based on $E(t)$, the human selects a motor command:

$$C(t) = K_c \cdot E(t) + K_i \int E(\tau)d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{dE}{dt}$$

> **Note:** This looks exactly like a **PID controller** because, in skilled tasks, the human behaves mathematically like a PID regulator.

### Step 6 — Motor Actuation

The command is sent through muscles to the control:

$$M(t) = K_m \cdot C(t) + \nu_m(t)$$

### Step 7 — Machine Response

The machine alters the system state:

$$\frac{dX(t)}{dt} = f_{machine}(M(t), X(t))$$

### Step 8 — Feedback Closure

The new $X(t)$ re-enters Step 2, closing the loop.

### Step 3.2 — Consolidated System Equation

Combining steps into a single transfer-function form (Laplace domain):

$$\frac{X(s)}{G(s)} = \frac{K_{total} \cdot G_{PID}(s)}{1 + K_{total} \cdot G_{PID}(s) \cdot G_{machine}(s)}$$

Where $G_{PID}(s)$ = human controller transfer function and $G_{machine}(s)$ = machine dynamics.

This proves that a Human-Machine System is **mathematically equivalent to a closed-loop servomechanism** — a foundational concept in KTU's "Systems Thinking" module.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Human vs. Machine Capabilities

A classic **2-mark or 12-mark KTU question** asks to compare human and machine strengths. Use this matrix:

| Attribute | Human (Superior) | Machine (Superior) |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Speed of calculation** | ✗ Slow | ✓ Microseconds |
| **Pattern recognition** | ✓ Excellent | ✗ Limited (without AI) |
| **Repetitive precision** | ✗ Fatigue sets in | ✓ Unlimited |
| **Adaptability** | ✓ High | ✗ Rigid |
| **Reasoning / judgment** | ✓ Strong | ✗ None |
| **Force output** | ✗ Limited | ✓ Huge |
| **Memory capacity** | ✗ Limited (~7±2 chunks) | ✓ Massive |
| **Sensitivity to environment** | ✗ Affected | ✓ Immune |
| **Improvisation** | ✓ Excellent | ✗ Cannot |
| **Reliability over time** | ✗ Declines | ✓ Constant |

---

## 3.3 Allocation of Function — Decision Framework

Given this matrix, the HFE designer asks: *"Who should do what?"* Allocation rules:

1. **Machines** → Tasks that are **repetitive, dangerous, high-precision, long-duration, or computationally intense**.
2. **Humans** → Tasks requiring **judgment, creativity, pattern recognition, improvisation, or moral decision-making**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "machines are better than humans" or vice-versa. The KTU-correct answer is **functional allocation** — assign each task to whichever agent does it better, keeping the human as the *supervisor* of the system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Human-Machine System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Goal / Reference Input Gt] --> B[Display Machine to Human]
    B --> C[Sensory Input Vision Hearing Touch]
    C --> D[Central Processor Brain Cognition Memory]
    D --> E[Motor Output Muscles Limbs]
    E --> F[Controls Levers Buttons Steering]
    F --> G[Machine Engine Process Equipment]
    G --> H[Environment Workspace Noise Light Temp]
    H --> G
    G --> A
    H --> C
    D -. Feedback .-> A
```

---

## 4.2 Systems Thinking — Hierarchical System View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Suprasystem Global Aviation Industry] --> S2
    S2[System Aircraft Cockpit] --> S3
    S3[Subsystem Human Machine Environment Task]
    S3 --> P1[Human Pilot]
    S3 --> P2[Machine Avionics Displays]
    S3 --> P3[Environment Cockpit Lighting]
    S3 --> P4[Task Takeoff Climb Cruise Landing]
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P4
    P4 --- P1
```

---

## 4.3 Human Information Processing Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST1[Stimulus Display Light Sound] --> ST2[Receptors Eyes Ears Skin]
    ST2 --> ST3[Sensory Memory 250 ms]
    ST3 --> ST4[Working Memory 7 plus minus 2]
    ST4 --> ST5[Long Term Memory Unlimited]
    ST5 --> ST6[Decision Motor Selection]
    ST6 --> ST7[Response Action]
    ST7 -. Proprioceptive Feedback .-> ST4
```

---

## 4.4 Allocation of Function Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Task Type?} -->|Repetitive Precise| MQ[Assign to Machine]
    Q -->|Judgment Improvisation| HQ[Assign to Human]
    Q -->|High Force Hazardous| MQ
    Q -->|Pattern Recognition Creative| HQ
    Q -->|Long Duration Monotonous| MQ
    Q -->|Ethical Decision Making| HQ
    MQ --> R[Machine Execution with Human Supervision]
    HQ --> R2[Human Execution with Machine Support]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Human Factors Engineering. List any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

Human Factors Engineering is the discipline that applies knowledge of human capabilities and limitations to the design of systems, equipment, tools, and workplaces so as to improve safety, comfort, productivity, and overall system performance.

The four primary objectives are:

1. **Safety** — Reduction of accidents and occupational injuries.
2. **Productivity** — Increased efficiency and throughput.
3. **Comfort & Well-being** — Minimisation of fatigue, stress, and musculoskeletal disorders.
4. **Reliability** — Reduction of human error and improvement of system uptime.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a Human-Machine System? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A Human-Machine System is an integrated system in which a human operator and a machine work together to accomplish a task, with information flowing from machine to human via displays and from human to machine via controls.

**Examples:**
1. A pilot operating an aircraft cockpit.
2. A surgeon performing robotic-assisted laparoscopic surgery.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay-Type Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires **internal choice**. Two fully solved alternatives are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **multi-disciplinary approach** of Human Factors Engineering with a neat block diagram. Discuss the contributions of at least **four disciplines**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Systems Approach** to Human Factors. How does it differ from the **Anthropometric Approach**? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

Human Factors Engineering cannot be practised from a single disciplinary viewpoint because the human operator simultaneously interacts with mechanical, cognitive, and environmental elements. The **multi-disciplinary approach** integrates contributions from:

- **Engineering (Mechanical/Industrial):** Designs physical hardware — controls, displays, workspace layout. Example: ergonomic placement of a CNC machine's control panel within the operator's primary reach envelope.
- **Psychology:** Provides insight into perception, attention, learning, decision-making, and motivation. Example: determining optimal warning-signal frequency on a hospital monitor.
- **Anatomy & Physiology:** Defines human limits — muscle strength, oxygen consumption, heart-rate response. Example: setting maximum acceptable workloads in manual material handling.
- **Anthropometry:** Supplies body dimension data — stature, arm reach, hand size — used to design for the 5th–95th percentile population.
- **Biomechanics:** Analyses posture, joint torque, and repetitive motion injury risk.
- **Industrial Design:** Combines aesthetics with usability.

Refer to **Diagram 4.2** above for the integrated view.

#### Model Solution (b)

The **Systems Approach** treats the operator, machine, environment, task, and organisation as a **single, coupled, closed-loop system**. Optimisation is holistic — improving the *whole* rather than any single component. The human is modelled as a controller within a feedback loop (see Section 3.1).

The **Anthropometric Approach**, in contrast, focuses exclusively on **body dimensions** and physical fit. It designs static dimensions (seat height, reach distance) for a target population range.

| Basis | Systems Approach | Anthropometric Approach |
|-------|-----------------|------------------------|
| **Focus** | Whole system | Body dimensions only |
| **Scope** | Human + Machine + Environment + Task + Organisation | Physical fit only |
| **Tools** | Modelling, feedback loops, task analysis | Percentile tables, range charts |
| **Optimisation** | Overall system performance | Physical accommodation |
| **Output** | Safe, productive work system | Dimensionally correct equipment |

The systems approach is broader and **encompasses** the anthropometric approach within it.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **Human-Machine System model**. Identify the **display channel** and **control channel**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **human** and **machine** capabilities on at least **eight parameters**. Explain the principle of **allocation of function**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

The Human-Machine System consists of three principal blocks: **Human, Machine, and Environment**, interconnected through two channels:

1. **Display Channel (Machine → Human):** Carries information about the system's state. Examples: pressure gauge, warning light, audible alarm, monitor screen.
2. **Control Channel (Human → Machine):** Carries the operator's commands to the machine. Examples: steering wheel, push button, joystick, throttle lever.

A reference block diagram is shown below (also see Diagram 4.1):

> **Operator** → (senses) → **Display** → (perceives) → **Brain/Cognition** → (decides) → **Motor Action** → **Control** → **Machine** → (alters) → **Environment** → (feedback) → **Operator**.

The human thus acts as a **closed-loop controller** with sensory input, central processing, and motor output — analogous to a feedback amplifier in control theory.

#### Model Solution (b)

See the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.2** for the eight-parameter comparison.

**Principle of Allocation of Function:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Once the human-machine capability comparison is made, the designer must decide **which agent performs each sub-task** in a way that maximises overall system performance.

The rule of thumb (from KTU 2024 syllabus):

- **Assign to the machine:** repetitive, precise, hazardous, high-force, long-duration, mathematically intensive tasks.
- **Assign to the human:** tasks involving judgment, improvisation, pattern recognition, ethics, or moral responsibility.
- **Always keep the human in the loop as supervisor** — even when a machine is the primary executor — to handle abnormal and unforeseen conditions.

**Example:** In an automatic train, the locomotive handles speed regulation and braking routine (machine), but the driver retains override authority and handles passenger emergencies (human).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Conflating Ergonomics with Human Factors** — In KTU, both are accepted as synonyms, but mentioning **both terms with their regional origins** (Ergonomics = European, Human Factors = American) fetches extra marks.
> 2. **Skipping the block diagram** — A 7-mark question *without* a labelled block diagram is treated as incomplete; expect 2–3 marks deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the feedback loop** — When describing the HMS model, students often draw a one-way arrow from human to machine. Always show the **bidirectional** flow via display + control channels.
> 4. **Writing "machines are better"** — This is a conceptual error. Allocation of function is *task-specific*, not agent-specific.
> 5. **Ignoring environment and task** — Many students only mention human and machine. KTU's systems approach demands **all five components**: Human, Machine, Environment, Task, Organisation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ☐ Human Factors = Ergonomics = the science of fitting the job to the worker.
- ☐ Multi-disciplinary approach merges Engineering + Psychology + Physiology + Anthropometry + Biomechanics + Industrial Design.
- ☐ **Systems Approach** is holistic (Human + Machine + Environment + Task + Organisation).
- ☐ **Anthropometric Approach** is narrower (body dimensions only, 5th–95th percentile).
- ☐ Human-Machine System has two channels: **Display (M→H)** and **Control (H→M)**.
- ☐ Human acts as a **closed-loop controller** mathematically equivalent to a **PID regulator**.
- ☐ **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ — more choices → slower reaction.
- ☐ **Fitts' Law:** Larger targets, shorter distances → faster movement.
- ☐ **Anthropometric Design Range:** accommodate **5th to 95th percentile**.
- ☐ **Allocation of Function:** assign each sub-task to whichever agent performs it better; keep human as supervisor.
- ☐ Six objectives: **Safety, Productivity, Comfort, Satisfaction, Reliability, Cost-reduction**.
- ☐ Always draw a **labelled block diagram** showing feedback in HMS questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Characteristics of Systems — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System (KTU 2024 HFE Definition):** A *system* is an organized collection of interdependent, interacting components (subsystems, processes, human operators, machines, software, and environment) that work together to achieve a defined **goal** by accepting **inputs**, transforming them through internal **processes**, producing **outputs**, and continuously self-correcting through **feedback** loops.

In **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)**, the term *system* is used to describe the **human–machine–environment triad** operating as a single integrated entity. A pilot flying an aircraft, a surgeon operating a robot, or a driver controlling a car are all classic *man–machine systems*.

## 1.2 System Constituents (The Five Pillars)

Every system, irrespective of its complexity, is composed of five irreducible pillars:

1. **Inputs** — Energy, information, raw materials, or operator commands entering the system.
2. **Process** — The transformation or conversion mechanism (physical, cognitive, or computational).
3. **Outputs** — The desired product, decision, or physical action produced.
4. **Feedback** — Information about the output that is returned to refine future performance.
5. **Environment / Boundary** — The external conditions and constraints within which the system operates.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Car as a System"**
> Think of a car as a system:
> - **Input** → Turning the steering wheel and pressing the accelerator.
> - **Process** → Engine converts fuel to mechanical energy; gears transmit torque.
> - **Output** → Vehicle moves forward at a desired speed.
> - **Feedback** → Speedometer reading tells the driver whether to accelerate or brake.
> - **Boundary / Environment** → Road surface, traffic, weather.
> - The **driver is the human subsystem** who senses, processes, and acts.

This is exactly how HFE engineers view a workplace: as an *integrated socio-technical system* where humans, machines, and environment co-evolve.

## 1.4 The Human as a Subsystem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In HFE, the **operator (human)** is treated as an *information-processing subsystem* with three internal stages: **Sensation → Cognition → Motor Response**. The characteristics of this subsystem (e.g., reaction time ≈ **250 ms**, channel capacity ≈ **7 ± 2 chunks**) must be matched with the machine subsystem for *systemic compatibility*.

## 1.5 Why Study "Characteristics of Systems"?

> [!NOTE]
> According to the **KTU 2024 HFE syllabus (Module 1)**, understanding system characteristics is foundational because it enables the designer to:
> - Identify **bottlenecks** in a man–machine loop.
> - Diagnose **human error** as a system failure, not an individual fault.
> - Optimize **reliability, safety, and efficiency** of integrated workplaces.
> - Apply **systems theory** (von Bertalanffy) to ergonomic design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 12 Core Characteristics of Systems (KTU High-Yield)

A system exhibits the following universal characteristics, each of which is a **favourite board-exam point**:

1. **Wholeness & Integrity** — The system behaves as a single entity, *greater than the sum of its parts* (synergy).
2. **Interdependence** — Each subsystem depends on the outputs of others.
3. **Hierarchy** — Systems contain sub-systems, which themselves contain sub-sub-systems (recursive structure).
4. **Goal-Directedness** — All components are organized toward a *common objective*.
5. **Open vs Closed Nature** — Open systems exchange matter/energy/information with the environment; closed systems do not.
6. **Boundary** — A real or conceptual line separating the system from its environment.
7. **Equifinality** — A system can reach the same final state from *different initial conditions* and by *different paths* (open-system property).
8. **Homeostasis (Steady State)** — The system maintains internal equilibrium through self-regulation.
9. **Feedback (Negative & Positive)** — Negative feedback = stability; positive feedback = amplification/growth.
10. **Adaptation / Self-Organization** — Ability to modify internal structure in response to environmental change.
11. **Determinism vs Probabilistic Behaviour** — Deterministic systems produce predictable outputs; probabilistic systems involve uncertainty (most human subsystems are probabilistic).
12. **Emergence** — New properties appear at the system level that do *not* exist at the subsystem level (e.g., "teamwork" emerges from individuals).

## 2.2 Classification of Systems (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **verbatim board-exam material** — memorize the contrasts.

| **Classification Axis** | **Type 1** | **Type 2** | **HFE Implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interaction with environment | **Open System** | **Closed System** | Man–machine systems are open. |
| Time-dependence | **Static System** | **Dynamic System** | Human response is dynamic. |
| Predictability | **Deterministic System** | **Probabilistic (Stochastic) System** | Human behaviour is probabilistic. |
| Component nature | **Physical System** | **Abstract / Conceptual System** | HFE uses both. |
| Complexity | **Simple System** | **Complex System** | Aircraft cockpit is complex. |
| Linearity | **Linear System** | **Non-Linear System** | Humans introduce non-linearity. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet — Systems & Human Information Processing**

| **#** | **Formula / Law** | **Meaning** | **Used in** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Reliability (Series)} \; R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Components must all function for system success. | System reliability |
| 2 | $\text{Reliability (Parallel)} \; R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Redundancy / backup paths. | Safety-critical HFE |
| 3 | $\text{Fitts' Law} \; MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ | Index of Difficulty (ID) for pointing tasks. | Display/control design |
| 4 | $\text{Hick's Law} \; RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | Choice reaction time grows with number of options. | Interface design |
| 5 | $\text{Steady-State Error} \; e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | Long-term deviation in feedback system. | Closed-loop HMI |
| 6 | $\text{Open-Loop Gain} \; G_{OL} = G_c \cdot G_p$ | Forward path of control system. | Automation design |
| 7 | $\text{Closed-Loop Transfer Function} \; \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | System response with feedback H(s). | Ergonomic control loop |
| 8 | $\text{Channel Capacity (Miller)} \; C = 7 \pm 2 \; \text{chunks}$ | Working memory limit. | Display design |
| 9 | $\text{Signal Detection} \; d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Sensitivity index of human operator. | Vigilance tasks |
| 10 | $\text{Synergy Factor} \; SF = \frac{\text{System Output}}{\sum \text{Subsystem Outputs}}$ | SF > 1 indicates emergent performance. | Team HFE |

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use proper LaTeX vertical bars via `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert G(s) \vert$) — never use the unescaped pipe `|` inside markdown tables.

## 2.4 Open System vs Closed System — The Defining Dichotomy

| **Property** | **Open System** | **Closed System** |
|---|---|---|
| Exchange with environment | Yes (matter, energy, info) | No |
| Reach equilibrium | Dynamic equilibrium (steady state) | True equilibrium or entropy death |
| Equifinality | Exhibits | Does not exhibit |
| Example in HFE | Hospital operating theatre | Sealed pressure chamber |
| Entropy behaviour | Imports negative entropy | Tends to maximum entropy |
| Relevance to HFE | **Primary focus** | Theoretical reference only |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aviation HFE:** The cockpit is modelled as a closed-loop man–machine system. Pilot behaviour is a *stochastic subsystem*; aircraft dynamics is a *deterministic subsystem*. Mis-matched characteristics cause *pilot-induced oscillations*.
> - **Industrial Safety:** A chemical plant is an *open system* where human error acts as the dominant *probabilistic failure mode*. Reliability block diagrams (formulas 1 & 2) directly predict accident probability.
> - **UI/UX Design:** Fitts' Law and Hick's Law (formulas 3 & 4) are applied to size buttons and limit menu options for optimal human-system performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although HFE is largely conceptual, **board questions frequently test derivation-style problems** in three areas: (i) System Reliability, (ii) Fitts' Law Application, and (iii) Closed-Loop Transfer Function. All three are derived below in **exhaustive step-by-step form**.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series System Reliability

**Statement:** For *n* independent subsystems connected in *series*, the system functions *only if* all subsystems function. Derive the expression for series reliability $R_s$.

**Step 1 — Define reliability of a single subsystem.**
The reliability $R_i$ of subsystem $i$ is the probability that it functions correctly over a mission time $t$:

$$R_i = P(\text{Subsystem } i \text{ works})$$

**Step 2 — State the independence assumption.**
For subsystems operating in series with **statistical independence**, the joint probability is the product of the individual probabilities:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the multiplicative rule across all *n* subsystems.**

$$R_s = P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \dots \cap S_n)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the independence product.**

$$R_s = P(S_1) \cdot P(S_2) \cdots P(S_n)$$

**Step 5 — Replace each $P(S_i)$ with $R_i$ to obtain the final series formula.**

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**
> *Result:* Series system reliability is **always less than or equal to the weakest link** in the chain. For identical $R_i = R$:

$$R_s = R^n$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel System Reliability

**Statement:** For *n* independent subsystems in *parallel redundancy*, derive $R_p$.

**Step 1 — Define failure probability.**
Failure probability of subsystem $i$ is $Q_i = 1 - R_i$.

**Step 2 — Probability of *total* system failure.**
The system fails only if **all** subsystems fail simultaneously. With independence:

$$Q_p = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

**Step 3 — Probability of *survival* (reliability).**
Reliability is the complement of failure:

$$R_p = 1 - Q_p$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q_p$.**

$$R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

**Step 5 — Special case (two identical components, $R_1 = R_2 = R$).**

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 2R - R^2$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation for HFE.**
> *Result:* Parallel redundancy is the mathematical basis of **safety-critical ergonomic design** (e.g., dual braking systems in automobiles, redundant cockpit displays in aircraft).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Man–Machine System

Consider a feedback system where $R(s)$ is the reference input (desired), $C(s)$ is the controlled output (human + machine response), $G(s)$ is the forward path (machine dynamics + human transfer function), and $H(s)$ is the feedback path (display/sensor).

**Step 1 — Define the error signal.**
The actuating error is the difference between the reference and the feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 2 — Output is the forward-path gain times the error.**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the error expression into the output equation.**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the product.**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Collect the $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $C(s)$.**

$$C(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$.**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

**Step 8 — HFE Interpretation.**
> If the human operator acts as the controller $G_c(s)$ and the display as the feedback $H(s)$, this formula predicts whether the human-machine closed loop will be **stable**, **oscillatory**, or **unstable** — a cornerstone of HFE analysis for aviation, nuclear control rooms, and driving.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Fitts' Law Application)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question (KTU-style):** A touchscreen button has width $W = 2 \text{ cm}$ and the user must move the finger a distance $D = 10 \text{ cm}$. Empirical constants are $a = 50 \text{ ms}$ and $b = 100 \text{ ms/bit}$. Calculate the Index of Performance (IP) and the average movement time (MT).

**Step 1 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID).**

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10}{2}\right) = \log_2(10)$$

$$\log_2(10) = \frac{\ln 10}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.3026}{0.6931} \approx 3.3219 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Movement Time using Fitts' Law.**

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID = 50 + 100 \times 3.3219$$

$$MT = 50 + 332.19 = 382.19 \; \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Index of Performance.**

$$IP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{3.3219}{0.38219} \approx 8.69 \; \text{bits/s}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**
> *Result:* A larger IP indicates more efficient human–machine interaction. Designers use this metric to **size touchscreen targets** so that the human subsystem can interact optimally with the machine subsystem.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams map the **functional architecture** of systems concepts in the HFE context. All node IDs are alphanumeric (per safety rules), and labels are clean plain text.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Generic Open System with Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Env["Environment"] -->|Disturbance D| SumNode
    Input["Input R"] --> SumNode
    SumNode -->|Error E| Process["Process G"]
    Process --> Output["Output C"]
    Output --> Env
    Output --> Feedback["Feedback H"]
    Feedback --> SumNode
    Human["Human Subsystem"] -.->|Acts on| Process
    Human -.->|Senses| Feedback
```

**Reading the diagram (left-to-right):**
- The **Environment** injects a *disturbance* $D$ into the summing junction.
- The **Input** $R$ (operator's command) combines with the disturbance to form the **Error** $E$.
- The **Process** $G$ transforms the error into a controlled **Output** $C$.
- A portion of $C$ is routed back through the **Feedback** path $H$ to be compared again with the input — this is **negative feedback**, the heart of stable man–machine systems.
- The **Human Subsystem** (dashed) both *acts on* the process and *senses* the feedback — closing the cognitive–motor loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Open vs Closed System Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OpenSys["OPEN SYSTEM"]
        O1["Boundary is permeable"] --> O2["Exchanges matter, energy, info"]
        O2 --> O3["Shows equifinality"]
        O3 --> O4["Example: Operating Theatre"]
    end
    subgraph ClosedSys["CLOSED SYSTEM"]
        C1["Boundary is sealed"] --> C2["No exchange with environment"]
        C2 --> C3["Tends to maximum entropy"]
        C3 --> C4["Example: Sealed Chamber"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of a Socio-Technical System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LEVEL 1 - TOTAL SYSTEM - Aircraft Cockpit"] --> L2a["LEVEL 2A - Human Subsystem - Pilot"]
    L1 --> L2b["LEVEL 2B - Machine Subsystem - Avionics"]
    L1 --> L2c["LEVEL 2C - Environment - Airspace and Weather"]
    L2a --> L3a["Pilot Subsystem A1 - Perception"]
    L2a --> L3b["Pilot Subsystem A2 - Cognition"]
    L2a --> L3c["Pilot Subsystem A3 - Motor Action"]
    L2b --> L3d["Machine Subsystem B1 - Displays"]
    L2b --> L3e["Machine Subsystem B2 - Controls"]
    L2b --> L3f["Machine Subsystem B3 - Flight Computer"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Series vs Parallel System Reliability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SeriesArch["SERIES - All must work"]
        S1["Subsystem 1"] --> S2["Subsystem 2"] --> S3["Subsystem 3"] --> Sout["Output C"]
    end
    subgraph ParallelArch["PARALLEL - Any one works"]
        P1a["Subsystem A"] --> Pout["Output C"]
        P1b["Subsystem B"] --> Pout
        P1c["Subsystem C"] --> Pout
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define a "system" as understood in Human Factors Engineering. List any four characteristics of a system.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
A *system* in HFE is an organized, goal-directed assembly of interdependent human, machine, and environmental components that transform inputs into outputs through feedback. **Four characteristics** are: (1) **Wholeness** (the system is greater than the sum of its parts), (2) **Interdependence** (subsystems depend on one another), (3) **Goal-directedness** (every element serves a common purpose), and (4) **Equifinality** (the same final state can be reached from different initial conditions in an open system).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**Distinguish between an open system and a closed system. Give one HFE example of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 70 words):**

| **Open System** | **Closed System** |
|---|---|
| Exchanges matter, energy, info with the environment. | No exchange with the environment. |
| Exhibits equifinality. | Tends to thermodynamic equilibrium. |
| **Example:** Driver–car–road system. | **Example:** Hermetically sealed incubator. |

In HFE practice, *virtually all man–machine systems are open* because the human continuously exchanges information with the machine and the environment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **twelve core characteristics of a system** with a relevant HFE illustration for each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A safety-critical display unit has three sub-modules $A$, $B$, $C$ connected in **series** with reliabilities $0.95$, $0.92$, and $0.90$ respectively. Compute the system reliability. If a **parallel redundant** copy of module $B$ is added, recompute the overall reliability. Comment on the engineering significance.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **open system model with negative feedback**. Define the closed-loop transfer function and derive the expression $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A touchscreen button of width $W = 4 \text{ cm}$ is located at a distance $D = 16 \text{ cm}$ from the user's current finger position. The empirical constants are $a = 60 \text{ ms}$ and $b = 150 \text{ ms/bit}$. Using **Fitts' Law**, calculate the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, the **Movement Time (MT)**, and the **Index of Performance (IP)**. Interpret the result for ergonomic button design.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### (a) The Twelve Characteristics of a System (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each characteristic carries ~0.5 mark. Include the HFE example explicitly.*

1. **Wholeness** — System behaves as one entity. *Example:* A cockpit cannot be understood by studying instruments alone.
2. **Interdependence** — Subsystems rely on each other. *Example:* Display failure → pilot cannot read altitude.
3. **Hierarchy** — Subsystems nest within larger systems. *Example:* Pilot → Perception → Vision.
4. **Goal-directedness** — All elements aim at a common goal. *Example:* Aircraft must complete a safe flight.
5. **Open nature** — Exchanges energy/information with environment. *Example:* Driver–car–road.
6. **Boundary** — Defines the system limit. *Example:* Aircraft fuselage.
7. **Equifinality** — Same output via different paths. *Example:* Reaching a hospital by car or ambulance.
8. **Homeostasis** — Internal stability via feedback. *Example:* Body temperature regulation.
9. **Feedback** — Output information is returned. *Example:* Speedometer to driver.
10. **Adaptation** — System modifies itself. *Example:* Pilot learning a new aircraft type.
11. **Determinism vs Probabilistic** — Human behaviour is probabilistic. *Example:* Reaction time varies across trials.
12. **Emergence** — System-level property absent at component level. *Example:* Team coordination.

#### (b) Reliability Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Original series system reliability.**

$$R_{s,\text{original}} = R_A \times R_B \times R_C = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90$$

$$R_{s,\text{original}} = 0.7698 \; (\approx 76.98\%)$$

**Step 2 — Reliability of the parallel pair (B and its redundant copy, both $R = 0.92$).**

$$R_{p,B} = 1 - (1 - 0.92)(1 - 0.92) = 1 - (0.08)^2 = 1 - 0.0064 = 0.9936$$

**Step 3 — New overall series reliability (A, parallel B-block, C).**

$$R_{s,\text{new}} = R_A \times R_{p,B} \times R_C = 0.95 \times 0.9936 \times 0.90$$

$$R_{s,\text{new}} = 0.8496 \; (\approx 84.96\%)$$

**Step 4 — Engineering significance (≈ 2 marks for explanation).**
> The reliability jumped from **76.98 % to 84.96 %** (an absolute gain of ~**8 %**) by adding one parallel copy of the *weakest* module. This validates the HFE principle of **defence-in-depth** in safety-critical displays (e.g., Primary Flight Displays, Nuclear control panels). Designers should always place redundancy on the *least reliable* link for maximum benefit.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### (a) Open System with Negative Feedback (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (reproduce on answer script):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R(s) Reference"] --> S["+ -"]
    D["Disturbance"] --> S
    S -->|E(s)| G["G(s) Forward Path"]
    G --> C["C(s) Output"]
    C --> H["H(s) Feedback"]
    H --> S
```

**Derivation (must show all algebraic steps):**

Step 1 — Error signal: $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$

Step 2 — Output: $C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$

Step 3 — Substitute: $C(s) = G(s) [R(s) - H(s) C(s)]$

Step 4 — Expand: $C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$

Step 5 — Collect $C(s)$ terms: $C(s) [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) R(s)$

Step 6 — Solve:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**HFE Interpretation (≈ 2 marks):**
In a man–machine loop, $G(s)$ includes the **human transfer function** and the **machine dynamics**; $H(s)$ is the **sensory feedback** (visual, auditory, haptic). The formula tells the designer that *if* $G(s) H(s) = -1$ at some frequency, the system becomes **unstable** and oscillates — the classic cause of *pilot-induced oscillations* in high-speed aircraft.

---

#### (b) Fitts' Law Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $W = 4 \text{ cm}$, $D = 16 \text{ cm}$, $a = 60 \text{ ms}$, $b = 150 \text{ ms/bit}$.

**Step 1 — Compute Index of Difficulty.**

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 16}{4}\right) = \log_2(8) = 3 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Movement Time.**

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 150 \times 3 = 60 + 450 = 510 \; \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Index of Performance.**

$$IP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{3}{0.510} \approx 5.88 \; \text{bits/s}$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic interpretation (≈ 2 marks).**
> An IP of **5.88 bits/s** is on the lower side of typical human pointing performance (range 4–10 bits/s). To improve it, the designer can **enlarge the button width** $W$ (e.g., from 4 cm to 8 cm → new $ID = \log_2(4) = 2$ bits, new $MT = 360$ ms, new $IP = 5.56$ bits/s — actually $W$ must be carefully tuned since MT decreases more than linearly). This shows how Fitts' Law is used in **HFE-driven button design** for cockpits, dashboards, and mobile interfaces.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Characteristics of Systems" Questions**
> 1. **Confusing "open" with "closed"** — many students write that a "sealed cockpit" is a closed system. *Wrong:* a cockpit exchanges air, heat, and information → it is **open**.
> 2. **Missing the "+1" in Hick's Law** — it is $\log_2(n+1)$, *not* $\log_2(n)$. Half a mark lost.
> 3. **Failing to convert units** — Fitts' Law needs $D$ and $W$ in the **same units**; mixing cm and mm loses a full mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the feedback sign** — in the closed-loop derivation, the negative sign in $E = R - HC$ is mandatory for negative feedback.
> 5. **Writing $R_s = R_1 + R_2$** for series systems — this is a **classic error**; it should be the *product* $\prod R_i$.
> 6. **Not labelling diagrams** — the Mermaid block is for understanding; the exam answer **must be redrawn by hand on the answer booklet** with proper labels and arrows.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Characteristics of Systems**

- ✅ A **system** = Inputs + Process + Outputs + Feedback + Environment/Boundary.
- ✅ **HFE definition** always includes the *human* as a subsystem.
- ✅ **Open vs Closed** — open systems exchange matter/energy/information; closed systems do not. Man–machine systems are **open**.
- ✅ **12 characteristics**: Wholeness, Interdependence, Hierarchy, Goal-directedness, Open/Closed nature, Boundary, Equifinality, Homeostasis, Feedback, Adaptation, Determinism/Probabilistic, Emergence.
- ✅ **Synergy Factor** $SF > 1$ → system is more than the sum of parts.
- ✅ **Series reliability**: $R_s = \prod R_i$. **Parallel reliability**: $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$.
- ✅ **Fitts' Law**: $MT = a + b \log_2(2D/W)$ — Index of Difficulty in bits.
- ✅ **Hick's Law**: $RT = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ — note the **"+1"**.
- ✅ **Miller's Law**: Working memory capacity = $7 \pm 2$ chunks.
- ✅ **Closed-loop transfer function**: $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$.
- ✅ **Negative feedback** provides stability; **positive feedback** leads to divergence.
- ✅ **Equifinality** is an *open-system* property — same end from different paths.
- ✅ **Homeostasis** = dynamic steady state maintained by feedback.
- ✅ **Probabilistic nature** of human subsystem → use statistical analysis (reaction time distributions, signal detection theory).
- ✅ **Emergent properties** appear at the system level, not at the component level (e.g., "teamwork", "situational awareness").
- ✅ Always **redraw the block diagram by hand** in the exam; do not describe it in words.
- ✅ For safety-critical HFE design, **place redundancy on the weakest module** for maximum reliability gain.
- ✅ The five-pillar system model (Input–Process–Output–Feedback–Environment) is the **most repeated KTU diagram** — practice drawing it from memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Reliability. Human Beings as Information Processors - Information Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Reliability. Human Beings as Information Processors - Information Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 System Reliability in Human Factors Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *System Reliability* is the probability that a system (including its human, machine, and environmental sub-systems) will perform its intended function satisfactorily under specified operating conditions, for a specified period of time.

Mathematically, reliability is expressed as:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

Where $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure and $t$ is the specified mission duration.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you bought a new smartphone. The manufacturer claims it will "work properly for 2 years." This claim is essentially a **reliability statement**. If out of 1,000 phones, 990 still work flawlessly after 2 years, the reliability $R(2\text{ yrs}) = 0.990$ (or **99%**). Now, think of a human operating a nuclear power plant console — *System Reliability* asks: "What is the probability that the **combined human-machine system** will not fail to detect and respond to a critical alarm within the next 10 minutes?" This probability is what the Human Factors engineer must maximize through proper interface design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology Distinction:**
> - **Hardware Reliability** — failure of physical components (MTBF).
> - **Software Reliability** — failure due to latent code defects (defects/KLOC).
> - **Human Reliability** — probability that a person performs a task successfully within allowable limits (e.g., **THERP, HEART, SLIM** techniques).

## 1.2 Human Beings as Information Processors

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A human operator can be modeled as a *limited-capacity information-processing system* that receives stimuli from the environment, encodes them through sensory organs, processes them cognitively, and produces motor responses — analogous to a communication channel in Shannon's Information Theory.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Formula-1 race car driver. His eyes (cameras) capture the track, his brain (CPU) processes 200+ inputs/second — speed, gear, tire temp, rival's position — and his hands/feet (actuators) react within milliseconds. The driver is essentially a biological information processor with **limited bandwidth**. Designing a cockpit that respects these biological limits is the essence of Human Factors Engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Miller's Magical Number (1956):** The human short-term memory can hold only **$7 \pm 2$ chunks** of information at any instant. This is a foundational KTU design guideline.

## 1.3 Information Theory (Shannon, 1948)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Information Theory* is the mathematical framework that quantifies the production, transmission, and reception of information. In Human Factors, it provides tools to measure the **information load** placed on a human operator and to design displays that match the operator's channel capacity.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water pipe. The pipe's diameter (bits/second) determines how much "information water" can flow through. A human operator is like a narrow pipe — if too much information is forced in (information overload), some gets "spilled" (errors occur). Information Theory gives us the measuring jug to pour exactly the right amount.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Metric — The Bit:** A **bit** (binary digit) is the basic unit of information, representing the reduction of uncertainty by half. $\log_2(2) = 1$ bit = 1 yes/no answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reliability Function & Failure Rate

The reliability of non-repairable components follows an **exponential distribution** under the assumption of constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The corresponding **Unreliability / CDF of failure** is:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of failure time is:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Why the Exponential Model?
- The exponential distribution is "memoryless": $P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$.
- This matches the *useful-life region* of the **Bathtub Curve**, where random failures dominate.

## 2.2 MTTF, MTBF, MTTR, and Availability

| Symbol | Full Form | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|:------:|:---------:|:-------:|:--------------------|
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Average operating time before failure (non-repairable). |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Average downtime for repair, where $\mu$ is repair rate. |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Average cycle time (repairable systems). |
| **A** | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction of time system is operational. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** A common KTU trap is asking for *availability* when the question gives *MTTF* only. If a system is **non-repairable**, only MTTF is meaningful. If it is **repairable**, you must combine MTTF + MTTR to get MTBF, and then compute availability.

## 2.3 System Reliability — Series, Parallel & Combined Configurations

### 2.3.1 Series Configuration (Weakest-Link System)
All components must work for the system to work. The failure of *any one* element causes system failure.

$$R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n$$

> **Key Insight:** Series reliability is always **less than or equal to** the smallest component reliability. Adding more components *reduces* system reliability.

### 2.3.2 Parallel Configuration (Redundant System)
The system works as long as **at least one** component works.

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

> **Key Insight:** Parallel redundancy **increases** reliability but at the cost of weight, cost, and complexity. Used in aerospace (e.g., triple-redundant flight computers).

### 2.3.3 Combined (Mixed) Configuration
The system is decomposed into series blocks and parallel sub-blocks. Compute the parallel block's reliability first, then multiply in series.

## 2.4 The Human as an Information Processor — Processing Stages

The **Stages Model** (based on Broadbent's Filter Theory and Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory):

1. **Sensory Reception** — Stimuli captured by sense organs (vision, hearing, touch).
2. **Sensory Memory / Pre-Attentive Processing** — Iconic (visual, $\sim$0.25 s) and echoic (auditory, $\sim$4 s) stores.
3. **Perceptual Encoding** — Pattern recognition, feature extraction.
4. **Central (Cognitive) Processing** — Decision-making, working memory manipulation.
5. **Response Selection** — Choosing the appropriate motor program.
6. **Motor Execution** — Physical action output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Channel Theory (Welford, 1968):** The central processor is a **single-channel, serial processor** with a limited capacity of approximately **5–7 bits per second** for conscious decision-making. Bottleneck occurs at **Stage 4**.

## 2.5 Shannon's Information Theory — Core Equations

### 2.5.1 Information Content of a Single Event
$$I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i \quad \text{[bits]}$$

Where $p_i$ is the probability of event $x_i$.

### 2.5.2 Shannon Entropy (Average Information per Symbol)
$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \quad \text{[bits/symbol]}$$

Maximum entropy occurs for a **uniform distribution**: $H_{\max} = \log_2 n$.

### 2.5.3 Channel Capacity (Shannon–Hartley Theorem, for noisy channels)
$$C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/second]}$$

Where $B$ is bandwidth in Hz, $S/N$ is the signal-to-noise power ratio.

### 2.5.4 Information Rate (Time-based)
$$R = \frac{H(X)}{T_{\text{decision}}} \quad \text{[bits/second]}$$

### 2.5.5 Human Channel Capacity (Empirical)
$$C_{\text{human}} \approx 5 \text{ to } 7 \text{ bits/second (sustained cognitive work)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Redundancy Principle:** If a display presents information at a rate $R > C_{\text{human}}$, the design is **informationally overloaded**. Add *redundancy* (color, size, location, modality) to lower the effective information rate per channel.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|:-:|:-------:|:-------:|:-----:|:-----------------|
| 1 | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda$ in failures/hr |
| 2 | MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | hours | Non-repairable |
| 3 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | hours | Repairable |
| 4 | Availability | $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ | dimensionless | $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 5 | Series System | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | dimensionless | $R_s \le \min(R_i)$ |
| 6 | Parallel System | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ | dimensionless | $R_p \ge \max(R_i)$ |
| 7 | Information | $I = -\log_2 p$ | bits | $p \in [0,1]$ |
| 8 | Entropy | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | Max when $p_i$ uniform |
| 9 | Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | bits/sec | Shannon–Hartley |
| 10 | Information Rate | $R = H/T$ | bits/sec | Must be $\le C_{\text{human}}$ |
| 11 | Hick's Law | $T = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ | seconds | $n$ = choice alternatives |
| 12 | Fitts' Law | $T = a + b \log_2(2D/W)$ | seconds | $D$ = distance, $W$ = width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used to design aircraft cockpits, nuclear control rooms, medical device UIs, and driver assistance systems. Any time you compute *"can the human process this signal in time?"* — you are applying System Reliability + Information Theory.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Series System Reliability

**Problem Setup:** A safety-critical system has three components A, B, C connected in series. The reliabilities of individual components over a mission time $t = 100$ hours are $R_A = 0.99$, $R_B = 0.98$, $R_C = 0.97$. Compute the system reliability.

**Step 1 — State the Series Reliability Postulate.**
For a series system, all components must function. Assuming **statistical independence** of component failures:

$$R_{\text{system}} = P(\text{A works AND B works AND C works})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Multiplicative Rule for Independent Events.**

$$R_{\text{system}} = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values.**

$$R_{\text{system}} = R_A \times R_B \times R_C$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Multiplication.**

$$R_{\text{system}} = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97$$

**Step 5 — Sequential Evaluation.**

$$0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.9702$$

**Step 6 — Final Multiplication.**

$$0.9702 \times 0.97 = 0.941094$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer.**

$$R_{\text{system}} \approx 0.9411 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 94.11\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that even though every component is highly reliable ($\ge 97\%$), the **system reliability dropped to $\approx 94.11\%$**. This illustrates the **reliability multiplication penalty** in series design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Parallel (Redundant) System Reliability

**Problem Setup:** Two identical pumps (P1, P2) operate in parallel to supply coolant. Each pump has reliability $R = 0.90$ over the mission. The system works if *at least one* pump works. Compute $R_{\text{system}}$.

**Step 1 — Define Component Unreliability (Probability of Failure).**

$$Q_i = 1 - R_i = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — State the Parallel System Postulate.**
The parallel system *fails* only if **all** components fail simultaneously. Hence, system failure is the intersection of all individual failures.

$$Q_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for 2 Pumps.**

$$Q_{\text{system}} = Q_1 \times Q_2 = 0.10 \times 0.10 = 0.01$$

**Step 4 — Compute System Reliability by Complement.**

$$R_{\text{system}} = 1 - Q_{\text{system}} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$R_{\text{system}} = 0.99 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 99\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Adding a second (parallel) pump *upgraded* the system reliability from **90% → 99%** at the cost of an extra pump. This is the **power of redundancy**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Reliability from Failure Rate (Exponential Model)

**Problem Setup:** A sensor has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Compute (a) reliability at $t = 100$ hr, (b) MTTF, (c) reliability at MTTF.

**Step 1 — Apply the Exponential Reliability Formula.**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $t = 100$ hr.**

$$R(100) = e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation.**

$$e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer for (a).**

$$R(100) \approx 0.9048 \;\; (90.48\%)$$

**Step 5 — Compute MTTF.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \;\text{hours}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Reliability at $t = \text{MTTF}$.**

$$R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-\lambda \cdot (1/\lambda)} = e^{-1} = 0.3679 \;\; (36.79\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive result:** At the *Mean* Time to Failure, the reliability is only **~36.8%**, not 50%! This is a property of the exponential distribution. Many students lose marks here.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Shannon Entropy for a 4-Alternative Display

**Problem Setup:** A warning light on a dashboard can be in 4 equally-likely states (OK, Caution, Warning, Critical). Compute the information content per state and the average entropy.

**Step 1 — Compute Single-Event Information (Uniform Probability).**
Since all 4 states are equally likely, $p_i = 1/4$ for each $i$.

$$I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i = -\log_2(1/4) = \log_2(4) = 2 \;\text{bits}$$

**Step 2 — Verify with First Principles.**
A 2-bit signal can distinguish $2^2 = 4$ states. ✓

**Step 3 — Compute Entropy (Average Information per Symbol).**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i \log_2 p_i = -4 \times \left(\frac{1}{4} \log_2 \frac{1}{4}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$H(X) = -4 \times \frac{1}{4} \times (-2) = 2 \;\text{bits/symbol}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Entropy Verification.**
For a uniform distribution: $H_{\max} = \log_2 n = \log_2 4 = 2$ bits. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means the operator must process **2 bits per symbol glance**. If the warning light flashes every 0.3 seconds, the required information rate is $R = 2/0.3 \approx 6.67$ bits/sec — just within the human capacity.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Channel Capacity via Shannon–Hartley

**Problem Setup:** A voice radio channel has bandwidth $B = 4000$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N = 15$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Shannon–Hartley Theorem.**

$$C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values.**

$$C = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 15) = 4000 \times \log_2(16)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Logarithm.**

$$\log_2(16) = 4 \;\text{bits/sample}$$

**Step 4 — Final Computation.**

$$C = 4000 \times 4 = 16{,}000 \;\text{bits/second} = 16 \;\text{kbps}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Human Channel Capacity.**

$$C_{\text{human}} \approx 5 \text{–} 7 \;\text{bits/sec} \;\; \ll \;\; C_{\text{channel}} = 16{,}000 \;\text{bits/sec}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **machine channel capacity is huge**; the **bottleneck is the human**. Designers must reduce information rate *into* the human, not upgrade the channel.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Formula

| Scenario | Key Indicator | Formula to Use |
|:---------|:--------------|:---------------|
| Single component, constant failure rate | "Random failures" | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| Multiple components, all must work | "System fails if any one fails" | $R_s = \prod R_i$ |
| Multiple components, any one can fail-safe | "Backup / redundant" | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ |
| Repairable system, fraction of uptime | "Operating ratio" | $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ |
| Display design with $n$ equally-likely states | "Information per glance" | $H = \log_2 n$ |
| Noisy communication channel | "Maximum data rate" | $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ |
| Operator choice reaction time | "Reaction time vs. choices" | $T = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ (Hick's) |
| Pointing / selection time | "Movement time vs. target" | $T = a + b \log_2(2D/W)$ (Fitts') |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Human as an Information Processor — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional flow* of information through a human operator, equivalent to a signal-processing pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment Stimulus] --> B[Sensory Receptor]
    B --> C[Sensory Memory Store]
    C --> D[Perceptual Encoder]
    D --> E[Working Memory Buffer]
    E --> F[Central Decision Processor]
    F --> G[Response Selector]
    G --> H[Motor Effector]
    H --> I[Physical Action Output]
    F -.feedback loop.-> E
    G -.feedback loop.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Information enters from the environment, is buffered in sensory memory, encoded perceptually, held briefly in working memory (the **7±2 chunk** bottleneck), processed centrally, and finally executed. The dotted feedback loops represent internal monitoring and self-correction.

---

## 4.2 Series vs. Parallel System Reliability — Architectural View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SERIES["Series Configuration - All Must Work"]
        direction LR
        S1[Component A] --> S2[Component B] --> S3[Component C] --> S_OUT[System Output]
    end

    subgraph PARALLEL["Parallel Configuration - Any One Can Work"]
        direction LR
        P_IN[System Input] --> P1[Component A]
        P_IN --> P2[Component B]
        P_IN --> P3[Component C]
        P1 --> P_OUT[System Output]
        P2 --> P_OUT
        P3 --> P_OUT
    end
```

---

## 4.3 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BATHUB["Bathtub Curve - Failure Rate vs Operating Time"]
        direction LR
        X[Time t hours] --> Y[Failure Rate Lambda]
    end

    Y_axis[Lambda] --- R1["Infant Mortality Region - Decreasing Lambda"]
    R1 --> R2["Useful Life Region - Constant Lambda Random Failures"]
    R2 --> R3["Wear Out Region - Increasing Lambda Aging Failures"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Useful Life Region** is where the exponential model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid. During infant mortality, *burn-in* testing is used to eliminate early failures; during wear-out, *preventive maintenance* is scheduled.

---

## 4.4 Information-Theoretic Display Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Raw Process Variable e.g. Boiler Pressure] --> ENC["Encoder: Map to Display Element"]
    ENC --> INFO["Information Content I = -log2 p bits"]
    INFO --> CHK{Rate R less than Human Capacity C?}
    CHK -- Yes --> SAFE[Display Approved]
    CHK -- No --> RED[Add Redundancy: Color, Size, Modality]
    RED --> RATE["Recompute R = H divided by T"]
    RATE --> CHK
```

> [!NOTE]
> This iterative loop embodies the **Human Factors design principle**: *reduce information rate to match human channel capacity* by using redundant coding.

---

## 4.5 Combined (Mixed) System Reliability — Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SYS[System] --> SB1[Series Block 1]
    SYS --> SB2[Series Block 2]
    SB1 --> PB1["Parallel Sub-block: Pump 1 + Pump 2"]
    SB1 --> CB1[Component C]
    SB2 --> CB2[Component D]
    SB2 --> PB2["Parallel Sub-block: Sensor 1 + Sensor 2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-down procedure:** (1) Compute $R_{PB1}$ using parallel formula. (2) Compute $R_{PB2}$ similarly. (3) Compute $R_{SB1} = R_{PB1} \times R_{C}$. (4) Compute $R_{SB2} = R_{D} \times R_{PB2}$. (5) Compute $R_{\text{system}} = R_{SB1} \times R_{SB2}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define *System Reliability* in the context of Human Factors Engineering. Differentiate between MTTF and MTBF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*System Reliability* is the probability that a system (human + machine + environment) performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time, expressed as $R(t) = P(T > t)$ **[1 mark]**.

| Aspect | MTTF | MTBF |
|:-------|:-----|:-----|
| Applicable to | Non-repairable systems | Repairable systems |
| Definition | Mean Time To Failure | Mean Time Between Failures |
| Formula | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ **[2 marks]** |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**State and explain Shannon's formula for average information (entropy) per symbol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The average information content (entropy) of a source emitting symbols from set $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ with probabilities $p_i$ is **[1 mark]**:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \quad \text{[bits/symbol]}$$

The information content of a single event is $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ bits. Entropy is the *expected* (average) information per symbol. It is maximum when all symbols are equiprobable, $H_{\max} = \log_2 n$ **[2 marks]**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With the aid of suitable expressions, explain **Series and Parallel system reliability models**. A safety system has 3 components in series with reliabilities 0.95, 0.92, and 0.90. Compute the system reliability. If a fourth identical component (reliability 0.90) is added in **parallel** with the weakest component, recompute the new system reliability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC machine has MTTF = 8000 hours and MTTR = 50 hours. Compute (i) MTBF, (ii) Steady-state availability, (iii) the reliability at $t = 2000$ hours, and (iv) the unreliability at the same instant. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Series Configuration (1.5 marks):**
In a series configuration, *all* components must function for the system to function. For $n$ statistically independent components:

$$R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n$$

Series systems are *weakest-link* configurations. Adding more components **decreases** reliability.

**Step 2 — Parallel Configuration (1.5 marks):**
In a parallel configuration, the system works if *at least one* component works:

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

Parallel systems add **redundancy** and increase reliability at the cost of extra hardware.

**Step 3 — Original Series Computation (2 marks):**

$$R_{\text{original}} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90$$

**Step 4 — Arithmetic:**

$$0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874$$

**Step 5 — Final Multiplication:**

$$0.874 \times 0.90 = 0.7866$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{original}} = 0.7866 \;\;(78.66\%)}$$

**Step 6 — Add Parallel Redundancy to the Weakest Component (0.90) (2 marks):**

$$R_{\text{parallel-pair}} = 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.90) = 1 - 0.10 \times 0.10 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Step 7 — Recompute Series System:**

$$R_{\text{new}} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.99$$

**Step 8 — Arithmetic:**

$$0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874$$

**Step 9 — Final Multiplication:**

$$0.874 \times 0.99 = 0.8653$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{new}} = 0.8653 \;\;(86.53\%)}$$

**Conclusion:** Adding a parallel redundant component improved system reliability from **78.66% → 86.53%**, an increase of $\approx 7.87$ percentage points.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** MTTF = 8000 hr, MTTR = 50 hr.

**Step 1 — Compute MTBF (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 8000 + 50 = 8050 \;\text{hr}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Failure Rate (1 mark):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{8000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{failures/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Steady-State Availability (1.5 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{8050}{8050 + 50} = \frac{8050}{8100} \approx 0.9938$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 99.38\%}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Reliability at $t = 2000$ hr (2 marks):**

$$R(2000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Exponent:**

$$-\lambda t = -0.25$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation:**

$$R(2000) = e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$$

$$\boxed{R(2000) \approx 77.88\%}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Unreliability (1 mark):**

$$F(2000) = 1 - R(2000) = 1 - 0.7788 = 0.2212$$

$$\boxed{F(2000) \approx 22.12\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **Human Information Processing Model** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the significance of Miller's "Magical Number Seven" in display design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cockpit warning light can be in 5 equally-likely states. The light is updated every 2 seconds. Apply Shannon's Information Theory to compute (i) information per state, (ii) average entropy per update, (iii) information rate in bits/sec, and (iv) comment on whether the design respects the human channel capacity. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Define the Model (1 mark):**
The human operator is modelled as a *limited-capacity information processor* with discrete stages: sensory reception → sensory memory → perceptual encoding → working memory → central decision → response selection → motor execution.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
- **Sensory Reception:** Eyes, ears, skin capture raw stimuli.
- **Sensory Memory:** Iconic (~0.25 s) and echoic (~4 s) buffers.
- **Perceptual Encoding:** Pattern recognition using long-term memory schemata.
- **Working Memory:** Limited-capacity buffer (**$7 \pm 2$ chunks**, Miller 1956).
- **Central Decision:** Single-channel serial processor (~5–7 bits/sec).
- **Response Selection & Motor Output:** Choice of motor program and execution.

**Step 3 — Significance of Miller's $7 \pm 2$ (3 marks):**
George Miller (1956) showed that the *span of immediate attention* is approximately **7 ± 2 chunks** of information. Implications for display design:
- A single instrument panel should not present more than **5–9 distinct indicators** without grouping.
- Use **chunking** (e.g., grouping altitude + speed + heading into a "Primary Flight Display").
- Avoid display designs that force the operator to remember more than 7 elements; instead, place critical information in the environment.
- This rule underlies the design of *multi-function displays*, *hierarchical menus*, and *alarm prioritization*.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $n = 5$ equally-likely states, update period $T = 2$ s.

**Step 1 — Information per State (1.5 marks):**

$$I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i = -\log_2\left(\frac{1}{5}\right) = \log_2 5$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**

$$\log_2 5 = \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.6094}{0.6931} \approx 2.3219 \;\text{bits}$$

$$\boxed{I(x_i) \approx 2.32 \;\text{bits/state}}$$

**Step 3 — Average Entropy per Update (1.5 marks):**
For a uniform distribution, $H = \log_2 n = \log_2 5$:

$$\boxed{H(X) \approx 2.32 \;\text{bits/update}}$$

**Step 4 — Information Rate (2 marks):**

$$R = \frac{H(X)}{T} = \frac{2.3219 \;\text{bits}}{2 \;\text{s}} = 1.161 \;\text{bits/sec}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 1.16 \;\text{bits/sec}}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Human Channel Capacity (2 marks):**
Human sustained cognitive capacity: $C_{\text{human}} \approx 5\text{–}7$ bits/sec.

Since $R = 1.16 \;\text{bits/sec} \ll 5 \;\text{bits/sec}$, **the design respects the human channel capacity**. The operator has significant cognitive reserve to handle other concurrent tasks. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If** the update period were 0.3 seconds instead, $R = 2.32/0.3 \approx 7.74$ bits/sec, which would **exceed** human capacity → information overload → design must be revised with redundancy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Series vs. Parallel Confusion:** Students often write the parallel formula for a series system (or vice versa). *Always* identify the configuration from the problem statement: "all must work" → series; "any one can work" → parallel.
> 2. **MTTF vs. MTBF Mix-up:** Using MTTF directly in the availability formula. Remember: $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$, not MTTF.
> 3. **Forgetting Independence:** Series/parallel formulas assume **statistically independent** component failures. If failures are dependent (common-cause), the formulas overestimate reliability.
