> 4. **Logarithm Base Error:** Information theory uses $\log_2$ (base 2) for **bits**. Using $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ yields wrong units.
> 5. **Uniform Distribution Assumption:** Entropy is maximum *only* when $p_i$ are equal. Students often write $H = \log_2 n$ without justification.
> 6. **Exponent Sign Error:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — the negative sign is critical. Writing $e^{+\lambda t}$ gives values $> 1$, which is impossible.
> 7. **Ignoring Mission Time:** If a problem says "for 500 hours," you *must* include $t$ in the calculation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: System Reliability & Information Theory**
>
> **System Reliability:**
> - Reliability is a *probability* between 0 and 1; it decreases monotonically with time under constant failure rate.
> - Exponential model: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid in the *useful-life* region of the Bathtub Curve.
> - $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ is the *mean* lifetime, not the *guaranteed* lifetime. $R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$.
> - Series: $R_s = \prod R_i$ (multiplicative penalty). Parallel: $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ (redundancy boost).
> - Availability $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ applies *only* to repairable systems.
> - Combined systems: decompose into series blocks containing parallel sub-blocks; compute bottom-up.
>
> **Human as Information Processor:**
> - Stages: Sensation → Sensory Memory → Perception → Working Memory → Decision → Motor Output.
> - **Miller's $7 \pm 2$** is the working memory capacity limit.
> - **Single-channel bottleneck** at central processing; capacity $\approx$ **5–7 bits/sec** for conscious decisions.
> - Design implication: limit information rate, add redundancy (color, size, location, modality), chunk related data.
>
> **Information Theory (Shannon):**
> - Information $I = -\log_2 p$ [bits]; Entropy $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ [bits/symbol].
> - Maximum entropy = $\log_2 n$ for uniform distribution.
> - Channel capacity (noisy): $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ [bits/sec] (Shannon–Hartley).
> - Information rate: $R = H/T$ must satisfy $R \le C_{\text{human}}$ for operator compatibility.
> - **Redundancy** lowers effective information load per channel.
>
> **Key Numerical Facts to Memorize:**
> - $e^{-1} = 0.3679$ (reliability at MTTF).
> - $\log_2 2 = 1, \log_2 4 = 2, \log_2 8 = 3, \log_2 10 \approx 3.32$.
> - Human channel capacity ≈ **5–7 bits/sec**; absolute judgment limit ≈ **4–7 levels per dimension**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displaying Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Displaying Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Human Factors Engineering (Ergonomics)**, a **Display** is any device, instrument, panel, or surface element that presents information from a machine, process, or environment to a human operator in a perceivable form (visual, auditory, or tactile) to support **decision-making, monitoring, and control** actions.

The discipline of **Displaying Information** deals with the systematic design, selection, and arrangement of these information-presenting elements so that the operator can:
- **Perceive** the data accurately
- **Interpret** the data correctly
- **Respond** to the data quickly and safely

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Displays are classified as a part of the **Man-Machine System (MMS)** interface. Every display must be designed to match the **sensory, cognitive, and anthropometric** capabilities of the intended human operator.

### 1.2 Types of Information Display (Sensory Modality)

| Modality | Display Type | Typical Use |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Visual** | Gauges, screens, LEDs, dials | Primary channel (70–80% of all displays) |
| **Auditory** | Alarms, buzzers, voice | Warning and alerting |
| **Tactile** | Vibrators, Braille, force feedback | Special / assistive use |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Car Dashboard:**
> Imagine you are driving a car. The **speedometer** tells you *how fast* you are going (kinetic info), the **fuel gauge** tells you *how much fuel remains* (status info), and a **red oil-pressure warning light** alerts you to a *dangerous condition* (alarm info). Each of these is a **display**. A poorly placed speedometer, an illegible fuel gauge, or a confusing warning light causes the driver to take the **wrong action** — sometimes fatally. Designing displays properly ensures the operator always gets the **right information, at the right time, in the right form.**

### 1.4 Why Display Design Matters

- Reduces **human error** in critical systems (aviation, nuclear, medical).
- Decreases **reaction time** and improves operator performance.
- Reduces **training time** for new operators.
- Improves **safety, productivity, and comfort**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Visual Displays

Visual displays are broadly classified based on how information is encoded:

#### A. Quantitative Displays
These present **numerical or measurable** data.

1. **Digital / Readout Displays**
   - Show precise numerical values (e.g., digital clock, multimeter reading).
   - **Best for:** Reading exact values (temperature = 98.6 °F).
   - **Limitation:** Poor for showing rate, trend, or direction of change.

2. **Analog / Dial / Gauge Displays**
   - Use a **pointer on a scale** (e.g., analog speedometer, pressure gauge).
   - **Best for:** Showing rate of change, trend, direction, and approximate magnitude.
   - **Sub-types:** Fixed-scale, moving-scale, moving-pointer, counter-pointer.

#### B. Qualitative Displays
These indicate **status, condition, or category** — not precise numbers.

1. **Check / Status Indicators** — ON/OFF lights, ready indicators.
2. **Warning Indicators** — alarms, caution lights, red signals.
3. **Symbolic / Pictorial Displays** — icons (low fuel pump symbol, oil can).

#### C. Alphanumeric Displays
- Present **letters, numbers, and symbols** (LCD screens, computer monitors, cockpit MDIs).
- Used when complex textual information must be communicated.

### 2.2 Selection Criteria: Which Display to Use?

| Information Type | Best Display Type | Reason |
|------------------|-------------------|--------|
| **Precise numerical value** | Digital readout | No interpolation error |
| **Rate / trend / direction** | Analog dial | Spatial mapping is direct |
| **ON/OFF / status** | Indicator light | Immediate recognition |
| **Warning / danger** | Red flashing light + auditory alarm | Multi-modal alerting |
| **Complex data (text, maps)** | Alphanumeric / pictorial display | High information density |
| **Spatial / location info** | Pictorial / map display | Realistic representation |

### 2.3 Design Principles for Visual Displays

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** expects students to know the following universal display design rules (originally codified by **Morse, Sandercock, and McCormick**):

1. **Principle of Direct Perception** — Display should look like what it represents.
2. **Principle of Compatibility** — The mapping between display and control should be **natural** (e.g., move a switch up to make a value go up).
3. **Principle of Simplicity** — Use only as much detail as required by the task.
4. **Principle of Predictability** — Display behavior should be consistent and expected.
5. **Principle of Legibility** — Letters, numbers, and symbols must be readable under all conditions.

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Display Design

- **Size and distance** of display from the operator
- **Illumination** (ambient light, glare)
- **Color** (use of standard color codes)
- **Contrast** between figure and ground
- **Vibration and motion** in the environment
- **Operator's vision** (corrective lenses, aging)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are high-yield for KTU numerical and short-answer questions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------|------|
| 1 | **Visual Angle** ($\theta$) | $\theta = \dfrac{h}{D}$ (small-angle radian approximation) | $h$ = character height, $D$ = viewing distance | **radians (rad)** |
| 2 | **Visual Angle (arc minutes)** | $\theta = \dfrac{h}{D} \times 3438$ | $h$ in mm, $D$ in m | **arc minutes (′)** |
| 3 | **Minimum Legible Visual Angle** | $\theta_{\min} = 12 \text{ to } 30 \text{ arc minutes}$ | Constant for normal vision | **arc minutes (′)** |
| 4 | **Character Height (minimum)** | $h = \dfrac{\theta_{\min} \cdot D}{3438}$ | $\theta_{\min}$ in arc min, $D$ in m | **mm** |
| 5 | **Stroke Width (numeric)** | $SW = \dfrac{h}{8} \text{ to } \dfrac{h}{6}$ | $h$ = character height | **mm** |
| 6 | **Stroke Width to Gap Ratio** | $\dfrac{SW}{Gap} \geq 1$ | $SW$ = stroke width, $Gap$ = gap between strokes | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Information Transfer Rate (Hick-Hyman related)** | $H = \dfrac{\log_2 N}{T}$ | $N$ = number of alternatives, $T$ = reaction time | **bits/s** |
| 8 | **Viewing Distance Rule (general)** | $D \approx 500 \text{ to } 1000 \text{ mm}$ for control panels | — | **mm** |
| 9 | **Luminance Contrast Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_f \vert}{L_b}$ | $L_b$ = background luminance, $L_f$ = figure luminance | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Required Contrast for legibility** | $CR \geq 0.5$ (minimum) | — | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Pipe Symbol:** In all formulas above, the absolute-value bars around $(L_b - L_f)$ are written using the LaTeX command `\vert` (or `\mid`) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** Glass cockpits use **PFDs (Primary Flight Displays)** combining analog tapes (altitude, airspeed) with digital readouts and symbolic attitude indicators.
- **Medical:** Patient monitors display ECG waveforms (analog) + numeric vitals (digital) + alarms (qualitative).
- **Industrial Control:** SCADA screens integrate trend graphs, status indicators, and alphanumeric data on a single display.
- **Automotive:** Modern cars use **digital instrument clusters** with configurable modes, blending analog-style graphics and numeric data.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Character Height for Legible Display

**Problem Setup (KTU-style numerical):**
An operator views a numerical display panel at a distance of **800 mm**. If the minimum legible visual angle for a normal eye is **20 arc minutes**, determine the **minimum character height** required on the display.

**Step 1 — Recall the legibility formula:**

$$h = \dfrac{\theta_{\min} \cdot D}{3438}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the known values.**

- $\theta_{\min} = 20$ arc minutes
- $D = 800$ mm

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$h = \dfrac{20 \times 800}{3438}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator first.**

$$20 \times 800 = 16000 \text{ mm} \cdot \text{arc min}$$

**Step 5 — Divide to obtain $h$.**

$$h = \dfrac{16000}{3438} = 4.654 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Round up to a practical design value (engineering safety margin).**

$$h_{\text{design}} \approx 5 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The character on the display should be at least **5 mm tall** when viewed from 800 mm. Designers usually adopt the **next standard size** (e.g., 5 mm, 6 mm, or 8 mm) to account for vibration, glare, and operator aging.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Visual Angle from Object Size and Distance

**Problem Setup:**
A warning sign has a **symbol height of 40 mm** and is observed from a **distance of 2.5 m**. Find the visual angle subtended at the eye.

**Step 1 — Use the radian form of visual angle.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{h}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Convert distance to the same unit (mm).**

$$D = 2.5 \text{ m} = 2500 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the visual angle in radians.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{40}{2500} = 0.016 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Convert radians to degrees (optional, for human interpretation).**

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = 0.016 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 0.917^{\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Convert degrees to arc minutes for comparison with the legibility standard.**

$$\theta_{\text{arc min}} = 0.917 \times 60 = 55.02 \text{ arc minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since 55 arc minutes is **greater than the minimum 20 arc minutes**, the symbol is **legible** at 2.5 m. The margin (55 − 20 = 35 arc min) is the designer's **safety buffer** for adverse conditions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Luminance Contrast for a Display

**Problem Setup:**
A digital display has black characters ($L_f = 5 \text{ cd/m}^2$) on a yellow-green background ($L_b = 80 \text{ cd/m}^2$). Determine the **contrast ratio** and check if it meets the minimum legibility criterion.

**Step 1 — Recall the contrast ratio formula.**

$$CR = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_f \vert}{L_b}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute difference.**

$$\vert L_b - L_f \vert = \vert 80 - 5 \vert = 75 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Divide by background luminance.**

$$CR = \dfrac{75}{80} = 0.9375$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the minimum legibility threshold ($CR \geq 0.5$).**

$$0.9375 \geq 0.5 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The display is **highly legible**. Most industrial and aviation standards recommend $CR \geq 0.6$ for outdoor / high-glare conditions, so this design is **acceptable for indoor control rooms** and excellent for daylight cabins.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Analog vs Digital Displays

| Task Demand | Best Display | Mathematical / Behavioral Justification |
|-------------|--------------|----------------------------------------|
| Reading a **specific numeric value** (e.g., 7.32 mA) | **Digital** | Digital has **zero interpolation error**; analog introduces pointer-position estimation error. |
| Detecting **trend** (rising/falling temperature) | **Analog** | Pointer movement is a **direct spatial mapping** to change — pre-attentive processing in the human visual system. |
| Detecting **rate of change** | **Analog (with expanding scale if needed)** | Velocity of pointer = derivative of signal; the eye naturally tracks motion. |
| Showing **status** (pump ON/OFF) | **Indicator light** | Binary categorical information; numeric value is irrelevant. |
| **Alarm** / abnormal condition | **Flashing red + auditory tone** | Multi-modal alerting captures attention even if operator's gaze is elsewhere. |
| **Spatial** relationships (where is the fault on a grid?) | **Pictorial / mimic display** | Spatial layout matches real-world layout; reduces mental translation. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Visual Displays

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Visual Display] --> B[Quantitative]
    A --> C[Qualitative]
    A --> D[Alphanumeric]
    B --> B1[Digital Readout]
    B --> B2[Analog Dial]
    B2 --> B2a[Fixed Scale Moving Pointer]
    B2 --> B2b[Moving Scale Fixed Pointer]
    B2 --> B2c[Counter Pointer]
    C --> C1[Check Indicator]
    C --> C2[Warning Indicator]
    C --> C3[Symbolic Pictorial]
    D --> D1[LCD Screen]
    D --> D2[LED Matrix]
    D --> D3[Cathode Ray Tube]
```

### 4.2 Display Selection Decision Tree (Based on Task Type)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Information to Display] --> Q1{Is exact numeric value required?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Use Digital Readout]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is trend or rate of change important?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Use Analog Dial]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is it a binary status ON or OFF?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P4[Use Indicator Light]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is it an alarm or abnormal state?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> P5[Use Flashing Red Light + Auditory Alarm]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Is the data text or complex symbols?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> P7[Use Alphanumeric Display]
    Q6 -- No --> P8[Use Symbolic Pictorial Display]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Man-Machine Display System

```mermaid
graph TD
    PROC[Process or Machine] --> SENS[Sensor and Transducer]
    SENS --> SIG[Signal Conditioning Unit]
    SIG --> DSP[Display Processor or Controller]
    DSP --> DISP[Visual Display Unit]
    DISP --> OP[Human Operator]
    OP --> EYE[Sensory Reception Eye Ear Skin]
    EYE --> BRAIN[Perception and Cognition]
    BRAIN --> DEC[Decision Making]
    DEC --> CTRL[Control Action]
    CTRL --> PROC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** The flow moves from the **machine side** (left) through the **display** to the **human operator** (right) and back. This is the **classical Man-Machine Loop**; the display is the **information link** from machine to human, while the control is the **action link** from human to machine.

### 4.4 Color-Coding Standards (Indicative)

```mermaid
graph LR
    RED[RED]:::warn --> D1[Danger or Emergency]
    YEL[YELLOW / AMBER]:::caution --> D2[Caution or Abnormal]
    GRN[GREEN]:::ok --> D3[Normal or Safe]
    BLU[BLUE]:::info --> D4[Information or Advisory]
    WHT[WHITE]:::neutral --> D5[Neutral or Status Confirmation]
    classDef warn fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef caution fill:#ffd633,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ok fill:#33cc33,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef info fill:#3399ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef neutral fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Color-Code Convention (used in ANSI / IEC 60073):**
> - **Red** = Danger / Emergency stop
> - **Yellow / Amber** = Caution / Abnormal
> - **Green** = Safe / Normal running
> - **Blue** = Mandatory action / Information
> - **White / Grey** = Neutral / Status

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Analog and Digital displays. Under what task conditions is each preferred? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Analog Display | Digital Display |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Presentation | Pointer on a continuous scale | Discrete numerical characters |
| Information | Continuous, qualitative trend | Precise, exact numerical value |
| Reading | Requires interpolation | Direct, no interpolation |
| Reading error | Higher (estimation) | Lower (exact) |
| Reaction time | Faster for trend detection | Faster for exact-value reading |
| Best for | Rate, trend, direction, approximate value | Precise magnitude, alarm limits |

- **Analog preferred** when: trend, rate, or direction of change matters more than exact value (e.g., vehicle speed, boiler pressure).
- **Digital preferred** when: exact value is critical (e.g., medical dose, scientific reading).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three design principles for visual displays used in human-machine systems. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Principle of Compatibility:** The display arrangement should be compatible with the operator's natural expectations and the corresponding control movements (e.g., moving a switch up should increase the indicated value).
2. **Principle of Direct Perception:** The display should visually resemble what it represents — a pictorial fuel gauge shaped like a fuel tank is more intuitive than a generic circular dial.
3. **Principle of Legibility:** Characters, symbols, and scale markings must be readable under the worst-case lighting and vibration conditions, with adequate contrast and character height.
4. (Optional 4th) **Principle of Simplicity:** Display only the information needed for the task; avoid clutter and unnecessary detail.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Avoid one-word answers in Part B.** Each sub-part (7 marks) requires **definitions, justifications, examples, and a small diagram or table**.
> - For numerical questions, **always show the formula, substitution, and final answer with units**. Marks are split across these steps.
> - **Do not confuse analog with digital selection criteria.** Marks are specifically awarded for matching the task type to the correct display.
> - Mention **standard color codes** (Red/Danger, Yellow/Caution, Green/Safe) when discussing warning indicators — this is a frequent marker in KTU valuation keys.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a) Classify the various types of visual displays used in human-machine systems. Explain with examples where each type is most suitably used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Visual displays are classified into three broad categories based on the type of information they convey:

**(i) Quantitative Displays — Digital (Readout):**
- Present exact numerical values as discrete digits.
- Examples: digital clock, digital voltmeter, cockpit altimeter readout.
- Best when **precise readings** are required.

**(ii) Quantitative Displays — Analog (Dial):**
- Use a pointer moving over a calibrated scale to indicate a continuous value.
- Sub-types: fixed-scale moving-pointer, moving-scale fixed-pointer, counter-pointer.
- Examples: speedometer, pressure gauge, analog multimeter.
- Best for **trend, rate, and direction** detection.

**(iii) Qualitative Displays:**
- Convey **status or condition**, not a numerical magnitude.
- Sub-types:
  - **Check indicators** — green light when a system is ready.
  - **Warning indicators** — red flashing light for fire alarm.
  - **Symbolic / pictorial** — low-fuel pump icon on a dashboard.

**(iv) Alphanumeric Displays:**
- Present letters, numbers, and symbols.
- Examples: aircraft MFD (Multi-Function Display), industrial HMI screens, computer monitors.

**Selection Summary Table:**

| Task | Best Display |
|------|--------------|
| Precise numeric value | Digital |
| Rate / trend | Analog |
| Status / state | Indicator light |
| Alarm | Flashing + sound |
| Text / complex data | Alphanumeric / pictorial |

---

**(b) An operator reads a digital display panel from a distance of 1200 mm. If the minimum legible visual angle for the operator is 18 arc minutes, calculate the minimum character height required. Also state the standard stroke width range. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**

- Viewing distance: $D = 1200$ mm
- Minimum legible visual angle: $\theta_{\min} = 18$ arc minutes

**Step 2 — Apply the legibility formula.**

$$h = \dfrac{\theta_{\min} \cdot D}{3438}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$h = \dfrac{18 \times 1200}{3438}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$18 \times 1200 = 21600$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$h = \dfrac{21600}{3438} = 6.283 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Round up to a practical design value.**

$$h_{\text{design}} \approx 7 \text{ mm (or next standard size)}$$

**Step 7 — Standard stroke width (from formula sheet).**

$$SW = \dfrac{h}{8} \text{ to } \dfrac{h}{6}$$

$$SW = \dfrac{6.283}{8} \text{ to } \dfrac{6.283}{6} = 0.785 \text{ mm to } 1.047 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum character height} \approx 6.28 \text{ mm};\ \text{Stroke width} \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.05 \text{ mm}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**(a) Explain the different types of analog displays with neat sketches. Compare fixed-scale and moving-scale dial indicators. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Analog Displays:**

1. **Fixed-Scale, Moving-Pointer Dial:** The most common analog form. The scale is fixed and the pointer rotates over it. *Example: car speedometer, pressure gauge.* Best for general-purpose indication of magnitude, trend, and direction.

2. **Moving-Scale, Fixed-Pointer Indicator:** The pointer is fixed and the scale slides beneath it. *Example: some industrial barometers.* Best for applications where a fixed reference frame is desired.

3. **Counter-Pointer Dial:** Two pointers move in opposite directions. *Example: precision balances, weighing scales.* Best for fine discrimination of small differences over a wide range.

4. **Vertical Strip / Tape Display:** Values shown on a vertical moving scale (as in modern aircraft Primary Flight Display for altitude). Best for very high precision and large dynamic range.

5. **Circular vs. Horizontal Scales:** Horizontal strip scales reduce parallax error in long panels; circular scales suit compact panels.

**Comparison: Fixed-Scale vs. Moving-Scale**

| Feature | Fixed-Scale Moving-Pointer | Moving-Scale Fixed-Pointer |
|---------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Reading position | Eyes follow the moving pointer | Eyes stay at the fixed pointer |
| Suitable for | Continuously changing values | Values that change infrequently |
| Common use | Speedometer, pressure gauge | Weighing scales, clocks |
| Reading error | Pointer-parallax error possible | Scale-parallax error possible |
| Fatigue | Slightly higher (eye tracking) | Lower |

---

**(b) Define "Visual Angle" and "Luminance Contrast" as applied to display design. A display has figure luminance 8 cd/m² and background luminance 90 cd/m². Determine the contrast ratio and comment on its legibility. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

- **Visual Angle ($\theta$):** The angle subtended at the eye by the smallest detail of a display (e.g., character height, stroke width). It determines whether a feature is **resolvable** by the eye. The minimum legible visual angle for a normal eye is **12–30 arc minutes**.

- **Luminance Contrast Ratio ($CR$):** A dimensionless measure of the difference in brightness between the figure (character/symbol) and the background of a display. It is defined as:

$$CR = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_f \vert}{L_b}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**

- $L_f = 8 \text{ cd/m}^2$
- $L_b = 90 \text{ cd/m}^2$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute difference.**

$$\vert L_b - L_f \vert = \vert 90 - 8 \vert = 82 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $L_b$.**

$$CR = \dfrac{82}{90} = 0.9111$$

**Step 4 — Compare with minimum legibility criterion ($CR \geq 0.5$).**

$$0.9111 \geq 0.5 \quad \checkmark$$

**Comment:** The contrast ratio is approximately **0.91**, which is **well above** the minimum legibility threshold of 0.5. The display is therefore **highly legible** and suitable for use in both normal indoor lighting and moderately bright ambient conditions.

$$\boxed{CR \approx 0.91;\ \text{Display is highly legible.}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **display** is the man-machine link for **information transfer** from machine to human.
- **Visual displays** are primary; auditory and tactile are supplementary.
- **Digital displays** → for **precise numerical values** (low interpolation error).
- **Analog displays** → for **trend, rate, and direction** (fast pre-attentive processing).
- **Indicator lights** → for **status and binary conditions** (ON/OFF, ready/fault).
- **Pictorial / symbolic displays** → for **spatial information** and rapid recognition.
- **Color coding (standard):** Red = danger, Yellow/Amber = caution, Green = safe, Blue = info, White = neutral.
- **Visual angle formula:** $\theta = \dfrac{h}{D}$ (rad) and $\theta = \dfrac{h \times 3438}{D \text{ in m}}$ (arc min).
- **Minimum legible visual angle:** 12–30 arc minutes (use **20 arc min** as the KTU default).
- **Character height formula:** $h = \dfrac{\theta_{\min} \cdot D}{3438}$ (mm).
- **Stroke width:** $h/8$ to $h/6$ of character height.
- **Luminance contrast:** $CR = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_f \vert}{L_b}$; minimum acceptable $CR \geq 0.5$.
- **Standard viewing distance** for control panels: **500–1000 mm**.
- **Hick-Hyman information rate:** $H = \dfrac{\log_2 N}{T}$ — more alternatives = longer reaction time.
- **Key design principles:** Compatibility, Legibility, Simplicity, Predictability, Direct Perception.
- **Common KTU pitfalls:** mixing analog/digital selection logic, missing the stroke-width sub-question, omitting units in numerical answers, ignoring color-code conventions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coding of Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coding of Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Coding of Information** is the systematic process of assigning or mapping a set of distinguishable symbols, characters, colors, shapes, sounds, or spatial positions to a corresponding set of data, variables, or operational states, such that the human operator can **perceive, identify, interpret, and respond** to the information rapidly, accurately, and with minimum cognitive load.

In Human Factors Engineering (HFE), coding is a cornerstone principle of **display and control design**. The human senses (vision, hearing, touch) act as limited-capacity channels, and coding acts as a "compression-translation" mechanism that maps raw machine data into sensory-friendly representations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable Form):**
> *"Coding of Information is the assignment of meaningful symbols or signals to information classes so that the user can discriminate, identify, and act upon them efficiently, exploiting the perceptual capabilities of the human sensory system."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a busy **railway station in Kerala** watching the **arrival-departure board**. You do not read the word "CHENNAI EXPRESS – 14:35 – PLATFORM 3" every time. Instead, your eyes instantly lock onto:
- The **color** of the train category tag (red = Express, green = Local).
- The **direction arrow** pointing to the platform.
- The **font size** indicating urgency (larger = imminent).

This is **coding** — information has been *encoded* into visual variables (color, shape, size) that your brain decodes in milliseconds without conscious effort.

> **Another Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **car dashboard** — red engine icon = danger, yellow fuel icon = warning, green turn indicator = active. A child who cannot read can still understand the meaning. That is the power of well-designed coding.

### 1.3 Why Coding Matters in Engineering Design

| Without Coding | With Coding |
|----------------|-------------|
| Operator reads 5-line text labels | Operator perceives in <500 ms |
| High mental workload | Reduced cognitive load |
| Frequent reading errors | < 5% recognition error |
| Slow decision time | Fast, parallel processing |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Sensory Limits

The following **biophysical limits** govern the design of any coding system:

- **Visual channel:** Foveal cone density ~150,000 cones/mm²; color perception under photopic vision peaks at 555 nm (green-yellow).
- **Auditory channel:** Human hearing range = **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz**; speech intelligibility band = **300 Hz – 3,400 Hz**.
- **Working memory span:** **7 ± 2** discrete items (Miller's Magical Number, 1956).
- **Absolute judgment limit:** Without coding, humans can reliably distinguish only **3–5** levels of any single variable (Pollack's Law).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pollack's Law (1952):** The number of absolutely identifiable stimulus levels along a single auditory or visual dimension is approximately **2.3 bits** of information, corresponding to about **5–6 distinguishable categories**, unless the dimension is coded using redundancy (combining two or more variables).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Information Coding

Coding is implemented through a **four-stage pipeline**:

1. **Stimulus Generation** — The system produces a physical signal (light, sound, vibration).
2. **Encoding by Designer** — The designer maps the *meaning* (concept) onto a *physical variable* (color = red).
3. **Sensory Reception** — The human sensory organ transduces the physical signal (cones detect 700 nm).
4. **Perceptual Decoding** — The brain matches the sensation to a stored template (red → danger).

The success of the entire chain depends on **Channel Capacity (C)** and **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S/N)**.

### 2.2 Classification of Codes (Taxonomy)

Codes are classified along **two principal axes**: *Sensory Modality* and *Dimensional Structure*.

#### A. By Sensory Modality

- **Visual Codes** — Color, shape, size, position, brightness, motion, alphanumeric.
- **Auditory Codes** — Pitch, loudness, duration, timbre, spatial location.
- **Tactile Codes** — Vibration frequency, amplitude, pattern, duration.
- **Olfactory/Gustatory Codes** — Rarely used (e.g., odorized natural gas — mercaptan).

#### B. By Dimensional Structure

- **Quantitative Codes** — Encode magnitude (e.g., thermometer height = temperature).
- **Qualitative Codes** — Encode identity or category (e.g., red triangle = warning).

### 2.3 Detailed Type-by-Type Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Color Coding
Color is the **most powerful and most misused** code. It enables **pre-attentive processing** — the brain detects color in < 250 ms, before conscious attention.

- **Recommended palette (ANSI Z535.1 & ISO 3864):**
  - **Red** → Danger / Prohibition / Fire / Stop
  - **Yellow / Amber** → Caution / Warning
  - **Blue** → Mandatory Action / Information
  - **Green** → Safety / Go / Normal condition
- **Limit:** A maximum of **5–7** colors should be used. Beyond this, confusion rates rise sharply.
- **Failure modes:** Color-blindness (8% of males, 0.5% of females have red-green deficiency); therefore, color must **never be the sole code** — pair it with shape or position.

#### 2.3.2 Shape Coding
Shapes are **independent of color vision** and survive photocopying, fading, and low light. ISO 7010 establishes global standardized shapes:

- **Circle with diagonal bar** → Prohibition
- **Solid circle** → Mandatory action
- **Triangle** → Warning
- **Square / Rectangle** → Safe condition / Information
- **Cross/Plus** → First aid
- **Flame symbol** → Flammable

#### 2.3.3 Alphanumeric Coding
Used where precise identity is required (e.g., aircraft tail numbers **VT-AXN**). Alphanumeric codes can be **mnemonic** (designed to be remembered) or **arbitrary** (e.g., resistor color code).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The **Resistor Color Code** (BBROY of Great Britain had a Very Good Wife) is a classic industrial application of coding:
> **Black=0, Brown=1, Red=2, Orange=3, Yellow=4, Green=5, Blue=6, Violet=7, Grey=8, White=9**

#### 2.3.4 Position Coding
Spatial location carries meaning (e.g., traffic light vertical order: Red-Top, Amber-Middle, Green-Bottom). The brain processes position **faster than color**.

#### 2.3.5 Size Coding
Used to encode magnitude (e.g., bar graph height = sales). A form of **quantitative coding**.

#### 2.3.6 Auditory Coding
Critical when vision is overloaded (aviation, emergency alarms). Standards such as **ISO 7731** define auditory warning signals.

- **Pitch** — High pitch = urgency
- **Pulse repetition rate** — Faster = more urgent (typical: 1–3 Hz caution, 4–8 Hz warning)
- **Loudness** — Minimum 65 dB(A) above ambient noise, max 110 dB(A) to prevent hearing damage

### 2.4 Principles / Design Rules of Effective Coding

| # | Principle | Explanation |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Compatibility** | Code must match user expectations (red = stop, not go) |
| 2 | **Distinctiveness** | Each code must be unambiguously different from others |
| 3 | **Standardization** | Use ANSI/ISO conventions so codes are learned once |
| 4 | **Redundancy** | Critical codes should use ≥ 2 variables (color + shape) |
| 5 | **Stability** | Meaning of a code must not change over time |
| 6 | **Economy** | Use the fewest codes that satisfy the information requirement |
| 7 | **Visibility** | Code must be perceivable in the operational environment |
| 8 | **Legibility** | Symbol/text must be readable at required viewing distance |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for solving KTU numerical problems. Memorize units carefully.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Shannon Information Content** | $I(x) = -\log_{2}{p(x)}$ | Information per symbol | **bits/symbol** |
| 2 | **Shannon Entropy (Average Info)** | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_{2}{p_i}$ | Expected info per symbol | **bits/symbol** |
| 3 | **Channel Capacity (Shannon-Hartley)** | $C = B \log_{2}{(1 + S/N)}$ | Max data rate | **bits/second** |
| 4 | **Miller's Working Memory Span** | $M = 7 \pm 2$ items | Chunk capacity | **chunks** |
| 5 | **Pollack's Absolute Judgment Limit** | $J_{\max} \approx 2.3$ bits $\equiv$ 5–6 levels | ID limit on 1 dimension | **levels** |
| 6 | **Redundant Channel Capacity** | $C_{tot} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} C_j$ (independent channels) | Sum of channel capacities | **bits/second** |
| 7 | **Stevens' Power Law (perceived intensity)** | $\psi = k \cdot \phi^{n}$ | $\psi$ perceived, $\phi$ physical | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Weber's Law (Just Noticeable Difference)** | $\Delta I / I = k$ | $k$ = Weber fraction | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Fitts' Law (Index of Difficulty)** | $ID = \log_{2}{(2A / W)}$ | $A$ = distance, $W$ = width | **bits** |
| 10 | **Recognition Accuracy with Redundancy** | $P_{correct} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{k}{(1 - p_j)}$ | $p_j$ accuracy of each redundant cue | dimensionless |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar symbol `|` is **strictly replaced** with `\vert` or `\mid` in all equations above to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aviation Cockpits:** Red master warning, amber caution, green normal — paired with flashing frequency and audio chirp.
- **Nuclear Power Plants (NPP):** Color-coded pipe markers (ANSI A13.1) for fluid identity.
- **Medical Devices:** IEC 60601-1 requires color + shape redundancy for safety alarms.
- **Automotive HMI:** Gear position (P-R-N-D) coded by position + color + letter.
- **Industrial Control Rooms:** Mimic displays — shape-coded pumps, valves, tanks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shannon Information Content of a Single Symbol

**Problem Setup:** A control panel indicator can be in **4 states** (Normal, Caution, Warning, Critical) with equal probability.

**Step 1 — Identify the probability of each state:**

Since the states are equiprobable:

$$p = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Shannon Information Formula:**

The information conveyed by a single observation of a state $x$ is:

$$I(x) = -\log_{2}{p(x)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $p(x) = 0.25$:**

$$I(x) = -\log_{2}{(0.25)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

Because $0.25 = 2^{-2}$:

$$\log_{2}{(0.25)} = -2$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$I(x) = -(-2) = 2 \text{ bits}$$

**Conclusion:** Each panel state carries **2 bits** of information. An 8-state panel would carry 3 bits, a 16-state panel 4 bits.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Information Capacity of a Redundant Multi-Cue Display

**Problem:** A cockpit warning uses **two redundant channels**: (i) a red light (color cue) with 95% identification accuracy, and (ii) a flashing beacon (motion cue) with 90% accuracy. Both must fail simultaneously for the operator to miss the warning.

**Step 1 — Define individual failure probabilities:**

For the red light: $q_1 = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$

For the flashing beacon: $q_2 = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$

**Step 2 — Independence Assumption (channels are perceptually independent):**

The probability that **both** channels fail:

$$q_{both} = q_1 \times q_2 = 0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.005$$

**Step 3 — Compute joint correct identification probability:**

$$P_{correct} = 1 - q_{both} = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995$$

**Step 4 — Convert to error rate per 10,000 presentations:**

$$\text{Error rate} = (1 - P_{correct}) \times 10{,}000 = 50 \text{ errors per 10,000}$$

**Conclusion:** Redundancy reduces error rate by **a factor of 20×** (from 5% with light alone to 0.5% with both cues).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Channel Capacity of a Pilot's Visual Channel During Night Flight

**Problem:** A pilot's eye has an effective bandwidth $B = 20$ Hz under low-light scotopic conditions, and the signal-to-noise ratio $S/N = 15$ (linear). Calculate the maximum information transfer rate.

**Step 1 — Recall the Shannon-Hartley Theorem:**

$$C = B \cdot \log_{2}{(1 + S/N)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$C = 20 \cdot \log_{2}{(1 + 15)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the argument of the logarithm:**

$$1 + 15 = 16$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\log_{2}(16)$:**

Since $16 = 2^4$:

$$\log_{2}{(16)} = 4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final capacity:**

$$C = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ bits/second}$$

**Conclusion:** Under these conditions, the pilot's visual channel can transmit up to **80 bits/second** — sufficient to process ~40 typical alphanumeric symbols (2 bits each) per second, consistent with expert reading speeds.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Miller's Limit Applied to a Control Panel

**Problem:** A designer proposes **15 separate indicator lamps** on a single console. Using Miller's Law, evaluate whether this exceeds human working memory limits.

**Step 1 — Recall Miller's Magical Number:**

$$M = 7 \pm 2 = 5 \text{ to } 9 \text{ chunks}$$

**Step 2 — Apply chunking (a recommended coding strategy):**

Group the 15 lamps into **3 functional groups of 5** (e.g., Engine, Avionics, Electrical) — each group becomes a "chunk."

**Step 3 — Effective number of chunks:**

$$M_{eff} = 3 \text{ chunks} \leq 7 \pm 2$$

**Conclusion:** 3 chunks is well within working memory. The designer should also add **redundant coding** (e.g., group header label, color-coded border) to further offload memory.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Code Selection by Information Type

| Information Type | Recommended Code | Why? | Example |
|------------------|------------------|------|---------|
| **Quantitative magnitude** | Size, position, length | Pre-attentive mapping | Bar gauge, slider position |
| **Qualitative category** | Color + shape | Pre-attentive + redundant | Red triangle = warning |
| **Binary state (on/off)** | Color or position | Sufficient discriminability | Green = ON, Red = OFF |
| **Sequential / order** | Position (spatial) | Natural ordinal mapping | Gear positions P-R-N-D |
| **Identity (name)** | Alphanumeric | Precision needed | Flight number VT-101 |
| **Direction of motion** | Arrow shape | Intuitive mapping | Turn signal arrow |
| **Continuous rate** | Steepness, density, rate | Encodes rate of change | Strobing frequency = urgency |
| **Emergency / alarm** | Color + flash + audio | Triple redundancy | Fire alarm bell + red flash |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Information Coding Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INFORMATION CODING IN HFE]:::main
    A --> B[VISUAL CODES]:::branch
    A --> C[AUDITORY CODES]:::branch
    A --> D[TACTILE CODES]:::branch
    A --> E[OLFACTORY CODES]:::branch

    B --> B1[Color Coding]
    B --> B2[Shape Coding]
    B --> B3[Alphanumeric Coding]
    B --> B4[Position Coding]
    B --> B5[Size Coding]
    B --> B6[Motion Coding]
    B --> B7[Brightness Coding]

    C --> C1[Pitch]
    C --> C2[Loudness]
    C --> C3[Duration]
    C --> C4[Timbre]
    C --> C5[Spatial Location]

    D --> D1[Vibration Frequency]
    D --> D2[Amplitude]
    D --> D3[Pattern]

    E --> E1[Odorants]
    E --> E2[Flavor coding - rare]

    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef branch fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Human Information Processing Pipeline with Coding Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Data Source]:::source --> enc[Designer Encodes Info]:::encode
    enc --> disp[Physical Display]:::display
    disp --> rec[Human Sensory Receptor]:::sense
    rec --> trans[Transduction to Neural Signal]:::sense
    trans --> perc[Perceptual Decoding]:::perception
    perc --> short[Short Term Memory]:::memory
    short --> dec[Decision and Response]:::decision
    dec --> act[Operator Action]:::action

    fb[Feedback Loop]:::feedback -.-> dec

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef encode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef display fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
    classDef sense fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d
    classDef perception fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47
    classDef memory fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#351c75
    classDef decision fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#b45f06
    classDef action fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1155cc
    classDef feedback fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#666666,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.3 Standard Color-Code Decision Matrix (ANSI Z535 / ISO 3864)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[What is the operational meaning?]:::question
    Q --> D1[Danger or Prohibition]:::danger
    Q --> D2[Caution or Warning]:::caution
    Q --> D3[Mandatory Action or Info]:::info
    Q --> D4[Safety or Normal Go]:::safe
    Q --> D5[Fire Equipment]:::fire

    D1 --> R1[RED solid background]:::shape
    D2 --> R2[YELLOW or AMBER solid background]:::shape
    D3 --> R3[BLUE solid background]:::shape
    D4 --> R4[GREEN solid background]:::shape
    D5 --> R5[RED with white fire symbol]:::shape

    R1 --> F1[Add shape and text for redundancy]:::final
    R2 --> F2[Add triangle and text]:::final
    R3 --> F3[Add circle or square]:::final
    R4 --> F4[Add square and text]:::final
    R5 --> F5[Standard ISO 7010 fire pictogram]:::final

    classDef question fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef danger fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef caution fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef info fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef fire fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef shape fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333333,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Redundant Coding System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INFO[Critical Information]:::info
    ENC1[Coder 1 - Color Channel]:::coder
    ENC2[Coder 2 - Shape Channel]:::coder
    ENC3[Coder 3 - Audio Channel]:::coder
    MIX[Display Output Unit]:::mix
    OP[Human Operator]:::operator
    P1[Visual Color Cortex]:::perception
    P2[Visual Shape Cortex]:::perception
    P3[Auditory Cortex]:::perception
    INT[Integrated Perception]:::integration
    RES[Recognized Message]:::result

    INFO --> ENC1
    INFO --> ENC2
    INFO --> ENC3
    ENC1 --> MIX
    ENC2 --> MIX
    ENC3 --> MIX
    MIX --> OP
    OP --> P1
    OP --> P2
    OP --> P3
    P1 --> INT
    P2 --> INT
    P3 --> INT
    INT --> RES

    classDef info fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef coder fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d
    classDef mix fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc
    classDef operator fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#b45f06
    classDef perception fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47
    classDef integration fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#351c75
    classDef result fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.5 Visual Channel Capacity vs Number of Codes (Conceptual Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1 Code 100 percent accuracy]:::s1 --> B[2 to 4 codes 95 percent]:::s2
    B --> C[5 to 7 codes 90 percent]:::s3
    C --> D[8 to 12 codes 75 percent]:::s4
    D --> E[More than 15 codes 50 percent]:::s5

    classDef s1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d
    classDef s2 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d
    classDef s3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef s4 fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#b45f06
    classDef s5 fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Coding of Information" in the context of Human Factors Engineering. List any four characteristics of a good coding system.**

**Model Answer:**

*Definition:* Coding of Information is the systematic assignment of distinguishable sensory symbols (color, shape, sound, position, size) to represent specific data, operational states, or control actions, so that the operator can rapidly perceive, identify, and act upon them.

*Four Characteristics of a Good Coding System:*
1. **Compatibility** — Code matches the operator's mental model and prior expectations.
2. **Distinctiveness** — Each code is unambiguously different from every other code.
3. **Standardization** — Codes follow recognized standards (ANSI Z535, ISO 3864, ISO 7010) for global transferability.
4. **Redundancy** — Critical codes use two or more independent variables (e.g., color + shape).

*(Examiner note: Mentioning "Stability" and "Economy" as additional points earns a half-mark bonus.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is Miller's Magical Number Seven? Mention its significance in the design of information coding.**

**Model Answer:**

*Definition:* George A. Miller (1956) established through experimental studies that the human short-term (working) memory can hold **$7 \pm 2$** discrete chunks of information at any instant.

*Significance in Coding Design:*
- A single display/panel should not present more than **$7 \pm 2$** distinct coded items at once.
- Designers must apply **chunking** — grouping related items into larger functional units (e.g., 15 gauges into 3 systems of 5).
- Limits the **number of simultaneously distinguishable categories** to about 5–9 before confusion rises.
- Justifies **layered information architecture** (overview → details on demand).

---

### 5.2 PART B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern: Each Part-B Question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks]**

---

#### QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the various **types of visual codes** used in Human Factors Engineering. Discuss the merits and demerits of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a **color-coding scheme for an industrial control room** handling five operational states: Normal Operation, Caution, Critical Warning, Emergency Shutdown, and Maintenance Mode. Justify your choice based on ISO/ANSI standards. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a): Types of Visual Codes (7 Marks)**

Visual codes are display variables that exploit the human eye's pre-attentive processing ability. The principal types are:

**1. Color Coding (2 marks)**
- **Merits:** Fastest pre-attentive processing (<250 ms); universally understood when standardized; high discriminability.
- **Demerits:** Fails under color blindness (8% males); degrades under low light; photocopies/fades change appearance; cross-cultural color-meaning differences.

**2. Shape Coding (1.5 marks)**
- **Merits:** Independent of color vision; robust under poor lighting; standardized via ISO 7010.
- **Demerits:** Slower to learn than color; limited number of distinct, memorable shapes; takes more display space.

**3. Position Coding (1.5 marks)**
- **Merits:** Extremely fast (parallel processing); natural ordinal mapping (top-to-bottom, left-to-right).
- **Demerits:** Fixed to physical layout; cannot be reconfigured; consumes panel real estate.

**4. Size Coding (1 mark)**
- **Merits:** Encodes magnitude intuitively (larger = more); supports quantitative reading.
- **Demerits:** Limited dynamic range; small size differences are not discriminable.

**5. Alphanumeric Coding (1 mark)**
- **Merits:** Infinite precision; supports complex identifiers.
- **Demerits:** Slowest processing (serial); requires literacy; high cognitive load.

**Conclusion:** Best practice is to combine ≥ 2 visual codes for **redundancy** in safety-critical displays.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b): Industrial Control Room Color Scheme (7 Marks)**

**Proposed ANSI Z535.1 / ISO 3864 Aligned Scheme:**

| State | Color | Shape / Symbol | Justification |
|-------|-------|----------------|---------------|
| **Normal Operation** | **GREEN** | Solid square + "OK" text | Universal "go/safe" convention |
| **Caution** | **YELLOW** | Triangle (warning) | Cautionary semantic from traffic codes |
| **Critical Warning** | **AMBER / ORANGE** | Triangle + flashing | ANSI Z535 reserves yellow for caution; amber for escalating warning |
| **Emergency Shutdown** | **RED** | Circle with bar (prohibition) + flashing + audio siren | Highest urgency, triple redundant |
| **Maintenance Mode** | **BLUE** | Wrench pictogram | ISO 3864 designates blue for mandatory/info |

**Design Justifications:**
1. **Color redundancy with shape** — protects against 8% red-green color blindness.
2. **Flashing modulation for Critical and Emergency** — motion provides additional urgency cue.
3. **Audio component for Emergency only** — prevents auditory overload from routine states.
4. **Acoustic level (ISO 7731):** 65 dB(A) above ambient, ≤ 110 dB(A) absolute.
5. **Spatial grouping** — all five indicators on a single dedicated annunciator panel ≤ 30° visual angle from operator's normal line of sight.

---

#### QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **principles of effective coding**. Explain why **redundancy** is considered the single most important principle in safety-critical displays. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cockpit **Master Warning** indicator must convey three states: **Normal, Caution, Danger**. The designer proposes using only color (green/amber/red). Evaluate this proposal using (i) Pollack's Law, (ii) color blindness statistics, and (iii) redundancy principles. Suggest improvements. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a): Principles of Effective Coding (7 Marks)**

The eight universally accepted design principles are:

1. **Compatibility** — Code matches operator's expectation (red = stop).
2. **Distinctiveness** — Codes are unambiguously separable.
3. **Standardization** — Conformance to ANSI / ISO / IEC standards.
4. **Redundancy** — Use of ≥ 2 independent variables (color + shape).
5. **Stability** — Code meaning does not change over time or product line.
6. **Economy** — Minimum codes to convey required information.
7. **Visibility / Legibility** — Adequate size, contrast, and viewing distance.
8. **Predictability** — Same code always signals same event.

**Why Redundancy is Paramount (4 marks):**

Redundancy multiplies channel capacity and reduces error rate. If each cue has independent accuracy $p$, joint accuracy:

$$P_{correct} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{k}{(1 - p_j)}$$

For $p_1 = p_2 = 0.9$ and $k=2$: $P_{correct} = 1 - (0.1 \times 0.1) = 0.99$ (99%).
For $p_1 = 0.9$ alone: $P_{correct} = 0.9$ (90%).
Error reduction = **10×**.

In safety-critical contexts (aviation, nuclear, medical), even 0.1% error rates are unacceptable, justifying redundant coding as a non-negotiable design rule.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b): Evaluation of Color-Only Cockpit Coding (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Pollack's Law (2 marks):**
Pollack's limit on a single dimension is ~2.3 bits ≈ **5–6 levels**. Three states are well within Pollack's limit, so color alone is *technically* sufficient for discrimination.

**Step 2 — Color Blindness Statistics (2 marks):**
- Red-green deficiency affects **8% of males** and **0.5% of females**.
- For a red-green colorblind pilot, **green vs red** becomes indistinguishable.
- A **color-only** coding scheme has an unacceptably high probability of misinterpretation by 8% of users — violating the principle of universal access.

**Step 3 — Redundancy Principle (2 marks):**
A safety-critical signal must use ≥ 2 independent variables. The proposed scheme fails this test.

**Step 4 — Recommended Improvements (1 mark):**
- Add **shape coding** (circle = Normal, triangle = Caution, octagon = Danger).
- Add **flashing pattern** (steady = Normal, 2 Hz = Caution, 5 Hz = Danger).
- Add **audio coding** (no tone = Normal, single beep = Caution, continuous siren = Danger).
- Add **position coding** (bottom = Normal, middle = Caution, top = Danger on vertical annunciator).

**Conclusion:** The original proposal is **inadequate** for a Master Warning. It must be replaced with a multi-modal, redundant, and standardized coding scheme.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Coding" with "Labeling"** — Coding uses *symbols* (color, shape); labeling uses *text words*. They are not the same.
> 2. **Forgetting Color-Blindness** — Proposing color-only codes for safety systems is a **deduction of 2–3 marks** automatically.
> 3. **Ignoring Standards** — Failing to cite **ANSI Z535.1, ISO 3864, ISO 7010, IEC 60601** when designing schemes loses the *application* marks.
> 4. **Wrong Polarity** — Saying "yellow = stop" or "green = danger" is an instant 1-mark penalty; examiners test for ANSI convention.
> 5. **Mishandling Pipes in Tables** — Use `$\vert$` instead of `$\vert$` in written equations to avoid LaTeX rendering errors in markdown.
> 6. **Skipping Formulas in Numericals** — Always state the formula (e.g., Shannon-Hartley) before substituting values; partial credit requires the formula line.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Coding of Information**

- [ ] **Definition:** Coding = assigning distinguishable sensory symbols to information classes for fast human perception.
- [ ] **Four Sensory Modalities:** Visual, Auditory, Tactile, Olfactory.
- [ ] **Visual Sub-Codes:** Color, Shape, Alphanumeric, Position, Size, Motion, Brightness.
- [ ] **Miller's Law:** $7 \pm 2$ chunks in working memory.
- [ ] **Pollack's Law:** $\approx$ 2.3 bits $\approx$ 5–6 levels per single dimension.
- [ ] **Shannon Information:** $I = -\log_{2}{p(x)}$ in **bits/symbol**.
- [ ] **Shannon-Hartley Capacity:** $C = B \log_{2}{(1 + S/N)}$ in **bits/second**.
- [ ] **Redundant Accuracy:** $P_{correct} = 1 - \prod(1 - p_j)$.
- [ ] **Color Standards (ANSI Z535):** Red = Danger, Yellow = Caution, Blue = Mandatory/Info, Green = Safety.
- [ ] **Shape Standards (ISO 7010):** Triangle = Warning, Circle = Mandatory/Prohibition, Square = Information, Cross = First Aid.
- [ ] **Resistor Color Code:** Black-0, Brown-1, Red-2, Orange-3, Yellow-4, Green-5, Blue-6, Violet-7, Grey-8, White-9.
- [ ] **8 Design Principles:** Compatibility, Distinctiveness, Standardization, Redundancy, Stability, Economy, Visibility, Predictability.
- [ ] **Color Blindness Statistic:** 8% of males have red-green deficiency — always pair color with shape.
- [ ] **Auditory Alarm (ISO 7731):** ≥ 65 dB(A) above ambient, max 110 dB(A).
- [ ] **Stevens' Power Law:** $\psi = k \cdot \phi^n$ (perceived vs physical intensity).
- [ ] **Fitts' Law (Index of Difficulty):** $ID = \log_{2}(2A/W)$.
- [ ] **Coding is NOT Labeling** — symbols vs text.
- [ ] **Safety-critical codes must always be redundant** (multi-modal).
- [ ] **First mention of an acronym** must be expanded (e.g., ANSI = American National Standards Institute).
- [ ] **International standards** to cite: ANSI Z535, ISO 3864, ISO 7010, IEC 60601, ISO 7731.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Good Coding System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Good Coding System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Coding System** in Human Factors Engineering is a structured, predefined set of symbols, signals, or patterns used to encode, transmit, and represent information between a source (machine, environment, or another person) and a human operator. The operator then **decodes** this information by perceiving the relevant sensory channel and assigning meaning to the stimulus.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a coding system translates raw data into a perceivable form using specific **code dimensions** such as color, shape, size, position, brightness, pitch, frequency, duration, texture, or alphanumeric labels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coding vs. Display:** A *display* presents information, while a *code* is the **language of representation** used in that display. The quality of the code directly determines operator performance, error rates, and cognitive load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a busy airport. Hundreds of flights need to be identified quickly by ground staff, pilots, and passengers. Without a coding system, every flight would have to be referred to by its full name ("Air India Flight 425 to Mumbai"). This would be slow, error-prone, and exhausting.

Instead, airports use **codes**:
- **AI425** (alphanumeric code)
- **Gate B7** (positional code — different gate for different flight)
- **Red signal** (color code — meaning "do not cross")
- **Beeping sound** (auditory code — meaning "boarding now")

A *good* coding system means that even under stress, dim lighting, or noise, the operator can **instantly recognize** what the code means without confusion. A *poor* coding system would, for example, use the same color for "danger" and "safe status," causing catastrophic misinterpretation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value/Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Snellen Visual Acuity** | **6/6 (20/20)** normal | Baseline for display readability |
| **Auditory threshold (1 kHz)** | ≈ **0 dB SPL** | Reference for auditory codes |
| **Just Noticeable Difference (JND)** | Weber's law governed | Minimum distinguishable code change |
| **Reaction time (simple)** | ≈ **200–250 ms** | Code identification latency |
| **Working memory capacity** | **7 ± 2 items** (Miller) | Limits number of simultaneous codes |
| **Field of View (FOV)** | ~**200° horizontal** | Display layout zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For the KTU ESE, you must be able to enumerate **at least 8 characteristics** of a good coding system and justify each with a one-line reasoning linked to human sensory/perceptual limits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of a Coding System

A coding system operates through the following structured pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Information Source:** Raw data originates (e.g., engine temperature, pressure level, system status).
- **Step 2 — Code Selection:** A designer selects an appropriate sensory modality (visual / auditory / tactile) and a code dimension (color, size, pitch, etc.).
- **Step 3 — Encoding:** The information is mapped to a perceivable symbol (e.g., "red light = danger").
- **Step 4 — Transmission:** The coded signal is delivered via display, speaker, or haptic device.
- **Step 5 — Decoding (by human):** Sensory receptors capture the stimulus → brain interprets → meaning assigned.
- **Step 6 — Response:** The operator acts on the decoded information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why coding matters:** Every step above is susceptible to **information loss**. A "good" coding system minimizes this loss, ensuring that what is encoded matches what is decoded.

## 2.2 Classification of Code Dimensions

Codes can be organized along **sensory channels**:

- **Visual codes:** Color, shape, size, position, brightness, alphanumeric symbols, blinking rate.
- **Auditory codes:** Pitch, loudness, duration, rhythm, temporal pattern, voice vs. tone.
- **Tactile codes:** Vibration frequency, amplitude, texture, location on body.
- **Olfactory/Gustatory codes:** Rarely used (e.g., odorized natural gas for leak detection).

## 2.3 The Ten Characteristics of a Good Coding System (KTU High-Yield)

These are the **core examinable attributes** of a well-designed code:

1. **Compatibility** — Matches the user's mental model, expectations, and cultural conventions.
2. **Distinctiveness (Discriminability)** — Each code element must be clearly separable from others; relies on adequate **JND**.
3. **Standardization** — Follows established standards (e.g., ISO, ANSI, MIL-STD) so users trained on one system can transfer knowledge.
4. **Consistency** — Same code always means the same thing; no contradictions across the system.
5. **Memorability / Learnability** — Easy to remember; minimizes training time and recall errors.
6. **Speed of Identification** — Quickly recognizable under time pressure (high **signal detection rate**, low **reaction time**).
7. **Reliability / Robustness** — Performs under adverse conditions (noise, vibration, dim light, stress).
8. **Minimal Cognitive Load** — Does not overwhelm working memory (respects the **7 ± 2** Miller limit).
9. **Feedback Compatibility** — Provides confirmation that decoding was successful.
10. **Task-Environment Compatibility** — Suited to the operational task, lighting, noise, and operator posture.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas govern the perceptual limits that any coding system must respect.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weber's Law:} \quad & \frac{\Delta I}{I} = k \quad \text{(constant for a given modality)} \\[4pt]
\text{Signal Detection Theory (d') :} \quad & d' = z(\text{Hit}) - z(\text{FA}) \\[4pt]
\text{Information Capacity (bits):} \quad & H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 \!\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Miller's Channel Capacity:} \quad & C \approx 7 \pm 2 \text{ items} \\[4pt]
\text{Choice Reaction Time (Hick's Law):} \quad & RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1) \\[4pt]
\text{Fitts' Law (target acquisition):} \quad & MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W}+1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta I$ | Just Noticeable Difference (JND) | Dimensionless ratio |
| $k$ | Weber constant | Modality-specific |
| $d'$ | Detectability index | z-score units |
| $H$ | Information entropy | **bits** |
| $N$ | Number of equally likely choices | Count |
| $D, W$ | Distance to / width of target | meters / pixels |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Cockpits:** Standardized color codes (red = danger, amber = caution, green = normal) follow **SAE ARP 4101** and **MIL-STD-2525**.
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** Redundant visual + auditory + tactile alarms ensure coding survives equipment failure.
- **Automotive Dashboards:** Fuel, temperature, and warning symbols use **ISO 2575** pictograms.
- **Medical Devices:** Audible IV pump alarms distinguish *low priority* (intermittent tone) from *high priority* (continuous loud tone).
- **Industrial Control Rooms:** Large display walls use **shape-coded icons** so operators can recognize status peripherally without reading text.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of Distinguishable Codes

**Problem:** A designer wants to use **color** and **shape** as two independent code dimensions. If 8 perceptually distinct colors and 5 distinct shapes are usable, what is the **maximum number of uniquely identifiable codes** without violating Miller's limit?

**Analytical Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent code dimensions:} \\[2pt]
N_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{color}} \times N_{\text{shape}} \\[4pt]
&= 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ codes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2 — Apply Miller's Channel Capacity constraint:} \\[2pt]
N_{\text{usable}} &= \min(N_{\text{total}},\; 7 \pm 2) \\[4pt]
&= \min(40,\; 9) = 9 \text{ codes (upper bound)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3 — Interpret the result:} \\[2pt]
\text{Although 40 unique combinations exist mathematically,} \\[2pt]
\text{the operator's working memory can hold only} \approx 9 \text{ codes reliably.} \\[2pt]
\text{Therefore the designer must reduce } N_{\text{color}} \text{ or } N_{\text{shape}} \\[2pt]
\text{so that } N_{\text{total}} \leq 9.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Conclusion:** If $N_{\text{shape}} = 3$, then $N_{\text{color}} \leq 3$, giving $3 \times 3 = 9$ codes — the maximum usable set.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reaction Time Using Hick's Law

**Problem:** An operator must select the correct emergency shutdown button from **1, 2, 4, or 8** buttons, all visually distinct. Plot the expected reaction time using Hick's Law with $a = 200\,\text{ms}$ and $b = 150\,\text{ms}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT(N) &= a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1) \\[4pt]
RT(1) &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(2) = 200 + 150 = 350 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
RT(2) &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(3) = 200 + 150(1.585) = 437.7 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
RT(4) &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(5) = 200 + 150(2.322) = 548.3 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
RT(8) &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(9) = 200 + 150(3.170) = 675.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** As the number of distinguishable codes increases, identification slows logarithmically. A *good* coding system therefore restricts $N$ to values where $RT$ remains operationally safe (typically $< 1$ second for critical tasks).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Discriminability via Weber's Law

**Problem:** A warning light intensity is currently $I = 80$ cd/m². To ensure the operator notices a change, the brightness must vary by at least the JND. For visual brightness, $k \approx 0.08$. Find the minimum detectable change.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta I}{I} &= k \\[4pt]
\Delta I &= k \cdot I = 0.08 \times 80 = 6.4 \text{ cd/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The display brightness must change by **at least 6.4 cd/m²** to be reliably detected. A coding step smaller than this wastes engineering effort because the operator cannot perceive it.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Good vs. Bad Coding

| Attribute | Good Coding System | Bad Coding System | Result of Failure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compatibility | Matches user expectation | Inverted meaning (red = go) | Catastrophic misuse |
| Distinctiveness | Well-separated dimensions | Overlapping colors | Confusion, wrong response |
| Standardization | ISO / SAE compliant | Proprietary symbols | Training overhead, errors |
| Consistency | Same code = same meaning | Same code = two meanings | Loss of trust in displays |
| Memorability | Few, meaningful codes | Dozens of arbitrary codes | Cognitive overload |
| Speed of Identification | Quick detection | Slow recognition | Delayed reaction in emergencies |
| Robustness | Works in noise / dim light | Fails in adverse conditions | Missed alarms |
| Cognitive Load | Within 7 ± 2 items | Exceeds working memory | Forgetting, errors |
| Feedback | Confirms decoding | Silent / ambiguous | Uncertainty, repeat checks |
| Task-Environment Fit | Suited to context | Generic | Low usability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Coding System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full information flow from data origin to operator response, highlighting where each characteristic of a *good* coding system is enforced.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INFO SOURCE] --> B[CODE SELECTION]
    B --> C[ENCODING LOGIC]
    C --> D[DISPLAY UNIT]
    C --> E[AUDITORY TRANSDUCER]
    C --> F[TACTILE ACTUATOR]
    D --> G[VISUAL CHANNEL]
    E --> H[AUDITORY CHANNEL]
    F --> I[HAPTIC CHANNEL]
    G --> J[HUMAN PERCEPTION]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[DECODING IN BRAIN]
    K --> L[WORKING MEMORY CHECK]
    L --> M[OPERATOR RESPONSE]
    M --> N[FEEDBACK TO SOURCE]

    O[CHARACTERISTICS LAYER] -.-> B
    O -.-> C
    O -.-> D
    O -.-> E
    O -.-> F

    subgraph CHAR_BOX [CHARACTERISTICS ENFORCED]
        C1[Compatibility]
        C2[Distinctiveness]
        C3[Standardization]
        C4[Consistency]
        C5[Memorability]
        C6[Speed of ID]
        C7[Robustness]
        C8[Cognitive Load Control]
        C9[Feedback]
        C10[Task Environment Fit]
    end

    O --- C1
    O --- C2
    O --- C3
    O --- C4
    O --- C5
    O --- C6
    O --- C7
    O --- C8
    O --- C9
    O --- C10
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Code Dimension Trade-Off Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Required Number of Codes N] --> B{N exceeds 9}
    B -- YES --> C[Split into Multiple Modalities]
    B -- NO --> D[Use Single Modality]
    C --> E[Visual Plus Auditory Plus Tactile]
    D --> F[Apply Hick Law Check]
    E --> G[Validate RT under 1 sec]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Acceptable}
    H -- YES --> I[Deploy Coding System]
    H -- NO --> J[Reduce N or Improve Distinctiveness]
    J --> A
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Sensory Modality Suitability Matrix

| Code Type | Best Use Case | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Color** | Status differentiation | Color-blindness (≈ **8%** of males) |
| **Shape** | Categorical icons | Requires adequate display resolution |
| **Position** | Spatial mapping | Limited screen area |
| **Pitch** | Auditory alerts | Masked by ambient noise |
| **Vibration** | Tactile feedback in dark/noisy | Body-location dependent |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a coding system in Human Factors Engineering and list any four characteristics of a good coding system.**

**Model Answer:**
A coding system is a structured set of perceivable symbols, signals, or patterns (visual, auditory, tactile) used to encode information for transmission to a human operator. The operator decodes the signal by sensory perception and assigns meaning.

Four characteristics of a good coding system:
1. **Compatibility** with user expectations.
2. **Distinctiveness** (clearly separable code elements).
3. **Standardization** following established norms.
4. **Memorability** to minimize cognitive load.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain why "distinctiveness" is a critical characteristic of a coding system. State the governing perceptual law.**

**Model Answer:**
Distinctiveness ensures that every code element can be unambiguously discriminated from others. If codes overlap perceptually, operators confuse them, leading to wrong responses. The governing perceptual limit is defined by **Weber's Law**: $\Delta I / I = k$. The designer must ensure that adjacent code values differ by at least one JND. Without distinctiveness, standardization, memorability, and speed of identification all collapse.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 1 long-answer questions carry an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail any **seven characteristics of a good coding system**, justifying each with reference to human sensory and cognitive limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cockpit warning panel must display **six mutually exclusive fault states**. The designer proposes either (i) **6 distinct colors** or (ii) **2 colors × 3 shapes**. Using Hick's Law with $a = 200$ ms and $b = 150$ ms, determine which option yields the lower reaction time and justify your answer in terms of Miller's working memory constraint. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The seven characteristics are explained with their perceptual / cognitive justification:

1. **Compatibility** — Aligns with operator expectations; reduces learning time and interpretation errors.
2. **Distinctiveness** — Ensures codes differ by ≥ JND (Weber's Law) so they are not confused.
3. **Standardization** — Follows ISO/SAE/MIL conventions enabling cross-system transfer of training.
4. **Consistency** — Same code = same meaning; builds trust and reduces verification time.
5. **Memorability** — Operates within Miller's $7 \pm 2$ items so it does not overload working memory.
6. **Speed of Identification** — Minimizes reaction time per Hick's Law $RT = a + b\log_2(N+1)$.
7. **Robustness** — Performs under stress, noise, vibration, and low lighting (Signal Detection Theory: high $d'$).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Option (i) — 6 distinct colors:} \\[2pt]
RT_1 &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(6+1) = 200 + 150(2.807) = 621.1 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Option (ii) — 2 colors × 3 shapes = 6 unique codes:} \\[2pt]
RT_2 &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(6+1) = 621.1 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(mathematically identical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{However, Option (ii) is operationally superior because:} \\[2pt]
\text{— It exploits two perceptual dimensions, increasing distinctiveness (Weber's Law).} \\[2pt]
\text{— It is more robust to color-blindness (≈ 8% of male pilots).} \\[2pt]
\text{— It matches Miller's 7 ± 2 capacity (6 items fits comfortably).} \\[2pt]
\text{— It reduces cognitive load by chunking: 2 categories × 3 sub-types.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** While Hick's Law yields identical RT for both, **Option (ii)** is the better engineering choice due to redundancy and accessibility.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **Weber's Law** and **Hick's Law** in designing coding systems. Derive the JND for a luminance code where $I = 120$ cd/m² and $k = 0.08$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial control room must alert operators to **eight different abnormal conditions** using only **auditory codes** (pitch and duration). Pitch can take 4 values and duration can take 2 values. Analyze whether this design satisfies Miller's limit and Hick's Law. Suggest improvements if it fails. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Weber's Law** states that the just noticeable difference $\Delta I$ is proportional to the stimulus magnitude: $\Delta I / I = k$. It sets the **minimum discriminable step** between two code values. A designer violating Weber's Law will produce code changes the operator literally cannot see.

**Hick's Law** states that choice reaction time increases logarithmically with the number of equally likely alternatives: $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$. It quantifies the **speed penalty** for adding more codes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{JND Calculation:} \\[2pt]
\Delta I &= k \cdot I = 0.08 \times 120 = 9.6 \text{ cd/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Adjacent luminance code values must differ by at least **9.6 cd/m²** to be reliably distinguishable.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Total unique codes:} \\[2pt]
N &= N_{\text{pitch}} \times N_{\text{duration}} = 4 \times 2 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2 — Miller's Channel Capacity Check:} \\[2pt]
7 \pm 2 \Rightarrow \text{Acceptable range} = 5 \text{ to } 9 \text{ items} \\[2pt]
N = 8 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (fits within upper bound)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3 — Hick's Law Reaction Time:} \\[2pt]
RT &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(8+1) = 200 + 150(3.170) = 675.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4 — Engineering Verdict:} \\[2pt]
