## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define perception. Differentiate between sensation and perception.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Perception is the cognitive process of organizing, identifying, and interpreting sensory information to create a meaningful representation of the environment.

**Sensation (1 Mark):** Sensation is the **detection** of physical stimuli by sensory receptors and the initial neural transmission of these signals to the brain. It is a *bottom-up*, data-driven process.

**Perception (1 Mark):** Perception is the **interpretation and organization** of these raw sensory signals by the brain, integrating memory, expectation, and context. It is a *top-down*, meaning-driven process.

> **Distinction Table:**
>
> | Aspect | Sensation | Perception |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Nature | Passive recording | Active interpretation |
> | Level | Receptor-based | Brain-based |
> | Example | Detecting light on retina | Recognizing the light as a fire alarm indicator |

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Weber's Law. What is Weber's constant?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** Weber's Law states that the just noticeable difference (JND) in a stimulus is a constant proportion of the original stimulus intensity:

$$
\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k
$$

**Explanation (1 Mark):** The smallest increment $\Delta I$ that an observer can detect is proportional to the baseline intensity $I$. As the base stimulus grows, the JND must also grow proportionally to remain detectable.

**Weber's Constant (1 Mark):** $k$ is a **dimensionless** constant that depends on the sensory modality (e.g., $k \approx 0.02$ for weight discrimination, $k \approx 0.05$ for loudness). Lower $k$ values indicate finer discriminative ability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the absolute threshold and the difference threshold. Explain Signal Detection Theory (SDT) in detail, including the four possible outcomes and the two key parameters $d'$ and $\beta$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory operator monitors a temperature gauge that reads **500°C**. The Weber constant for visual pointer discrimination is **0.04**. A fire alarm system must trigger if the temperature rises by a perceivable amount. Calculate:
- (i) The JND in °C.
- (ii) The minimum alarm trigger temperature.
- (iii) Explain, with a Mermaid or block diagram, how SDT would model the operator's decision to activate the alarm. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks**

**Absolute Threshold (1 Mark):** The minimum stimulus intensity that can be detected **50% of the time** under ideal conditions. It marks the boundary between non-detection and detection.

**Difference Threshold (1 Mark):** Also called the JND, this is the minimum difference between two stimuli that an observer can detect 50% of the time.

**SDT — Four Outcomes (2 Marks):** Signal Detection Theory proposes that detection is a decision under uncertainty. The four outcomes are:
- **Hit:** Signal present, observer says "Yes" (correct).
- **Miss:** Signal present, observer says "No" (dangerous error).
- **False Alarm:** Signal absent, observer says "Yes" (nuisance).
- **Correct Rejection:** Signal absent, observer says "No" (correct).

**SDT — Two Parameters (2 Marks):**
- **Sensitivity $d'$:** Measures discriminability. Formula: $d' = Z(H) - Z(FA)$. Higher $d'$ = better ability to distinguish signal from noise.
- **Response Bias $\beta$:** Measures decision criterion. Formula: $\beta = P(x \mid \text{SN}) / P(x \mid \text{N})$. High $\beta$ = cautious (says "No" often). Low $\beta$ = reckless (says "Yes" often).

**SDT in Safety Context (1 Mark):** In a fire alarm context, low $d'$ means the operator cannot distinguish a real fire from background fluctuations. High $\beta$ (cautious) leads to false alarms; low $\beta$ (reckless) leads to missed fires.

---

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks**

**(i) JND Calculation (2 Marks):**

Applying Weber's Law:

$$
\Delta I = k \cdot I
$$

$$
\Delta I = 0.04 \times 500
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta I = 20°C}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Alarm Trigger Temperature (2 Marks):**

$$
T_{\text{trigger}} = I + \Delta I = 500 + 20
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{trigger}} = 520°C}
$$

**(iii) SDT Block Diagram for Operator Decision (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Temperature Signal: True Fire Condition or Background Noise] --> B{Sensor Reading on Gauge}
    B --> C[Operator's Internal Noise: Fatigue, Stress, Distraction]
    C --> D{Decision Criterion Beta: Cautious vs Reckless}
    D -->|Signal Above Beta| E[Operator Says YES: Activate Alarm]
    D -->|Signal Below Beta| F[Operator Says NO: Do Not Activate]
    E --> G[Hit: Fire Present, Alarm Activated CORRECT]
    E --> H[False Alarm: No Fire, Alarm Activated NUISANCE]
    F --> I[Miss: Fire Present, Alarm Not Activated DANGEROUS]
    F --> J[Correct Rejection: No Fire, Alarm Not Activated CORRECT]
    style G fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745,color:#000000
    style J fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745,color:#000000
    style H fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#FFC107,color:#000000
    style I fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#DC3545,color:#000000
```

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Gestalt Laws of Perceptual Organization. List and briefly describe **any five** laws with industrial/engineering examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Stevens' Power Law states that $S = k(I - I_0)^n$. An engineer is designing a fire alarm siren. The reference intensity is $I_0 = 40$ dB (absolute threshold), and the Stevens exponent for loudness is $n = 0.67$. If the engineer doubles the physical intensity from **80 dB to 160 dB**, by what factor does the perceived loudness change? Discuss the engineering implications. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks**

**Introduction to Gestalt Psychology (1 Mark):** Gestalt theory (founded by Max Wertheimer, Wolfgang Köhler, and Kurt Koffka) holds that *"the whole is greater than the sum of its parts."* The brain actively organizes sensory data into meaningful wholes using built-in heuristics.

**The Five Gestalt Laws (1 Mark Each = 5 Marks):**

| Law | Definition | Engineering Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Proximity** | Items close together are perceived as a group. | Emergency exit signs clustered on a door. |
| **Similarity** | Items sharing visual features are grouped. | Color-coded fire extinguisher labels (red for Class A, blue for Class B). |
| **Closure** | The brain fills in missing gaps to perceive a complete figure. | A partially obscured warning symbol is still recognized. |
| **Continuity** | Smooth, continuous patterns are preferred over abrupt ones. | A linear row of identical gauges is read left-to-right easily. |
| **Common Fate** | Elements moving or changing together are perceived as a unit. | Flashing red lights on an emergency vehicle are grouped as a single signal. |

**Critical Design Implication (1 Mark):** Designers exploit Gestalt laws to ensure critical safety information is grouped, color-coded, and spatially organized so that operators perceive it as a **coherent, meaningful system** rather than disconnected elements.

---

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute Perceived Loudness at $I_1 = 80$ dB (2 Marks):**

$$
S_1 = k (I_1 - I_0)^n
$$

$$
S_1 = k (80 - 40)^{0.67} = k (40)^{0.67}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
(40)^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \ln 40} = e^{0.67 \times 3.689} = e^{2.472} \approx 11.85
$$

Therefore:

$$
S_1 = 11.85k
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Perceived Loudness at $I_2 = 160$ dB (2 Marks):**

$$
S_2 = k (160 - 40)^{0.67} = k (120)^{0.67}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
(120)^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \ln 120} = e^{0.67 \times 4.787} = e^{3.207} \approx 24.77
$$

Therefore:

$$
S_2 = 24.77k
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$
\frac{S_2}{S_1} = \frac{24.77k}{11.85k} = 2.09
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{S_2}{S_1} \approx 2.09}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Implications (2 Marks):**

Although the **physical intensity** doubled (from 80 dB to 160 dB), the **perceived loudness** increased only by a factor of approximately **2.09** (about 109% increase). This is because Stevens' exponent for loudness is $n = 0.67 < 1$, meaning humans are *less sensitive* to loudness increases at higher intensities. Engineering implications:

- **Alarm design:** Doubling the decibel output of a siren does **not** double its perceived urgency. Engineers must triple or quadruple the output to achieve a perceived doubling.
- **Hearing conservation:** Conversely, even small increases above 85 dB produce disproportionately large perceived loudness, which is why **OSHA/NIOSH limits** exposure duration sharply above this level.
- **Warning perception:** Operators in noisy plants may require *much higher* alarm sound levels to perceive a meaningful change, justifying the use of **visual and tactile** redundant signals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Sensation with Perception** — Many students define them as synonyms. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if the distinction is not made explicit. Always state: *Sensation = detection; Perception = interpretation.*
> 2. **Wrong Exponent in Stevens' Law** — A frequent error is using $n = 0.67$ (loudness) for brightness problems ($n = 0.33$) or vice versa. **Memorize the modality-exponent table.**
> 3. **Forgetting the Reference Threshold $I_0$** — Stevens' formula requires $(I - I_0)$, not just $I$. Many students omit $I_0$ and lose 1 mark per sub-part.
> 4. **Misinterpreting $d'$** — $d'$ is NOT a percentage or a probability. It is a **z-score difference** (dimensionless). State this explicitly in your answer.
> 5. **Weber's Constant Units** — $k$ is **dimensionless**. Do not assign units to it.
> 6. **Gestalt Laws** — At least **five** must be named and explained. Simply listing "Proximity, Similarity" without engineering examples is insufficient for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Perception ≠ Sensation.** Sensation is detection; perception is interpretation.
- [ ] **Five sensory channels:** Vision, Audition, Touch (Somesthesis), Taste, Olfaction.
- [ ] **Absolute Threshold (AL):** 50% detection point — minimum detectable stimulus.
- [ ] **Difference Threshold (DL/JND):** Minimum detectable change between two stimuli.
- [ ] **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I / I = k$ — JND is a constant proportion of base intensity.
- [ ] **Weber's Constant $k$** is **dimensionless** and **modality-specific**.
- [ ] **Fechner's Law:** $S = k \log(I/I_0)$ — Sensation grows logarithmically with stimulus.
- [ ] **Stevens' Power Law:** $S = k(I - I_0)^n$ — More accurate than Fechner; exponent $n$ varies by modality.
- [ ] **Stevens' Exponent for Loudness = 0.67; Brightness = 0.33; Heaviness = 1.45; Shock = 3.50.**
- [ ] **Signal Detection Theory** has 4 outcomes: Hit, Miss, False Alarm, Correct Rejection.
- [ ] **$d'$ (sensitivity)** = $Z(\text{Hit}) - Z(\text{False Alarm})$ — measures ability.
- [ ] **$\beta$ (bias)** = likelihood ratio — measures decision criterion (cautious vs. reckless).
- [ ] **Gestalt Laws:** Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Common Fate — used to design grouped, coherent displays.
- [ ] **Sensory Adaptation** reduces sensitivity to constant stimuli — explains missed gas leaks and gradual alarms.
- [ ] **Selective Attention** filters 11 million bits/sec down to ~50 bits/sec — root cause of many human errors.
- [ ] **Engineering Applications:** Display tick spacing, alarm loudness calibration, color-coding, redundant multi-modal signals.
- [ ] **Always include engineering examples** in 14-mark answers — KTU examiners award bonus marks for application context.
- [ ] **Use $Z$-scores** (standard normal distribution table values) when computing $d'$.
- [ ] **Threshold Hierarchy:** No Sensation → AL → JND → Comfort Zone → Pain Threshold → Tissue Damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip for PESFT748:**
> Every perception concept must be linked to a **safety, fire, or industrial application** in your answer. Pure theoretical definitions without engineering context score *partial* marks at best. Frame your answer as: *"In a refinery control room, the operator's ability to detect a pressure spike on a gauge depends on..."* — this language is what KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)**, **Memory** is defined as the cognitive neurobiological function by which humans encode, consolidate, store, retain, and subsequently retrieve information and past experiences. From an HFE perspective, memory is a **critical human operator capability** that fundamentally limits and constrains the design of human-machine interfaces, control systems, displays, warning systems, and procedural task workflows.

According to KTU 2024 HFE syllabus nomenclature, memory is treated as a **bounded information-processing subsystem** of the human operator, characterized by:
- **Finite capacity** (limited number of items/chunks)
- **Time-dependent decay** (information degrades over time without rehearsal)
- **Stage-based architecture** (sensory → short-term → long-term flow)
- **Active and passive retrieval mechanisms** (recall vs. recognition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, Memory is one of the foundational cognitive topics that bridges human sensory limits to higher-order decision making. Examiner questions frequently test the **three-store model** and **working memory model** with direct 3-mark definition questions.

## 1.2 Types of Memory — The Classical Three-Store Model

The information-processing model of memory (Atkinson & Shiffrin, 1968) postulates three distinct stores:

| Memory Store | Capacity | Duration | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensory Memory** | Large (raw sensory input) | **250–500 ms** (iconic); **3–4 s** (echoic) | Brief buffering of raw sensory data |
| **Short-Term Memory (STM)** | **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law) | **15–30 seconds** (without rehearsal) | Active conscious processing |
| **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** | Essentially **unlimited** | Minutes to a lifetime | Permanent knowledge repository |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Desk Model**
>
> Imagine the human mind as a busy office worker:
>
> - **Sensory Memory = The Mailroom Tray** 📥 — Every letter (sight, sound, touch) that arrives at the office is briefly placed on the front desk tray. If the worker doesn't pick it up within a fraction of a second, it gets thrown away.
>
> - **Short-Term Memory = The Active Desktop** 🖥️ — The worker can only have about 7 papers spread across the desk at any time. If she doesn't keep glancing at them (rehearsal), they slide off and are lost.
>
> - **Long-Term Memory = The Filing Cabinet** 🗄️ — Papers deemed important are filed away. The cabinet is enormous, but finding a specific paper later (retrieval) takes effort and sometimes fails.
>
> **Engineering Implication:** If you design a cockpit instrument panel with 20 gauges the pilot must memorize, you are **violating the desktop's capacity**. The pilot will misread, forget, or shut down under stress. Good HFE design respects the 7±2 limit and uses **recognition (multiple choice) rather than recall (open-ended)** wherever possible.

## 1.4 Key Sensory Memory Subtypes (Required KTU Terminology)

- **Iconic Memory** — Visual sensory store; duration ~**250–500 milliseconds**
- **Echoic Memory** — Auditory sensory store; duration ~**3–4 seconds**
- **Haptic Memory** — Tactile sensory store; duration ~**2 seconds**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Miller, 1956):** The capacity of Short-Term Memory is **7 ± 2 chunks**. A *chunk* is a meaningful unit (a word, a number, a familiar face) — not a single bit. Expert chess players can recall board positions of ~7 chunks because each chunk is a "configuration," not a piece.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Store Model — Operational Breakdown

The classical model (Atkinson-Shiffrin) processes information sequentially:

1. **Sensory Registration** — Raw stimuli from vision, hearing, touch enter the **sensory registers**. Most of this data is **filtered out** (attentional bottleneck).
2. **Attention / Encoding** — Selected sensory data is **encoded** into Short-Term Memory via attention.
3. **Rehearsal** — Information in STM is maintained by **maintenance rehearsal** (silent repetition). Without rehearsal, STM traces decay within **15–30 seconds**.
4. **Encoding to LTM** — Through **elaborative rehearsal** (deep, semantic processing), information is transferred to Long-Term Memory.
5. **Retrieval** — Information is retrieved from LTM back into STM when needed for a task.
6. **Forgetting** — Failure at any stage leads to memory loss.

### Why Each Step Matters in HFE Design:

- **Why Sensory Memory matters:** Display flicker rates faster than iconic decay (~16 ms/frame at 60 Hz) will be perceived as continuous motion; alarms shorter than echoic duration may be missed.
- **Why STM matters:** Emergency procedures with >7 sequential steps will overload the pilot/operator. Solution: **chunking** and **checklists**.
- **Why LTM matters:** Highly trained skills (e.g., typing) reside in LTM and become **automatic**, freeing STM for new problem solving.

## 2.2 Baddeley & Hitch Working Memory Model (1974, Updated 2000)

The modern refinement of STM is the **Working Memory (WM) Model**, which is a **KTU 2024 high-yield topic**.

| Component | Function | Modality |
|---|---|---|
| **Central Executive** | Attention controller; coordinates subsystems; **no storage** | Supervisory |
| **Phonological Loop** | Verbal/acoustic information rehearsal | Auditory/verbal |
| **Visuospatial Sketchpad** | Visual and spatial information manipulation | Visual/spatial |
| **Episodic Buffer** (added 2000) | Integrates information from WM subsystems and LTM into coherent episodes | Multimodal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in design:** A control room operator monitoring a verbal radio channel (Phonological Loop) while tracking a moving object on a radar (Visuospatial Sketchpad) is loading **two WM subsystems simultaneously**. Adding a third task requiring conscious decision-making will exceed the Central Executive's attentional capacity, causing errors.

## 2.3 Memory Processes

| Process | Definition | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoding** | Converting input into a storable form | Use distinct, meaningful labels (semantic encoding) |
| **Storage** | Retaining encoded information over time | Use redundancy and reinforcement |
| **Retrieval** | Accessing stored information when needed | Provide **cues** in the environment (recognition) |

## 2.4 Forgetting — Causes and Theories

| Cause | Mechanism | HFE Design Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **Decay** (Trace Decay Theory) | Memory trace fades with time | Use refresh displays, periodic reminders |
| **Proactive Interference** | Old memories interfere with new learning | Use distinct patterns for codes/alarms |
| **Retroactive Interference** | New learning overwrites old memories | Train sequentially; minimize concurrent protocols |
| **Retrieval Failure** (Tip-of-the-tongue) | Cue is inadequate or context mismatched | Provide contextual retrieval cues |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Numerical Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Miller's Law (STM Capacity) | $C_{STM} = 7 \pm 2$ chunks | **chunks** | UI menu items, alarm count limits |
| 2 | Iconic Memory Duration | $t_{iconic} \approx 0.25 - 0.5$ s | **seconds** | Display flicker, warning lamp flash rates |
| 3 | Echoic Memory Duration | $t_{echoic} \approx 3 - 4$ s | **seconds** | Verbal alarm audibility window |
| 4 | STM Duration (no rehearsal) | $t_{STM} \approx 15 - 30$ s | **seconds** | Procedural step timeout limits |
| 5 | Forgetting Curve (Ebbinghaus) | $R = e^{-t/S}$ | **retention R, time t, strength S** | Predicting knowledge decay in training |
| 6 | Rehearsal Effect | $R_{rehearsed} \gg R_{unrehearsed}$ | qualitative | Procedural check-listing |
| 7 | Recognition vs. Recall Ratio | $P_{recog} / P_{recall} \approx 2 - 3$ | dimensionless | Interface design (multiple choice vs. typing) |
| 8 | Hick's Law (Choice Reaction) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | **time T, options n** | Number of control switches |
| 9 | Fitts' Law (Target Acquisition) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ | **time T, distance D, width W** | Button/touchscreen sizing |
| 10 | Chunking Compression | $n_{effective} = n / k$ | **chunks, k items/chunk** | Phone number grouping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note:** In the formulas above, all vertical bars have been written as `\vert` or rendered as descriptive text to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. When copying these into a LaTeX document, replace descriptive terms with `\vert x \vert` notation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The memory model directly drives:
- **Aviation Cockpit Design** — Primary Flight Displays use **redundant, continuous visual readout** to offload STM.
- **Medical Device UI** — Infusion pumps use **color + shape coding** to leverage recognition over recall.
- **Nuclear Control Rooms** — Mimic panels provide **spatial cues** to offload WM's Visuospatial Sketchpad.
- **Mobile App Design** — Onboarding flows chunked at ≤5 steps to respect WM limits.
- **Automotive HMI** — Voice commands shift load from Visuospatial to Phonological Loop to reduce driver distraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Estimating STM Capacity Under Chunking

**Problem Statement (Model KTU-style):**
Aircraft maintenance crew is given a 16-digit component serial number. Using Miller's Law, determine:
(a) The minimum number of training repetitions required if items are read as single digits.
(b) The reduced cognitive load if the digits are chunked into 4-digit groups.
(c) The qualitative difference in retrieval error rate.

### Given:
- $N_{digits} = 16$
- $C_{STM} = 7 \pm 2$ (Miller's Law)
- Number of chunks when chunked in 4s: $k = 4$

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Items without chunking:**

The number of items to retain is:

$$N_{unchunked} = 16 \text{ digits}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Miller's Capacity Check:**

We compare $N_{unchunked}$ to $C_{STM}$:

$$\frac{N_{unchunked}}{C_{STM}} = \frac{16}{7 \pm 2} = \frac{16}{5 \text{ to } 9}$$

This gives a range:

$$= 1.78 \text{ to } 3.20 \text{ overload factor}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion for (a):**

The 16-digit sequence **exceeds STM capacity** by a factor of 1.78 to 3.20. Holding the entire sequence in STM is infeasible; the operator will rely on **external aids (paper, screen)** or **rehearsal**.

**Step 4 — Items with chunking (4-digit groups):**

The number of chunks becomes:

$$N_{chunked} = \frac{N_{digits}}{k} = \frac{16}{4} = 4 \text{ chunks}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Miller's Capacity Check to chunks:**

$$\frac{N_{chunked}}{C_{STM}} = \frac{4}{7 \pm 2} = \frac{4}{5 \text{ to } 9}$$

This gives a range:

$$= 0.44 \text{ to } 0.80$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion for (b):**

With 4-digit chunking, the cognitive load is **0.44 to 0.80 of STM capacity** — well within limits. This is why **credit card numbers, phone numbers, and aircraft tail numbers are formatted with hyphens or spaces** (e.g., `AERO-7391-XK22-1147`).

**Step 7 — Conclusion for (c) — Retrieval Error Rate:**

Empirical studies (e.g., Ericsson et al., 1980) show that **chunking reduces retrieval error rate by approximately 60–80%** for digit-span tasks of length 10–16. The error rate ratio is approximated as:

$$E_{ratio} = \frac{E_{unchunked}}{E_{chunked}} \approx 3 \text{ to } 5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Always state that **chunking is a behavioral-level optimization**, not a change in biological STM capacity. Capacity remains 7 ± 2; what changes is the *granularity* of each chunk.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Forgetting Curve Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A new operator was trained on a critical safety procedure. Using Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve, calculate the retention probability after 7 days, given a memory strength parameter $S = 86400$ s (1 day).

### Given:
- Forgetting equation: $R(t) = e^{-t/S}$
- Time elapsed: $t = 7 \text{ days} = 7 \times 86400 \text{ s} = 604800 \text{ s}$
- Memory strength: $S = 86400 \text{ s}$

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Substitute the time ratio:**

$$\frac{t}{S} = \frac{7 \times 86400}{86400} = 7$$

**Step 2 — Apply the forgetting equation:**

$$R(7) = e^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$R(7) = 0.0009119$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage:**

$$R(7) \approx 0.091\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

After 7 days without reinforcement, retention drops to less than 0.1% of original. This is why **safety-critical procedures require periodic retraining and refresher drills** at intervals shorter than the half-life of memory:

$$t_{1/2} = S \cdot \ln(2) = 86400 \times 0.693 = 59875 \text{ s} \approx 16.6 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often forget to **convert units** (days → seconds) before substituting into the exponential. Always homogenize units first.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Recognition vs. Recall Performance

**Problem Statement:**
A medical device interface offers two confirmation methods: (i) the operator types a 6-digit drug code from memory (Recall), (ii) the operator selects the drug name from a dropdown list (Recognition). Estimate the relative cognitive load using a simplified model where Recall effort $E_R = k \cdot L$ and Recognition effort $E_{Rn} = k \cdot \log_2(L)$, with $k = 1.0$ and $L = 100$ possible drug codes.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Recall effort:**

$$E_R = 1.0 \times 100 = 100 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Recognition effort:**

$$E_{Rn} = 1.0 \times \log_2(100)$$

$$E_{Rn} = 1.0 \times 6.64 = 6.64 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effort ratio:**

$$\frac{E_R}{E_{Rn}} = \frac{100}{6.64} \approx 15.06$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Recognition reduces cognitive effort by a factor of **~15×** in this example. This is the mathematical justification for HFE's principle: **"Always design for recognition rather than recall."**

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Cross-Topic Comparison)

| Memory Subsystem | Capacity | Duration | Encoding Method | Retrieval Mode | Failure Mode | Design Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensory (Iconic) | Very high | **~250 ms** | Raw sensory | Pre-attentive | Masking | Distinct stimuli |
| Sensory (Echoic) | Very high | **~3 s** | Raw auditory | Pre-attentive | Masking | Alarm repetition |
| STM | **7 ± 2** chunks | **15–30 s** | Acoustic/visual | Conscious search | Decay, displacement | Chunking, checklists |
| WM (Phon. Loop) | ~2 s of speech | ~2 s | Verbal rehearsal | Subvocal rehearsal | Articulatory suppression | Multimodal displays |
| WM (Visuo. Sketchpad) | 3–4 objects | Brief | Visual coding | Spatial rotation | Visual masking | Spatial grouping |
| WM (Central Exec.) | 1 task at a time | Variable | Supervisory | Attention switch | Bottleneck | Task prioritization |
| LTM (Declarative) | Unlimited | Lifetime | Semantic/elaborative | Free recall/cued | Interference, decay | Mnemonics, training |
| LTM (Procedural) | Unlimited | Lifetime | Behavioral repetition | Automatic execution | Rusty skills | Periodic drills |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Atkinson-Shiffrin Three-Store Memory Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[External Stimulus] --> B[Sensory Memory]
    B -->|Attention| C[Short-Term Memory]
    C -->|Maintenance Rehearsal| C
    C -->|Elaborative Rehearsal| D[Long-Term Memory]
    D -->|Retrieval| C
    C -->|Decay| E[Forgotten]
    B -->|No Attention| E
    D -->|Interference| E
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333

```


## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Memory Process Flow in Human-Operator Task

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[1. Stimulus Presentation - Display or Alarm] --> P2[2. Sensory Encoding]
    P2 --> P3{3. Attention Filter}
    P3 -->|Selected| P4[4. WM Encoding - Phonological or Visuospatial]
    P3 -->|Filtered Out| P5[5. Loss of Information]
    P4 --> P6{6. LTM Match Search}
    P6 -->|Match Found| P7[7. Pattern Recognition - Cue Retrieved]
    P6 -->|No Match| P8[8. Problem Solving - Heuristic or Deliberation]
    P7 --> P9[9. Decision - Action Selected]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[10. Motor Response - Action Execution]
    P10 --> P11[11. Feedback Loop - Result Observation]
    P11 --> P1
    style P3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style P6 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style P5 fill:#AAAAAA,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Forgetting Mechanism Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Phase]
        E1[Encoded Memory Trace]
    end
    subgraph STORAGE[Storage Phase]
        S1[Time Passage Variable t]

        S3[Interference from New Learning]
        S4[Interference from Old Learning]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Phase]
        R1[Retrieval Cue Strength]
        R2[Context Match Score]
        R3[Final Retrieval Result]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S3 --> S2
    S4 --> S2
    S2 --> R3
    R1 --> R3
    R2 --> R3
    style E1 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333
    style R3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Memory-Aware Interface Design

```mermaid
graph LR
    O[Operator] -->|Visual Scan| D1[Display Layer]
    O -->|Auditory Listen| D2[Alarm Layer]
    D1 --> WM1[WM - Visuospatial Sketchpad]
    D2 --> WM2[WM - Phonological Loop]
    WM1 --> CE[Central Executive]
    WM2 --> CE
    CE --> LTM1[Trained Procedure Memory]
    CE --> LTM2[Domain Knowledge Base]
    LTM1 --> DEC[Decision Output]
    LTM2 --> DEC
    DEC --> M[Motor Action]
    M --> FB[Feedback Display]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Short-Term Memory. State its capacity and duration as per Miller's Law.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Short-Term Memory (STM) is the cognitive store that holds a limited amount of information in conscious awareness for active processing. According to **George Miller's Law (1956)**, the capacity of STM is **7 ± 2 chunks**, and the duration without rehearsal is approximately **15 to 30 seconds**. STM serves as the workspace for reasoning, comprehension, and learning. Information is maintained through **maintenance rehearsal** (silent repetition) and is either transferred to Long-Term Memory via **elaborative rehearsal** or lost through decay or displacement. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for capacity 7 ± 2, 1 mark for duration 15–30 s with rehearsal mention)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Proactive and Retroactive Interference with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proactive Interference | Retroactive Interference |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Old learning interferes with **new** learning | **New** learning interferes with **old** memory |
| **Example** | A mechanic trained on an old engine model makes errors when learning a new model | A new password overwrites the operator's recall of the previous system password |

(1 mark per row of correct directional explanation + 1 mark for valid examples)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the Atkinson-Shiffrin Three-Store Model of Memory with a neat block diagram. Discuss its relevance in human-machine interface design. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Atkinson-Shiffrin Three-Store Model** (1968) is the foundational multi-store model of human memory. It proposes that information flows through three sequential stores: **Sensory Memory**, **Short-Term Memory (STM)**, and **Long-Term Memory (LTM)**.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Stimulus → SENSORY MEMORY (Iconic / Echoic / Haptic)
                ↓ (Attention)
        SHORT-TERM MEMORY (7 ± 2 chunks, 15-30 s)
                ↓ (Elaborative Rehearsal)
         LONG-TERM MEMORY (Unlimited, Permanent)
                ↑ (Retrieval)
                ↓ (Decay / Displacement)
              FORGOTTEN
```

**Sensory Memory** briefly holds raw sensory input:
- **Iconic memory** for vision (~250–500 ms)
- **Echoic memory** for hearing (~3–4 s)
- Most data is filtered out unless attended to. (1 mark)

**Short-Term Memory (STM)** holds attended information in conscious awareness. Capacity is **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller, 1956), and duration is **15–30 seconds** without rehearsal. Maintenance rehearsal (silent repetition) keeps information active. (1 mark)

**Long-Term Memory (LTM)** is the relatively permanent store with virtually unlimited capacity. Information enters LTM through **elaborative rehearsal** (semantic, deep processing). LTM contains declarative (semantic + episodic) and procedural knowledge. (1 mark)

**Encoding, Storage, Retrieval:** The model describes three memory processes — encoding (input → store), storage (maintenance), and retrieval (LTM → STM → response). (1 mark)

**Relevance to HMI Design (3 marks):**
1. **Display Design:** Visual displays should be designed to be perceived within iconic memory duration (~250 ms); high-frequency flicker is perceived as motion.
2. **Alarm Design:** Auditory alarms must exceed echoic duration to be consciously heard; alarm repetition leverages echoic memory.
3. **Interface Chunking:** Menus, control groupings, and procedure steps should respect 7 ± 2 limit; chunking reduces cognitive load.
4. **Cueing and Recognition:** Recognition-based interfaces (icons, dropdowns) reduce recall load compared to typing from memory.
5. **Training and Documentation:** Long-interval retraining leverages LTM retention curves; elaborate rehearsal (meaningful practice) outperforms rote repetition.
6. **Checklist Use:** Checklists offload STM to external memory, reducing error in safety-critical tasks (aviation, surgery, nuclear).

(Remaining 1 mark for clean diagram and overall coherence)

---

**(b) Describe Baddeley and Hitch's Working Memory Model. Explain how the Central Executive coordinates the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad, with an example from aviation or process control. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Baddeley and Hitch (1974) replaced the unitary STM concept with a multi-component **Working Memory (WM) Model**, later updated in 2000 with the addition of the **Episodic Buffer**. WM is a limited-capacity system for **active manipulation** of information, not merely passive storage. (1 mark)

**Components:**

1. **Central Executive** — The attention controller of WM. It has **no storage capacity**; it coordinates the two slave systems, manages task switching, and selects between competing inputs. It is linked to **selective attention, divided attention, and inhibitory control**. (1 mark)

2. **Phonological Loop** — Handles **verbal and acoustic** information. It has two sub-parts:
   - **Phonological Store** (inner ear): holds speech-based sounds for ~2 seconds.
   - **Articulatory Rehearsal Process** (inner voice): subvocal repetition that refreshes the store.
   - **Susceptible to articulatory suppression** (interference from speech). (1 mark)

3. **Visuospatial Sketchpad** — Handles **visual and spatial** information. It manipulates mental images, tracks object locations, and supports spatial orientation. (1 mark)

4. **Episodic Buffer** (added 2000) — A multimodal integrative component that **binds** information from the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and LTM into coherent episodic representations. It has limited capacity (like STM) and connects WM to LTM. (1 mark)

**Coordination by Central Executive:**

The Central Executive does not store information; it **allocates attentional resources** to the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad based on task demands. When a task is verbal, it prioritizes the phonological loop; when visual-spatial, it prioritizes the sketchpad. In dual-task conditions, the Central Executive must **time-share** between them, often leading to **interference and performance decrement**. (1 mark)

**Example from Aviation (1 mark for example):**

A pilot flying an instrument approach in low visibility must simultaneously:
- **Listen to Air Traffic Control (ATC) clearances** — loading the **Phonological Loop**.
- **Monitor the Primary Flight Display (PFD) for altitude, heading, glide slope** — loading the **Visuospatial Sketchpad**.
- **Make control inputs to maintain course** — engaging the **Central Executive**.

If ATC issues a long verbal clearance at the same time the pilot must interpret a rapidly changing visual display, the **Central Executive becomes overloaded**, leading to **task shedding, omission errors, or mode confusion** (a well-documented cause of aviation accidents such as the 1995 American Airlines Flight 965 Cali crash, where attentional overload contributed to controlled-flight-into-terrain).

**Design Countermeasure:** Modern glass cockpits integrate auditory and visual information (e.g., synthetic voice callouts + visual altitude alerts) to **distribute load across both slave systems**, reducing Central Executive bottleneck.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Define memory. Explain the three types of sensory memory with their durations and the factors that determine transition from sensory to short-term memory. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Memory is the cognitive neurobiological process by which humans encode, store, and retrieve information. In HFE, memory is treated as a bounded information-processing subsystem with finite capacity and time-dependent decay.

**Three Types of Sensory Memory (4 marks):**

| Type | Modality | Duration | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Iconic Memory** | Visual | **~250–500 milliseconds** | Briefly holds visual image after stimulus offset |
| **Echoic Memory** | Auditory | **~3–4 seconds** | Briefly holds auditory trace; allows comprehension of speech |
| **Haptic Memory** | Tactile | **~2 seconds** | Briefly holds touch/pressure sensations |

**Additional Sensory Stores (for completeness):**
- **Olfactory Memory** (smell) — duration highly variable
- **Gustatory Memory** (taste) — duration highly variable

**Factors Determining Transition to STM (2 marks):**

The transition from sensory memory to STM is governed by **attention**, which acts as a **bottleneck filter**. Key factors are:

1. **Intensity of Stimulus** — Stronger signals (louder alarm, brighter display) capture attention more readily.
2. **Novelty** — Unexpected or sudden stimuli preferentially capture attention (cockpit warning lights flash to exploit this).
3. **Meaningfulness** — Stimuli that match patterns in LTM are more easily attended to (e.g., one's own name in a noisy room — the "cocktail party effect").
4. **Task Relevance / Top-Down Goals** — Operator's current goals bias attention (a pilot monitors engine instruments more than cabin lights).
5. **Spatial Location** — Sudden peripheral motion captures attention (visual orienting reflex).

Without sufficient attention, sensory traces **decay within their modality-specific duration** and never enter STM.

---

**(b) Discuss the Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve. A worker is trained on a 10-step assembly procedure. After how many days will retention drop to 25% if the memory strength parameter $S = 2$ days? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve (3 marks for theory):**

Hermann Ebbinghaus (1885) established the **Forgetting Curve**, describing how memory retention declines exponentially with time when no rehearsal occurs. The mathematical form is:

$$R(t) = e^{-t/S}$$

where:
- $R(t)$ = retention probability at time $t$
- $t$ = elapsed time
- $S$ = memory strength parameter (related to the learner's prior knowledge and rehearsal)

**Key Properties:**
- **Exponential decay** — most forgetting occurs shortly after learning.
- **Asymptote** — some residual memory persists even after long intervals.
- **Rehearsal** — active rehearsal and spaced repetition **flatten the curve** and increase $S$.
- **Individual differences** — meaningful material decays slower than nonsense syllables.

**HFE Application:** Safety-critical training (fire drills, cockpit procedures) must account for Ebbinghaus decay. Refresher intervals should be **shorter than the half-life** $t_{1/2}$:

$$t_{1/2} = S \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \cdot S$$

---

**Numerical Problem (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R(t) = 0.25$ (25% retention)
- $S = 2$ days
- Find: $t$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the equation:**

$$0.25 = e^{-t / 2}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln(0.25) = -\frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$\ln(0.25) = -1.3863$$

**Step 4 — Solve for t:**

$$-1.3863 = -\frac{t}{2}$$

$$t = 2 \times 1.3863 = 2.7726 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Retention drops to 25% after approximately **2.77 days (about 2 days 18 hours)**. The half-life in this case is:

$$t_{1/2} = 2 \times \ln(2) = 1.386 \text{ days}$$

**HFE Design Recommendation:** Refresher training should be scheduled **before 1.386 days** (i.e., within ~33 hours) to maintain retention above 50%. For long-term skill retention, **spaced repetition at increasing intervals** is recommended.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Memory Questions (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing STM with WM** — STM is a *store*; WM is an *active processing system*. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if you treat them as synonymous.
> 2. **Wrong capacity value** — Always quote **7 ± 2 (Miller, 1956)** for STM capacity. Writing "5 to 9" is acceptable; "short" is not.
> 3. **Confusing iconic and echoic durations** — Iconic is **0.25–0.5 s**, echoic is **3–4 s**. Mixing these up costs 1 mark easily.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** in $R = e^{-t/S}$ — Always write $t$ and $S$ in the **same units** (seconds, days, or hours) before computing.
> 5. **Not labeling the diagram** — In Q.(a) of Part B, the block diagram is worth 2 marks; nodes must be **labeled with names AND durations/capacities**.
> 6. **Ignoring HFE application** — Memory questions are in *Human Factors Engineering*; pure psychology answers without engineering application lose 30–40% of marks.
> 7. **Mixing up interference types** — Proactive = old → new; Retroactive = new → old. Examiner will deduct if direction is reversed.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Memory** = Encoding + Storage + Retrieval (3 processes, 1 mark each)
- [ ] **Three-Store Model**: Sensory → STM → LTM (Atkinson-Shiffrin, 1968)
- [ ] **STM Capacity**: **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law)
- [ ] **STM Duration**: **15–30 seconds** without rehearsal
- [ ] **LTM Capacity**: Unlimited; **Duration**: Lifetime
- [ ] **Iconic Memory**: Visual, **~250–500 ms**
- [ ] **Echoic Memory**: Auditory, **~3–4 s**
- [ ] **Haptic Memory**: Tactile, **~2 s**
- [ ] **Working Memory Model** (Baddeley & Hitch): Central Executive + Phonological Loop + Visuospatial Sketchpad + Episodic Buffer
- [ ] **Central Executive** has **NO storage capacity** — only attention control
- [ ] **Forgetting Curve**: $R(t) = e^{-t/S}$ (Ebbinghaus)
- [ ] **Half-life of Memory**: $t_{1/2} = S \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 S$
- [ ] **Proactive Interference**: Old interferes with new
- [ ] **Retroactive Interference**: New interferes with old
- [ ] **Recognition > Recall** by factor of 2–3 in error rate
- [ ] **Chunking** reduces cognitive load without changing biological capacity
- [ ] **HFE Principle**: "Design for recognition, not recall"

### 🎯 Common KTU Question Patterns

1. *Define STM and state its capacity and duration.* (3 marks, every year)
2. *Differentiate between sensory, STM, and LTM.* (3 marks)
3. *Explain Baddeley-Hitch Working Memory Model with diagram.* (7 or 14 marks)
4. *Explain Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve and compute retention.* (7 marks — numerical)
5. *Discuss HFE design implications of memory limitations.* (7 marks)
6. *Proactive vs Retroactive Interference with examples.* (3–7 marks)

### 📚 Mnemonic for Memory Subsystems

**"I Eat Hot Oatmeal Like Lunch"**
- **I**conic (visual)
- **E**choic (auditory)
- **H**aptic (touch)
- **O**lfactory (smell)
- **L**ike
- **L**ong-term memory (LTM)

(For sensory subtypes in order of typical exam importance)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decision Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Making** in Human Factors Engineering is defined as the **cognitive process** by which an individual or a team selects a course of action (or inaction) from among multiple alternative options, based on specific goals, constraints, available information, and perceived risks, in order to achieve a desired objective within a human-machine or human-system operating environment.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT748)**, Decision Making is treated as a **higher-order cognitive function** that bridges human perception, memory, reasoning, and motor execution. It is one of the central pillars of **cognitive ergonomics**, alongside perception, attention, and memory.

$$ \text{Decision Making} = f(\text{Perception}, \text{Memory}, \text{Reasoning}, \text{Experience}, \text{Context}) $$

The formal academic taxonomy classifies decisions along two primary axes:

| Dimension | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Cognitive Load** | Programmed (Routine) | Non-Programmed (Novel) |
| **Strategic Level** | Operational | Strategic / Tactical |
| **Time Pressure** | Deliberative | Real-Time / Time-Critical |
| **Certainty of Outcome** | Risk-Based | Under Certainty / Uncertainty |
| **Stakeholder Scope** | Individual | Group / Organizational |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu Problem"**

Imagine you walk into a large restaurant with a **200-item menu**. You are hungry, slightly distracted, your phone is buzzing, the waiter is waiting, and you have a limited budget. How do you choose?

This everyday act mirrors **engineering decision making**:

1. **Information Gathering (Input Stage):** You scan the menu, recall past meals, listen to the waiter.
2. **Option Generation (Deliberation Stage):** You narrow down to 3–4 dishes.
3. **Evaluation (Judgment Stage):** You compare taste, price, health, time-to-serve.
4. **Selection (Commitment Stage):** You point at one item and say "I will have this."
5. **Post-Decision Audit (Feedback Stage):** The food arrives; you judge whether your choice was good.

In an industrial cockpit, control room, or driver-assistance scenario, the human operator performs the **exact same sequence** but with **higher stakes**, **more information**, and **less time**. The **Human Factors Engineer** designs displays, controls, training, and procedures to make this *menu* as **clear, fast, and error-resistant** as possible.

---

## 1.3 Why Decision Making is Critical to Human Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A large fraction of industrial accidents (Three Mile Island, Air France 447, Chernobyl) are traced not to mechanical failure but to **flawed human decisions under stress, ambiguity, or information overload**. Studying decision making enables the engineer to design **defense-in-depth systems** that prevent, detect, and recover from cognitive errors.

**Standard KTU-recognized metrics for decision performance:**

- **Decision Latency (T_d):** Time elapsed from stimulus onset to action initiation, typically measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Decision Accuracy (A_d):** Ratio of correct decisions to total decisions made, expressed as a **percentage (%)** or **proportion (0–1)**.
- **Decision Confidence (C_d):** Subjective certainty rating, often on a **1–7 Likert scale**.
- **Information Utilization Rate (I_u):** Bits of relevant information actually integrated into the decision, measured in **bits/decision**.

---

## 1.4 The KTU 2024 Syllabus Position

In the **PESFT748 Module 1** curriculum, Decision Making is positioned as the **culminating cognitive process** that integrates outputs from:

- *Sensation & Perception*
- *Attention & Workload*
- *Short-Term & Long-Term Memory*
- *Learning & Skill Acquisition*
- *Reasoning & Problem Solving*

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 Mapping:** Decision Making serves as the **integration capstone** for all prior Module 1 sub-topics, before the course transitions to Module 2 (Human-Machine Systems, Displays, and Controls).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classical Decision-Making Pipeline

The **canonical decision-making pipeline** recognized by KTU examiners consists of six sequential stages. Each stage has measurable outputs and known failure modes that Human Factors Engineers must address.

### Stage 1 — **Information Acquisition (Sense & Perceive)**

The operator detects, decodes, and interprets sensory signals from the environment.

$$ \text{Stage 1 Output} = \text{Perceived State} \approx \text{True State} + \text{Perceptual Noise} $$

**Why it matters:** If the operator misreads an altimeter by 500 ft due to *misalignment*, every downstream decision inherits the error (the **Garbage-In-Garbage-Out principle**).

### Stage 2 — **Situation Assessment**

The operator forms an internal **mental model** of what is happening and why.

$$ M_{\text{mental}} = \text{Schema}_{\text{prior}} + \text{Evidence}_{\text{current}} \rightarrow \text{Situation Diagnosis} $$

### Stage 3 — **Option Generation (Hypothesis Formation)**

The operator retrieves or constructs candidate actions from **long-term memory**, **training scripts**, and **procedural manuals**.

$$ \text{Options} = \{A_1, A_2, A_3, \dots, A_n\} $$

### Stage 4 — **Option Evaluation & Risk Assessment**

Each candidate is appraised against the **goal state**, **constraints**, and **expected utilities**.

$$ \text{EU}(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} P_{ij} \cdot V_{ij} $$

Where:
- $EU(A_i)$ = Expected Utility of action $A_i$
- $P_{ij}$ = Probability of outcome $j$ given action $A_i$
- $V_{ij}$ = Value (subjective worth) of outcome $j$
- $m$ = total number of possible outcomes

### Stage 5 — **Action Selection (Commitment)**

The operator commits to one option. This is the **decision point** itself.

$$ A^* = \arg\max_{i} \, EU(A_i) \quad \text{(Rational Model)} $$

### Stage 6 — **Execution & Feedback Monitoring**

The action is executed, and outcomes are monitored against expectations to update the mental model.

$$ \Delta_{\text{error}} = \text{Predicted Outcome} - \text{Observed Outcome} $$

If $|\Delta_{\text{error}}| > \text{Threshold}$, the operator may **re-enter the decision cycle** (feedback loop).

---

## 2.2 Major Theoretical Models of Decision Making

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU 2024 examiner** expects students to be able to **compare and contrast** at least three major decision-making models. Below are the canonical five.

### Model A — **Rational (Classical) Model**

The operator is assumed to be a **Homo Economicus** with unlimited time, complete information, and stable preferences.

**Decision rule:**

$$ A^* = \arg\max_{i} \, EU(A_i) \quad \text{subject to: complete information, unlimited cognitive resources} $$

| Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|
| Mathematically elegant | Unrealistic in real time-critical systems |
| Provides normative baseline | Ignores human cognitive limits |
| Useful for AI/automation design | Cannot explain observed human biases |

### Model B — **Bounded Rationality (Herbert Simon)**

Humans are **satisficers**, not optimizers. They seek **"good enough"** rather than **"best possible."**

$$ A^* = \text{First option where } EU(A_i) \geq \text{aspiration level } \alpha $$

| Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|
| Realistic for time-pressured operators | Aspiration level is fuzzy |
| Explains shortcuts & heuristics | Hard to predict exact choice |
| Foundation of modern cognitive HF/E | May undervalue expertise-driven optimization |

### Model C — **Recognition-Primed Decision (RPD) Model — Klein**

Used extensively in **naturalistic decision making (NDM)** research (firefighters, military, nurses).

The experienced operator recognizes a situation as a **prototype** from past experience and retrieves a **course of action (CoA)** that worked before.

$$ \text{Decision} = f(\text{Situation Recognition}, \text{Experience Repertoire}, \text{Expectancies}) $$

### Model D — **Image Theory (Beach & Mitchell)**

Decisions are guided by three internal images:
- **Value Image:** Principles and moral standards.
- **Trajectory Image:** Long-term life/career goals.
- **Strategic Image:** Plans to achieve trajectory while respecting values.

$$ \text{Compatible Option} = \text{Value} \cap \text{Trajectory} \cap \text{Strategic} $$

### Model E — **Anchoring & Adjustment (Tversky-Kahneman Heuristics)**

Decision = Initial Anchor + Sequential Adjustments based on new information.

$$ \text{Estimate}_{final} = \text{Anchor} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Delta_k $$

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Decision Making

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Discuss the factors that influence human decision making in industrial settings."* The answer must cover the following **six classes**.

### Factor 1 — **Individual Factors**

- Age, fatigue, circadian rhythm, training, expertise, personality (Type A vs Type B), risk tolerance, and emotional state.

### Factor 2 — **Task Factors**

- Task complexity, information availability, time pressure, signal-to-noise ratio, and number of alternatives.

### Factor 3 — **Environmental Factors**

- Ambient noise (dB), lighting (lux), temperature (°C), vibration (m/s²), and gravitational stress (G).

### Factor 4 — **Organizational Factors**

- Crew Resource Management (CRM) quality, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), safety culture, and supervisory pressure.

### Factor 5 — **Cognitive Factors**

- Working memory load, attentional tunneling, confirmation bias, and anchoring.

### Factor 6 — **Informational & Display Factors**

- Compatibility of display-control mappings (Poulton, 1970), legibility, and **Population Stereotype Effects**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship you are expected to know for Module 1 Decision Making questions. **Memorize the symbol, the equation, and the units.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Key | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Utility (Normative) | $EU(A_i) = \sum_{j} P_{ij} V_{ij}$ | $P_{ij}$ = probability, $V_{ij}$ = value | Utility (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Subjective Expected Utility (SEU) | $SEU = \sum w_k \cdot P_k \cdot V_k$ | $w_k$ = subjective weight | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Decision Accuracy | $A_d = \dfrac{N_{\text{correct}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | $N$ = counts | Percent (%) |
| 4 | Decision Latency (Hick's Law) | $T_d = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of choices, $a,b$ = constants | Seconds (s) |
| 5 | Signal Detection (Sensitivity) | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | $z$ = z-score of Hit & False Alarm rates | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Response Criterion (Bias) | $\beta = \dfrac{f(S \vert N)}{f(S \vert S)}$ | Likelihood ratio at criterion | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Signal Detection (Accuracy) | $P_c = 0.5 + \dfrac{d'}{2\sqrt{2}}$ (approx.) | $d'$ = sensitivity | Proportion (0–1) |
| 8 | Bayes' Theorem Update | $P(H \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Posterior probability | Proportion (0–1) |
| 9 | Risk Acceptance (Von Neumann-Morgenstern) | $U(x) = \sum p_i \cdot u(x_i)$ | $u$ = utility function | Utility |
| 10 | Information Bits (Shannon) | $H = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = event probabilities | Bits |
| 11 | Aspiration Level Update | $\alpha_{t+1} = \alpha_t + \eta (V_t - \alpha_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate | Utility |
| 12 | Naturalistic Decision (RPD) | $CoA_{selected} = \text{Recognized Match}(\text{Situation})$ | Implicit recognition | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Reminder:** All absolute value symbols in formulas above use `\vert` style notation embedded in the equation structure (e.g., $|\Delta_{\text{error}}|$ written as `\vert \Delta_{\text{error}} \vert` to prevent markdown table breakage).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The decision-making framework directly informs the design of:

1. **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Decision Support Tools** — reduce decision latency via automated conflict detection.
2. **Anesthesia Alarm Prioritization** — reduce cognitive load via *smart alarm ranking*.
3. **Nuclear Reactor Control Rooms** — support option generation via *Safety Parameter Display Systems (SPDS)*.
4. **Automotive Driver-Assist (ADAS)** — implement Hick's-Law-compliant menu structures.
5. **Military Command & Control** — apply RPD-based training to accelerate recognition of battlefield patterns.
6. **Medical Triage Systems** — apply Bounded Rationality + heuristics to design rapid triage protocols.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hick's Law for Decision Latency

**Hick's Law** quantifies how the **number of alternative choices** affects the time required to make a decision. It is one of the most frequently tested formulas in KTU exams.

### Step 1 — Empirical Starting Point

William Edmund Hick (1952) observed experimentally that reaction time increases logarithmically with the number of equiprobable stimulus-response alternatives.

$$ T_d = a + b \cdot H $$

Where:
- $T_d$ = decision latency (seconds)
- $a$ = base sensory-motor reaction time (constant, typically 0.2–0.4 s)
- $b$ = scaling constant (typically 0.1–0.2 s/bit)
- $H$ = information entropy of the choice set (bits)

### Step 2 — Compute Entropy of the Choice Set

For $n$ **equiprobable** alternatives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
&= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{1}{n}\right) \log_2 \left(\dfrac{1}{n}\right) \\
&= -n \cdot \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot (-\log_2 n) \\
&= \log_2 n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Into the Base Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= a + b \cdot \log_2 n \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Hyman Correction for Forced-Choice Tasks

In forced-choice experiments, the operator must always select one of $n$ options (including the "no signal" option), so the modified form becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Hyman form** of Hick's Law, which is what KTU examiners prefer for exam answers.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A pilot must choose among 8 equally likely flap configurations during landing. Given $a = 0.30$ s and $b = 0.12$ s/bit, calculate the decision latency.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 8 \\
T_d &= a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(9) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.12 \cdot 3.1699 \\
&= 0.30 + 0.3804 \\
&= 0.6804 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Reducing choices from 8 to 4 cuts latency to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d(4) &= 0.30 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(5) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.12 \cdot 2.3219 \\
&= 0.30 + 0.2786 \\
&= 0.5786 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision saved:** $0.6804 - 0.5786 = 0.1018$ s ≈ **102 ms** per decision — significant in a 60-second final approach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Utility Maximization (Normative Decision Rule)

### Step 1 — Problem Setup

An air traffic controller must choose between two routing options $A$ and $B$ for an aircraft in distress. Outcomes and values are:

| Outcome | Probability (A) | Value (A) | Probability (B) | Value (B) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Safe landing | 0.80 | 100 utils | 0.65 | 100 utils |
| Diversion | 0.15 | 40 utils | 0.25 | 40 utils |
| Accident | 0.05 | -1000 utils | 0.10 | -1000 utils |

### Step 2 — Compute EU(A)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(A) &= \sum_{j=1}^{3} P(A_j) \cdot V(A_j) \\
&= (0.80 \times 100) + (0.15 \times 40) + (0.05 \times -1000) \\
&= 80 + 6 + (-50) \\
&= 36 \, \text{utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute EU(B)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(B) &= \sum_{j=1}^{3} P(B_j) \cdot V(B_j) \\
&= (0.65 \times 100) + (0.25 \times 40) + (0.10 \times -1000) \\
&= 65 + 10 + (-100) \\
&= -25 \, \text{utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Rational Choice Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^* &= \arg\max_{i} EU(A_i) \\
&= \arg\max \{EU(A), EU(B)\} \\
&= \arg\max \{36, -25\} \\
&= A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bounded Rationality Still Wins:** A real controller, fearing the -1000 utility tail risk, might **choose B despite its lower EU** — a phenomenon known as **risk-aversion bias**. KTU examiners reward answers that acknowledge this gap between *normative* (EU) and *descriptive* (real human) behavior.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayesian Updating of Beliefs

Decision making under uncertainty requires the operator to update beliefs as new evidence arrives. **Bayes' Theorem** is the formal engine.

### Step 1 — Prior Probability

A diagnostic indicator light shows *ON*. Engineering data says:
- $P(\text{Fault}) = 0.10$ (base rate of failure)
- $P(\text{Light ON} \vert \text{Fault}) = 0.95$ (sensitivity / hit rate)
- $P(\text{Light ON} \vert \text{No Fault}) = 0.08$ (false alarm rate)

### Step 2 — Compute Marginal Probability of the Evidence

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Light ON}) &= P(\text{Light ON} \vert \text{Fault}) \cdot P(\text{Fault}) \\
&\quad + P(\text{Light ON} \vert \text{No Fault}) \cdot P(\text{No Fault}) \\
&= (0.95 \times 0.10) + (0.08 \times 0.90) \\
&= 0.095 + 0.072 \\
&= 0.167
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Fault} \vert \text{Light ON}) &= \dfrac{P(\text{Light ON} \vert \text{Fault}) \cdot P(\text{Fault})}{P(\text{Light ON})} \\
&= \dfrac{0.95 \times 0.10}{0.167} \\
&= \dfrac{0.095}{0.167} \\
&= 0.5689 \approx 0.57
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> Seeing the warning light raised the belief of actual fault from a **10% prior** to a **57% posterior** — a 5.7× increase. The operator should treat this as **strong but not conclusive** evidence and seek corroborating information before acting (e.g., shut down the reactor).

### Step 5 — Updated Belief Becomes New Prior

If a second, independent sensor also shows ON with the same characteristics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Fault} \vert \text{Both ON}) &= \dfrac{0.95 \times 0.5689}{0.95 \times 0.5689 + 0.08 \times 0.4311} \\
&= \dfrac{0.5405}{0.5405 + 0.0345} \\
&= \dfrac{0.5405}{0.5750} \\
&= 0.9400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** Two independent corroborating signals push certainty to **94%** — enough to trigger shutdown.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Decision-Making Models

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical matrix is **high-yield exam content**. Examiners often give 14-mark questions that demand a tabular comparison plus a critical commentary.

| Attribute | Rational Model | Bounded Rationality | Recognition-Primed (RPD) | Image Theory | Heuristic Model |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Originator** | 18th-c. classical economics | 1957, H. Simon | 1986, G. Klein | 1990, Beach & Mitchell | 1974, Tversky & Kahneman |
| **Cognitive Load** | Maximum (complete analysis) | Moderate (aspiration cutoff) | Low (recognition-based) | Low–Moderate (3-image filter) | Very Low (rule of thumb) |
| **Time Required** | High | Moderate | Very Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Information Demand** | Complete | Partial | Pattern-based | Goal-aligned | Minimal |
| **Predictive Accuracy** | Normative only | Moderate–High | High for experts | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **Best Use Case** | Algorithm design, AI | Office knowledge work | Military, firefighting, ICU | Career/life planning | Everyday quick judgments |
| **Failure Mode** | Paralysis by analysis | Premature satisficing | Mis-recognition of pattern | Value-conflict freeze | Cognitive bias |
| **HF/E Design Implication** | Automate entirely | Provide decision aids | Train pattern recognition | Align displays with goals | Debias via checklists |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Decision-Making Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **six-stage decision pipeline** as a complete feedback-controlled system, including both human-cognitive and machine-environmental inputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENV[Environment and Stimulus Field] --> PER[Stage 1 Perception and Sense Making]
    PER --> ASS[Stage 2 Situation Assessment]
    MEM[Long Term Memory and Training Schemas] --> ASS
    ASS --> OPT[Stage 3 Option Generation]
    OPT --> EVA[Stage 4 Option Evaluation]
    EVA --> SEL[Stage 5 Action Selection and Commitment]
    SEL --> EXE[Stage 6 Execution and Motor Actuation]
    EXE --> FB[Feedback Monitoring and Outcome Comparison]
    FB --> ASS
    FB --> MEM
    TIME[Time Pressure and Workload] --> ASS
    TIME --> EVA
    CRM[Crew Resource Management and Team Input] --> OPT
    CRM --> EVA
    PROC[Standard Operating Procedures and SOPs] --> OPT
```

**Reading guide for KTU students:**
- Solid arrows = primary information flow direction.
- Feedback arrows (FB → ASS, FB → MEM) represent the **closed-loop** nature of decision making — outcomes reshape future assessments.
- Lateral inputs (TIME, CRM, PROC) represent **modulators** that influence multiple stages simultaneously.

---

## 4.2 Decision-Making Models Taxonomy

This Mermaid graph organizes the five canonical models into a single hierarchical taxonomy, making it easy to memorize their relationships for the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT[Decision Making Models]
    ROOT --> NORM[Normative Models]
    ROOT --> DES[Descriptive Models]
    ROOT --> NDM[Naturalistic Models]
    NORM --> RAT[Rational Expected Utility]
    RAT --> SEU[Subjective Expected Utility]
    NORM --> BAY[Bayesian Updating]
    DES --> BR[Bounded Rationality Simon]
    BR --> SAT[Satisficing]
    DES --> HEU[Heuristics Tversky Kahneman]
    HEU --> ANC[Anchoring]
    HEU --> AV[Availability]
    HEU --> REP[Representativeness]
    NDM --> RPD[Recognition Primed Klein]
    NDM --> IMG[Image Theory Beach Mitchell]
```

**Examiner tip:** When asked "Compare normative and descriptive models," use the diagram above to anchor your answer — *Normative* models prescribe what humans *should* do; *Descriptive* models describe what humans *actually* do.

---

## 4.3 Signal Detection Theory (SDT) Decision Block

This block diagram shows how an incoming sensory signal is processed through SDT to yield one of four outcomes — central to **decision-making-under-uncertainty** questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SIG[True Signal plus Noise Input] --> DEC[Internal Decision Threshold]
    NOI[Noise Only Input] --> DEC
    DEC -->|Signal Above Criterion| HIT[Hit Detection]
    DEC -->|Noise Above Criterion| FA[False Alarm]
    DEC -->|Signal Below Criterion| MISS[Miss]
    DEC -->|Noise Below Criterion| CR[Correct Rejection]
    HIT --> RESP1[Operator Responds Yes]
    FA --> RESP1
    MISS --> RESP2[Operator Responds No]
    CR --> RESP2
```

**Worked linkage to formulas:**
- **Sensitivity** $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ measures how well the operator discriminates signal from noise.
- **Bias** $\beta$ measures whether the operator is **conservative** (high $\beta$, few false alarms, many misses) or **liberal** (low $\beta$, many false alarms, few misses).

---

## 4.4 Hick's Law Visualization (Decision Latency vs Number of Choices)

This diagram illustrates the logarithmic relationship between the **number of alternatives** and **decision latency**, with the engineering principle of *option reduction* highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[n equals 2] --> T1[Td equals 0.58 s]
    N2[n equals 4] --> T2[Td equals 0.70 s]
    N3[n equals 8] --> T3[Td equals 0.85 s]
    N4[n equals 16] --> T4[Td equals 1.00 s]
    N5[n equals 32] --> T5[Td equals 1.15 s]
    T1 --> NOTE[Each doubling of n adds approximately 0.15 s to decision time]
    T2 --> NOTE
    T3 --> NOTE
    T4 --> NOTE
    T5 --> NOTE
```

**Design lesson:** Always limit **operator menu options to the smallest viable set**. *Less is more* in time-critical HF/E design.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Decision Making in the context of Human Factors Engineering. List any two factors that influence human decision making.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Decision Making is the cognitive process by which a human operator selects a course of action from among two or more alternatives, based on perceived information, prior experience, and goal constraints, within a human-machine system environment.

> **Any Two Factors (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Time Pressure** — short decision windows force satisficing.
> 2. **Information Quality** — poor display design causes mis-perception of state.
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: fatigue, training, stress, workload, organizational culture, noise, task complexity, risk perception.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Programmed and Non-Programmed decisions with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Programmed Decision | Non-Programmed Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Routine, repetitive, rule-based | Novel, unique, requires analysis |
| **Decision Maker** | Lower-level operator, automated system | Senior engineer, manager, AI system |
| **Time Available** | Short, real-time | Long, deliberative |
| **Cognitive Effort** | Low (scripted) | High (problem-solving) |
| **Example** | Pilot extends landing gear on final approach | Engineer redesigns reactor cooling after new fault data |

*(1 mark for definition pair, 1 mark for example pair, 1 mark for distinction.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — *Explain in detail the six stages of the human decision-making process with a suitable engineering example. Discuss how feedback loops operate within this pipeline.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The human decision-making pipeline, as formalized in cognitive Human Factors Engineering, comprises **six sequential stages**:

1. **Information Acquisition (Perception):** The operator senses and decodes environmental cues. Example: An anesthesiologist observes a sudden drop in SpO₂ waveform amplitude on the patient monitor.

2. **Situation Assessment:** The operator forms a mental diagnosis. Example: "The patient is becoming hypoxic — likely causes are airway obstruction or ventilator disconnection."

3. **Option Generation:** The operator retrieves candidate actions from training. Example: Options considered — check airway, check ventilator tubing, increase FiO₂, auscultate lungs.

4. **Option Evaluation:** Each candidate is assessed for expected utility, time cost, and risk. Example: "Check airway first (high probability, low cost); auscultation second (diagnostic value); increase FiO₂ last (defensive, lower specificity)."

5. **Action Selection (Commitment):** The operator commits. Example: "I will manually inspect the endotracheal tube placement immediately."

6. **Execution & Feedback Monitoring:** The action is performed, and the outcome (e.g., SpO₂ rising back to 98%) is monitored.

**Feedback Loops (Critical for 7-Mark Depth):**
- **Inner Loop (FB → Assessment):** Outcome observation triggers re-assessment. If SpO₂ continues to fall, the operator re-enters Stage 2 with a *new hypothesis* (e.g., tension pneumothorax).
- **Outer Loop (FB → Long-Term Memory):** Outcomes update the operator's *case library*. A successful intervention becomes a *schema* for future similar cases — the foundation of **Recognition-Primed Decision (RPD)** expertise.

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark per stage (×6 = 6) + 1 mark for feedback explanation = **7 marks**.

---

#### Part (b) — *Apply Bounded Rationality theory to explain why operators in time-critical systems rarely make optimal decisions. Suggest three Human Factors design interventions to mitigate this.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Theory (3 Marks):** Herbert Simon's Bounded Rationality (1957) asserts that human cognitive capacity is **limited** in three dimensions:
- **Bounded Attention:** Operators cannot attend to all cues simultaneously.
- **Bounded Information:** Sensor and display limitations restrict available data.
- **Bounded Time:** Real-time tasks truncate deliberation.

Consequently, operators **satisfice** — they select the first option that exceeds an **aspiration level** $\alpha$, rather than the mathematically optimal option.

**Three Design Interventions (4 Marks):**

1. **Decision Support Systems (DSS):** Embed algorithmic recommenders that pre-filter options, reducing the search space. Example: *TCAS Resolution Advisory* in aviation auto-suggests climb/descend commands.

2. **Tiered Information Display:** Present *only the most critical* variables at the top tier, with deeper detail on demand. Example: *NEP (Narrowed Engagement Panel)* in modern fighter cockpits.

3. **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs):** Encode satisficing heuristics into written procedures so all operators use the same "good enough" shortcut. Example: *Aviation Quick Reference Handbook (QRH)*.

4. *(Optional bonus)* Training in pattern recognition (RPD) to accelerate **Stage 2 Situation Assessment**.

> **Mark Allocation:** 3 marks for theory + 4 marks (1.3 each) for design interventions = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — *Explain Hick's Law. An aircraft pilot must select one of 8 flaps configurations during final approach. If the base reaction time is 0.30 s and the scaling constant is 0.12 s/bit, calculate the decision latency using the Hyman correction. Interpret the engineering significance.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Hick's Law (2 Marks):**
Hick's Law states that decision time increases logarithmically with the number of alternative choices. The Hyman-corrected form is:

$$ T_d = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1) $$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $a = 0.30$ s, $b = 0.12$ s/bit, $n = 8$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= 0.30 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(8 + 1) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(9) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.12 \cdot 3.1699 \\
&= 0.30 + 0.3804 \\
&= 0.6804 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Significance (2 Marks):**
A decision latency of **~680 ms** during final approach is operationally significant because the aircraft travels approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= v \cdot t = (75 \, \text{m/s}) \times (0.6804 \, \text{s}) \\
&= 51 \, \text{m} \approx 167 \, \text{ft}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reducing choices to 4 alternatives reduces latency to **0.5786 s** — saving **102 ms** and **~7.6 m** of ground track. Designers must therefore **prune unnecessary alternatives** from time-critical interfaces.

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for law statement + 3 marks for full calculation + 2 marks for interpretation = **7 marks**.

---

#### Part (b) — *Discuss the Recognition-Primed Decision (RPD) model with a real-world application. Explain how expertise transforms the decision pipeline.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**RPD Model (4 Marks):** Proposed by Gary Klein (1986), the Recognition-Primed Decision model asserts that experienced operators do not generate and compare multiple options in real time. Instead, they:

1. **Recognize** the situation as an instance of a **prior prototype** stored in long-term memory.
2. Retrieve the **course of action (CoA)** that worked in the previous instance.
3. **Mentally simulate** the CoA to check plausibility against the current situation.
4. If the simulation succeeds → **execute**; if it fails → retrieve the next best CoA.

**Real-World Application (1.5 Marks):** AICU charge nurse sees a patient with sudden hypotension + tachycardia + cool extremities → recognizes *septic shock prototype* → retrieves the *early-goal-directed therapy bundle* → executes without enumerating alternatives.

**Expertise Effect on Decision Pipeline (1.5 Marks):**
- **Novices:** Spend disproportionate time on Stage 4 (option evaluation); rely on conscious utility calculations.
- **Experts:** Collapse Stages 2–5 into a **single recognition-to-action** pattern; achieve decisions in **seconds** with high accuracy.
- **Implication:** Training should accelerate the *build-up* of pattern libraries — using case-based learning, simulation, and after-action reviews.

> **Mark Allocation:** 4 marks model + 1.5 application + 1.5 expertise discussion = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Decision-Making Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Normative" with "Descriptive":** Writing that humans *should* maximize EU instead of acknowledging that humans *actually* satisfice. Always distinguish both.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Feedback Loop:** Many answers describe the pipeline as strictly linear. Decision making is **cyclical** — always close the loop with a feedback arrow.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Units in Numerical Answers:** Hick's Law latency must carry the unit **seconds**; expected utility must be qualified as **utils** (dimensionless). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
>
> 4. **Misapplying Bayes' Theorem:** Common error is using $P(H)$ in the denominator instead of the full marginal $P(E)$. Always compute $P(E)$ via the law of total probability first.
>
> 5. **Listing Models without Comparison:** KTU expects **tabular comparison** for any 14-mark "Discuss models" question. Listing without contrast loses 3–4 marks.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the Bounded Rationality Critique:** Even when asked about the Rational model, you **must** mention Simon's critique for full marks. The examiner looks for the *limitation* section.
>
