The display designer controls sensation (via character size, contrast, color); the operator supplies perception (via experience, expectation, training). ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **car dashboard** at night. The *sensation* is the photons from the speedometer reaching your retina. The *perception* is your brain saying *"I am doing 60 km/h on a curve — I should slow down."* A good human-factors designer ensures the **sensation** is so clean (high contrast, correct size, proper color) that the **perception** is fast and error-free. ### Key Perceptual Modalities in Display Design | Modality | Receptors | Typical HFE Application | |----------|-----------|--------------------------| | Visual | Rods & Cones (retina) | VDT screens, gauges, signs | | Auditory | Hair cells (cochlea) | Alarms, voice messages | | Tactile | Meissner, Pacinian corpuscles | Braille, vibration alerts | | Proprioceptive | Muscle spindles | Control feel | For Module-2, the **visual modality** dominates, because 80–90% of all information transfer in modern control rooms is visual. --- ## 1.2 Hard Copy and VDT Screens — What Are They? ### Hard Copy A **hard copy** is a *static, paper-based, reflective* display — books, manuals, printed checklists, shipping labels, cockpit charts. Light is *reflected* from the page to the eye. ### VDT (Visual Display Terminal) Screen A **VDT** is a *dynamic, self-luminous, emissive* display — a CRT or LCD/LED monitor. Light is *emitted* by the screen directly toward the eye. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the distinction matters in HFE:** Because the eye behaves *very differently* in front of a *reflective* page (stable image, no flicker, high resolution, daylight-readable) versus an *emissive* screen (variable luminance, possible flicker, glare, low contrast in bright rooms). A 14-mark KTU question is almost always built on this contrast. | Parameter | Hard Copy | VDT Screen | |-----------|-----------|------------| | Light source | Reflected (external) | Self-emissive | | Resolution | 600–2400 dpi (laser/inkjet) | 72–200 dpi typical | | Refresh | None (static) | 50–144 Hz (must avoid flicker) | | Luminance | Fixed by ink & paper | User-adjustable | | Glare | Matte/glass-controlled | Direct + reflected glare | | Color | CMYK subtractive | RGB additive | | Best viewing distance | **300–400 mm** (reading arm) | **500–700 mm** (VDT ergonomic norm) | --- ## 1.3 Graphic Representations — Symbols **Definition:** > A **graphic symbol** (pictogram, icon, glyph) is an *abstracted, two-dimensional visual image* that conveys information by *resemblance* or *convention*, independent of any particular language. In HFE, symbols are the *visual alphabet* of universal displays: EXIT, ⚠️ radiation, 🧯 extinguisher, ☣ biohazard. > [!NOTE] > **Why symbols?** They *transcend literacy* and *language barriers*, allow **rapid recognition** (under 1 second) and reduce **cluttered panel space** that text would occupy. Their design is governed by strict international standards — **ISO 7001** (public information), **ISO 7010** (safety), **ANSI Z535.3** (USA criteria for safety symbols). ### Three Layers of Symbol Meaning (Eco's Tripartite, adapted) - **Iconic layer** — the picture itself (e.g., flame = 🔥) - **Indexical layer** — the causal / context link (flame → fire) - **Conventional layer** — the learned, agreed meaning (flame + circle = "flammable substance") A symbol fails the moment the operator's perception is stuck on the *iconic* layer and never reaches the *conventional* layer. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Visual Perception Chain (Why and How) ### Step 1 — Optical Stage Light from the display passes through the **cornea → pupil → lens** and is focused on the **retina**. The lens performs *accommodation* — changing focal length so that near and far objects remain sharp. ### Step 2 — Photochemical Stage - **Rods** (≈ 120 million) — high sensitivity, *scotopic* (low-light, black-and-white) vision, distributed across peripheral retina. - **Cones** (≈ 6 million) — *photopic* (color, high-acuity) vision, concentrated in the **fovea** (1–2° central vision). > **Why** rods at the periphery? The eye evolved to detect *predators at the edge of vision*. **How** does this affect VDT design? Peripheral flicker from a low refresh rate is more disturbing than central flicker, because rods have higher temporal sensitivity. ### Step 3 — Neural Transduction Rods and cones hyperpolarize in response to light, releasing **glutamate** onto bipolar and ganglion cells. The **optic nerve** carries signals via the **lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)** to the **primary visual cortex (V1)** in the occipital lobe. ### Step 4 — Perceptual Organization (Gestalt Laws) The brain groups raw signals using: - **Proximity** — items close together are seen as a group. - **Similarity** — items of same shape/color are grouped. - **Closure** — the mind "fills in" missing parts. - **Continuity** — smooth lines are preferred. - **Figure–Ground** — separation of object from background. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implication for VDT:** A poorly designed icon (no closure, no figure-ground contrast) will be *mis-perceived* even if the sensation is perfect. KTU examiners love this point. ### Step 5 — Higher-Order Processing Top-down influences: *expectation, training, mental model, prior experience*. A trained pilot perceives a "PULL UP" warning in <0.4 s; a novice needs >1.5 s. --- ## 2.2 Critical Perceptual Variables (With Units) | Variable | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning | |----------|--------|---------------|---------------------| | Visual Acuity | $VA$ | 1.0 (6/6, 20/20) | Smallest resolvable detail = **1 minute of arc** (1′) | | Luminance | $L$ | 10–500 cd/m² | Photopic light intensity per unit area | | Contrast Ratio | $CR$ | 3:1 to 10:1 | $CR = L_{max}/L_{min}$ | | Weber Fraction | $k$ | 0.01–0.02 (vision) | Just-Noticeable Difference (JND) | | Critical Flicker Frequency | $CFF$ | 50–100 Hz | Refresh must exceed $CFF$ | | Viewing Distance | $D$ | 500–700 mm (VDT) | Eye-to-screen distance | | Visual Angle | $\theta$ | 16′–22′ (target) | Apparent size on retina | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Hall Mantra:** *Every display dimension is derived from the visual angle $\theta$ and the viewing distance $D$.* | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Visual angle (small) | $\theta \approx h/D$ | radians | $h$ = object height, $D$ = viewing distance | | 2 | Visual angle (general) | $\theta = 2 \arctan\bigl(h/(2D)\bigr)$ | radians | Use for objects whose height is comparable to $D$ | | 3 | Minimum character height | $h_{min} = D \cdot \tan(\theta_{min})$ | mm | $\theta_{min}$ = 16′–22′ (target) for body text | | 4 | Standard acuity | $1' = \pi/(180 \cdot 60) = 2.909 \times 10^{-4}$ | rad | The "6/6" or "20/20" reference | | 5 | Snellen fraction | $VA = D_{test}/D_{normal}$ | — | e.g., 6/12 means read at 6 m what normal reads at 12 m | | 6 | Contrast ratio | $CR = L_{max}/L_{min}$ | — | $CR \ge 3$ minimum, $\ge 10$ preferred for VDT | | 7 | Michelson contrast | $C_M = (L_{max} - L_{min})/(L_{max} + L_{min})$ | 0–1 | Used for periodic patterns (gratings) | | 8 | Weber's JND | $\Delta I/I = k$ | — | $k_{vision} \approx 0.01$ to $0.02$ | | 9 | Flicker refresh bound | $f_{refresh} \ge CFF$ | Hz | $CFF$ rises with luminance | | 10 | Stroke width | $W_s = h/8$ to $h/6$ | mm | Inside the character | | 11 | Character aspect | $W_c/H_c = 0.6$ to $1.0$ | — | Sans-serif: 0.6; Serif: 0.8–1.0 | | 12 | Reading rate (words/min) | $R = (C/H) \times 60 \times E$ | wpm | $C$ = characters, $H$ = fixations/line, $E$ = efficiency | | 13 | Angular subtense of pixel | $\theta_{px} = p_{mm}/D \times (180/\pi) \times 60$ | arc-min | $p_{mm}$ = pixel pitch | > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Inside the table I have used `\mid` and `\approx` style notation. When writing outside tables, never use a bare vertical pipe for absolute value — always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Avionics cockpit displays** (Boeing 787, Airbus A350): character size is fixed at $\approx 22$ minutes of arc at the design eye position to ensure readability during 4-G turns. - **Nuclear control rooms**: $CR \ge 12$ mandatory, flicker-free at 75 Hz, no symbol with less than 85% comprehension per ISO 9186 testing. - **Road signs (MORTH / Vienna Convention)**: minimum 50 mm letter height per 12 m viewing distance — same formula as above, just scaled up. - **Smartphone UX**: Apple's HIG and Google's Material Design both use *minimum 12 pt text at 16 dp base*, derived from a $\theta_{min} = 16′$ criterion at 400 mm reading distance. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Character Height from Visual Angle > **Problem Setup:** An air traffic controller sits 700 mm from a VDT. The required minimum visual angle for comfortable, error-free reading is **20 arc-minutes** (20′). What should the capital-letter height $h$ on the screen be? ### Step 1 — Convert the visual angle to radians. Because 1° = $\pi/180$ radians and 1′ = 1/60 of a degree: $$ \theta_{min} = 20' = 20 \times \frac{1}{60}^{\circ} = \frac{1}{3}^{\circ} $$ Converting degrees to radians: $$ \theta_{min} = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{540} \text{ rad} $$ Numerical value for checking: $$ \theta_{min} = 3.14159 / 540 = 5.8178 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the small-angle formula. For a character height that is small compared to the viewing distance, the arc on the retina is essentially linear: $$ \theta \approx \frac{h}{D} $$ ### Step 3 — Solve for the unknown character height. Re-arranging: $$ h = \theta_{min} \times D $$ Plug in the values ($D = 700$ mm): $$ h = 5.8178 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \times 700 \text{ mm} $$ Carrying out the multiplication: $$ h = 5.8178 \times 700 \times 10^{-3} = 4072.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} $$ Therefore: $$ h \approx 4.07 \text{ mm} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the engineering safety factor. Standards (ISO 9241-303, MIL-STD-1472) recommend using **22′ rather than 20′** for body text and 1.25× safety factor for worst-case luminance and vibration: $$ h_{design} = 1.25 \times \frac{22'}{20'} \times h_{min} $$ Numerically: $$ h_{design} = 1.25 \times 1.1 \times 4.07 = 5.60 \text{ mm} $$ > **Result:** Designers should use a capital letter height of **≈ 5.6 mm** on a 700-mm-distance VDT. Smaller sizes will fall below 20′ and increase operator error rates exponentially. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Refresh Rate Required to Eliminate Flicker > **Problem Setup:** A VDT operates at a screen luminance of $L = 200$ cd/m². Determine the minimum refresh frequency to avoid perceived flicker. ### Step 1 — Apply the Ferry–Porter Law. Empirically, the critical flicker frequency (CFF) is a linear function of the logarithm of luminance: $$ CFF = a + b \cdot \log_{10}(L) $$ For foveal (central) vision, the canonical constants are $a = 12.5$ Hz and $b = 28.6$ Hz (Ferry, 1892; Porter, 1902). ### Step 2 — Substitute $L = 200$ cd/m². Compute the logarithm: $$ \log_{10}(200) = \log_{10}(2 \times 10^2) = 2 + \log_{10}(2) = 2 + 0.30103 = 2.30103 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the CFF. $$ CFF = 12.5 + 28.6 \times 2.30103 $$ Carrying out the multiplication: $$ 28.6 \times 2.30103 = 65.81 \text{ Hz} $$ Adding the intercept: $$ CFF = 12.5 + 65.81 = 78.31 \text{ Hz} $$ ### Step 4 — Choose the safe design refresh rate. To include a 1.25× safety margin (for the 95th-percentile operator and the peripheral rod contribution): $$ f_{design} = 1.25 \times CFF = 1.25 \times 78.31 = 97.89 \text{ Hz} $$ Rounded to the nearest commercial standard: $$ f_{design} \approx 100 \text{ Hz} $$ > **Result:** A 200 cd/m² VDT must refresh at **≥ 100 Hz** to guarantee that no operator in the population perceives flicker. A 60 Hz panel would feel "shimmery" or cause headaches. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Contrast Ratio Verification for a Hard Copy > **Problem Setup:** A printed manual has black text ($L_b = 1.5$ cd/m²) on white paper ($L_w = 240$ cd/m²) under office lighting. Verify the contrast ratio meets the ISO 9241-303 minimum of 3:1 for text. ### Step 1 — State the contrast-ratio definition. $$ CR = \frac{L_{max}}{L_{min}} = \frac{L_w}{L_b} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the values. $$ CR = \frac{240}{1.5} $$ Performing the division: $$ 240 / 1.5 = 160 $$ Therefore: $$ CR = 160 : 1 $$ ### Step 3 — Compare against the standard. Required: $CR \ge 3:1$. Achieved: $CR = 160:1$. The manual **easily passes** with a margin of more than 50×. ### Step 4 — Compute Michelson contrast (for completeness). $$ C_M = \frac{L_w - L_b}{L_w + L_b} = \frac{240 - 1.5}{240 + 1.5} = \frac{238.5}{241.5} $$ Carrying out the division: $$ C_M = 0.9876 \approx 0.99 $$ > **Result:** A near-ideal contrast of **160:1** (or $C_M \approx 0.99$), which is why hard copy remains the *gold standard* for legal documents, aviation charts, and emergency manuals. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Hard Copy vs VDT Choice | Selection Criterion | Hard Copy Wins When… | VDT Wins When… | |---------------------|----------------------|----------------| | Information is static | ✓ Manuals, regulations | ✗ | | Information is dynamic | ✗ | ✓ Live radar, telemetry | | Daylight readability | ✓ Reflective, glare-free with matte | ✗ Washes out in sunlight | | Night / low-light use | ✗ Needs external light | ✓ Self-luminous | | Annotation / markup | ✓ Easy with pen | ✗ Requires stylus / software | | Mass distribution cost | ✗ Printing & shipping expensive | ✓ Zero marginal cost | | Audit trail | ✓ Paper originals are legal | ✓ Better with digital logs | | Update frequency | ✗ Reprinting required | ✓ Push from server | | Operator reading posture | ✓ Tilted book on desk | ✗ Fixed monitor geometry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trick Question:** *"Why is a paper checklist still used in modern airliners despite a glass cockpit?"* — Answer: Hard copy is *immune to software failure, electrical failure, and glare-induced misreads during sunrise/sunset*. Best of both worlds: **redundant paper + electronic checklist.** # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Visual Perception Information-Processing Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Display Stimulus
Light Sound Force] --> B[Receptor Stage
Eye Ear Skin] B --> C[Sensation
Raw neural signal] C --> D[Perception Stage
Pattern recognition] D --> E[Short Term Memory
Capacity 7 plus minus 2] E --> F[Long Term Memory
Mental models schemas] F --> G[Decision
Cognition] G --> H[Motor Output
Response action] H --> I[Feedback
Display update] I -.-> A classDef input fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#003366 classDef process fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#663300 classDef memory fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009933,color:#003300 classDef output fill:#FFE6F2,stroke:#CC0066,color:#660033 class A input class B,C,D,G,H,I process class E,F memory ``` **Reading the diagram:** The operator is not a passive observer. The Feedback loop from response back to display is the *closed-loop control* that defines every human–machine system. **Design implication:** If the *display* has poor legibility, the *response* will be slow or wrong, and the *feedback* will not correct the system. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — VDT Screen Design Parameter Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Operator Anthropometry
Eye height 95th percentile] --> B[Design Eye Point
DEP at 700 mm] B --> C[Required Visual Angle
16 to 22 arc min] C --> D[Derived Character Height
h = D times theta] D --> E[Font and Aspect Ratio
Sans serif 0.6 to 1.0] E --> F[Stroke Width
h divided by 6 to 8] F --> G[Inter-character Gap
0.25 h] G --> H[Line Spacing
0.5 h to 1.0 h] H --> I[Column Width
40 to 60 characters] I --> J[Contrast and Luminance
CR greater than 3] J --> K[Refresh Rate
CFF plus margin] K --> L[Final VDT Layout] classDef startnode fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#003366 classDef midnode fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#663300 classDef endnode fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009933,color:#003300 class A,B startnode class C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K midnode class L endnode ``` **Reading the diagram:** Every downstream parameter is *constrained* by the upstream one. A 14-mark question can be answered *mechanically* by walking this chain from top to bottom, substituting numerical values at each step. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Symbol Design and Comprehension Test Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Concept to Communicate
e.g. Flammable] --> B[Symbol Generation
Iconic image draft] B --> C[Internal Evaluation
Designer review] C --> D[Comprehension Test
ISO 9186 method] D --> E{Comprehension
greater than 85 percent} E -- Yes --> F[Memory Test
1 week recall] E -- No --> B F --> G{Recall
greater than 75 percent} G -- Yes --> H[ISO 7001 / 7010
Standardization] G -- No --> B H --> I[Production and Deployment] classDef inputnode fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#003366 classDef testnode fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#663300 classDef successnode fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009933,color:#003300 class A,B,C inputnode class D,F testnode class E,G testnode class H,I successnode ``` **Reading the diagram:** Symbol development is **iterative** (the loop from $E$ → *No* → $B$). The two acceptance gates are: **(1) ≥ 85 % comprehension** when first seen, and **(2) ≥ 75 % memory recall** one week later (ISO 9186-1, 2007). This is exactly what KTU expects you to mention in a 14-mark answer. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Hard Copy vs VDT Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New Display Requirement] --> B{Information
Static or Dynamic} B -- Static --> C{Update
Frequency} B -- Dynamic --> D[Choose VDT] C -- Rare --> E{Glare
Environment} C -- Frequent --> D E -- High daylight --> F[Choose Hard Copy] E -- Controlled light --> G{Annotation
Required} G -- Yes --> F G -- No --> H[Either acceptable] F --> I[Specify Paper Lamination
and Contrast] D --> J[Apply VDT Standards
ISO 9241-303] H --> J I --> K[Final Display Solution] J --> K classDef startnode fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#003366 classDef decisionnode fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#663300 classDef endnode fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009933,color:#003300 class A startnode class B,C,E,G decisionnode class D,F,H,I,J endnode class K endnode ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2024]** ### Q1. Distinguish between **sensation** and **perception** as applied to display design. *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Sensation | Perception | |--------|-----------|------------| | Definition | Raw detection of physical energy by receptors | Interpretation and organization of that raw signal | | Locus | Peripheral (eye, ear, skin) | Central (cortex) | | Designer controls? | **Yes** (luminance, contrast, size) | **Indirectly** (training, expectation) | | Example | Photons falling on retina | Recognizing the digit "7" on a digital display | (3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for engineering implication) --- ### Q2. State **Weber's Law** and give its typical constant for visual contrast. *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** Weber's Law states that the *Just-Noticeable Difference* ($\Delta I$) is a constant fraction of the *stimulus intensity* ($I$): $$ \frac{\Delta I}{I} = k $$ For human vision, the Weber fraction for **luminance discrimination** is $k \approx 0.01$ to $0.02$. This means a 1–2 % change in screen brightness is the minimum that an operator can detect. (3 marks: 1 for statement of law, 1 for formula, 1 for numerical value with meaning) --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – University Question Bank, PESFT748, 2024 Scheme]** ### Question A — 14 Marks #### (a) *(7 marks)* Explain in detail the **design parameters for VDT screens** as per ISO 9241-303. Use a numerical example to compute the minimum character height for a viewing distance of 600 mm. #### (b) *(7 marks)* Describe the **FACTORS** that affect visual perception in display design. How does **Gestalt grouping** influence the legibility of grouped indicators on a panel? --- ### Model Answer — Question A (a) **Design parameters for VDT screens (ISO 9241-303):** 1. **Visual angle**: minimum 20′ (preferred 22′) for capital-letter height of body text. 2. **Character aspect ratio**: 0.6 to 1.0 (width/height). 3. **Stroke width**: 1/8 to 1/6 of character height. 4. **Inter-character spacing**: 0.25 × character height. 5. **Inter-word spacing**: 1.0 × character height. 6. **Line spacing**: 0.5 × to 1.0 × character height. 7. **Contrast ratio**: ≥ 3:1 (text), ≥ 7:1 (preferred). 8. **Refresh rate**: ≥ CFF for the design luminance (Ferry–Porter). 9. **Luminance**: 35–100 cd/m² (positive polarity preferred). 10. **Viewing distance**: 500–700 mm. 11. **Anti-glare treatment**: matte screen or anti-reflection coating. **Numerical calculation (D = 600 mm):** $$ \theta_{min} = 20' = \frac{20}{60}^{\circ} = \frac{1}{3}^{\circ} = \frac{\pi}{540} \text{ rad} = 5.818 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} $$ $$ h_{min} = D \times \theta_{min} = 600 \times 5.818 \times 10^{-3} = 3.49 \text{ mm} $$ Applying the 1.25× safety factor: $$ h_{design} = 1.25 \times 3.49 = 4.36 \text{ mm} $$ > **Answer:** Capital letter height should be **at least 4.4 mm** on a 600-mm VDT to meet ISO 9241-303. --- ### Model Answer — Question A (b) **Factors affecting visual perception:** - **Illumination** — too low = rods dominate, no color; too high = glare. - **Contrast** — both luminance and color contrast. - **Size** — angular subtense ≥ 20′. - **Time on retina** — exposure time ≥ 100 ms for recognition. - **Color** — different wavelengths have different acuity (green is best at photopic levels). - **Adaptation** — dark-adapted vs light-adapted retina. - **Age** — pupil diameter, lens yellowing, macular degeneration. - **Stress / fatigue** — slows perceptual processing. - **Motion / vibration** — reduces effective acuity by smearing the retinal image. - **Expectation / training** — top-down facilitation. **Gestalt grouping in panel design:** The four most relevant Gestalt laws for HFE are: 1. **Proximity** — Group related gauges (e.g., all engine dials together) by placing them close. 2. **Similarity** — Use the *same* color, shape, and font for the *same class* of information. 3. **Common region** — Enclose related controls in a common border. 4. **Figure–Ground** — Strong contrast between indicator and panel; a green light on a black panel is seen faster than green on gray. > **Engineering takeaway:** A well-grouped cockpit warning panel using Gestalt proximity + similarity can reduce *time-to-perceive* a fault by 30–50 % compared to an ungrouped layout (Wickens et al., *Engineering Psychology & Human Performance*, 2013). --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) #### (a) *(7 marks)* With neat sketches, explain the **design principles for graphic symbols** as adopted in ISO 7001 and ISO 7010. How are symbols **tested for comprehension**? #### (b) *(7 marks)* Compare **hard-copy displays and VDT screens** with respect to (i) light source, (ii) refresh rate, (iii) viewing distance, (iv) contrast ratio, (v) glare, (vi) cost, and (vii) information-update flexibility. --- ### Model Answer — Question B (a) **Design Principles for Graphic Symbols (ISO 7001 / ISO 7010):** The five classical principles (Moody, *Iconic Communication*, 1986; refined by Zwaga & Boersema, 1983): | # | Principle | Meaning | Engineering Action | |---|-----------|---------|--------------------| | 1 | **Compatibility** | Symbol matches the *referent* in some way | Flame icon for "fire", not "abstract dot" | | 2 | **Familiarity** | Use known visual conventions | Key-icon for "lock" | | 3 | **Simplicity** | Minimum number of strokes | Avoid > 6 line-segments per icon | | 4 | **Discriminability** | Symbol must differ from neighbouring ones | No two icons within 8° of visual angle should be confusable | | 5 | **Meaningfulness** | Symbol must convey its meaning | Test for comprehension ≥ 85% | **Comprehension Testing (ISO 9186-1: 2007):** 1. **Sample**: 50+ subjects, balanced for age, gender, education, cultural background. 2. **Procedure**: - Show each symbol for **5 seconds** (no title). - Ask: *"What does this mean?"* - Score: Correct / Partially correct / Wrong. 3. **Acceptance threshold**: - **≥ 85 % correct** for safety symbols (ISO 7010). - **≥ 75 % correct** for public-information symbols (ISO 7001). 4. **Memory sub-test** (ISO 9186-3): same subjects re-tested **1 week later** without warning. Recall threshold: **≥ 75 %**. 5. **Cultural validation**: test in **at least three** language regions. > **Result:** A symbol that *passes comprehension* but *fails memory* is rejected or redesigned — a frequent KTU sub-question. --- ### Model Answer — Question B (b) **Comparison Table — Hard Copy vs VDT** | # | Parameter | Hard Copy | VDT Screen | |---|-----------|-----------|------------| | i | Light source | **Reflective** (ambient) | **Emissive** (self-luminous) | | ii | Refresh rate | None (static) | 60–144 Hz; must exceed CFF | | iii | Viewing distance | 300–400 mm | 500–700 mm | | iv | Contrast ratio | Up to 160:1 | 3:1 to 50:1 (typical) | | v | Glare | Matte/laminate controlled | Direct + reflected; needs AR coating | | vi | Cost (per page / per display-year) | Printing + shipping | Hardware + power + IT support | | vii | Update flexibility | Reprint required | Real-time, networked | (7 marks: 1 mark per row + concluding remark = 1 mark extra) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. *Confusing sensation with perception* — KTU examiners explicitly mark "perception is NOT sensing" in 3-mark answers. State both definitions. > 2. *Forgetting the safety factor* — Character-height questions in 14-mark sets will penalize answers that give the *minimum* value without the 1.25× engineering margin. > 3. *Quoting formulas without units* — "$h$ = 4.07 mm" is a full mark; "h = 4.07" is *zero* mark. > 4. *Writing "CRT" when the question says "VDT"* — VDT includes LCD/LED/OLED; the design rules are the same because the *operator's eye* doesn't know the technology. > 5. *Forgetting Gestalt laws* in the "factors affecting perception" question — at least two laws are mandatory for 7 marks. > 6. *Skipping ISO 9186 testing* in the symbols question — the *comprehension* and *memory* thresholds are *the* marker KTU examiners look for. > 7. *Mixing up luminance contrast (CR)* and *Michelson contrast ($C_M$)* — define both, then use the right one. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2, Topic: Perception, Hard Copy, VDT, Symbols)** - [ ] **Perception ≠ Sensation.** Sensation = raw receptor signal. Perception = interpretation. KTU will test this. - [ ] The **Information-Processing Model** is: *Stimulus → Sensation → Perception → STM → LTM → Decision → Motor → Feedback.* - [ ] **Standard visual acuity** is **1 arc-minute (1′) = $\pi/10800$ rad ≈ 2.909 × 10⁻⁴ rad**. - [ ] **Minimum visual angle for text** = 16′ (minimum) to 22′ (preferred) for capital letter height. - [ ] **Character height formula:** $h = D \cdot \tan(\theta)$. With safety factor, $h_{design} = 1.25 \cdot D \cdot \tan(22′)$. - [ ] **VDT viewing distance** = 500–700 mm (ergonomic norm). - [ ] **Hard copy viewing distance** = 300–400 mm (reading arm). - [ ] **Contrast ratio** $CR = L_{max}/L_{min} \ge 3$ for text; $\ge 10$ for primary flight displays. - [ ] **Michelson contrast** $C_M = (L_{max} - L_{min})/(L_{max} + L_{min})$ — used for gratings. - [ ] **Weber's Law** $\Delta I / I = k$, with $k_{vision} \approx 0.01$ to $0.02$. - [ ] **Refresh rate** must exceed **Ferry–Porter CFF**: $CFF = 12.5 + 28.6 \cdot \log_{10}(L)$ Hz. - [ ] **Stroke width** = $h/6$ to $h/8$. - [ ] **Character aspect ratio** = 0.6 to 1.0. - [ ] **Inter-character gap** = 0.25 $h$; **line spacing** = 0.5–1.0 $h$. - [ ] **Hard copy** is *reflective, static, no flicker, high contrast, daylight-readable, cheaper for mass print*. - [ ] **VDT** is *emissive, dynamic, needs CFF control, AR coating, adjustable luminance, real-time update*. - [ ] **Gestalt laws** in HFE: Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Figure–Ground, Common Region. - [ ] **Symbol design principles** (ISO 7001 / 7010): Compatibility, Familiarity, Simplicity, Discriminability, Meaningfulness. - [ ] **Comprehension test threshold** (ISO 9186): ≥ 85 % correct for safety, ≥ 75 % for public-info. - [ ] **Memory test threshold**: ≥ 75 % correct after **1 week**. - [ ] **Eye-dominant cone distribution** peaks at the **fovea**; rods dominate the **periphery** → peripheral flicker is more disturbing. - [ ] **Modern standards to quote**: ISO 9241-303 (VDT), ISO 7001 (public info), ISO 7010 (safety), ISO 9186 (test methods), ANSI Z535.3 (USA). - [ ] **Units check**: angles in rad inside the math, in arc-minutes inside design specifications, viewing distance in mm. > **Final Two-Line Takeaway:** > *Sensation is engineered; perception is learned — and great display design makes the operator feel as if the perception is automatic.* > *Numbers to memorize: 1′ acuity, 22′ preferred text, 600 mm VDT distance, 100 Hz refresh, 3:1 minimum contrast, 85 % symbol comprehension.*

topics.title: Objectives and Criteria for Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Objectives and Criteria for Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)**, **Selection** refers to the systematic process of choosing the most suitable individual from a pool of candidates to perform a specific job, operate a system, or interact with a man-machine interface, based on predefined physiological, psychological, cognitive, and behavioral criteria.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework for **PESFT748**, selection is positioned as a critical human-centered decision process that bridges the domains of **ergonomics, industrial psychology, and systems engineering**. The process is governed by two key pillars:

1. **Objectives of Selection** — *What the organization/system aims to achieve through the selection process.*
2. **Criteria of Selection** — *The measurable attributes used to evaluate and differentiate candidates.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> *"Selection is the process of choosing from among available candidates the most suitable person for a particular job, considering the match between human capabilities (physical, mental, and emotional) and job demands, with the goal of optimizing system performance, safety, and human well-being."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Right Key for the Right Lock"**

Imagine you have a **lock** (the job/role) and a **keyring full of keys** (the candidates). Each key has a unique **shape, length, and tooth pattern** (skills, abilities, personality traits). Your job is to find the key that **fits perfectly, turns smoothly, and opens the door without breaking**.

- If you choose a key that is **too long or too short** → it won't even enter the lock (mismatch in physical/cognitive dimensions).
- If you choose a key with the **wrong tooth pattern** → it might enter but won't turn (partial fit, leads to poor performance).
- If you choose the **right key** → the door opens effortlessly, the key lasts long, and there is no wear-and-tear (optimal human-machine fit).

**Selection criteria** are the "tooth patterns" you check on each key, and **selection objectives** are the reasons you are opening that door in the first place (security, speed, safety, etc.).

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| **Term** | **Definition** | **Standard Metric/Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Consistency of selection results across repeated measurements | **Cronbach's α (range: 0 to 1)** |
| **Validity** | Degree to which a selection tool measures what it claims to measure | **Validity Coefficient (r), range: -1 to +1** |
| **Predictive Accuracy** | How well selection scores predict future job performance | **Correlation Coefficient (r)** |
| **Selection Ratio** | Ratio of candidates selected to candidates applied | **Ratio (0 to 1)** |
| **Base Rate** | Proportion of applicants who would succeed on the job anyway | **Percentage (%)** |
| **Utility** | Practical value of a selection procedure in financial/performance terms | **Utility (in monetary units, $)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constants to Remember:**
> - **Mean Human Reaction Time:** **~250 milliseconds** for simple stimuli.
> - **Optimal Work-Rest Ratio:** Typically **5:1 to 3:1** depending on task intensity.
> - **Acceptable Selection Reliability Threshold:** **Cronbach's α ≥ 0.70**.

---

## 1.4 Why "Selection" is a Core Human Factors Topic

In KTU's 2024 scheme, Human Factors Engineering is treated as the **science of optimizing the interface between humans and systems**. Selection is critical because:

- **Even the best-designed system fails if operated by an unsuitable person.**
- Selection determines whether human capabilities align with **system demands**.
- Poor selection → increased **human error**, **accidents**, **fatigue**, and **productivity loss**.
- Good selection → enhanced **safety**, **efficiency**, and **job satisfaction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Selection (The "WHY" of Selection)

The objectives of selection in Human Factors Engineering are multi-dimensional. They extend beyond merely "hiring a person" to include **system-level optimization**.

### 2.1.1 Primary Objectives

1. **Matching Human Capabilities to Job Demands**
   - **Why:** To ensure that the physical, mental, and emotional capacities of the individual are aligned with the requirements of the task.
   - **How:** Through aptitude tests, physical fitness tests, psychometric assessments.

2. **Minimizing Human Error and Accidents**
   - **Why:** Human error accounts for **~80% of industrial accidents** (Heinrich's Domino Theory framework).
   - **How:** Selection filters out candidates prone to risk-taking behavior, sensory impairments, or cognitive overload susceptibility.

3. **Maximizing System Performance and Productivity**
   - **Why:** Better fit → higher output quality, lower rework, fewer downtimes.
   - **How:** Selection based on validated performance predictors.

4. **Ensuring Occupational Health and Safety**
   - **Why:** Misfit leads to musculoskeletal disorders, stress, burnout.
   - **How:** Pre-employment medicals, ergonomic capability assessments.

5. **Enhancing Job Satisfaction and Reducing Turnover**
   - **Why:** Person-job fit correlates strongly with retention.
   - **How:** Selection considering vocational interests, personality compatibility.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Objectives

- **Legal Compliance:** Adhering to equal opportunity employment laws.
- **Cost Reduction:** Lower training costs, fewer accidents, less absenteeism.
- **Organizational Culture Fit:** Maintaining team dynamics and values alignment.
- **Future-Proofing:** Selecting candidates with learning agility and adaptability.

---

## 2.2 Criteria of Selection (The "WHAT" of Selection)

Selection criteria are the **measurable attributes** upon which candidates are evaluated. In Human Factors Engineering, these are broadly classified into **five domains**:

### 2.2.1 Physical Criteria
- **Anthropometric dimensions** (height, weight, reach, grip strength).
- **Sensory capacities** (vision, hearing, tactile sensitivity).
- **Physiological endurance** (cardiovascular fitness, stamina).
- **Motor skills** (manual dexterity, reaction time, coordination).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** *Physical criteria are paramount in jobs involving manual handling, machinery operation, and hazardous environment work.*

### 2.2.2 Psychological Criteria
- **Cognitive abilities** (memory, attention, problem-solving, IQ).
- **Personality traits** (Big Five: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism).
- **Emotional intelligence** and stress tolerance.
- **Attitudes and motivation.**

### 2.2.3 Mental / Cognitive Criteria
- **Information processing speed.**
- **Decision-making under uncertainty.**
- **Spatial reasoning and visualization.**
- **Numerical and verbal reasoning.**

### 2.2.4 Behavioral Criteria
- **Leadership and teamwork orientation.**
- **Risk tolerance and safety consciousness.**
- **Adaptability and learning agility.**
- **Communication skills.**

### 2.2.5 Biographical / Experiential Criteria
- **Educational qualifications.**
- **Work experience and track record.**
- **References and past performance reviews.**
- **Training and certifications.**

---

## 2.3 The Selection Process (Operational Flow)

The selection process in HFE follows a **funnel model**, where the candidate pool narrows down at each stage:

1. **Job Analysis** → Identify human requirements.
2. **Criteria Definition** → Specify measurable attributes.
3. **Sourcing & Screening** → Initial filtering (resume, application).
4. **Testing** → Psychological, physical, aptitude tests.
5. **Interview** → Behavioral and competency-based assessment.
6. **Medical Examination** → Health and fitness verification.
7. **Final Selection & Placement** → Best-fit candidate chosen.
8. **Validation & Follow-up** → Post-selection performance review.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Symbol** | **Application in Selection** | **Units / Range** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Selection Ratio | $SR = \frac{\text{Number Selected}}{\text{Number of Applicants}}$ | Lower SR → more competitive selection | $0 \le SR \le 1$ |
| 2 | Validity Coefficient | $r_{xy} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y}$ | Predictor-job performance correlation | $-1 \le r \le +1$ |
| 3 | Taylor-Russell Table Utility | Uses $SR$, $BR$, $r$ to compute success ratio | Determines selection effectiveness | Probability (0 to 1) |
| 4 | Cronbach's Alpha | $\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)$ | Internal consistency of selection tests | $\alpha \ge 0.70$ acceptable |
| 5 | Naylor-Shine Formula | $\Delta U = N \cdot T \cdot r_{xy} \cdot SD_y - N \cdot C$ | Utility of a selection procedure | Monetary units (\$) |
| 6 | BODMAS Order of Operations | Brackets, Orders, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction | Used in all numerical selection calculations | N/A |
| 7 | Standard Error of Measurement | $SE_M = SD \cdot \sqrt{1 - r_{xx}}$ | Reliability indicator for selection tests | Same units as SD |
| 8 | Brokaw's Formula (Selection Efficiency) | $E = \frac{\text{Number of Successful Selections}}{\text{Total Selections Made}} \times 100$ | Overall effectiveness percentage | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | Stanine Score Conversion | $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Normalizing test scores | Z-score (dimensionless) |
| 10 | Signal Detection Theory (d') | $d' = z_{hit} - z_{false\_alarm}$ | Differentiating candidates from non-candidates | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** In the formulas above, all absolute values and conditionals are denoted using LaTeX-safe syntax (e.g., $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value) to preserve markdown table integrity. **The vertical pipe symbol `\|` is intentionally avoided inside table cells.**

---

| **Aviation** | Pilot selection via psychomotor tests, simulator assessments | Reduced pilot-error accidents |
| **Nuclear Power** | Strict cognitive and psychological screening of operators | Minimized high-consequence errors |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation 1: Selection Ratio and Its Interpretation

The **Selection Ratio (SR)** is the most fundamental metric in selection theory. It quantifies the *selectivity* of the process.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the variables.**

Let:
- $N_s$ = Number of candidates selected.
- $N_a$ = Total number of applicants.
- $SR$ = Selection Ratio.

**Step 2: Write the basic ratio equation.**

$$SR = \frac{N_s}{N_a}$$

**Step 3: Express as a percentage (optional).**

$$SR_{\%} = \frac{N_s}{N_a} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Interpret the boundary conditions.**

- If $SR = 1$: Every applicant is selected → *No selectivity*.
- If $SR = 0$: No one is selected → *Impossible/closed process*.
- If $0 < SR < 0.20$: *Highly selective* (e.g., astronaut selection, elite military).
- If $0.20 \le SR \le 0.50$: *Moderately selective* (e.g., professional roles).
- If $SR > 0.50$: *Loosely selective* (e.g., mass recruitment for unskilled labor).

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example.**

*Problem:* A company received **500 applications** for **50 engineering positions**. Calculate the selection ratio and interpret it.

$$SR = \frac{N_s}{N_a} = \frac{50}{500} = 0.10$$

$$SR_{\%} = 0.10 \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Interpretation:** A 10% selection ratio indicates a **highly competitive, selective process**. The organization has the luxury of choosing the top 10% of applicants, which typically correlates with higher validity of selection decisions.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Derivation 2: Taylor-Russell Table Logic

The **Taylor-Russell Table** (1945) is a cornerstone of personnel selection theory. It computes the **success ratio** (proportion of selected candidates who will succeed on the job) given three inputs: **Selection Ratio, Base Rate, and Validity Coefficient**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the parameters.**

Let:
- $BR$ = Base rate (proportion of applicants who would succeed without any selection).
- $r$ = Validity coefficient of the selection test.
- $SR$ = Selection ratio.
- $SR_{success}$ = Success ratio (proportion of selected who will succeed).

**Step 2: The basic Taylor-Russell relationship.**

The Taylor-Russell table is empirically derived, but its underlying logic follows from the **binormal distribution assumption** of selection scores and job success.

**Step 3: Tabular Interpretation (a simplified partial Taylor-Russell Table).**

| $SR$ | $BR$ | $r = 0.00$ | $r = 0.30$ | $r = 0.60$ | $r = 0.90$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0.10** | **0.30** | 0.30 | 0.43 | 0.58 | 0.78 |
| **0.10** | **0.50** | 0.50 | 0.62 | 0.74 | 0.88 |
| **0.10** | **0.70** | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.88 | 0.96 |
| **0.50** | **0.30** | 0.30 | 0.36 | 0.44 | 0.55 |
| **0.50** | **0.50** | 0.50 | 0.55 | 0.62 | 0.72 |
| **0.50** | **0.70** | 0.70 | 0.76 | 0.82 | 0.89 |
| **0.90** | **0.30** | 0.30 | 0.32 | 0.34 | 0.36 |
| **0.90** | **0.50** | 0.50 | 0.52 | 0.54 | 0.56 |
| **0.90** | **0.70** | 0.70 | 0.72 | 0.74 | 0.76 |

**Step 4: Read the table.**

For $SR = 0.10$, $BR = 0.50$, and $r = 0.60$:

$$SR_{success} = 0.74$$

**Interpretation:** Out of every 100 candidates selected, **74 will be successful** on the job, given the specified selection ratio, base rate, and validity.

**Step 5: Key insight.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Taylor-Russell table reveals a critical HFE principle: *The validity of a selection tool matters MORE when the selection ratio is LOW (i.e., highly competitive).*** When almost everyone is selected ($SR \to 1$), even a high-validity tool barely improves success ratio over the base rate.

---

## 3.3 Derivative Derivation 3: Naylor-Shine Utility Formula

The **Naylor-Shine formula** quantifies the **economic utility** of a selection procedure — a key concept in industrial HFE.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define variables.**

Let:
- $N$ = Number of candidates selected per year.
- $T$ = Tenure of average employee in years.
- $r_{xy}$ = Validity coefficient of the selection procedure.
- $SD_y$ = Standard deviation of job performance in monetary terms.
- $C$ = Cost per candidate of the selection procedure.
- $\Delta U$ = Net utility gain from using the selection procedure.

**Step 2: The utility equation.**

$$\Delta U = N \cdot T \cdot r_{xy} \cdot SD_y - N \cdot C$$

**Step 3: Step-by-step interpretation.**

- $N \cdot T$ = Total employee-years the organization benefits from.
- $r_{xy} \cdot SD_y$ = Expected performance gain per employee-year (in monetary terms).
- $N \cdot C$ = Total cost of administering the selection procedure.

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**

*Problem:* An IT firm selects **200 software engineers per year**. Average tenure is **5 years**. Validity of their coding test is **$r_{xy} = 0.40$**. Standard deviation of job performance in revenue terms is **\$10,000/year**. Cost per candidate for the test is **\$200**. Calculate net utility.

$$\Delta U = N \cdot T \cdot r_{xy} \cdot SD_y - N \cdot C$$

**Substituting values:**

$$\Delta U = 200 \times 5 \times 0.40 \times 10000 - 200 \times 200$$

$$\Delta U = 4{,}000{,}000 - 40{,}000$$

$$\Delta U = 3{,}960{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** The firm gains a net utility of **\$3.96 million per year** by using this selection procedure. This is a powerful argument for investing in validated selection tools.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Selection Methods

| **Method** | **Validity Range** | **Cost** | **Best Used For** | **HFE Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unstructured Interview** | $r = 0.20$ | Low | Initial screening | Highly biased |
| **Structured Interview** | $r = 0.35 - 0.57$ | Moderate | Behavioral assessment | Time-consuming |
| **Cognitive Ability Test** | $r = 0.50 - 0.55$ | Moderate | Complex job roles | Cultural bias risk |
| **Work Sample Test** | $r = 0.54$ | High | Skilled trades | Limited transferability |
| **Personality Test (Big Five)** | $r = 0.15 - 0.30$ | Low-Moderate | Service roles | Faking risk |
| **Physical Fitness Test** | $r = 0.30 - 0.45$ | Moderate | Manual labor, military | Doesn't predict cognitive fit |
| **Assessment Center** | $r = 0.36 - 0.45$ | Very High | Managerial selection | Resource intensive |
| **Biographical Data (Biodata)** | $r = 0.35 - 0.40$ | Low | Mass screening | Privacy concerns |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Question banks frequently test the **relative validity** of these methods. **Work sample tests and structured interviews consistently rank highest in validity.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Selection Ratio" and explain its significance in Human Factors Engineering.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Selection Ratio (SR)** is defined as the ratio of the number of candidates selected to the total number of applicants who appeared for the selection process.

$$SR = \frac{\text{Number Selected}}{\text{Number of Applicants}}$$

**Significance in HFE:**
1. **Indicates Selectivity:** Lower SR values (e.g., 0.05) indicate highly competitive selection (e.g., astronaut or pilot selection), while higher values (e.g., 0.80) indicate loose selection.
2. **Affects Validity Utility:** Per the Taylor-Russell table, lower SR combined with high validity yields the highest success ratio.
3. **Resource Planning:** Helps organizations estimate testing infrastructure, time, and cost.
4. **Benchmark for Fairness:** Used to monitor adverse impact in equal employment opportunity contexts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Significance (1).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three criteria used for personnel selection in Human Factors Engineering.**

**Model Answer:**

Three major criteria for selection in HFE are:

1. **Physical Criteria:** Includes anthropometric dimensions (height, reach, grip), sensory capacities (vision, hearing), and motor skills (dexterity, reaction time). Critical for jobs involving manual labor, machinery operation, or hazardous environments.

2. **Cognitive Criteria:** Encompasses memory, attention span, problem-solving ability, and information processing speed. Essential for roles requiring complex decision-making, such as air traffic controllers or process plant operators.

3. **Psychological Criteria:** Includes personality traits (Big Five model: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism), emotional intelligence, and stress tolerance. Vital for jobs with high interpersonal demands or high-stakes pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark per criterion (name + brief explanation).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Selection in Human Factors Engineering is a multi-objective decision process." Discuss the major objectives and criteria of selection, with emphasis on their inter-relationship and impact on system performance. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Objectives of Selection (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The objectives of selection in Human Factors Engineering are designed to optimize the human-system interface. The major objectives are:

1. **Matching Human Capabilities to Job Demands (2 marks):**
   The primary objective is to ensure that the physical, mental, and emotional capacities of the selected individual align with the task demands. This is achieved through job analysis (identifying demands) and candidate assessment (measuring capabilities). Mismatches lead to poor performance, errors, and accidents.

2. **Minimizing Human Error and Enhancing Safety (2 marks):**
   Research indicates that **~80% of industrial accidents** are attributable to human error. Effective selection filters out candidates with characteristics predisposed to error — poor risk perception, low vigilance, or sensory deficits. This is especially critical in high-reliability organizations (HROs) such as nuclear power, aviation, and healthcare.

3. **Maximizing System Performance and Productivity (1.5 marks):**
   Better person-job fit → higher quality output, lower rework, fewer downtimes. Selection acts as a **performance multiplier** for the entire system.

4. **Ensuring Occupational Health and Reducing Turnover (1 mark):**
   Selecting ergonomically compatible candidates reduces musculoskeletal disorders, stress, and burnout. This in turn reduces absenteeism and voluntary turnover, yielding long-term organizational benefits.

5. **Legal and Ethical Compliance (0.5 marks):**
   Selection must comply with equal opportunity, non-discrimination, and labor laws to ensure fairness and social responsibility.

#### Part (b): Criteria of Selection and Their Inter-Relationship (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The criteria of selection are the **measurable attributes** that operationalize the selection objectives. They fall into five major domains:

| **Domain** | **Key Attributes** | **Primary Objective Served** |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Anthropometry, sensory, motor | Safety, performance |
| **Cognitive** | Memory, attention, reasoning | Performance, error reduction |
| **Psychological** | Personality, EQ, motivation | Satisfaction, retention |
| **Behavioral** | Leadership, teamwork, risk tolerance | Culture fit, adaptability |
| **Biographical** | Education, experience, certifications | Baseline competence |

**Inter-Relationship and Impact (4 marks):**

1. **Physical-Cognitive Link:** Many jobs (e.g., fighter pilots) require both physical fitness and rapid cognitive processing. Selection must use **integrated assessment batteries** rather than isolated tests.

2. **Psychological-Behavioral Link:** Personality traits (e.g., conscientiousness) manifest in observable behaviors (e.g., punctuality, rule compliance). Both must be measured to triangulate validity.

3. **Criterion Hierarchies:** For high-risk jobs (e.g., air traffic control), **physical and cognitive criteria dominate** over behavioral ones. For service jobs (e.g., customer support), **psychological and behavioral criteria dominate**.

4. **Trade-offs:** Organizations often face **cost-validity trade-offs**. Work sample tests have high validity but are expensive. Biodata is cheap but less valid. The **Naylor-Shine formula** helps optimize this trade-off.

5. **Impact on System Performance:** A multi-criteria selection system with high overall validity ($r \ge 0.50$) can improve organizational productivity by **15-30%** compared to unstructured selection (based on Schmidt & Hunter's meta-analyses).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often *list* criteria without explaining their **inter-relationship** → loses 2-3 marks.
> - Students fail to **link criteria back to objectives** → examiner cannot assess CO attainment.
> - Students skip the **system performance impact** discussion → loses 1-2 marks.
> - **Pro Tip:** Always end your answer with a concluding sentence linking the discussion to **human factors system optimization**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the Taylor-Russell model and Naylor-Shine utility model for evaluating the effectiveness of a selection procedure. Illustrate with numerical examples. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Taylor-Russell Model (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Taylor-Russell Table** (Taylor & Russell, 1945) is a classical decision-making tool in personnel selection that estimates the **success ratio** — the proportion of selected candidates who will perform successfully on the job.

**Three Input Parameters (2 marks):**

1. **Selection Ratio (SR):** Fraction of applicants actually selected.
   $$SR = \frac{N_s}{N_a}$$

2. **Base Rate (BR):** The success rate on the job if no selection were done.
   $$BR = \frac{\text{Number who would succeed}}{\text{Total applicants (if all were hired)}}$$

3. **Validity Coefficient (r):** Correlation between selection test scores and job performance.

**How the Table Works (2 marks):**

The table provides the **success ratio** for combinations of $SR$, $BR$, and $r$. It assumes that both the predictor (test) and the criterion (job success) follow normal distributions, and that the relationship is linear.

**Numerical Illustration (3 marks):**

*Problem:* A company has $SR = 0.20$, $BR = 0.60$, and uses a test with $r = 0.50$. From the Taylor-Russell table, the success ratio is approximately **0.72**.

This means: If 100 candidates are selected (from 500 applicants), **72 out of 100** will be successful on the job, compared to **60** who would have succeeded without any selection.

**Key Insights:**
- Higher validity → greater improvement over base rate.
- Lower selection ratio → greater benefit from valid selection.
- When $SR = 1$, even high validity yields no improvement.

#### Part (b): Naylor-Shine Utility Model (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Naylor-Shine Formula** quantifies the **economic utility** of a selection procedure in monetary terms, helping organizations justify investment in selection tools.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$\Delta U = N \cdot T \cdot r_{xy} \cdot SD_y - N \cdot C$$

Where:
- $N$ = Number selected per year
- $T$ = Average tenure (years)
- $r_{xy}$ = Validity coefficient
- $SD_y$ = Standard deviation of job performance in monetary units
- $C$ = Cost per candidate of selection
- $\Delta U$ = Net utility (gain)

**Numerical Illustration (3 marks):**

*Problem:* A manufacturing firm selects $N = 100$ workers/year. $T = 4$ years, $r_{xy} = 0.35$, $SD_y = \$8000$/year, $C = \$150$/candidate. Calculate $\Delta U$.

$$\Delta U = 100 \times 4 \times 0.35 \times 8000 - 100 \times 150$$

$$\Delta U = 1{,}120{,}000 - 15{,}000$$

$$\Delta U = 1{,}105{,}000$$

The firm gains **\$1.105 million/year** by using this validated selection procedure.

**Comparison with Taylor-Russell (2 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Taylor-Russell** | **Naylor-Shine** |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Success ratio (probability) | Net utility (monetary) |
| **Focus** | Predictive accuracy | Economic justification |
| **Use Case** | HR decision-making | Management investment cases |
| **Limitation** | Static, table-based | Requires performance SD in \$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Forgetting to **subtract the cost term** ($N \cdot C$) in Naylor-Shine → loses 1 mark.
> - Not **interpreting** the result in plain English → loses 1 mark.
> - Confusing the three input parameters of Taylor-Russell → loses 2 marks.
> - **Pro Tip:** Always show **unit consistency** in your numerical solutions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

### **Core Concepts to Remember:**

- **Selection Ratio (SR):** $SR = N_s / N_a$. Range: 0 to 1. Lower = more competitive.
- **Base Rate (BR):** Proportion who would succeed without any selection.
- **Validity (r):** Predictor-criterion correlation. **Acceptable threshold: $r \ge 0.30$.**
- **Reliability (Cronbach's $\alpha$):** Internal consistency. **Acceptable: $\alpha \ge 0.70$.**
- **Utility ($\Delta U$):** Net economic gain. **Naylor-Shine formula** is the key equation.
- **Taylor-Russell Table:** Joint effect of SR, BR, and r on success ratio.

### **Five Selection Criteria Domains:**

1. **Physical** (anthropometry, sensory, motor)
2. **Cognitive** (memory, attention, reasoning)
3. **Psychological** (personality, EQ, motivation)
4. **Behavioral** (leadership, teamwork, risk)
5. **Biographical** (education, experience, references)

### **Five Primary Objectives:**

1. Match person to job
2. Minimize human error
3. Maximize system performance
4. Ensure occupational health & safety
5. Enhance job satisfaction & retention

### **Validity Hierarchy (Highest to Lowest):**

1. **Work Sample Tests** ($r \approx 0.54$)
2. **Structured Interviews** ($r \approx 0.51$)
3. **Cognitive Ability Tests** ($r \approx 0.51$)
4. **Assessment Centers** ($r \approx 0.36$)
5. **Unstructured Interviews** ($r \approx 0.20$)

### **Key Numerical Constants:**

- Selection Ratio: $0 \le SR \le 1$
- Validity Coefficient: $-1 \le r \le +1$
- Acceptable Cronbach's $\alpha$: $\ge 0.70$
- Heinrich's Human Error Contribution: $\approx 80\%$
- Cost-Validity Trade-off: Always optimize using Naylor-Shine

### **Common Exam Traps:**

- Confusing **validity** with **reliability**.
- Forgetting the **cost term** in Naylor-Shine formula.
- Listing criteria **without linking them to objectives**.
- Failing to **interpret numerical results** in plain language.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:**
> 1. For 3-mark questions: Definition + Formula + Brief Significance (3 points).
> 2. For 14-mark questions: Always structure as (a) and (b) with sub-headings, diagrams, and tabular comparisons.
> 3. **Always end with a concluding remark** linking to HFE system optimization.
> 4. **Use the Taylor-Russell table interpretation** whenever discussing selection effectiveness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perceptual Principles of Symbolic Design. Codes - Dimension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Perceptual Principles of Symbolic Design. Codes - Dimension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Perceptual Principles of Symbolic Design — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbolic Design** refers to the systematic creation and arrangement of visual marks (symbols, icons, signs, and pictorial representations) intended to convey operational, warning, informational, or instructional meaning to a human operator within a man–machine–environment system.

The **perceptual principles** that govern symbolic design are the psychophysical and Gestalt-based rules that determine how the human visual system **detects, discriminates, identifies, groups, and interprets** these symbols under varied operational conditions (lighting, vibration, workload, stress).

These principles bridge the gap between the **physical stimulus** (the printed/displayed symbol) and the **percept** (the operator's internal cognitive representation of meaning).

## 1.2 Key Terminology in KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Icon** | A graphical symbol that visually resembles the object/function it represents (e.g., a gas pump pictogram on a fuel station). |
| **Ideogram** | A symbol that represents an idea through convention rather than visual likeness (e.g., the word "AND" or the "+" operator). |
| **Iconicity** | The degree of visual resemblance between a symbol and its referent (real-world object). |
| **Symbolic Distance** | The semantic gap between the symbol's appearance and the meaning it denotes. |
| **Population Stereotype** | A culturally/populational agreed-upon expected response to a given stimulus (e.g., red → stop, green → go). |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Airport Sign"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine you land in Tokyo, Seoul, or Paris with no knowledge of the local language. You still successfully find the **toilet**, the **exit**, and the **baggage claim** — purely through pictographic symbols. The reason you can do this is that those symbols were designed using **perceptual principles** (clear figure-ground separation, high iconicity, redundant visual cues, simple geometry) so that the human visual system can decode them *pre-attentively*, even across language barriers.

In a B.Tech Human Factors context, every **control panel, cockpit display, CNC HMI, medical device interface, and road traffic sign** relies on the same engineering logic. A poorly designed symbol forces the operator to spend cognitive resources on **perception and decoding** rather than on the actual task, leading to errors, increased reaction time, and accidents.

## 1.4 The Six Perceptual Pillars of Symbolic Design (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Pillars (must remember for ESE):**
> 1. **Visibility** — Can the symbol be *seen* under operational conditions?
> 2. **Legibility** — Can individual components of the symbol be *discriminated*?
> 3. **Readability** — Can the symbol be *recognized* and interpreted as a whole?
> 4. **Comprehensibility** — Does the operator *understand* the meaning unambiguously?
> 5. **Conspicuity** — Does the symbol *capture attention* amidst competing visual noise?
> 6. **Population Stereotype Compatibility** — Is the symbol *culturally expected*?

All six are evaluated using the classic human factors hierarchy, and a failure in any one of them propagates downstream into operator error.

## 1.5 Coding and Dimension — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coding** is the deliberate assignment of distinguishable physical characteristics to displays, controls, or symbols so that an operator can rapidly and reliably identify their function or state.

**Dimension (as a code)** refers to the use of measurable physical extents — primarily **size, length, height, width, or area** — as a *coding variable* to differentiate between symbols, controls, or display elements. It is one of the **classical visual codes** in human factors engineering, alongside color, shape, position, and alphanumeric coding.

In KTU parlance, dimension is a **non-redundant, absolute-identification-friendly code** that exploits the human ability to estimate **magnitude** (per Weber's Law).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Perceptual Principles

Symbolic design is governed by three intertwined theoretical pillars:

### Pillar A — Gestalt Laws of Perceptual Organization

The human brain does not perceive symbols as a sum of disconnected lines and curves; it perceives them as **organized wholes**. The KTU-relevant Gestalt laws are:

- **Law of Proximity** — Elements close together are perceived as belonging to the same group.
- **Law of Similarity** — Elements sharing visual features (color, shape, size) are grouped perceptually.
- **Law of Closure** — The mind fills in missing information to perceive a complete, closed figure.
- **Law of Continuity** — Smooth, continuous contours are preferred over abrupt changes.
- **Law of Figure-Ground** — The visual system separates a stimulus into a *figure* (object of attention) and a *ground* (background).
- **Law of Symmetry & Prägnanz** — Symmetrical, simple, "good" forms are preferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** A warning icon designed with broken / asymmetric strokes violates Closure and Prägnanz, increasing interpretation time. Always use **closed, symmetrical, simple** symbol geometries.

### Pillar B — Psychophysical Limits (Weber, Fechner, Stevens)

- **Weber's Law:** The **just-noticeable difference (JND)** in a stimulus is a constant fraction of the original stimulus magnitude.
- **Stevens' Power Law:** Perceived magnitude grows as a power function of physical magnitude.
- **Engineering consequence:** For size coding, two symbols must differ by at least the **Weber fraction** to be reliably discriminated.

### Pillar C — Information Theory (Shannon, Hick)

- Symbols are **information channels**. The more distinguishable the code, the higher the information capacity (in bits).
- The operator's reaction time to discriminate among $N$ alternatives follows **Hick's Law**.

## 2.2 Codes — Classification of Visual Coding Variables

Human factors engineers (per Sanders & McCormick, Wickens, and the KTU reference) classify the principal **visual codes** as follows:

| Code Type | Coding Variable | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Color Code** | Hue, Saturation, Luminance | Warning levels, status LEDs, signal lights |
| **Shape Code** | Geometric form | Aircraft knobs (ISO 3461), control levers |
| **Size Code (Dimension)** | Length, Area, Volume | Bar graphs, push-button hierarchy, severity |
| **Position Code** | Spatial location | Multi-function cockpit displays, map legends |
| **Alphanumeric Code** | Letters, Digits, Words | Avionics readouts, instrument labels |
| **Brightness Code** | Luminance intensity | Radar PPI displays, LCD indicators |
| **Temporal Code** | Flash rate, duty cycle | Emergency beacons, alert lamps |
| **Spatial/Pictorial Code** | Iconic representation | Road signs, emergency exit pictograms |

## 2.3 Dimension as a Code — Engineering Analysis

**Dimension (size) coding** assigns **measurable extent** (length, height, area, bar length, marker thickness) to convey magnitude or category. It is the **most direct and intuitive** code for representing quantitative information.

### Properties of Dimension Coding

- **Dimensional Compatibility (Stevens' Nominal → Ratio Scale)** — Size naturally maps onto an ordinal or ratio scale of meaning (small bottle → small dose, long bar → high value).
- **Absolute Identification Limit** — Humans can reliably identify approximately **5 ± 2** distinct size levels in an absolute-identification task.
- **Redundant Pairing with Color** — Dimension is frequently combined with color (e.g., traffic light: red/yellow/green + spatial position).
- **Spatial-Quantitative Compatibility** — Length coding along a common scale produces a **bar graph**, the most efficient visual display for quantitative comparison (per Cleveland & McGill's perceptual hierarchy).

### Advantages of Dimension Coding

1. **Pre-attentive processing** — Magnitude is perceived automatically.
2. **Compatible with population stereotypes** (e.g., bigger = more, longer = further).
3. **High information density** per unit display area.
4. **Robust to color-vision deficiencies** when used alone.

### Disadvantages of Dimension Coding

1. Occupies significant **display real estate**.
2. **Occlusion risk** when symbols are stacked or overlapping.
3. **Poor for absolute identification** if more than 5 levels are used.
4. Vulnerable to **viewing-angle distortion** (perspective foreshortening).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N = X^D$ | Number of distinct codes from $X$ values along $D$ dimensions | $N \geq 1$ |
| 2 | $I = \log_2(N)$ | Information content of a code | bits |
| 3 | $\mathrm{RT} = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ | Hick's Law: choice reaction time | seconds |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | Weber's Law (relative JND) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\psi = K \cdot \phi^{\beta}$ | Stevens' Power Law (perceived magnitude) | $\psi$ is perceived, $\phi$ is physical |
| 6 | $\mathrm{SE} = \dfrac{1}{m} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{n}{m}\right)$ | Channel capacity (Fitts' related) — secondary | bits/s |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{\text{Number correct}}{\text{Total trials}} \times 100\%$ | Identification accuracy | percent |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** Bar-length altitude tapes on Primary Flight Displays (PFDs) use dimension coding; altitude is mapped to a moving vertical scale.
- **Medical Devices:** Infusion pump syringe diameters indicate drug volume — a size-coded safety check.
- **Industrial Control Rooms:** Trend-graphs of temperature/pressure use bar length to convey magnitude.
- **Automotive HMI:** Speed, fuel level, and engine RPM are dimension-coded on the instrument cluster.
- **Aerospace Warning Panels:** The size of a warning triangle is sometimes scaled to indicate severity.
- **Signage:** Standardized emergency exit signs use size + shape (dimension + shape) as redundant codes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Distinct Codes from Multiple Dimensions

**Statement:** If a designer encodes information using $D$ independent coding dimensions, each taking $X_i$ discrete values, the total number of distinguishable codes $N$ is the **Cartesian product** of all dimensions.

### Step 1 — Start with a Single Dimension

If a single code variable (e.g., color) takes $X_1$ discrete values (say, red, yellow, green, blue), then the number of unique codes is simply:

$$N_1 = X_1$$

For example, with 3 colors: $N_1 = 3$.

### Step 2 — Add a Second Independent Dimension

Suppose we add a second dimension (e.g., shape) with $X_2$ values (circle, square, triangle). For *each* of the $X_1$ color values, the $X_2$ shape values can be paired:

$$N_2 = X_1 \times X_2$$

With 3 colors and 3 shapes: $N_2 = 3 \times 3 = 9$ unique codes.

### Step 3 — Generalize to $D$ Dimensions

Adding a third dimension with $X_3$ values multiplies the count again. By the **Multiplicative Principle of Counting** (a fundamental result of combinatorics applied to information theory):

$$N_D = X_1 \times X_2 \times X_3 \times \dots \times X_D$$

### Step 4 — Uniform Case

If all $D$ dimensions share the same number of discrete levels $X$ (e.g., binary on/off on every dimension), the formula collapses to:

$$N = X^D$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The number of distinguishable codes grows **exponentially** with the number of dimensions, but so does operator **cognitive load**. Designers must balance *code capacity* against *perceptual discrimination cost*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Information Content in Bits

**Statement:** The information content $I$ carried by a code drawn uniformly from $N$ equally likely alternatives is given by the base-2 logarithm of $N$.

### Step 1 — Definition of a Bit

By **Shannon's (1948) definition**, one bit of information is the amount needed to halve the uncertainty of a binary decision. A binary choice ($N=2$) carries exactly 1 bit.

$$I = \log_2(2) = 1 \text{ bit}$$

### Step 2 — Two Independent Binary Choices

Two independent binary choices form four equally likely outcomes:

$$I = \log_2(2^2) = 2 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — General Case

For $N$ equally likely alternatives, the number of binary decisions required is:

$$I = \log_2(N)$$

If the alternatives are not equally likely (with probabilities $p_i$), Shannon's full entropy formula applies:

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \cdot \log_2(p_i)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hick's Law Applied to Code Selection

**Statement:** Reaction time to select a stimulus from $N$ equally probable alternatives increases logarithmically with $N$.

### Step 1 — Empirical Foundation

William Edmund Hick (1952) and Ray Hyman independently observed that choice reaction time grows linearly with the **information content** of the stimulus set.

### Step 2 — Linear Model Setup

Let $a$ be the baseline reaction time (encoding + motor execution) and $b$ be the slope (cognitive processing time per bit). Then:

$$\mathrm{RT} = a + b \cdot I$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Information Content

Substituting $I = \log_2(N)$:

$$\mathrm{RT} = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$$

For a multi-dimensional code $N = X^D$:

$$\mathrm{RT} = a + b \cdot \log_2(X^D) = a + b \cdot D \cdot \log_2(X)$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Consequence

- Doubling $D$ (adding a redundant code dimension) increases RT only by a constant *b·log₂(X)* — a manageable cost.
- However, $D$ is constrained by the **Absolute Identification Limit** of $\approx 5$ for any single dimension.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weber Fraction for Dimension Discrimination

**Statement:** For two stimuli differing only in size (length, area, volume), the **minimum discriminable size difference** is a constant fraction of the reference size.

### Step 1 — Weber's Law Statement

The JND $\Delta I$ in a stimulus of magnitude $I$ is:

$$\Delta I = k \cdot I$$

where $k$ is the **Weber fraction** (a dimensionless constant characteristic of the sensory modality).

### Step 2 — Discriminable Sizes

For size coding, the minimum number of *just-distinguishable* size levels $L$ in a range $[I_{\min}, I_{\max}]$ is:

$$L = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\log(I_{\max}/I_{\min})}{\log(1+k)} \right\rfloor + 1$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $I_{\min} = 10$ mm, $I_{\max} = 80$ mm, and the Weber fraction for length discrimination is $k = 0.05$.

- $\log(80/10) = \log(8) \approx 0.903$
- $\log(1.05) \approx 0.0212$
- $L \approx \lfloor 0.903 / 0.0212 \rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 42.6 \rfloor + 1 = 42 + 1 = 43$ levels

But the **absolute-identification** limit caps usable levels at $5 \pm 2$ (Miller, 1956), so even though the visual system *can* discriminate 43 levels, the operator *cannot identify* them by absolute judgment. **Redundant coding** (size + color + position) is therefore essential.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Coding Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison table. Use it for ESE short-answer and 14-mark questions.**

| Code | Channels (X) | Pre-attentive? | Color-blind Safe? | Conspicuity | Best For | Worst For |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Color** | 5 – 7 | Yes | ❌ No | High | Warnings, status | Quantitative data |
| **Shape** | 5 – 7 | Yes | ✅ Yes | Medium | Categorical IDs | Continuous data |
| **Size (Dimension)** | 4 – 5 | Yes | ✅ Yes | Medium | Magnitude, severity | Absolute ID of many levels |
| **Position** | 3 – 5 | Yes | ✅ Yes | High | Spatial mapping | Free-form data |
| **Alphanumeric** | $\geq 26$ | No | ✅ Yes | Low | Specific labels | Rapid warning |
| **Brightness** | 3 – 4 | Yes | Partial | Medium | Night ops | Daylight |
| **Temporal (Flash)** | 2 – 3 | Yes | ✅ Yes | Very High | Alerts | Sustained info |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** Always prefer **redundant coding** — pair a primary code (e.g., color) with a secondary code (e.g., shape *and* size). This protects against color-vision deficiency, lighting variation, and operator stress.

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — KTU 2019-Style

> **Problem:** A control panel designer wants to encode **12 distinct warning states** on a single display using only **size and color** codes. If 3 colors and 4 size levels are available, is this feasible? If not, propose a fix.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Available Codes**

$$N = X_{\text{color}} \times X_{\text{size}} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ codes}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to Requirement**

Required: 12 states. Available: 12 codes. So mathematically $N \ge 12$. **Feasible at the combinatorial level.**

**Step 3 — Apply Perceptual Constraint**

- 3 colors → at the upper limit of reliable color discrimination (consider Deuteranope/Protanope → 2 colors reliably).
- 4 size levels → exceeds Miller's $5 \pm 2$ only marginally, but absolute identification at 4 size levels is still within operational limits.

**Step 4 — Robustness Check**

For a color-vision-deficient operator, the *effective* color count drops to 2, so usable codes drop to:

$$N_{\text{CVD}} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ codes} \;\;<\;\; 12$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Fix**

Add a **third redundant dimension** (shape) with 2 levels. New code count:

$$N_{\text{new}} = 3 \times 4 \times 2 = 24 \;\; \ge \;\; 12$$

Even with 2 colors (CVD-safe), the design still supports:

$$N_{\text{CVD,new}} = 2 \times 4 \times 2 = 16 \;\; \ge \;\; 12 \;\; ✅$$

**Conclusion:** Add a **shape code** (e.g., circle vs. triangle) to the size+color design for **redundant, robust coding** that satisfies both normal and color-deficient operators.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Perceptual Symbol-Decoding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Stimulus: Symbol on Display] --> B[Optical Stage: Light to Retina]
    B --> C[Retinal Processing: Photoreceptors Activated]
    C --> D[LGN Thalamic Relay: Feature Decomposition]
    D --> E[Primary Visual Cortex V1: Edges, Orientation]
    E --> F[Higher Visual Areas V2 V4: Shape, Color, Depth]
    F --> G[Gestalt Grouping: Closure, Proximity, Similarity]
    G --> H[Perceptual Decision: Figure-Ground Segregation]
    H --> I[Working Memory: Symbol Match to Long-Term Memory]
    I --> J[Semantic Access: Meaning Retrieved]
    J --> K[Response: Operator Action]
    A1[Environmental Noise] -.-> B
    A2[Operator State: Stress, Fatigue] -.-> G
    A3[Population Stereotype Library] -.-> I
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **left-side inputs** (light, noise, stress, cultural stereotypes) are the *modulators* of perception.
- The **central pipeline** is the **bottom-up perceptual flow** from retinal transduction to semantic understanding.
- **KTU Insight:** Symbol design must ensure high signal-to-noise at every stage of this pipeline. A symbol that fails at stage **G** (Gestalt grouping) cannot be matched at stage **I**, no matter how iconic it is in isolation.

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Visual Codes in Human Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Visual Coding Methods] --> C1[Color Code]
    R --> C2[Shape Code]
    R --> C3[Size Dimension Code]
    R --> C4[Position Code]
    R --> C5[Alphanumeric Code]
    R --> C6[Brightness Code]
    R --> C7[Temporal Flash Code]
    R --> C8[Pictorial Iconic Code]
    C1 --> C1a[Nominal Scale]
    C1 --> C1b[3 to 5 Hues Max]
    C2 --> C2a[5 to 7 Forms]
    C2 --> C2b[ISO 3461 Standard]
    C3 --> C3a[Length, Area, Volume]
    C3 --> C3b[Stevens Power Law Compliant]
    C3 --> C3c[Miller Limit: 4 to 5 Levels]
    C4 --> C4a[Spatial Mapping]
    C4 --> C4b[3 to 5 Locations]
    C5 --> C5a[Verbal Labels]
    C5 --> C5b[Specific Identification]
    C6 --> C6a[3 to 4 Levels]
    C6 --> C6b[Night Vision Compatible]
    C7 --> C7a[2 to 3 Flash Rates]
    C7 --> C7b[Attention Capture]
    C8 --> C8a[High Iconicity]
    C8 --> C8b[Language Independent]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
