- The **dimension code** branch (C3) explicitly notes the *Stevens Power Law* compliance and the *Miller 5±2* absolute-identification ceiling.
- This tree is the canonical KTU answer framework for "Classify the visual codes used in display design."

## 4.3 Redundant Coding Strategy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Message: 12 Distinct Warnings] --> ENC[Coding Subsystem]
    ENC --> CL[Color Layer: 3 Levels]
    ENC --> SL[Shape Layer: 2 Levels]
    ENC --> SZ[Size Layer: 4 Levels]
    CL --> MIX[Redundant Channel Combiner]
    SL --> MIX
    SZ --> MIX
    MIX --> DISP[Visual Display Surface]
    DISP --> OP[Human Operator]
    OP --> DEC[Perceptual Decoder: 3D Code]
    DEC --> ID[Reliable Identification 12 States]
    OP -.-> CVD[Color Vision Deficiency Filter]
    CVD -.-> DEC
    DEC -.-> OK[Robust Even Under CVD: 16 Codes]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The operator receives a **3-dimensional redundant code** (color × shape × size).
- Even if the color channel is lost (CVD filter), $2 \times 4 = 8$ codes remain for the 4 most critical warnings, with shape and size still discriminating the remaining 8.
- This is the **engineering rationale** for redundant coding in KTU board answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - Students often **forget the Miller 5±2 limit** when proposing size codes → 2–3 marks lost.
> - Confusing **iconic** (resemblance-based) with **symbolic** (convention-based) symbols → conceptual ambiguity penalty.
> - Omitting **redundant coding** when justifying a display design → loses 4 marks in the "design rationale" sub-part.
> - Writing "color" where "shape" or "size" is meant (and vice versa) → 1 mark per misuse.
> - Failing to cite **ISO 3461** or **ANSI Z535** standards for symbolic design → loses the "industry relevance" bonus mark.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define "iconicity" in symbolic design. How does it influence the comprehensibility of a symbol?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Iconicity** is the degree of visual resemblance between a symbol (icon, pictogram) and the real-world object or function it represents. It is a *continuous* property — a photograph has high iconicity, an abstract letter has low iconicity.
>
> **Influence on comprehensibility:**
> - **High iconicity** → faster learning, lower training cost, cross-cultural transfer (e.g., the international "no smoking" cigarette-with-slash pictogram).
> - **Low iconicity** → requires convention learning (e.g., the letter "P" for parking) but saves display space and avoids cultural taboos.
>
> In engineering, the goal is **optimal iconicity** — high enough for instant recognition, abstract enough to avoid visual clutter or unintended associations.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**State Weber's Law. Why is it important in the design of size-coded (dimension) symbols?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Weber's Law** states that the *just-noticeable difference* (JND) in a stimulus magnitude is a constant fraction of the original magnitude:
> $$\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$$
> where $k$ is the Weber fraction.
>
> **Importance in size coding:**
> - Two size-coded symbols must differ by **at least $k \cdot I$** to be reliably discriminated by the operator.
> - If consecutive symbol sizes are spaced below the Weber fraction, the operator perceives them as identical — a **silent failure mode** in the design.
> - For length discrimination, $k \approx 0.05$; for area, $k \approx 0.10$. Designers must apply the *larger* fraction to avoid under-specification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2023]

**"Symbolic design is a perceptual engineering discipline." Critically discuss the perceptual principles that govern the design of visual symbols in man–machine systems. With examples, explain how size (dimension) is used as a coding variable.**

#### (a) Perceptual Principles of Symbolic Design (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Symbolic design is governed by the following perceptual principles, all of which are tested in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Visibility and Conspicuity** — The symbol must be visible under all operational conditions (lighting, vibration, ambient contrast). It must capture attention against the visual background. *Example:* Aircraft "FASTEN SEATBELT" signs use high-contrast yellow-on-black to dominate cabin visual field.

2. **Legibility** — The *internal components* of the symbol (strokes, gaps, terminations) must be discriminable. Minimum stroke width, minimum gap, and minimum feature size are governed by the **Snellen-style acuity** of the human eye (≈ 1 arc-minute). *Example:* ISO 7010 emergency signs use stroke widths of at least $1/8$ of the symbol height to remain legible at 16 m viewing distance.

3. **Readability (Figure-Ground)** — The symbol must be separable from its background using the **Law of Figure-Ground**. Adequate contrast ratio (≥ 3:1 for symbols, ≥ 7:1 for text) is mandated by ANSI Z535.2.

4. **Closure and Prägnanz (Gestalt)** — The mind prefers complete, simple, symmetrical forms. Symbols with broken contours are perceived as *incomplete* and slow to interpret. *Example:* The fire extinguisher pictogram is a single closed contour, not a fragmented sketch.

5. **Similarity and Proximity** — Related symbols (e.g., three warning levels of a hazard) are placed close together and use a common geometric style. This signals *semantic relatedness* to the operator.

6. **Iconicity vs. Convention** — A balance must be struck. Highly iconic symbols (e.g., a stylized flame for "flammable") are intuitive; conventional symbols (e.g., triangle for warning) require training but allow abstraction.

7. **Population Stereotype Compatibility** — The symbol must align with cultural expectations (red = stop in most cultures; white = purity in the West, mourning in parts of Asia). ISO 3864 standardizes color semantics globally to mitigate this.

8. **Redundancy** — Multiple codes (color + shape + size) reinforce meaning, supporting operators under stress, fatigue, or sensory deficit (color-blindness, low light).

(7 marks distributed across 8 principles, ≈ 0.875 each; examiner's discretion)

#### (b) Dimension as a Coding Variable (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Dimension (size) coding** uses the physical extent of a stimulus (length, height, area, volume) to convey magnitude or category. It is one of the classical visual codes in human factors engineering.

**Forms of Dimension Coding:**

1. **Length Coding** — Bar graphs, altitude tapes, signal-strength bars. Most efficient for quantitative comparison (top of Cleveland-McGill hierarchy).

2. **Area Coding** — Bubble charts, scope-screen returns, icon-size scaling (e.g., markers on a map proportional to population).

3. **Volume / 3D Coding** — Used sparingly due to perspective distortion; common in CAD and process-flow visualizations.

4. **Categorical Size Coding** — Discrete size levels (small, medium, large) for severity, priority, or hierarchy.

**Design Rules (KTU High-Yield):**

- Apply **Weber's Law** → consecutive sizes must differ by at least the Weber fraction ($k \approx 0.05$ for length).
- Apply **Miller's Law** → no more than $5 \pm 2 = 7$ absolute-identification size levels.
- Apply **Stevens' Power Law** → if the meaning is "intensity" (loudness, brightness), use $\beta \approx 0.6$ to 0.7; if "length," $\beta \approx 1.0$ to 1.1.
- **Spatial-Quantitative Compatibility** → length grows along the dimension it represents (horizontal for time, vertical for magnitude).

**Engineering Examples:**

- **Cockpit Altimeter (PFD):** The altitude tape is a vertical bar of fixed width, with a numeric readout — a classic *length-coded* display.
- **Infusion Pump Syringe:** The diameter of the syringe barrel visually indicates the drug volume — *area-coded* safety check.
- **Process Control Trends:** Temperature and pressure trends are length-coded line graphs on the SCADA HMI.
- **Traffic Sign Severity Hierarchy:** A "Caution" sign is smaller than a "Danger" sign in ISO-compliant industrial safety labeling.

**Advantages of Dimension Coding:**
- Pre-attentive magnitude perception
- High accuracy in quantitative comparison
- Robust against color-vision deficiency (when used alone)
- Culturally invariant (longer = more is a near-universal stereotype)

**Disadvantages:**
- Limited number of absolute-identification levels (~5)
- Consumes display area
- Vulnerable to viewing-angle distortion in 3D forms
- Less conspicuous than color or flash codes

(7 marks distributed across forms, design rules, examples, and trade-offs)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alt Choice]

**Explain the classification of visual codes used in display design. With a suitable analytical framework, justify the use of redundant coding with special emphasis on size (dimension) as a code.**

#### (a) Classification of Visual Codes (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Visual codes in human factors engineering (per Wickens, Hollands, and the KTU reference) are classified into **eight principal categories**:

1. **Color Code** — Exploits hue discrimination. Effective for $5$ – $7$ categorical states. Best for warnings and status. Vulnerable to color-vision deficiency (≈ 8% of male population).

2. **Shape Code** — Exploits form discrimination. Effective for $5$ – $7$ distinct shapes. Standardized under **ISO 3461** for safety symbols. Color-vision safe.

3. **Size (Dimension) Code** — Exploits magnitude perception. Effective for $4$ – $5$ categorical levels, or continuous quantitative data via bar length. Color-vision safe.

4. **Position Code** — Exploits spatial location. Effective for $3$ – $5$ locations. Most pre-attentive of all codes; supports spatial mapping.

5. **Alphanumeric Code** — Highest capacity (≥ 26 letters, 10 digits, thousands of words). Slowest to decode; requires literacy. Always paired with a graphic.

6. **Brightness Code** — Exploits luminance contrast. Effective for $3$ – $4$ levels. Critical in night-vision goggle (NVG) compatible displays.

7. **Temporal (Flash) Code** — Exploits time-varying luminance. Used for $2$ – $3$ states (steady, slow-flash, fast-flash). Highest conspicuity.

8. **Pictorial / Iconic Code** — Highest iconicity; language-independent; slowest to design but fastest to interpret for well-trained operators.

**Analytical Selection Criteria** (per the KTU syllabus and Sanders & McCormick):

| Criterion | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Discriminability** | Can the operator distinguish all code values? |
| **Conspicuity** | Does the code attract attention? |
| **Capacity** | How many distinct states can the code represent? |
| **Compatibility** | Does the code match population stereotype and Stevens' mappings? |
| **Robustness** | Does the code survive degraded conditions (CVD, low light, stress)? |
| **Cost** | Display real estate, training, and implementation cost |

#### (b) Redundant Coding with Emphasis on Size (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Redundant coding** is the engineering practice of conveying the same information through **two or more independent coding dimensions**. It is a cornerstone of **defense-in-depth** in human factors design.

**Justification for Redundancy:**

1. **Failure Tolerance** — If one channel fails (e.g., color-blind operator, NVG-bloomed display, smoke-obscured cabin), the other channel(s) still convey the message.
2. **Reduced Reaction Time** — Multiple pre-attentive channels activate parallel processing pathways in the visual cortex, lowering Hick's-law RT.
3. **Improved Conspicuity** — A red triangle (color + shape) is more attention-grabbing than red alone.
4. **Population-Stereotype Reinforcement** — Conflicting cultural mappings (e.g., red = danger in the West, red = prosperity in China) are bypassed by *triple* coding (color + shape + text).
5. **Compliance with Standards** — ISO 3864, ANSI Z535, and ICAO Doc 9137 mandate redundant coding for safety-critical signs.

**Role of Size (Dimension) as a Redundant Code:**

Dimension coding is the **most quantitatively accurate** of the visual codes and pairs naturally with both color and shape:

- **Size + Color:** A red triangle (danger) that is *larger* than a yellow triangle (caution) — magnitude of hazard is encoded twice.
- **Size + Shape:** A larger circle (alert) vs. a smaller circle (information) — hierarchy is reinforced.
- **Size + Alphanumeric:** A large-font "DANGER" word vs. small-font "Notice" — severity is dimensionally emphasized.

**Quantitative Justification:**

For a code with $D$ independent dimensions of $X$ levels each, total code capacity is:

$$N = X^D$$

A 2D code (color + size, $X=3, X=4$) yields:

$$N = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ states}$$

A 3D code (color + size + shape, $X=3, X=4, X=2$) yields:

$$N = 3 \times 4 \times 2 = 24 \text{ states}$$

Hick's Law RT penalty is:

$$\Delta \mathrm{RT} = b \cdot \log_2(24/12) = b \cdot 1 \text{ bit}$$

A modest **1-bit increase in RT** doubles the code capacity — an excellent engineering trade-off.

**Real-World Engineering Examples:**

- **Aviation TCAS Display:** Color (red/yellow/green) + shape (square/circle/diamond) + size + altitude numeric readout — quadruple-redundant traffic advisory.
- **Medical Infusion Pump:** Color (red for high-dose) + size of syringe icon + alphanumeric drug name + audible alarm — quadruple redundancy for life-critical dosing.
- **Industrial Control Valves:** Color-coded handles (red = closed, green = open) + shape (round vs. lever) + size (small = manual, large = motorized) + position label.
- **Automotive Dashboard:** Engine warning — color (red) + shape (oil-can icon) + size (large icon on LCD) + alphanumeric (text "LOW OIL PRESSURE") — quadruple redundancy for safety-critical alerts.

**Conclusion:** Redundant coding, with dimension (size) as a quantitative anchor, is the **gold standard** for safety-critical, high-consequence human-machine interfaces. It costs minimal cognitive resources (1–2 bits) while multiplying information robustness by an order of magnitude.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 ESE**

### ✅ Perceptual Principles (Module 2 Core)

- **Six Perceptual Pillars:** Visibility → Legibility → Readability → Comprehensibility → Conspicuity → Population Stereotype Compatibility.
- **Gestalt Laws (must list at least 4 in ESE):** Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Figure-Ground, Prägnanz, Symmetry.
- **Miller's Law:** $5 \pm 2$ absolute-identification chunks per code dimension.
- **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I / I = k$ — governs minimum size discriminability.
- **Stevens' Power Law:** $\psi = K \phi^{\beta}$ — magnitude matching for size coding.

### ✅ Codes — Dimension (Size) — Key Points

- **Definition:** Use of measurable physical extent (length, area, volume) as a coding variable.
- **Forms:** Length, area, volume, categorical size levels.
- **Capacity:** $4$ – $5$ categorical levels (absolute ID); continuous via bar length.
- **Standard:** ISO 3461 (safety symbols), ISO 3864 (color & shape), ANSI Z535 (hazard communication).
- **Best For:** Quantitative magnitude, severity hierarchy, trend graphs.
- **Worst For:** Many distinct categorical states (use color/shape instead).
- **Robustness:** Color-vision safe; lighting sensitive; viewing-angle vulnerable in 3D.

### ✅ Code Capacity Formulas (Memorize)

- $N = X^D$ — total distinct codes.
- $I = \log_2(N)$ — information content in bits.
- $\mathrm{RT} = a + b \log_2(N)$ — Hick's Law.
- $\Delta I / I = k$ — Weber's Law.
- $\psi = K \phi^{\beta}$ — Stevens' Power Law.

### ✅ Engineering Heuristics (for ESE design questions)

1. **Redundancy is mandatory** for safety-critical displays.
2. **Avoid more than 7 distinct values** on any single code dimension.
3. **Use length, not area, for quantitative data** (Cleveland-McGill hierarchy).
4. **Match the code to the operator's stereotype** (red = danger, up = more, etc.).
5. **Test for color-vision deficiency** if color is a primary code.
6. **Provide a redundant alphanumeric label** for all safety symbols.
7. **Pre-attentive channels** (color, size, orientation) are always preferred over attentive (alphanumeric) for warnings.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Perceptual Principles of Symbolic Design · Codes — Dimension**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colour. Design of Dynamic Information Displays
Uses of Dynamic Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Uses of Dynamic Information # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Dynamic Information** refers to any data, signal, or visual cue presented to a human operator that changes continuously or discretely over time in response to variations in a system, process, or environment. In the context of Human Factors Engineering (HFE) and display design, dynamic information is conveyed through **Dynamic Displays** — instruments, screens, or visual interfaces whose indicators (needles, bars, digits, graphics, colour fields) move, update, flash, or change state to reflect real-time system behaviour. According to the canonical taxonomy established by **Sanders & McCormick (Human Factors in Engineering and Design, 7th–10th Edition)** and reinforced in KTU's PESFT748 Module 2 syllabus, dynamic information is the operational counterpart of *static information* (fixed labels, legends, scale markings) and serves three principal operator tasks: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT748 - Module 2)** > *"Dynamic displays are those whose indications change with time to reflect the variable status of a system parameter. They enable operators to perform quantitative reading, qualitative trend interpretation, check-reading, and event monitoring functions in process control, aviation, automotive, medical, and consumer systems."* The four universally accepted **uses of dynamic information** in human-machine systems are: | # | Use of Dynamic Information | Operator's Cognitive Task | |---|----------------------------|---------------------------| | 1 | **Quantitative Reading** | Read a precise numeric value at an instant in time | | 2 | **Qualitative / Trend Reading** | Determine the *direction* and *rate of change* | | 3 | **Check Reading** | Confirm whether a value lies within an acceptable band | | 4 | **Event Monitoring & Tracking** | Detect, follow, and anticipate discrete occurrences | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Module 2 of PESFT748 explicitly asks students to classify displays by information type (static vs. dynamic) and to explain the four functional uses. Memorising these four uses — and being able to pair each one with a representative instrument — is a guaranteed 7–8 mark question. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Analogy Imagine you are driving a car on a hill road at night. You glance at the **speedometer** — its needle points to **62 km/h**. That is *static information at that instant* (the number you read). But the *motion of the needle* — creeping up from 60 to 62 to 65 — is **dynamic information**. It tells you not just "how fast" but "how fast am I accelerating". Now imagine your fuel gauge: the needle drops steadily toward empty. The dynamic downward sweep warns you long before the static E mark does. Finally, the blinking red **engine-temperature warning** light is dynamic *event* information — a discrete state-change alert demanding immediate action. So, dynamic information is essentially **information-in-motion**. It compresses time: instead of asking an operator to read five snapshots and mentally subtract, a moving pointer lets the eye and brain *integrate* the change naturally. This is why dynamic displays are the backbone of every modern cockpit, control room, ICU monitor, and even the smartphone in your pocket. > [!TIP] > **Real-world dynamic information sources you interact with daily:** ECG waveforms on hospital monitors, scrolling stock tickers, animated bar-graph Wi-Fi indicators, radar sweeps, autopilot mode annunciators, and the spinning compass rose on Google Maps. All are governed by the same Human Factors principles discussed in this module. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used The following **standard human-factors metrics** govern the design of dynamic displays and are routinely referenced in KTU exam answers: - **Minimum Legible Viewing Distance** for a moving pointer: **~50 cm** (sandard reading distance for panel instruments). - **Visual Reaction Time (simple stimulus)**: **~0.2 s** (Boff & Lincoln, 1988). - **Flicker Fusion Threshold** for dynamic CRT/LED displays: **~50 Hz** (refresh rate at which flicker becomes imperceptible). - **Reading Time** for a 3-digit numeric display: **≈ 1.5 s** (versus 0.5 s for a single moving pointer to reach a value). - **Just-Noticeable Difference (JND)** in pointer position on a circular dial: **~1.5° of arc** (general-purpose industrial standard). > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners repeatedly deduct marks when students confuse **static** displays (fixed scales, legends, gauges that don't change) with **dynamic** displays. Always begin an answer with the explicit distinction. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Dynamic Information Exists From a human-information-processing perspective (Wickens' **Multiple Resource Theory**, 2008), dynamic information exploits the human visual system's native ability to detect **motion** far faster and more reliably than it can decode **numerals**. Specifically: 1. **Motion perception is pre-attentive.** The magnocellular pathway in the visual cortex detects movement within ~30 ms, well before focal attention is engaged. 2. **Trend extrapolation is subconscious.** A moving pointer allows the operator to project the future state (e.g., "engine temperature will hit redline in 8 seconds") without explicit calculation. 3. **Change-blindness is bypassed.** Sudden state changes (alarm lights, blinking numerals) are almost impossible to miss because dynamic elements actively attract the *attention capture* reflex. Thus, designers leverage dynamic information whenever the operator task requires **speed**, **trend awareness**, or **salience** — tasks where static information would force slow, serial, attention-heavy cognition. ## 2.2 The Four Canonical Uses — Deep Analysis ### Use 1 — Quantitative Reading - **Goal:** Obtain a precise numerical value at a single moment. - **Best displays:** Digital numeric readouts (7-segment LEDs, LCDs), moving-pointer analog gauges with calibrated scales. - **When to use:** High-precision single-point reading (e.g., altimeter in feet, voltmeter to 0.1 V). - **Limitation:** Poor for showing rate-of-change or trends. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off rule (Sanders & McCormick):** *Quantitative digital displays are read faster and more accurately than analog pointers when the task is single-point reading, but analog dynamic pointers are superior for trend and rate perception.* ### Use 2 — Qualitative (Trend) Reading - **Goal:** Determine *direction* and *rate of change* — "is it rising, falling, or stable, and how fast?" - **Best displays:** Moving-pointer circular or vertical meters, scrolling strip-chart recorders, moving bar-graph displays (like CPU/RAM monitors), trend graphs on HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces). - **When to use:** Process control, engine monitoring, financial dashboards. - **Cognitive benefit:** The angular velocity of a pointer (or the slope of a trend line) directly maps to rate of change — the eye performs an analogue integration. ### Use 3 — Check Reading - **Goal:** Verify that a value lies within a *normal operating range* (green band), an *acceptable but cautionary* range (yellow band), or a *dangerous* range (red band). - **Best displays:** Banded analog gauges, range-indicator lights, Go/No-Go lights, bargraphs with coloured zones. - **Why dynamic?** Because the pointer *enters* and *leaves* bands over time, the operator perceives proximity-to-limit naturally. ### Use 4 — Event Monitoring & Tracking - **Goal:** Detect, follow, and react to **discrete events** that occur at unpredictable times. - **Best displays:** Blinking warning lights, annunciator panels, radar sweeps, audio-visual alarms, animated status icons, scope traces. - **Salience:** Event displays exploit the human *novelty detector* — a flashing red light captures attention even in a visually cluttered cockpit. ## 2.3 Classification Matrix of Dynamic Displays | Display Class | Information Type | Typical Use | Speed Advantage | |---------------|------------------|-------------|-----------------| | Moving-pointer analog | Quantitative + Trend | Engine RPM, Pressure | Moderate | | Digital numeric (7-seg) | Quantitative | Altimeter, Clock | High for point reading | | Strip-chart / Scrolling graph | Trend | Weather, ECG, Process | Very high for trend | | Bar-graph (moving fill) | Trend + Check | Fuel, Signal strength | High | | Blinking / colour-coded light | Event monitoring | Alarms, Status | Highest for salience | | Radar / sonar scope | Tracking + Event | Navigation, Defence | High for motion | | Cathode-Ray Tube (CRT/LCD) | All four | Process control HMI | Versatile | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table > [!NOTE] > **The table below consolidates every quantitative relationship, design threshold, and rule-of-thumb examiners expect in PESFT748 answers on dynamic information.** | # | Concept / Rule | Mathematical Form / Value | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Simple visual reaction time | $t_r \approx 0.2$ | seconds (s) | | 2 | Choice reaction time (4 choices) | $t_{cr} \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$ | seconds | | 3 | Pointer reading time (analog) | $t_p \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ | seconds per gauge | | 4 | Numeric display reading time | $t_n \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ | seconds for 3-digit value | | 5 | Flicker fusion threshold | $f_{ff} \approx 50$ | Hz (above this, flicker invisible) | | 6 | Refresh rate for "smooth" motion | $f_r \geq 60$ | Hz (modern displays) | | 7 | JND for pointer position | $\theta_{JND} \approx 1.5°$ | degrees of arc | | 8 | Display legibility distance | $d \approx 50$ to $70$ | cm (panel) | | 9 | Colour-coding contrast ratio | $CR \geq 3:1$ | for symbol discrimination | | 10 | Luminance contrast for grey scale | $\Delta L / L_b \geq 0.1$ | Weber-Fechner band | | 11 | Human operator tracking bandwidth | $\omega_{bw} \approx 1.0$ to $2.0$ | rad/s | | 12 | Display update rate (minimum) | $\geq 5 \times$ system dynamics | Hz (Nyquist-based rule) | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Dynamic Information is Mission-Critical - **Aviation:** Attitude Indicator (AI), Horizontal Situation Indicator (HSI), engine EPR gauges — all rely on dynamic pointers for spatial orientation and trend awareness. - **Nuclear Power Plants:** Control-room HMIs use scrolling trend graphs and banded bargraphs so operators perceive slow drift toward unsafe limits. - **Medical:** ICU monitors (ECG, SpO₂ plethysmograph, arterial waveform) are purely dynamic; a flatline is itself the most important dynamic event. - **Automotive:** Speedometer, tachometer, fuel gauge, and modern head-up displays (HUD) project dynamic information into the driver's line of sight. - **Defence:** Radar and sonar displays track moving targets by exploiting dynamic information — without motion, the operator cannot perform the tracking task at all. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on derivations in HFE:** Human Factors Engineering is a *behavioural science* grounded in experimental psychophysics; therefore, the "derivations" here are **quantitative reasoning chains** that link experimental observations to design rules. KTU examiners award full marks when the logic — not just the final number — is shown. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why a Moving Pointer is Faster than a Digital Display for Trend Reading **Step 1.** Define the operator's reading task. For a digital numeric display, the operator must: - Read digit 1, then digit 2, then digit 3 (serial saccadic fixations). - Hold each digit in working memory. - Mentally assemble the full number. This is an **n-digit serial reading task** with average dwell time per digit: $$t_{digit} \approx 0.30 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2.** Total reading time for a 3-digit numeric display: $$t_{numeric} = n \times t_{digit} = 3 \times 0.30 = 0.90 \text{ s}$$ (This is the *minimum*; with eye-movement overhead, field values of 1.5–2.0 s are observed experimentally.) **Step 3.** For an analog pointer, the operator performs a **single fixation** at the pointer tip and reads the underlying scale. Reading time is approximately: $$t_{pointer} \approx 0.50 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4.** Compare the two: $$\frac{t_{numeric}}{t_{pointer}} = \frac{0.90}{0.50} = 1.80$$ So a 3-digit numeric display takes roughly **1.8 × longer** than a pointer for a single point reading. **Step 5.** However, when the task is *trend* reading, the pointer's angular velocity $\omega_p$ (°/s) directly maps to rate of change: $$\omega_p = \frac{d\theta}{dt} \approx \frac{\Delta \text{value}}{\Delta t}$$ The operator perceives this velocity pre-attentively. For a numeric display, computing the trend requires *two* sequential readings and a subtraction: $$v_{trend}^{numeric} = \frac{\text{Reading}_2 - \text{Reading}_1}{\Delta t}$$ requiring cognitive effort $E_{trend} \approx 2 \times t_{numeric} + t_{subtraction} \approx 2.5$ s. **Step 6.** Conclusion (this is the answer examiners expect): > *A moving pointer is **inferior** to a digital display for single-point quantitative reading by a factor of ~1.8, but **superior** for trend reading because the pointer's angular velocity conveys rate-of-change in a single pre-attentive fixation.* ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Refresh Rate to Appear "Continuous" The human eye perceives a sequence of static frames as continuous motion if the frame rate exceeds the **flicker fusion threshold**. **Step 1.** Empirical finding (Granit & Harper, 1979; reaffirmed in modern HFE texts): $$f_{ff} \approx 50 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2.** To ensure *safe* operation in critical displays (aviation, medical), apply a safety factor of 1.5×: $$f_{design} = 1.5 \times f_{ff} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ Hz}$$ This is why cockpit primary flight displays, modern monitors, and VR headsets operate at **60 Hz, 75 Hz, 90 Hz, or 120 Hz** — never at the bare 50 Hz threshold. **Step 3.** For tracking dynamic systems whose fastest significant frequency content is $f_{sig}$, the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling criterion** dictates: $$f_{sample} \geq 2 \times f_{sig}$$ For human-tracking tasks, $f_{sig}$ is rarely above 1 Hz (operator bandwidth ≈ 1 rad/s ≈ 0.16 Hz), so: $$f_{sample} \geq 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$ Yet modern displays oversample by 25–60× to keep the visual experience smooth and free of perceptual artefacts. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantitative Justification for Banded "Check-Reading" Displays **Step 1.** In a check-reading task, the operator must answer: *"Is value $V$ within the safe band $[V_{min}, V_{max}]$?"* **Step 2.** For a numeric display, the operator must: - Read the value $V$. - Compare $V$ against two thresholds mentally. Total cognitive load: $$C_{numeric} = t_{numeric} + 2 \times t_{compare}$$ with $t_{compare} \approx 0.15$ s, giving: $$C_{numeric} \approx 1.5 + 0.30 = 1.80 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3.** For a banded analog gauge, the colour-coded background zones (green/yellow/red) provide *external cognition* (Norman, 1988). The pointer's mere *position* in space is the answer: $$C_{banded} \approx 0.50 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4.** Speed-up factor: $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{C_{numeric}}{C_{banded}} = \frac{1.80}{0.50} = 3.6\times$$ **Step 5.** Error-rate consideration: by Hick's Law, decision time for $n$ equally-likely choices: $$T_{Hick} = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$$ A banded display reduces the decision space to *3 perceptually distinct regions*, so $n=3$: $$T_{Hick} = a + b \cdot \log_2(3) \approx a + 1.58b$$ This is much faster than parsing a 3-digit number and deciding against two thresholds sequentially. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Dynamic Display to Choose | Task Type | Best Display Choice | Justification (Quantitative / Qualitative) | |-----------|---------------------|--------------------------------------------| | Single precise value | 7-segment numeric | $t_{read} \approx 0.9$ s, no parallax error | | Rate / trend perception | Analog pointer or scrolling graph | Angular velocity mapped pre-attentively | | Within-range check | Banded analog gauge | Speedup factor **3.6×** over numeric | | Discrete event alerting | Blinking light / audible chime | Captures attention via novelty reflex | | Continuous tracking | CRT/LCD scope or radar | Supports operator bandwidth ~1 rad/s | | Multi-variable overview | Integrated HMI with mixed display types | Reduces scan time by parallel presentation | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Dynamic Information Flow in a Human-Machine System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Physical Process or Plant] --> nodeB[Sensor Array
Temperature, Pressure, Flow] nodeB --> nodeC[Signal Conditioning
and ADC Conversion] nodeC --> nodeD[Display Controller
Microprocessor or FPGA] nodeD --> nodeE[Dynamic Display
Pointer, Numeric, Bargraph, Light] nodeE --> nodeF[Human Visual System
Eye and Visual Cortex] nodeF --> nodeG[Perception and
Cognition] nodeG --> nodeH[Decision and Motor Action] nodeH --> nodeI[Control Input
to the Plant] nodeI --> nodeA nodeF -. Visual Feedback .-> nodeD ``` **Interpretation:** The plant generates a physical variable, the sensor transduces it to an electrical signal, the controller formats the data, and the dynamic display renders it in motion. The human operator closes the loop through perception, cognition, and action. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Dynamic Displays and Their Primary Uses ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Dynamic Information
in HFE Displays] nodeA --> nodeB[Quantitative Reading] nodeA --> nodeC[Qualitative Trend Reading] nodeA --> nodeD[Check Reading] nodeA --> nodeE[Event Monitoring and Tracking] nodeB --> nodeF[Digital Numeric Display
7-segment LEDs, LCD digits] nodeB --> nodeG[Pointer Analog Gauge
Calibrated circular scale] nodeC --> nodeH[Moving Pointer Meter] nodeC --> nodeI[Scrolling Strip Chart Recorder] nodeC --> nodeJ[Animated Bar Graph Display] nodeD --> nodeK[Banded Analog Gauge
Green, Yellow, Red Zones] nodeD --> nodeL[Range Indicator Lights] nodeE --> nodeM[Blinking Warning Lamps] nodeE --> nodeN[Annunciator Panels] nodeE --> nodeO[Radar and Sonar Scopes] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Human Operator's Cognitive Pathway for Dynamic Information ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Visual Stimulus
Moving Pointer or Light] --> nodeB[Sensory Register
~250 ms] nodeB --> nodeC[Pattern Recognition
Pre-attentive Motion Detection] nodeC --> nodeD{Interpretation} nodeD --> nodeE[Quantitative Read
Allocate Attention] nodeD --> nodeF[Trend Extrapolation
Project Future State] nodeD --> nodeG[Zone Classification
Safe, Caution, Danger] nodeD --> nodeH[Event Detection
Alarm or Anomaly] nodeE --> nodeI[Decision] nodeF --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ[Motor Response
Control Action] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Banded Analog Gauge Showing All Four Uses Simultaneously ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Engine Temperature Gauge] --> nodeB1[Green Zone 60 to 90 C
Safe Operating Range] nodeA --> nodeB2[Yellow Zone 90 to 105 C
Caution Range] nodeA --> nodeB3[Red Zone 105 to 120 C
Danger Range] nodeA --> nodeC[Moving Pointer
Dynamic Quantitative Value] nodeA --> nodeD[Pointer Angular Velocity
Dynamic Trend Rate] nodeA --> nodeE[Zone Colour Coding
Dynamic Check Reading] nodeA --> nodeF[Over-temperature Warning Lamp
Dynamic Event Alert] ``` **Interpretation:** A single well-designed gauge illustrates *all four* canonical uses of dynamic information simultaneously — a fact KTU examiners reward in diagram-based answers. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define dynamic information and list its four principal uses in display design. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Dynamic information is data presented on a display whose visual representation **changes with time** to reflect the variable status of a system parameter. The four principal uses are: 1. **Quantitative Reading** — obtaining a precise instantaneous value. 2. **Qualitative (Trend) Reading** — perceiving the direction and rate of change. 3. **Check Reading** — verifying whether a value lies within an acceptable range. 4. **Event Monitoring** — detecting and tracking discrete occurrences such as alarms. --- ### Question 2: Why is a moving-pointer gauge preferred over a digital numeric display for trend-reading tasks? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A moving pointer conveys **rate-of-change** through its angular velocity, which is perceived pre-attentively by the human visual system within ~30 ms. A digital display requires the operator to read two successive values, hold them in working memory, and mentally subtract. Thus, for trend tasks, the pointer is faster, requires less cognitive effort, and produces a lower error rate than the numeric display. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently list the *types* of displays but fail to *map them to the four uses*. For full marks (14/14), every display example must be explicitly tied to a specific use and supported with a quantitative justification (reading time, JND, or speedup factor). --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a)** With suitable examples, explain the **four uses of dynamic information** in Human Factors Engineering. Relate each use to a representative display type. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A nuclear power plant operator must monitor 12 process variables on a single control panel. Justify, with reference to human visual processing and the relevant reading-time equations, why **banded analog gauges combined with selective alarm lights** are preferable to a 12-line alphanumeric numeric display for this task. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — Four Uses of Dynamic Information (7 marks)** | Use | Definition | Example Display | |-----|------------|----------------| | Quantitative Reading | Read a precise instantaneous value | 7-segment digital altimeter, pointer pressure gauge | | Qualitative Trend Reading | Determine direction and rate of change | Scrolling strip-chart, moving bargraph, pointer meter | | Check Reading | Confirm value lies within an acceptable band | Banded green/yellow/red gauge | | Event Monitoring | Detect discrete occurrences | Blinking warning lamp, annunciator panel | Each use exploits a different human visual capability: focal attention for quantitative reading, motion-sensitive magnocellular pathway for trend, perceptual grouping for check-reading, and attention-capture reflex for events. **(b) — Justification of Banded Analog + Alarms over 12-line Alphanumeric (7 marks)** - **Reading-time argument:** A 12-line alphanumeric read requires $n = 12$ sequential saccades. With $t_{digit} = 0.30$ s, the minimum total time is: $$t_{scan} = 12 \times 0.30 = 3.6 \text{ s}$$ This exceeds the typical operator scan cycle (≈ 2–3 s in control rooms), guaranteeing that some variables are missed. - **Trend argument:** Numeric displays cannot convey rate-of-change at a glance, forcing mental subtraction across successive readings. The moving pointer's angular velocity directly maps to rate: $$\omega_p = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$ - **Check-reading argument:** From §3.3, the banded gauge delivers a 3.6× speedup over numeric comparison. - **Event-monitoring argument:** Alarms exploit the pre-attentive novelty reflex; an alphanumeric "TEMP HIGH" string can be missed if the operator is fixated elsewhere, but a flashing red lamp captures attention automatically. - **Conclusion:** The combination of banded analog gauges (for trend + check) and alarms (for events) is human-factor-optimal. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a)** Compare **static and dynamic displays** in terms of information content, operator task suitability, and design complexity. Provide two examples of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The reading time for a 3-digit numeric display is experimentally found to be 1.5 s, while a single analog pointer reading takes 0.5 s. A process-control operator must read 8 variables and identify which one (if any) is trending toward a dangerous limit. Calculate the **total information-acquisition time** for both display strategies and recommend the optimal configuration. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — Static vs Dynamic Comparison (7 marks)** | Criterion | Static Display | Dynamic Display | |-----------|----------------|-----------------| | Information content | Fixed labels, legends, scales | Variable values, trends, events | | Operator task | Identification, lookup | Reading, monitoring, tracking | | Design complexity | Low (printed scales, signs) | High (sensors, controllers, wiring) | | Examples | Gauge dial markings, panel labels, legends | Moving-pointer RPM gauge, ECG trace, blinking alarm | | Update mechanism | None (manual reference) | Continuous or event-driven | Static and dynamic displays are *complementary*: a gauge needs static scale markings to be readable, while the pointer provides the dynamic information. Neither is sufficient alone. **(b) — Calculation of Total Information-Acquisition Time (7 marks)** **Strategy 1 — All 8 variables on numeric displays:** $$t_{total}^{numeric} = 8 \times 1.5 = 12.0 \text{ s}$$ Within this 12 s, the operator has only *point values*. To detect a trend, two successive scans are required: $$t_{trend}^{numeric} = 2 \times 12.0 = 24.0 \text{ s}$$ **Strategy 2 — All 8 variables on analog pointers (with trend perception):** $$t_{total}^{pointer} = 8 \times 0.5 = 4.0 \text{ s}$$ Trend is perceived *within* this single scan because pointer angular velocity conveys rate-of-change pre-attentively: $$t_{trend}^{pointer} = 4.0 \text{ s}$$ **Comparison:** $$\frac{t_{numeric}}{t_{pointer}} = \frac{12.0}{4.0} = 3.0 \times \text{ (point reading)}$$ $$\frac{24.0}{4.0} = 6.0 \times \text{ (trend detection)}$$ **Recommendation:** Use analog pointers (banded with colour zones for check-reading) for all 8 variables, supplemented by dedicated alarm lights for event monitoring. This gives a 6× faster trend-detection capability — a critical advantage in process control where slow drift toward danger must be caught early. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition first.** Always begin with the formal definition of dynamic information before listing uses. - **The Four Uses** — *Quantitative, Qualitative (Trend), Check, Event*. Memorise the order and a representative example for each. - **Static ≠ Dynamic.** Static = labels, legends, fixed markings. Dynamic = values that change with time. - **Pointer vs Numeric trade-off:** Pointer wins for *trend*; numeric wins for *single-point precision*. This is a guaranteed exam question. - **Banded gauge speedup:** Numeric check-reading takes ~3.6× longer than a banded analog gauge — quote this factor. - **Flicker fusion:** 50 Hz minimum refresh, 60–75 Hz recommended for safe design. - **Pre-attentive motion:** The magnocellular visual pathway detects motion in ~30 ms — this is the *biological* reason dynamic displays work so well. - **Hick's Law:** Banded displays reduce the decision space to 3 perceptually distinct zones, lowering decision time. - **Nyquist rule:** Sample at least 2× the system's highest significant frequency for stable dynamic representation. - **Common pitfall:** Forgetting to mention that *static scale markings* are still required on dynamic instruments. KTU examiners mark this as a design omission. - **Always pair each use with a real instrument:** altimeter (quantitative), engine RPM (trend), fuel gauge (check), fire-warning lamp (event). - **Cite Sanders & McCormick** when discussing the four uses — this is the textbook source KTU expects. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute revision mantra:** *"Dynamic info shows change. Four uses: read, trend, check, alarm. Pointers trend, numbers read, bands check, lights alarm."*

topics.title: Design of Quantitative Visual Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Quantitative Visual Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Quantitative Visual Display** is any human-machine interface element that conveys precise numerical information to an operator through the visual sensory channel. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, quantitative displays present data on a continuous or discrete numerical scale, allowing the user to *read off* an exact value (e.g., a pressure reading of **142.6 kPa**) or *compare* a value against a known reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim-style):**
> Quantitative displays are visual displays designed to communicate *numerical* information, as distinct from *qualitative* displays (which show status, mode, or category) and *check-reading* or *warning* displays (which signal the presence or absence of a condition).

They are formally classified into two principal families:

| Display Family | Sub-Type | Reading Task |
|---|---|---|
| **Analog (Pointer–Scale)** | Moving pointer, fixed scale | Estimate value by interpolation between tick marks |
| **Digital (Numeric Readout)** | 7-segment, dot-matrix, LCD, LED | Absolute, discrete numeric reading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** Whenever a question asks for a *comparison* between the two, remember the examiner's golden rule — **"Pointer scales are better for TREND and RATE reading; Digital displays are better for ABSOLUTE VALUE reading."**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Speedometer vs. The Odometer

Imagine you are driving a car. The **speedometer** is a classic quantitative analog display — a needle sweeping across a numbered dial showing **km/h**. Notice three things:

1. You don't need to *read* a number to know whether you are speeding up or slowing down — the *position* of the needle on the arc tells you that intuitively.
2. But if a traffic cop asks, "Exactly how fast were you going?", you have to *interpolate* between the tick marks — your estimate is approximate.
3. Now imagine a *digital* speedometer flashing "**074 km/h**" in bright LEDs. The reading is exact, but the *trend* (accelerating vs. decelerating) is much harder to perceive at a glance.

**This is the central design trade-off in quantitative visual displays:** *Reading accuracy* (digital wins) vs. *Trend perception + situational awareness* (analog wins).

Another analogy: a **bathroom weighing scale with a swinging dial** (analog) vs. a **bathroom scale with a digital LCD readout** ("72.4 kg"). Same physical quantity, two very different perceptual tasks.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition check for students:** Think of an analog display as a *map* (you see the whole landscape, but coordinates are approximate) and a digital display as *GPS coordinates* (precise, but you cannot see the road shape at a glance). Designers choose between them based on the operator's task.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & KTU-Mandated Values

The following standard values appear frequently in KTU board questions and must be memorized:

- **Snellen visual acuity standard:** A person with 20/20 (6/6) vision can resolve a character subtending a visual angle of **5 arc-minutes** (≈ 0.0833°).
- **Preferred numeral height on a display:** roughly **16 to 24 arc-minutes** of visual angle (≈ 0.27° to 0.40°) for comfortable, error-free reading.
- **Minimum legible numeral height:** about **10 to 12 arc-minutes** (≈ 0.17° to 0.20°).
- **Pointer width (optimum):** approximately **1/10** of the scale length, but never thinner than **10 arc-minutes** of visual angle.
- **Tick mark (minor) spacing:** minimum **0.6° to 1.0°** of visual angle for unambiguous separation.
- **Maximum recommended viewing distance for a display** is governed by Snellen-derived geometry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse **arc-minutes** with **minutes of visual angle** written as "MOA." Both are the same. Also, **1° = 60 arc-minutes = 3,600 arc-seconds**. Memorize this conversion — it appears in nearly every numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operator's Reading Task — A Structured Breakdown

The KTU 2024 module identifies **three primary reading tasks** for quantitative displays. Every design decision flows from which task the operator must perform:

1. **Reading (Absolute-Value Task):** Read the value exactly. *Best served by digital displays.*
2. **Checking (Set-Point Comparison Task):** Compare displayed value to a known reference. *Best served by a fixed reference index on a moving pointer scale (e.g., a tachometer with a red-line mark).*
3. **Tracking / Trend (Rate Task):** Track changes in value over time. *Best served by moving-pointer analog displays.*

### 2.1.1 Why the task drives the technology

- A pilot monitoring *altitude* in level cruise flight → **digital** (precise readouts of "35,000 ft").
- A pilot *executing a descent* → **analog vertical-tape** (rate of change is more salient).
- A nuclear-plant operator watching *reactor power* during a transient → **analog trend recorder** (history of the value is critical).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why commercial aircraft cockpits (Glass Cockpit / EFIS) use a *hybrid* — Primary Flight Display (PFD) is a vertical-tape analog format wrapped inside a digital readout, and the Engine Indicating and Crew Alerting System (EICAS) uses both.

---

## 2.2 Design Parameters of Quantitative Displays

The KTU board exam typically asks the student to **list and explain** the design parameters. The exhaustive list is:

### A. Display Element (Pointer / Numeral) Geometry
- **Numeral height (h_n):** in mm or arc-minutes of visual angle.
- **Numeral width-to-height ratio:** typically **3 : 5** or **1 : 2** depending on font (e.g., Arial, Helvetica, Gothic).
- **Stroke width of numeral:** ≈ **1/8** to **1/6** of numeral height.
- **Pointer width (w_p):** optimum ≈ **0.1 × scale length**, but never less than the *minimum perceptible width* (≈ 1 arc-minute).
- **Pointer length (L_p):** should not extend across major tick marks; tip should rest *just outside* the scale numerals.
- **Pointer tip shape:** tapered to a point (best for precise reading) or a knife-edge (best for low-light conditions).

### B. Scale Geometry
- **Scale type:** linear, logarithmic, square-root, or non-linear.
- **Number of major divisions (N):** typically **5 to 11** for optimum reading speed and accuracy (Miller's / Cameron's research: beyond 11, reading error rises sharply).
- **Minor tick marks per major division:** usually **5** (so the smallest readable increment is 1/5 of a major division).
- **Scale length (L_s):** minimum **50 mm** for a panel-mounted display; minimum visual angle **5°** to **8°**.
- **Scale direction:** increasing values to the **right** (horizontal) or **clockwise** (circular) — these are the *cultural* defaults.
- **Zero suppression:** allowed but must be clearly indicated.

### C. Optical / Environmental Parameters
- **Illumination level (E):** measured in **lux (lx)**. Display should be readable at the design ambient illuminance (e.g., 100 lx office, 50,000 lx direct sunlight, 1 lx nighttime).
- **Contrast ratio (C):** ratio of display luminance to background luminance. Minimum legible **C = 1.5 : 1**; recommended **C ≥ 3 : 1**; for poor viewing conditions **C ≥ 10 : 1**.
- **Luminance (L):** in **cd/m²**.
- **Color coding:** red = danger, amber/yellow = caution, green = normal, blue = informational (this is an *international aviation/automotive convention*).
- **Glare control:** anti-reflective coatings, recessed bezels, filters.

### D. Task-Environment Parameters
- **Viewing distance (D):** in mm or m.
- **Viewing angle:** within **± 15°** of the display normal for accurate reading.
- **Vibration and motion:** pointer can "jitter" — may require damping or use of digital readouts.
- **Exposure time:** time the operator has to read the display (this drives the *display update rate*).

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — All board-relevant formulas for Quantitative Visual Displays. Memorize this table.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visual Angle (small-angle approx.)** | $\theta \approx \dfrac{h}{D}$ (radians) | $h$ = character height (m), $D$ = viewing distance (m) | Quick approximation when $h \ll D$ |
| 2 | **Visual Angle (exact)** | $\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2D}\right)$ | $\theta$ in radians; convert to degrees or arc-min as needed | Exact value for KTU numerical problems |
| 3 | **Numeral-height from required visual angle** | $h = 2D \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | Given required $\theta$ (e.g., 20 arc-min) and $D$ | Design of display letter size |
| 4 | **Stevens' Power Law (perceived magnitude)** | $\psi = C \cdot I^{n}$ | $\psi$ = perceived magnitude, $I$ = physical stimulus, $C$ = constant, $n$ = Stevens' exponent | Predicting how an operator will *perceive* a physical change |
| 5 | **Snellen acuity (20/20 standard)** | $\theta_{\min} = 5 \text{ arc-min}$ for letter stroke | Visual angle of critical detail | Minimum readable character |
| 6 | **Pointer-width optimum** | $w_p \approx 0.1 \cdot L_s$ | $w_p$ = pointer width, $L_s$ = scale length | Pointer geometry design |
| 7 | **Tick-mark spacing (minimum)** | $s_{\min} \approx 0.6^{\circ}$ to $1.0^{\circ}$ of visual angle | Spacing of minor ticks | Avoid tick-mark crowding |
| 8 | **Contrast ratio** | $C = \dfrac{L_{\text{foreground}}}{L_{\text{background}}}$ | $L$ = luminance (cd/m²) | Legibility assurance |
| 9 | **Reading time (approximate)** | $T \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ s per numeral}$ | For digital readouts | Display update-rate design |
| 10 | **Luminance contrast (Michelson)** | $C_M = \dfrac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$ | Bounded $0 \le C_M \le 1$ | High-contrast display design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical: KTU 2024 numerical questions almost always involve the *Visual Angle* formula (rows 1, 2, 3).** Practice converting between **radians, degrees, arc-minutes, and arc-seconds**:
> $$1 \text{ rad} = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \text{ deg} = \dfrac{180 \times 60}{\pi} \text{ arc-min} \approx 3{,}437.75 \text{ arc-min}$$

### 2.3.1 Stevens' Power Law — The "n" Values You Must Know

Stevens' Law is heavily tested in Part A (3-mark) questions. The exponent $n$ determines *how strongly* the perception changes with the physical stimulus:

| Sensory Continuum | Stevens' Exponent $n$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Brightness (visual) | ≈ 0.33 | A doubling of luminance feels like only ~26% brighter |
| Lightness / Reflectance | ≈ 1.2 (varies) | Almost linear perception |
| Length (visual) | ≈ 1.0 | Linear — the basis for linear scales |
| Area | ≈ 0.7 | Area underestimates; bar charts need calibration |
| Electric shock | ≈ 3.5 | Strongly over-estimated — safety-critical |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for KTU:** "**LEAD** = 1.0" — **L**ength has $n = 1$. All *visual-spatial* quantities (length, position, distance) are roughly linear, which is why **analog scales work as they do.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quantitative visual displays are not an academic abstraction — they underpin:
- **Aviation:** Primary Flight Display (PFD), altimeter, vertical speed indicator.
- **Process control:** SCADA dashboards, DCS consoles, nuclear control rooms.
- **Automotive:** Speedometer, tachometer, fuel gauge, battery SoC indicator.
- **Medical devices:** Patient monitor (heart rate, SpO₂, blood pressure).
- **Consumer electronics:** Thermostat, weighing scale, multimeter.

> A poorly designed quantitative display can cause *operator error* — the classic case being the **Therac-25** radiation therapy accidents, where mis-read numeric displays contributed to overdoses. Good display design is therefore a **safety-critical engineering discipline**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Visual Angle of a Display Character

### Problem Statement
A digital display has a numeral height of **$h = 8$ mm** and is viewed from a distance of **$D = 600$ mm**. Compute the visual angle subtended at the eye, in degrees and in arc-minutes.

### Step 1: Identify the geometry

The eye, the top of the numeral, and the bottom of the numeral form an isosceles triangle. The visual angle $\theta$ is the apex angle at the eye.

### Step 2: Use the exact formula

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{2D}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the values

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{8 \text{ mm}}{2 \times 600 \text{ mm}}\right)
$$

Explain: Numerator is the *full character height*; denominator is *twice the viewing distance*. The fraction inside is the half-tangent.

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{8}{1200}\right)
$$

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(0.00\overline{6}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the arctangent

Use the small-angle approximation first to cross-check:

$$
\theta_{\text{approx}} \approx \frac{h}{D} = \frac{8}{600} = 0.01333\ldots \text{ rad}
$$

Convert to degrees:

$$
\theta_{\text{approx}} = 0.01333 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.7639^{\circ}
$$

Convert to arc-minutes:

$$
\theta_{\text{approx}} = 0.7639 \times 60 = 45.84 \text{ arc-min}
$$

### Step 5: Exact value (since $h/D < 0.02$, the approximation error is below 0.01% — well within KTU tolerance)

$$
\theta_{\text{exact}} = 2 \arctan(0.006666\ldots) = 2 \times 0.38197^{\circ} = 0.7639^{\circ} \approx 45.84 \text{ arc-min}
$$

### Step 6: Compare to the KTU minimum

The KTU-recommended comfortable reading threshold is **20 arc-minutes**. Our display offers **45.84 arc-min** — comfortably above the minimum, so the display is **legible at this viewing distance.**

> [!NOTE]
> **General rule for KTU numericals:** When $h/D \le 0.1$, the small-angle approximation $\theta \approx h/D$ (in radians) gives an error of less than 1.5%, which is acceptable. For higher precision, use the exact arctangent form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Character Height from a Specified Visual Angle (Inverse Problem)

### Problem Statement
A process-control panel is to be viewed from **$D = 1.2$ m** in a normally lit control room. The designer requires that the numerals subtend a minimum of **$\theta = 20$ arc-minutes** for reliable reading. Calculate the required numeral height $h$.

### Step 1: Convert the visual angle to radians

$$
\theta = 20 \text{ arc-min} = \frac{20}{60} \text{ deg} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ deg}
$$

$$
\theta \text{ in rad} = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{540} \approx 0.005818 \text{ rad}
$$

Explain: We divide the angle in arc-minutes by 60 to get degrees, then multiply by $\pi/180$ to convert to radians.

### Step 2: Solve the visual-angle equation for $h$

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{2D}\right) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{h}{2D} = \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; h = 2D \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the values

$$
h = 2 \times 1.2 \text{ m} \times \tan\!\left(\frac{\pi/540}{2}\right)
$$

$$
h = 2.4 \times \tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{1080}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the tangent

Using the small-angle approximation since $\pi/1080$ is very small:

$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{1080}\right) \approx \frac{\pi}{1080} \approx 0.002909
$$

### Step 5: Final calculation

$$
h \approx 2.4 \times 0.002909 = 0.006982 \text{ m} \approx 6.98 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Designer's quick rule of thumb:** $h \text{ (mm)} \approx D \text{ (m)} \times 6$. For a 1.2 m viewing distance, $h \approx 7.2$ mm. This matches our exact answer of 6.98 mm within 3%. KTU examiners will accept the exact answer but the quick rule is useful for sanity checks.

### Step 6: Round up to a manufacturable size

Choose the next standard size up: **$h = 8$ mm**. This is a common KTU expected answer in design problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Applying Stevens' Power Law to Display-Design Decisions

### Problem Statement
A designer must choose between two indicator lights: one that increases its *luminance* from 10 to 20 cd/m², and one that increases its *area* (at constant luminance) from 10 to 20 cm². If both represent the *same* nominal "warning level" doubling, which indicator will the operator perceive as changing *more dramatically*?

### Step 1: Recall Stevens' Law

$$
\psi = C \cdot I^{n}
$$

The *ratio* of perceived magnitudes for a stimulus doubling from $I_1$ to $I_2 = 2 I_1$:

$$
\frac{\psi_2}{\psi_1} = \left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right)^{n} = 2^{n}
$$

### Step 2: Apply to the luminance case

Stevens' exponent for **brightness** is $n_{\text{bright}} \approx 0.33$:

$$
\frac{\psi_{\text{bright,2}}}{\psi_{\text{bright,1}}} = 2^{0.33} \approx 1.257
$$

So the operator perceives only a **25.7% increase** in brightness — a *weak* perceived change.

### Step 3: Apply to the area case

Stevens' exponent for **area** is $n_{\text{area}} \approx 0.7$:

$$
\frac{\psi_{\text{area,2}}}{\psi_{\text{area,1}}} = 2^{0.7} \approx 1.625
$$

So the operator perceives a **62.5% increase** in the area-based signal — a *stronger* perceived change.

### Step 4: Engineering conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a *safety-critical warning*, **changing the area** of an indicator is more salient than changing its brightness by the same nominal factor. Designers of warning panels should therefore use *geometric size changes* (e.g., growing an icon) rather than *brightness changes alone* when possible. This is a *classic* KTU short-answer expected conclusion.

### Step 5: General formula for any $k$-fold change

$$
\frac{\psi_{2}}{\psi_{1}} = k^{\,n}
$$

This single formula is one of the most heavily tested in KTU Part A.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reading Time and Display Update Rate

### Problem Statement
A digital multimeter has 4½ digits (4 full digits + 1 half digit). An operator must read the display during a transient lasting 2 seconds. Estimate the *minimum update rate* the display must have to allow a complete read.

### Step 1: Estimate reading time per digit

Empirical data (often cited in KTU textbooks):

$$
T_{\text{per digit}} \approx 0.5 \text{ s to } 1.0 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 2: Total reading time for 4 full digits + 1 half

$$
T_{\text{read}} = 4 \times 0.75 \text{ s (mean)} + 0.5 \times 0.75 \text{ s} = 3.0 \text{ s} + 0.375 \text{ s} = 3.375 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 3: Compare to the available 2-second window

Since $T_{\text{read}} = 3.375 \text{ s} > 2 \text{ s}$, the operator **cannot complete a full read** during the transient. The update rate *f* must be:

$$
f \ge \frac{1}{T_{\text{read}}} \approx \frac{1}{0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ s/digit} \times 4.5 \text{ digits}} \approx 0.22 \text{ Hz to } 0.44 \text{ Hz}
$$

A display update rate of at least **1 Hz** is recommended to give a margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Expectation:** The examiner will accept the conclusion as "*the display must refresh at least 1 to 2 times per second* to support comfortable reading under transient conditions.*" Always state the assumption (reading time per digit) explicitly.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Display-Type Decision Table

Use this matrix in design questions:

| Operator Task | Display-Type Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Reading** an absolute value (e.g., altitude) | **Digital** | No interpolation error |
| **Tracking** a trend (e.g., descent rate) | **Moving-pointer analog** | Operator perceives direction & rate via Stevens' $n \approx 1$ for length |
| **Checking** against a setpoint (e.g., red-line RPM) | **Fixed-scale, moving pointer with reference mark** | Deviation is read by the eye as a length difference |
| **Reading** under vibration / high G | **Digital (with damping)** | Pointer jitter on analog |
| **Reading** in low light at long distance | **Large digital (LED/LCD with backlight)** | Analog scale's fine ticks become illegible |
| **Simultaneous** read of 4+ variables | **Grouped digital (e.g., PFD strip)** | Compact, glance-readable |
| **Continuous** history (last 30 s) | **Strip-chart / trend graph** | Provides *temporal* information |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** When the question says "*an aircraft altimeter*" — think **analog (3-pointer type) for rate perception**, even though digital altimeters exist. When the question says "*a digital blood-pressure monitor*" — think **digital for absolute read-out by a nurse**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Quantitative Visual Displays

The Mermaid diagram below captures the complete taxonomy of quantitative visual displays as expected by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["QUANTITATIVE VISUAL DISPLAYS"] --> B["ANALOG DISPLAYS"]
    A --> C["DIGITAL DISPLAYS"]
    A --> D["HYBRID DISPLAYS"]

    B --> B1["Moving-Pointer Scale"]
    B --> B2["Moving-Scale Pointer"]
    B --> B3["Counter Drum"]

    B1 --> B1a["Circular Dial"]
    B1 --> B1b["Horizontal Strip"]
    B1 --> B1c["Vertical Tape"]

    C --> C1["7-Segment LED"]
    C --> C2["14-Segment LED"]
    C --> C3["Dot Matrix LCD"]
    C --> C4["Electroluminescent"]

    D --> D1["PFD Altitude Tape"]
    D --> D2["PFD Airspeed Tape"]
    D --> D3["Engine EICAS Display"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style C3 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style C4 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style D1 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style D2 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style D3 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A **Quantitative Visual Display** branches into three top-level families. The Analog family splits by *what moves* (the pointer or the scale), the Digital family splits by *display technology* (LED, LCD, EL), and the Hybrid family captures the modern aviation/automotive displays that combine the best of both.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Display Design Process

The Mermaid flow diagram below maps the *engineering design process* for a quantitative visual display, from the operator's task analysis through to the final validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["STEP 1: DEFINE OPERATOR TASK"]:::step --> P2["STEP 2: SELECT DISPLAY TYPE"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3: SPECIFY VIEWING DISTANCE D"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4: COMPUTE CHARACTER HEIGHT h"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5: DESIGN SCALE GEOMETRY"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6: SET CONTRAST AND COLOR"]
    P6 --> P7["STEP 7: VALIDATE WITH HUMAN TRIAL"]
    P7 --> P8{"PASSES LEGIBILITY TEST?"}:::decision
    P8 -- YES --> P9["FINAL DESIGN APPROVED"]:::success
    P8 -- NO --> P2

    classDef step fill:#cfe2f3,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#f4b084,color:#000000
    classDef success fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The design process is **iterative** — if the human trial at Step 7 fails, the designer loops back to Step 2 and re-selects the display type (e.g., from analog to digital). This reflects the human-centered design philosophy taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Generic Quantitative Display Subsystem

The Mermaid diagram below shows the *signal flow* from the physical variable to the operator's perception.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Physical Variable\nPressure / Temperature / Speed"]:::input --> T["Transducer / Sensor"]:::process
    T --> SIG["Electrical Signal\nVoltage / Current"]:::process
    SIG --> DRV["Display Driver\nADC + Microcontroller"]:::process
    DRV --> DSP["Display Element\nPointer or LED/LCD"]:::output
    DSP --> OP["Operator Eye\nRetina / Visual Cortex"]:::human
    OP --> BR["Perceived Value\nStevens' Law Applied"]:::human

    classDef input fill:#d9d2e9,color:#000000
    classDef process fill:#b4c7e7,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    classDef human fill:#f4b084,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The physical variable is transduced, conditioned, and converted into a *visible* element. The *operator* is explicitly modeled as a subsystem whose response is governed by Stevens' Power Law. This is the *information-processing* model underlying the KTU 2024 module.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a quantitative visual display. How does it differ from a qualitative display? Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *quantitative visual display* is a display that presents information in the form of precise numerical values on a continuous or discrete scale, allowing the operator to *measure* or *read off* a value (e.g., a digital thermometer reading "36.8 °C"). A *qualitative display* conveys only *status*, *category*, or *condition* — for example, a green/red power-on indicator or a "filter needs replacement" warning lamp. The key difference is that a quantitative display supports *measurement* (with a numerical answer), whereas a qualitative display supports *detection* (with a yes/no answer). Examples: a *pressure gauge* (quantitative) and a *cabin-pressure warning light* (qualitative).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*State Stevens' Power Law. For a doubling of the physical stimulus, what is the perceived-magnitude ratio for (i) apparent length and (ii) brightness? Comment on the design implication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stevens' Power Law states that the perceived magnitude $\psi$ of a stimulus is related to the physical intensity $I$ by $\psi = C \cdot I^{n}$, where $n$ is Stevens' exponent.
- (i) For **apparent length**, $n \approx 1.0$, so a doubling gives $\psi_2/\psi_1 = 2^{1.0} = 2.0$ — a *perfect* doubling of perceived length.
- (ii) For **brightness**, $n \approx 0.33$, so a doubling gives $\psi_2/\psi_1 = 2^{0.33} \approx 1.257$ — only a ~26% increase in perceived brightness.

**Design implication:** Length changes (e.g., a moving pointer sweeping across a scale) are perceived *linearly* and are therefore ideal for analog trend displays. Brightness changes, being compressive, are *less salient* — designers of warning systems should prefer *size* or *color* changes over *brightness* changes alone for strong perceptual impact.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Module 2, 14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare analog and digital quantitative displays. Discuss, with justification, which type is preferred for (i) a *trend-reading* task and (ii) an *absolute-value reading* task. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A process-control operator views a digital numeric display from a distance of **900 mm**. The display is required to subtend a minimum visual angle of **22 arc-minutes** for reliable reading. Calculate (i) the *required numeral height* and (ii) the *visual angle (in degrees and arc-minutes)* if the actual numeral height used is **10 mm**. State whether the chosen height is *acceptable*. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Analog (Pointer-Scale) | Digital (Numeric) |
|---|---|---|
| Reading mechanism | Interpolation between ticks | Direct absolute read |
| Reading error | ~ ± 1 minor division | ~ 0 (resolution-limited) |
| Trend perception | Excellent (Stevens' $n \approx 1$ for length) | Poor (operator must remember previous value) |
| Discrete-step perception | Poor (subtle changes hard to read) | Excellent (small changes visible) |
| Vibration tolerance | Low (pointer jitters) | High (no moving parts) |
| Illumination tolerance | Tick marks may fade in glare | LED/LCD with backlight is robust |
| Power consumption | Lower (passive scale) | Higher (active illumination) |
| Cost & complexity | Mechanical (gauges) — moderate | Electronic — moderate to high |

**Justification:**
- **(i) Trend-reading task → Analog.** The operator must perceive *rate* and *direction* of change. Stevens' Law for length is $n \approx 1.0$, so a pointer's angular displacement is perceived *linearly*. The eye-brain system integrates this effortlessly. *Example:* aircraft vertical-speed indicator.
- **(ii) Absolute-value reading task → Digital.** The operator needs the *exact* numeric value, e.g., a flight altitude of "35,000 ft." Digital readouts provide this without interpolation. *Example:* a digital voltmeter showing "12.34 V."

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "digital is always better" or "analog is always better." This will be marked **wrong** by the KTU examiner. Marks are awarded only for the *task-dependent* justification. Always pair the *task* with the *display type* explicitly.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Given**
- $D = 900 \text{ mm} = 0.9 \text{ m}$
- Required $\theta_{\min} = 22$ arc-min
- Actual $h_{\text{act}} = 10 \text{ mm}$

**Step 2: Part (i) — Required numeral height**

Convert $\theta$ to radians:

$$
\theta_{\min} = 22 \text{ arc-min} = \frac{22}{60} \text{ deg} = 0.3667^{\circ}
$$

$$
\theta_{\min} \text{ in rad} = 0.3667 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.00640 \text{ rad}
$$

Apply the visual-angle formula $h = 2D \tan(\theta/2)$:

$$
h_{\text{req}} = 2 \times 900 \text{ mm} \times \tan\!\left(\frac{0.00640}{2}\right) = 1800 \times \tan(0.00320)
$$

$$
h_{\text{req}} = 1800 \times 0.00320 \approx 5.76 \text{ mm}
$$

(The small-angle approximation $\tan(x) \approx x$ is justified here as the error is well below 0.01%.)

**Answer (i): $h_{\text{req}} \approx 5.76$ mm** (rounded up to next standard size: 6 mm).

**Step 3: Part (ii) — Visual angle of the actual 10 mm character**

Small-angle approximation:

$$
\theta_{\text{act}} \approx \frac{h_{\text{act}}}{D} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{900 \text{ mm}} = 0.01111 \text{ rad}
$$

Convert to degrees:

$$
\theta_{\text{act}} = 0.01111 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.6366^{\circ}
$$

Convert to arc-minutes:

$$
\theta_{\text{act}} = 0.6366 \times 60 = 38.20 \text{ arc-min}
$$

**Answer (ii): $\theta_{\text{act}} \approx 0.637^{\circ}$ or $38.2$ arc-min.**

**Step 4: Acceptability check**

Since $38.2 \text{ arc-min} > 22 \text{ arc-min}$ (the required minimum), the 10 mm numeral **exceeds** the design requirement by a comfortable margin. The chosen height is **acceptable** and provides a safety margin of approximately **16 arc-min** (about 73% above the minimum).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** A frequent mistake is to use $D$ in meters but $h$ in millimeters without conversion. This produces an answer that is wrong by a factor of 1000. Always keep units consistent — *convert both to mm, or both to m, before dividing*.

---

### Question B-B (Module 2, 14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **three reading tasks** that operators perform on quantitative displays. For each task, identify the *most suitable* display type and justify using Stevens' Power Law. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An aircraft altimeter is to be redesigned as a digital display. The design requirement is that the numerals subtend at least **20 arc-minutes** of visual angle at a cockpit viewing distance of **750 mm**. Compute (i) the *minimum numeral height* required and (ii) the *visual angle (in arc-minutes)* subtended if the designer chooses a standard character height of **6 mm**. State whether the standard 6 mm is acceptable. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The three reading tasks for quantitative displays are:

1. **Absolute-Value Reading Task:** The operator must read the *exact* value of the variable. Example: a pilot reading the *current altitude* in feet. **Most suitable display: Digital (numeric readout).** Stevens' Law justification: because digital readouts present the value in *symbolic form* (numerals), there is *no interpolation* — the perceived value equals the displayed value. The reading error is limited only by the display's resolution and the operator's *acuity threshold* (governed by the visual-angle formula).

2. **Set-Point Checking Task:** The operator must compare the displayed value to a *known reference* (setpoint, redline, or limit). Example: a driver checking speed against the speed limit. **Most suitable display: Analog pointer-scale with a *fixed reference mark*.** Stevens' Law justification: the deviation between pointer and reference is perceived as a *length* difference, and for length $n \approx 1.0$. So a 10 km/h deviation feels exactly twice as large as a 5 km/h deviation — this linearity is what allows reliable set-point checks.

3. **Trend / Tracking Task:** The operator must perceive the *rate* and *direction* of change in the variable. Example: a pilot monitoring the *vertical speed* (rate of climb or descent). **Most suitable display: Moving-pointer analog (e.g., a vertical-tape altimeter).** Stevens' Law justification: the *rate of pointer motion* is perceived as a *velocity* (length per unit time), and $n \approx 1.0$ for length. Hence a 1,000 ft/min rate feels exactly twice as fast as a 500 ft/min rate — supporting accurate manual control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students do not explicitly link their *display choice* to *Stevens' exponent n*. The KTU 2024 answer key specifically requires the n-value citation. Always include it.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Given**
- $D = 750 \text{ mm}$
- $\theta_{\min} = 20$ arc-min

**Step 2: Part (i) — Minimum numeral height**

Convert the visual angle:

$$
\theta_{\min} = 20 \text{ arc-min} = \frac{20}{60} \text{ deg} = 0.3333^{\circ}
$$

$$
\theta_{\min} \text{ in rad} = 0.3333 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.005818 \text{ rad}
$$

Apply $h = 2D \tan(\theta/2)$. Use the small-angle approximation $\tan(\theta/2) \approx \theta/2$:

$$
h_{\min} \approx 2 \times 750 \times \frac{0.005818}{2} = 750 \times 0.005818 = 4.364 \text{ mm}
$$

**Answer (i): $h_{\min} \approx 4.36$ mm.**

**Step 3: Part (ii) — Visual angle of 6 mm character**

Small-angle approximation:

$$
\theta_{\text{act}} \approx \frac{6 \text{ mm}}{750 \text{ mm}} = 0.00800 \text{ rad}
$$

Convert to degrees:

$$
\theta_{\text{act}} = 0.00800 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.4584^{\circ}
$$

Convert to arc-minutes:

$$
\theta_{\text{act}} = 0.4584 \times 60 = 27.50 \text{ arc-min}
$$
