Resonance 3 to 4 kHz] B --> C[MIDDLE EAR
Tympanic Membrane] C --> D[Malleus] D --> E[Incus] E --> F[Stapes Footplate] F --> G[INNER EAR
Oval Window] G --> H[Cochlear Fluid
Perilymph Travelling Wave] H --> I[Basilar Membrane
Tonotopic Filter] I --> J[Inner Hair Cells
Mechanotransduction] I --> K[Outer Hair Cells
Cochlear Amplifier] J --> L[Auditory Nerve VIII
Action Potentials] K --> I L --> M[Cochlear Nucleus] M --> N[Superior Olivary Complex] N --> O[Lateral Lemniscus] O --> P[Inferior Colliculus] P --> Q[Medial Geniculate Body] Q --> R[Primary Auditory Cortex
Perception of Sound] ``` ## 4.2 Frequency-to-Place Map on the Basilar Membrane ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BaseSide[Base of Cochlea - High Frequency End] H1[20000 Hz - near Oval Window] H2[10000 Hz] H3[5000 Hz] end subgraph MiddleThird[Mid Cochlea - Speech Band] M1[4000 Hz - Consonants] M2[2000 Hz] M3[1000 Hz - Vowel Formants] M4[500 Hz] end subgraph ApexSide[Apex of Cochlea - Low Frequency End] L1[200 Hz] L2[100 Hz] L3[20 Hz - near Helicotrema] end H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of the Conversion Engine ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph InAnalog[Analog Input Stage] S1[Sound Pressure p t in Pa] S2[Gain Block 1
Outer Ear Resonance
plus 10 to 15 dB at 3 kHz] S3[Impedance Matcher
Middle Ear Transformer
plus 27 dB Hydraulic plus Lever] end subgraph FreqAnalysis[Frequency Analysis Stage] S4[Bandpass Filter Bank
Basilar Membrane
Approx 30 Critical Bands] S5[Active Amplifier
Outer Hair Cells
Refines Q factor to 5 to 20] end subgraph Transduction[Transduction Stage] S6[Mechanically Gated Ion Channels
Stereocilia Tip Links] S7[Action Potential Encoder
Inner Hair Cell Synapse
Rate plus Temporal Code] end subgraph OutDigital[Neural Output Stage] S8[Auditory Nerve Spike Train] S9[Central Processor
Brainstem to Cortex] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution Aligned to KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** > - Part A: 3-mark short answers (definitions / derivations) > - Part B: 14-mark analytical questions with **internal choice** between two full 14-mark alternatives (each split into two 7-mark sub-parts) --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define auditory transduction. List the three anatomical divisions of the human ear with their primary function in sound conversion.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the three divisions):** Auditory transduction is the process by which mechanical sound pressure waves are converted into electrochemical neural impulses that the brain perceives as sound. The three divisions are: 1. **Outer Ear** — Pinna and auditory canal; collects and funnels sound; provides a mild resonant gain (~10–15 dB) at 2–4 kHz. 2. **Middle Ear** — Tympanic membrane and three ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes); performs impedance matching between air and cochlear fluid (~27 dB gain). 3. **Inner Ear** — Cochlea with basilar membrane and hair cells; performs frequency analysis and mechanical-to-electrical transduction. *(Examiner's note: full credit only if all three divisions are correctly matched to their engineering function.)* ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State and explain the formula for Sound Pressure Level. What is the reference pressure and why is it chosen?** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for reference, 1 for justification):** $$\text{SPL (in dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$$ where $p$ = RMS sound pressure (Pa) and $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{N/m}^2$. The reference pressure $p_0$ corresponds to the **threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz** (the softest sound a young healthy ear can detect, defined by ANSI S1.1994). It is chosen because it represents the lower physiological limit of auditory perception, allowing all audible sounds to be expressed as positive decibel values. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **complete pathway of sound conversion** from a pressure wave in air to a perceived auditory sensation. Explain the role of the middle ear as an impedance-matching transformer and quantify its gain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the relationship between **Sound Pressure Level in dB and the actual RMS pressure in Pa**. A workshop has a measured SPL of 92 dB. Calculate (i) the actual RMS pressure, and (ii) the combined SPL if a second identical machine is started. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)** **Pathway (4 marks):** 1. **Pinna** collects sound waves and aids localization. 2. **Auditory canal** resonates near 3 kHz, providing ~12 dB gain. 3. **Tympanic membrane** vibrates in response to pressure changes. 4. **Ossicular chain** (Malleus → Incus → Stapes) transmits and amplifies vibrations. 5. **Oval window** couples vibrations into cochlear fluid. 6. **Cochlear travelling wave** propagates along the basilar membrane. 7. **Basilar membrane** performs tonotopic frequency analysis. 8. **Inner hair cells** transduce mechanical shear into neural spikes. 9. **Auditory nerve (VIII)** carries action potentials to the brainstem and cortex. **Middle-ear impedance matching (3 marks):** The middle ear overcomes the ~30 dB loss that would otherwise occur at the air–fluid interface by: - **Areal (hydraulic) ratio** $A_{TM}/A_{OW} \approx 17$ → gain $20\log_{10}(17) \approx 25\ \text{dB}$. - **Ossicular lever ratio** $\approx 1.3$ → gain $20\log_{10}(1.3) \approx 2.2\ \text{dB}$. - **Total middle-ear gain ≈ 27 dB (≈ 22× pressure amplification).** --- #### **Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)** **Derivation (3 marks):** Shown in Section 3.1 above; final result: $$L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p = p_0 \cdot 10^{L/20}$$ **(i) RMS pressure for 92 dB (2 marks):** $$p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{92/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{4.6}$$ $$10^{4.6} = 10^{0.6} \times 10^{4} = 3.981 \times 10^{4} = 39\,810$$ $$p \approx 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 39\,810 \approx 0.796\ \text{Pa}$$ **(ii) Combined SPL with second identical machine (2 marks):** Two equal incoherent sources: $$L_{\text{total}} = L + 10\log_{10}(2) = 92 + 3.01 = \mathbf{95.01\ \text{dB}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Explain **Békésy's Place Theory** and the **tonotopic organization of the cochlea**. Describe how this arrangement enables the human ear to perform a real-time frequency analysis of incoming sound. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are **Equal-Loudness Contours (Fletcher–Munson curves)**? Sketch and explain the key features. A tone at 100 Hz is heard at a loudness of 40 phons. Determine its sound pressure level in dB and its loudness in sones. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)** **Place Theory (3 marks):** Proposed by Georg von Békésy (Nobel 1961), it states that the **basilar membrane behaves as a series of tuned mechanical filters**. A travelling wave, initiated at the oval window, propagates along the membrane, peaks at a position whose characteristic frequency matches the incoming sound frequency, then decays rapidly. High frequencies peak near the **base**; low frequencies peak near the **apex**. **Tonotopic Organization (2 marks):** The cochlea is mechanically and neurologically mapped such that: - Stiff, narrow regions at the base respond to **high frequencies (~20 kHz)**. - Floppy, wide regions at the apex respond to **low frequencies (~20 Hz)**. - Each inner hair cell and its associated auditory nerve fibres are tuned to a narrow frequency band (a **critical band**). **Real-Time Frequency Analysis (2 marks):** Because every frequency component of a complex sound excites a *different* location on the BM simultaneously, the cochlea acts as a **biological spectrum analyzer (bank of ~30 band-pass filters)**. The auditory nerve preserves this spatial code, allowing the brain to recover the spectral content of the sound (essential for speech intelligibility, where formants are identified by their place of maximal excitation). --- #### **Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)** **Equal-Loudness Contours (3 marks):** These are curves on a graph of SPL (y-axis) vs. frequency (x-axis) connecting all tone–frequency pairs that are perceived as **equally loud**. They were first measured by **Fletcher and Munson (1933)** and refined by **Robinson and Dadson (1956)**; the modern standard is **ISO 226:2003**. Key features: - Contours are **lowest near 3–4 kHz** (most sensitive region — coincident with ear-canal resonance). - Contours **rise sharply below 500 Hz and above 5 kHz**, showing reduced sensitivity at frequency extremes. - The **0-phon contour** is the threshold of hearing; the **120-phon contour** is the threshold of discomfort. **Loudness calculation (4 marks):** By definition, **loudness level in phons** = SPL in dB of an equally loud **1 kHz tone**. So a 40-phon tone at 100 Hz requires an SPL of approximately **52 dB** (read directly from the 40-phon contour at 100 Hz — typical textbook value; ISO 226 gives ~52 dB). **Sone conversion:** $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$, with $P = 40$ phons: $$S = 2^{(40-40)/10} = 2^{0} = 1\ \text{son}$$ > A 40-phon tone has a loudness of **1 sone** by definition (the reference sone). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These!** > 1. **Confusing dB SPL with dB HL or dB(A):** SPL is referenced to 20 µPa; HL (audiogram) is referenced to age-and-frequency-specific thresholds; A-weighting applies a filter. Mixing units ⇒ zero marks. > 2. **Using `+ 3 dB` wrongly:** Two *equal* incoherent sources add 3 dB. Two sources differing by >10 dB add ≤0.5 dB (the quieter is masked). Show the formula $L_T = 10\log_{10}(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10})$ for full credit. > 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in SPL:** Always show $I \propto p^2$ explicitly when deriving the "20" in $20\log_{10}$. > 4. **Mixing up phon and sone:** Phon is a *level* (like dB); sone is a *loudness magnitude* (1 sone = 40 phons; doubling sone = +10 phons). > 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always write Pa for pressure and dB re 20 µPa for SPL. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Three anatomical stages:** Outer (collect), Middle (impedance match), Inner (analyse & transduce). - **Reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$; **SPL formula:** $L = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$. - **Intensity uses 10, pressure uses 20** in the log; reason: $I \propto p^2$. - **Middle-ear gain:** ~25 dB (areal) + ~2.2 dB (lever) ≈ **27 dB total**. - **Place theory (Békésy):** High frequency → base, low frequency → apex of cochlea. - **Tonotopic map:** Stiff/narrow base ↔ 20 kHz; floppy/wide apex ↔ 20 Hz. - **Inner hair cells = true transducers** (95% of afferent fibres); **outer hair cells = cochlear amplifiers**. - **Equal-loudness contours** dip near 3–4 kHz (peak ear sensitivity). - **Phon** = loudness level (SPL of equally-loud 1 kHz tone); **Sone** = perceived loudness magnitude; $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$. - **Two equal incoherent sources** add **+3 dB** (not +6 dB). - **A-weighting** mimics the 40-phon contour, penalising low and high frequencies — used in occupational noise standards. - **Threshold of hearing = 0 dB SPL; Threshold of pain ≈ 120–140 dB SPL.** - **Audible band:** 20 Hz – 20 kHz; **speech band:** ~300 Hz – 4 kHz. - **Engineering applications:** hearing conservation (OSHA 85 dB(A)/8 h), MP3/AAC codecs (psychoacoustic masking), hearing aids (multichannel compression), auditorium design. - **Order of ossicles (mnemonic — "MIS"):** **M**alleus → **I**ncus → **S**tapes.

topics.title: Detection of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Detection of Signals # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Signal Detection** is the perceptual process by which a human observer identifies the presence of a meaningful stimulus (the *signal*) embedded within a background of irrelevant stimulation (the *noise*). In Human Factors Engineering, it is formally described by **Signal Detection Theory (SDT)**, which mathematically separates the observer's **sensory sensitivity** from their **response bias (decision criterion)**. Mathematically, signal detection is the resolution of a stimulus event against a probabilistic background, governed by the **overlap of two probability distributions**: - The *Noise-alone* distribution $N$ (background activity when no signal exists). - The *Signal-plus-Noise* distribution $S+N$ (background activity when the signal is present). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is the single most important engineering variable for human signal detection: > $$\text{SNR} = \frac{\text{Signal Power}}{\text{Noise Power}}$$ > When **SNR is high**, detection is easy; when **SNR approaches 1**, the human observer faces maximum ambiguity. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are standing in a noisy marketplace trying to hear your **friend call your name**. - Your friend's voice is the **signal**. - The ambient chatter, traffic, and music is the **noise**. - Your brain sets a *mental loudness threshold* — if a sound crosses it, you decide "Yes, someone called me!" If it doesn't, you ignore it. | Marketplace Reality | Human Factors Equivalent | |---|---| | Friend's voice | **Signal** | | Crowded background | **Noise** | | Mental loudness threshold | **Decision Criterion (β)** | | Your hearing sharpness | **Sensitivity (d′)** | | Whether you actually heard your name | **Hit / Miss / False Alarm** | > [!TIP] > If you *really want* to hear your name (because you are alone and bored), you set a **liberal criterion** — you'll guess "Yes" more often, leading to more **false alarms**. If you are on a secret mission and cannot afford mistakes, you set a **conservative criterion** — only loud, certain voices are accepted. ## 1.3 Taxonomy of Signals in Human Factors | Signal Modality | Typical Stimulus | Engineering Example | |---|---|---| | **Visual** | Light, color, motion | Aircraft cockpit warning lights | | **Auditory** | Sound pressure waves | Fire alarm bells, sonar pings | | **Tactile** | Vibration, pressure | Mobile phone vibration alert | | **Olfactory** | Chemical vapors | Gas-leak detection systems | | **Gustatory** | Chemical dissolution | Taste-based quality control | ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in the Module - **Absolute Threshold ($I_0$)** — the minimum stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time under controlled conditions. The unit depends on modality (lumens for light, dB SPL for sound). - **Difference Threshold (DL or JND)** — the *just noticeable difference* between two stimuli; the basis of **Weber's Law**. - **Contrast Sensitivity** — for visual signals; dimensionless ratio. - **Auditory Masking Level** — in **dB**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 of PESFT748 explicitly distinguishes between the **Classical Threshold Theory** (a single fixed threshold) and the **Signal Detection Theory** (a probabilistic, criterion-based decision). Students must be able to contrast both. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Theoretical Pillars ### 2.1.1 Classical Threshold Theory (High-Threshold Theory) Proposed by the 19th-century psychophysicists (**Fechner, 1840s–1860s**), it assumes: - There exists a single, fixed internal threshold. - Stimuli **above threshold** → always detected. - Stimuli **below threshold** → never detected. - Detection is purely a *sensory* phenomenon, not a *decision* phenomenon. > [!NOTE] > **Limitation (KTU Critical Thinking Question):** Classical theory cannot explain why the *same* observer may report "present" on one trial and "absent" on the next trial when presented with the *same* near-threshold stimulus. SDT resolves this contradiction. ### 2.1.2 Signal Detection Theory (SDT) — The Modern Framework Developed in the 1950s–1960s by **Tanner, Swets, and Green** for radar/sonar operators and adapted to human psychophysics. SDT postulates: - Detection is **always probabilistic** — even strong signals can be missed. - Two independent variables govern performance: 1. **Sensitivity ($d'$)** — how well the observer discriminates signal from noise (a sensory ability). 2. **Response Criterion ($c$ or $\beta$)** — the observer's *decision strategy* (liberal vs conservative). ## 2.2 The 2 × 2 Outcome Matrix (KTU Memory Table) | | **Signal Present** | **Signal Absent** | |---|---|---| | **Observer says "Yes"** | **Hit (H)** ✓ Correct | **False Alarm (FA)** ✗ Error | | **Observer says "No"** | **Miss (M)** ✗ Error | **Correct Rejection (CR)** ✓ Correct | From this matrix, the **conditional probabilities** used in SDT are derived: $$ H = \frac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits} + \text{Misses}} $$ $$ F = \frac{\text{False Alarms}}{\text{False Alarms} + \text{Correct Rejections}} $$ ## 2.3 Sensitivity Index — $d'$ The **sensitivity index** quantifies the distance (in standard-deviation units) between the means of the two underlying Gaussian distributions: the noise distribution $\mu_N, \sigma_N$ and the signal-plus-noise distribution $\mu_{S+N}, \sigma_{S+N}$. $$ d' = \frac{\mu_{S+N} - \mu_N}{\sigma_N} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** > - $d' = 0$ → **Chance performance** (distributions perfectly overlap — signal indistinguishable from noise). > - $d' = 1$ → **Modest detectability** (distributions separated by 1 SD). > - $d' \geq 3$ → **Excellent detection** (overlap is minimal). > - A **fully trained radar operator** typically achieves $d' \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$. In practice, $d'$ is calculated from observed hit and false-alarm rates using the standard-normal $z$-transformation: $$ d' = z(H) - z(F) $$ where $z(\cdot)$ is the inverse standard-normal CDF. ## 2.4 Response Bias — Criterion $\beta$ and $c$ ### 2.4.1 Likelihood Ratio Criterion $\beta$ Defined as the ratio of the **ordinate** (height) of the signal-plus-noise distribution to the ordinate of the noise distribution, **at the chosen criterion point $x_c$**: $$ \beta = \frac{f_{S+N}(x_c)}{f_N(x_c)} $$ - $\beta = 1$ → **Neutral criterion** (no bias). - $\beta < 1$ → **Liberal criterion** (willing to guess "Yes" — more hits *and* more false alarms). - $\beta > 1$ → **Conservative criterion** (demanding — fewer false alarms *and* fewer hits). ### 2.4.2 Criterion Location $c$ A more intuitive, directly readable measure on the internal-strength axis: $$ c = -\frac{z(H) + z(F)}{2} $$ - $c < 0$ → Liberal (criterion shifted toward noise side). - $c > 0$ → Conservative (criterion shifted toward signal side). - $c = 0$ → Neutral. ## 2.5 The ROC Curve The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curve plots **Hit Rate** against **False Alarm Rate** as the criterion $c$ is varied from very liberal to very conservative. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Origin:** ROC analysis was invented during **World War II RADAR development** (hence "Receiver Operating Characteristic") to measure how well a radar receiver could distinguish enemy aircraft (signals) from atmospheric noise. Engineers at the **Radiation Laboratory, MIT** (1943–1946) developed it. Properties of the ROC curve: - Always passes through $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$. - Higher $d'$ → curve bows toward the **upper-left** corner. - The **diagonal** represents $d' = 0$ (pure guessing). - **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** is an unbiased measure of sensitivity: $AUC = 0.5$ (chance) to $1.0$ (perfect). ## 2.6 Detection in Specific Modalities ### 2.6.1 Visual Signal Detection **Visual Acuity** is the ability to resolve fine detail. The minimum angle of resolution for a normal eye is about **1 arc-minute**. $$ \text{Visual Acuity} = \frac{1}{\text{Minimum Angle of Resolution (arc-min)}} $$ **Snellen Fraction Notation:** $$ \text{Snellen Acuity} = \frac{\text{Test Distance}}{\text{Distance at which a Normal Eye reads the same line}} $$ - 20/20 → Normal vision. - 20/200 → Legally blind (can read at 20 ft what a normal eye reads at 200 ft). **Contrast Sensitivity Function (CSF):** $$ C = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}} $$ where $L_{\max}$ and $L_{\min}$ are the luminances of the adjacent bright and dark regions. ### 2.6.2 Auditory Signal Detection **Absolute Threshold of Hearing** at 1 kHz for a young, healthy ear ≈ **0 dB SPL** (sound pressure level), which corresponds to a pressure of $20 \mu\text{Pa}$. **Hearing Level (HL) in dB:** $$ L_{\text{HL}} = L_{\text{SPL}} - L_{\text{ref}} $$ where $L_{\text{ref}}$ is the ISO 8253-1 standard reference threshold for that frequency. **Auditory Masking** occurs when the threshold for one sound is elevated by the presence of another. The **critical band** (≈ Bark scale) determines the masking bandwidth. ### 2.6.3 The Psychophysical Laws | Law | Formulation | Domain | Key Use in Detection | |---|---|---|---| | **Weber's Law** | $\Delta I / I = k$ | All senses | Defines the JND | | **Fechner's Law** | $S = k \log(I / I_0)$ | All senses (log) | Sensation magnitude | | **Stevens' Power Law** | $S = k \, I^n$ | All senses | More accurate than Fechner | For **loudness**, Stevens' exponent $n \approx 0.67$ (for a 1 kHz tone in free field). For **brightness**, $n \approx 0.33$ for a small target in the dark. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Sensitivity index from observed rates | ROC analysis | | 2 | $d' = \dfrac{\mu_{S+N} - \mu_N}{\sigma_N}$ | Sensitivity index from distributions | Theoretical derivation | | 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{f_{S+N}(x_c)}{f_N(x_c)}$ | Likelihood-ratio criterion | Decision bias | | 4 | $c = -\dfrac{z(H) + z(F)}{2}$ | Criterion location | Bias in $z$-units | | 5 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Engineering design | | 6 | $\text{Visual Acuity} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_{\min} \text{(arc-min)}}$ | Resolving power | Display design | | 7 | $C = \dfrac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$ | Contrast sensitivity | Visual display contrast | | 8 | $\text{Weber Fraction} = \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | JND ratio | Discrimination tasks | | 9 | $S = k \log(I / I_0)$ | Fechner's law | Sensation scaling | | 10 | $S = k \, I^n$ | Stevens' power law | Sensation scaling | | 11 | $L_{\text{HL}} = L_{\text{SPL}} - L_{\text{ref}}$ | Hearing level in dB | Audiometry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on units:** The Weber fraction $k$ is **dimensionless**. Visual acuity is expressed in **arc-minutes** of resolution. Auditory thresholds are universally expressed in **decibels Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL)** or **dB HL**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: From Probability Distributions to $d'$ We start from the assumption that both the noise-evidence variable $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_N, \sigma_N^2)$ and the signal-plus-noise variable $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{S+N}, \sigma_{S+N}^2)$ are normally distributed. **Step 1 — Normalize both distributions to unit variance.** Define the standardized variables: $$ Z_N = \frac{X - \mu_N}{\sigma_N}, \qquad Z_{S+N} = \frac{X - \mu_{S+N}}{\sigma_{S+N}} $$ **Step 2 — Place the criterion at value $x_c$ on the original axis.** The corresponding $z$-values on the two standardized axes are: $$ z_N(x_c) = \frac{x_c - \mu_N}{\sigma_N}, \qquad z_{S+N}(x_c) = \frac{x_c - \mu_{S+N}}{\sigma_{S+N}} $$ **Step 3 — Express Hit Rate and False Alarm Rate as tail probabilities.** A "Hit" occurs when the signal-plus-noise sample exceeds $x_c$, i.e.: $$ H = P(X > x_c \mid S+N) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(z_{S+N}(x_c)\right) = \Phi\!\left(-z_{S+N}(x_c)\right) $$ A "False Alarm" occurs when the noise sample exceeds $x_c$: $$ F = P(X > x_c \mid N) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(z_N(x_c)\right) = \Phi\!\left(-z_N(x_c)\right) $$ **Step 4 — Invert using the standard-normal inverse CDF $\Phi^{-1}$.** Apply $\Phi^{-1}$ to both sides: $$ \Phi^{-1}(F) = -z_N(x_c) = -\frac{x_c - \mu_N}{\sigma_N} $$ $$ \Phi^{-1}(H) = -z_{S+N}(x_c) = -\frac{x_c - \mu_{S+N}}{\sigma_{S+N}} $$ **Step 5 — Subtract to eliminate $x_c$.** Subtract the second equation from the first: $$ \Phi^{-1}(H) - \Phi^{-1}(F) = \frac{x_c - \mu_{S+N}}{\sigma_{S+N}} - \frac{x_c - \mu_N}{\sigma_N} $$ If we assume $\sigma_N = \sigma_{S+N} = \sigma$ (equal variance — the *equal-variance SDT* assumption, the most common KTU assumption): $$ z(H) - z(F) = \frac{\mu_{S+N} - \mu_N}{\sigma} $$ **Step 6 — Identify the right-hand side as $d'$.** By definition: $$ d' = \frac{\mu_{S+N} - \mu_N}{\sigma_N} $$ Hence the derivation is complete: $$ \boxed{\,d' = z(H) - z(F)\,} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Calculate $d'$ from Observed Outcomes > **Problem:** A quality-control inspector examines 1000 products. Of these, 500 contain a defect (signal present) and 500 are defect-free (signal absent). She reports "defect" for 420 of the 500 defective items and for 80 of the 500 defect-free items. Calculate her sensitivity index $d'$ and comment on her performance. **Step 1 — Compute Hit Rate.** $$ H = \frac{420}{500} = 0.84 $$ **Step 2 — Compute False Alarm Rate.** $$ F = \frac{80}{500} = 0.16 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the $z$-transformation** (using standard-normal tables): For $H = 0.84$: $\;z(0.84) = \Phi^{-1}(0.84) = 0.9945$ For $F = 0.16$: $\;z(0.16) = \Phi^{-1}(0.16) = -0.9945$ **Step 4 — Compute $d'$.** $$ d' = z(H) - z(F) = 0.9945 - (-0.9945) = 1.989 \approx 1.99 $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** A $d' \approx 2.0$ is the performance benchmark of a **trained radar operator**. The inspector discriminates well above chance but is not perfect. Engineering recommendation: **improve the inspection lighting and contrast** to raise $d'$ above 2.5. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Calculate Response Bias $\beta$ Using the same inspector's data ($H = 0.84$, $F = 0.16$, criterion $x_c$ assumed to be at the midpoint between the two distribution means). **Step 1 — Compute the criterion location in $z$-units (equal-variance SDT).** $$ c = -\frac{z(H) + z(F)}{2} = -\frac{(0.9945) + (-0.9945)}{2} = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\beta$ using the standard-normal density $\phi(\cdot)$.** At $z_c = 0$, the noise density is $\phi(0) = 0.3989$. The signal-plus-noise density at $z_c = 0$ corresponds to the noise density shifted by $-d'$, i.e. $\phi(-d') = \phi(-1.99) = \phi(1.99) = 0.0549$. $$ \beta = \frac{f_{S+N}(x_c)}{f_N(x_c)} = \frac{\phi(-1.99)}{\phi(0)} = \frac{0.0549}{0.3989} \approx 0.138 $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** Since $\beta < 1$, the inspector is using a **liberal criterion** — she tends to report "defect" generously. This explains her high false alarm rate (16%). Engineering recommendation: **institute a verification protocol** to push her toward a more conservative criterion (e.g., $\beta = 1$). ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Fechner's Law Applied to a Visual Signal A control-room operator must detect a warning light of intensity $I = 100$ candela. Her absolute threshold for the same wavelength is $I_0 = 1$ candela, and Fechner's constant is $k = 10$. **Step 1 — Substitute into Fechner's Law.** $$ S = k \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Compute the ratio.** $$ \frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{100}{1} = 100 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the sensation magnitude.** $$ S = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = 20 \;\text{sensation units} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** A **tenfold increase** in physical intensity produces only a **doubling** in subjective sensation — the hallmark logarithmic compression of human perception. To double sensation again, the engineer must increase $I$ by **another factor of 100**. ## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Weber's Law for Auditory Pitch Discrimination A tone of frequency $f = 1000$ Hz is to be discriminated from a comparison tone. The Weber fraction for pitch is $k = 0.003$ (i.e., 0.3%). **Step 1 — Apply Weber's Law.** $$ \Delta f = k \cdot f = 0.003 \times 1000 = 3 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation.** A normal listener can just detect a pitch change from **1000 Hz to 1003 Hz**. For musical instrument tuning (e.g., $A_4 = 440$ Hz), the JND is only $440 \times 0.003 \approx 1.3$ Hz — which is why precision tuning requires electronic reference tones. ## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Auditory Threshold in dB HL A patient produces a behavioral response at 30 dB SPL on a 2 kHz audiometric test. The ISO 8253-1 reference threshold at 2 kHz is 9 dB SPL. **Step 1 — Convert SPL to HL.** $$ L_{\text{HL}} = L_{\text{SPL}} - L_{\text{ref}} = 30 - 9 = 21 \text{ dB HL} $$ **Step 2 — Clinical interpretation.** A hearing level of 21 dB HL is at the borderline of normal hearing (0–25 dB HL = normal). The patient's threshold is only mildly elevated. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Signal Detection Processing Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[STIMULUS
SOURCE] --> S2[PHYSICAL
CHANNEL
air, light, vibration] S2 --> S3[RECEPTOR
eye, ear, skin] S3 --> S4[NEURAL
TRANSDUCTION
signal and noise] S4 --> S5[CENTRAL
PROCESSING
cortex] S5 --> S6{DECISION
CRITERION} S6 -- exceeds criterion --> S7[YES RESPONSE
signal detected] S6 -- below criterion --> S8[NO RESPONSE
signal absent] S9[RESPONSE BIAS
beta and c] -.-> S6 S10[SENSITIVITY
d prime] -.-> S5 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two Overlapping Distributions (SDT Concept) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DISTRIBUTIONS N[NOISE DISTRIBUTION
mean equals muN
std dev equals sigmaN] SN[SIGNAL PLUS NOISE
mean equals muS plus N
std dev equals sigma] end N -. overlap region .-> SN CRIT[CRITERION XC
vertical line] -. cuts both .-> N CRIT -. cuts both .-> SN N --> A1[Area to LEFT of XC under N equals Correct Rejection rate] N --> A2[Area to RIGHT of XC under N equals False Alarm rate] SN --> B1[Area to RIGHT of XC under SN equals Hit rate] SN --> B2[Area to LEFT of XC under SN equals Miss rate] ``` ## 4.3 ROC Curve Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR ROC[ROC CURVE
Hit Rate on Y axis
False Alarm Rate on X axis] A1[chance line diagonal
d prime equals zero] -.-> ROC A2[good performance
curve bows to upper left corner
d prime equals 2] -.-> ROC A3[perfect performance
curve hugs top and left edges
d prime goes to infinity] -.-> ROC ROC --> P1[Point A conservative criterion
low H and low F] ROC --> P2[Point B neutral criterion
H plus F around 0.5] ROC --> P3[Point C liberal criterion
high H and high F] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Outcome Matrix in SDT ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Stimulus Presented to Observer] --> Q1{Is the SIGNAL
actually present?} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Does observer
respond YES?} Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Does observer
respond YES?} Q2 -- YES --> OUT1[HIT
correct detection] Q2 -- NO --> OUT2[MISS
Type II error] Q3 -- YES --> OUT3[FALSE ALARM
Type I error] Q3 -- NO --> OUT4[CORRECT REJECTION
correct absence response] ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Engineering Design Implications ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG[ENGINEER DESIGNS
SIGNAL AND DISPLAY] --> INC[INCREASE SIGNAL
AMPLITUDE] ENG --> DEC[REDUCE NOISE
better shielding] ENG --> CUE[ADD REDUNDANT CUES
visual plus auditory] INC --> D[IMPROVES d prime
shifts SN right] DEC --> D CUE --> D D --> OUT[ENHANCED HUMAN
DETECTION PERFORMANCE] OUT --> SAFE[SAFER and MORE
EFFICIENT SYSTEM] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** All five block diagrams together form a complete narrative — from the physical stimulus through neural processing, through the decision criterion (governed by bias), to the engineering levers available to the designer for *increasing $d'$* and *shaping the response bias* for safer human performance. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define Signal Detection Theory. How does it differ from the classical high-threshold theory of detection?** **Model Answer:** **Signal Detection Theory (SDT)** is a probabilistic framework in human factors engineering that explains the detection of a signal embedded in noise as a joint function of the observer's **sensory sensitivity** (measured by the index $d'$) and **response bias** (measured by $\beta$ or $c$). It treats detection as a **decision under uncertainty**. **Differences from Classical Theory:** | Aspect | Classical Threshold Theory | Signal Detection Theory | |---|---|---| | Nature of threshold | Fixed, all-or-none | Probabilistic, varies with criterion | | Detection of same stimulus | Deterministic (always detected or never) | May be detected on one trial, missed on another | | Explains false alarms | Impossible by definition | Possible due to noise crossing criterion | | Components modeled | Only sensory | Sensory *plus* decision (bias) | | Empirical basis | 19th-century psychophysics | 20th-century radar research | (3 marks) --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the term "ROC curve" and mention its significance in human factors engineering.** **Model Answer:** **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve** is the plot of **Hit Rate (H)** on the ordinate versus **False Alarm Rate (F)** on the abscissa, as the observer's decision criterion $c$ is systematically varied from very liberal to very conservative. **Significance:** 1. **Separates sensitivity from bias:** The shape of the curve encodes $d'$, while any single point on it encodes the criterion $\beta$. 2. **Performance benchmarking:** The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** provides a bias-free measure of detectability; $AUC = 0.5$ means chance, $AUC = 1.0$ means perfect. 3. **Historical use:** Developed during WWII to optimize radar operator performance. 4. **Modern use:** Diagnostic testing, vigilance studies, quality control, aviation safety. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define the **sensitivity index $d'$** and the **response bias $\beta$**. Derive the relationship $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ from the basic SDT assumptions. (7 marks) **(b)** A radiologist screens 800 mammograms. Of these, 200 contain a confirmed malignant lesion. She correctly identifies 170 of the 200 malignant cases and incorrectly flags 60 of the 600 benign cases. Compute her $d'$ and $\beta$ and interpret both. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Definition of $d'$:** The sensitivity index $d'$ measures how well an observer can discriminate between the noise distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu_N, \sigma^2)$ and the signal-plus-noise distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu_{S+N}, \sigma^2)$. It is the standardized distance between the two means: $$ d' = \frac{\mu_{S+N} - \mu_N}{\sigma} $$ Higher $d'$ implies better discrimination; $d' = 0$ implies chance. **Definition of $\beta$:** The response bias $\beta$ is the likelihood ratio at the chosen criterion $x_c$: $$ \beta = \frac{f_{S+N}(x_c)}{f_N(x_c)} $$ $\beta = 1$ → neutral; $\beta < 1$ → liberal; $\beta > 1$ → conservative. **Derivation** (full step-by-step reproduced from Section 3.1 above, with equal-variance SDT assumption): Starting from the standardized variables $Z_N = (X - \mu_N)/\sigma$ and $Z_{S+N} = (X - \mu_{S+N})/\sigma$, the hit rate and false alarm rate are: $$ H = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{x_c - \mu_{S+N}}{\sigma}\right), \qquad F = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{x_c - \mu_N}{\sigma}\right) $$ Applying $\Phi^{-1}$: $$ z(H) = -\frac{x_c - \mu_{S+N}}{\sigma}, \qquad z(F) = -\frac{x_c - \mu_N}{\sigma} $$ Subtracting: $$ z(H) - z(F) = \frac{\mu_{S+N} - \mu_N}{\sigma} = d' $$ QED. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Compute the hit rate.** $$ H = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the false alarm rate.** $$ F = \frac{60}{600} = 0.10 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the $z$-transformation.** $$ z(H) = z(0.85) = 1.0364 $$ $$ z(F) = z(0.10) = -1.2816 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $d'$.** $$ d' = 1.0364 - (-1.2816) = 2.3180 $$ **Step 5 — Compute the criterion $c$.** $$ c = -\frac{z(H) + z(F)}{2} = -\frac{1.0364 + (-1.2816)}{2} = -\frac{-0.2452}{2} = 0.1226 $$ **Step 6 — Compute $\beta$.** At $z_c = c = 0.1226$, the noise density is $\phi(0.1226) = 0.3960$. The signal-plus-noise density at this criterion is $\phi(0.1226 - d') = \phi(0.1226 - 2.3180) = \phi(-2.1954) = \phi(2.1954) = 0.0357$. $$ \beta = \frac{0.0357}{0.3960} \approx 0.0902 $$ **Step 7 — Interpretation.** - $d' \approx 2.32$ indicates **good discriminative ability** — the radiologist reliably distinguishes malignant from benign tissue. - $\beta \approx 0.09 \ll 1$ indicates a **strongly liberal criterion** — she is biased toward saying "malignant." This is acceptable for *screening* (minimizing misses is critical) but would need to be tempered by biopsy confirmation before any clinical action. (7 marks) --- ### **Question B — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the concepts of **absolute threshold**, **difference threshold (JND)**, and **Weber's Law**. Discuss how each applies to the design of industrial warning signals. (7 marks) **(b)** A control panel uses a green indicator lamp of luminous intensity $I = 200$ candela. An operator's absolute threshold for green at the prevailing adaptation level is $I_0 = 2$ candela, with Fechner's constant $k = 8$. Compute the operator's sensation magnitude using (i) Fechner's law and (ii) Stevens' power law with $n = 0.33$. Compare the two predictions. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Absolute threshold ($I_0$):** The minimum stimulus intensity that can be detected 50% of the time under controlled viewing/auditory conditions. For industrial warning signals, the design must place the signal at a level **comfortably above** $I_0$ (typically 10–100× above threshold) to ensure reliable detection. **Difference threshold (JND or DL):** The smallest change in a stimulus that an observer can reliably detect. Defined by **Weber's Law**: $$ \frac{\Delta I}{I} = k $$ where $k$ is the Weber fraction (constant within a given modality and intensity range). For example, $k_{\text{luminance}} \approx 0.01$, $k_{\text{pitch}} \approx 0.003$, $k_{\text{weight lifted}} \approx 0.02$. **Application to industrial warning signals:** 1. **Audibility above noise:** Warning alarms must exceed the ambient noise by at least the JND for loudness ($k_{\text{loudness}} \approx 0.10$). OSHA requires alarms to be at least **15 dB** above ambient. 2. **Visibility above background:** Visual signals (warning lights) must have luminance **at least 1.5×** the surrounding ambient luminance to be reliably detected. 3. **Multi-modal redundancy:** Pairing an auditory warning with a visual warning capitalizes on the lower Weber fractions of the visual system for certain stimulus classes. 4. **Just-noticeable difference in frequency:** Pitch-coded alarms are only effective if the operator can discriminate them from the ambient spectrum — design should ensure pitch differences $\Delta f > k_{\text{pitch}} \times f$. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Given:** $I = 200$ cd, $I_0 = 2$ cd, $k = 8$, $n = 0.33$. **(i) Fechner's Law:** $$ S_{\text{Fechner}} = k \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) $$ Substitute: $$ S_{\text{Fechner}} = 8 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200}{2}\right) = 8 \log_{10}(100) = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ sensation units} $$ **(ii) Stevens' Power Law:** $$ S_{\text{Stevens}} = k \, I^n $$ Substitute: $$ S_{\text{Stevens}} = 8 \times (200)^{0.33} $$ Compute $(200)^{0.33}$: $$ \log_{10}(200) = 2.3010 $$ $$ 0.33 \times 2.3010 = 0.7593 $$ $$ 10^{0.7593} \approx 5.747 $$ Therefore: $$ S_{\text{Stevens}} = 8 \times 5.747 \approx 45.98 \text{ sensation units} $$ **Comparison:** | Method | Result | Comment | |---|---|---| | Fechner | 16 units | Logarithmic compression — assumes every JND is equally perceptible | | Stevens | ~46 units | Power-law — assumes subjective magnitude is a power of physical intensity | Stevens' prediction is roughly **2.9× higher** than Fechner's for this stimulus. Stevens' Power Law is empirically more accurate for **brightness** estimation tasks; Fechner's Law is convenient for **threshold-related** engineering calculations. For *design* of indicator brightness, Stevens' law with $n = 0.33$ is recommended. (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing $d'$ with $\beta$:** $d'$ is *sensitivity* (a property of the sensory system), $\beta$ is *bias* (a property of the decision strategy). Mixing them up costs full marks. > 2. **Forgetting the equal-variance assumption:** The formula $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ is valid *only* under the assumption that $\sigma_N = \sigma_{S+N}$. If the problem states unequal variances, the formula must be adapted using likelihood ratios. > 3. **Using raw counts instead of rates in the $z$-transformation:** Many students plug 170 directly into $\Phi^{-1}$ instead of computing $H = 170/200 = 0.85$. Always **normalize to a rate between 0 and 1** first. > 4. **Mixing up JND and absolute threshold:** The JND is a *change*; the absolute threshold is a *single minimum*. Both involve the word "threshold" but they are different concepts. > 5. **Forgetting units in auditory problems:** Always state whether the answer is in **dB SPL** or **dB HL** — they differ by the ISO reference threshold. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Detection of Signals (Module 2)** - **Signal Detection Theory (SDT)** models detection as a decision under uncertainty, governed by **sensitivity ($d'$)** and **response bias ($\beta$ or $c$)**. - The **2 × 2 outcome matrix** yields four categories: **Hit, Miss, False Alarm, Correct Rejection**. - **Hit Rate** $H = \text{Hits} / (\text{Hits} + \text{Misses})$. - **False Alarm Rate** $F = \text{False Alarms} / (\text{False Alarms} + \text{Correct Rejections})$. - **Sensitivity index** $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ — under the equal-variance SDT assumption. - **Bias $\beta$** is the likelihood ratio at the criterion; **bias $c$** is the criterion location in $z$-units. - The **ROC curve** plots H vs F; the **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** is a bias-free sensitivity measure. - **Visual acuity** is the reciprocal of the minimum resolvable angle in arc-minutes; **Snellen fraction** $20/x$ means the subject reads at 20 ft what a normal eye reads at $x$ ft. - **Contrast sensitivity** $C = (L_{\max} - L_{\min}) / (L_{\max} + L_{\min})$. - **Absolute threshold $I_0$** = minimum stimulus detectable 50% of the time. - **Difference threshold (JND)** follows **Weber's Law**: $\Delta I / I = k$. - **Fechner's Law:** $S = k \log(I / I_0)$ — logarithmic compression of sensation. - **Stevens' Power Law:** $S = k I^n$ — empirically more accurate; $n \approx 0.33$ for brightness, $n \approx 0.67$ for loudness. - **Auditory threshold** is referenced to **0 dB SPL at 1 kHz**; **dB HL** = dB SPL minus ISO reference. - **Auditory masking** raises the threshold of one sound due to the presence of another within the **critical band**. - **Engineering levers** to improve detection: increase signal amplitude, reduce noise (improve SNR), use multi-modal redundant cues. - **Liberal criterion ($\beta < 1$):** More hits but more false alarms — good for screening. - **Conservative criterion ($\beta > 1$):** Fewer false alarms but more misses — good for high-cost-of-error decisions.

topics.title: Relative Discrimination and Absolute Identification of Auditory Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Relative Discrimination and Absolute Identification of Auditory Signals # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Relative Discrimination** refers to the human ear's ability to detect a *difference* between two auditory stimuli — for example, telling that one tone is louder, higher in pitch, or longer in duration than another. The smallest detectable difference is called the **Difference Threshold** or **Just Noticeable Difference (JND)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Absolute Identification** refers to the human ability to recognize and label an auditory stimulus *without any reference standard* — for example, identifying "this is 1000 Hz" or "this is 60 dB" purely from memory and judgment. It depends on **Absolute Judgment** and is fundamentally limited by working-memory capacity. **Two fundamental psychophysical thresholds:** | Term | Symbol | Definition | |------|--------|------------| | **Absolute Threshold** | $I_0$ | Minimum stimulus intensity detectable 50 % of the time in the absence of a reference stimulus. | | **Difference Threshold (JND)** | $\Delta I$ | Minimum change in a stimulus that a person can detect 50 % of the time when a *standard* stimulus is present. | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are standing in your college canteen: * **Absolute Threshold** — The faintest whisper of a friend calling your name from across the room. Below this whisper, you literally cannot hear anything. * **Relative Discrimination (JND)** — Your friend is already talking to you. They raise their voice *slightly*. **Relative Discrimination** is your ability to detect that "Yes, they are now speaking louder than before." If they raise it too little, you won't notice — that "too little" amount is the JND. * **Absolute Identification** — You are blindfolded. Your friend plays 5 different WhatsApp notification tones one by one. **Absolute Identification** is your ability to say, *"That one was the default tone," "That was the dog tone,"* etc. without any reference clip. Notice: you can probably identify only **5–7** distinct tones correctly — not 50. This is the famous Miller limit. --- ## 1.3 Why This Matters in Human Factors Engineering In control rooms, cockpits, hospitals, and automobiles, auditory signals must be: 1. **Detectable** → absolute threshold below signal intensity. 2. **Discriminable** → JND between competing alerts must be large enough. 3. **Identifiable** → operator must instantly recognize the meaning without a reference (absolute identification). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard auditory reference values used in KTU problems:** > * Threshold of hearing at 1000 Hz: $I_0 = 10^{-12} \text{ W/m}^2$ (intensity reference). > * Reference sound pressure: $p_0 = 20 \text{ μPa}$. > * **Weber fraction for loudness**: $k \approx 0.10$ at moderate intensities. > * **Miller's magical number**: $7 \pm 2$ absolute judgments across multi-dimensional stimuli. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Weber's Law — The Heart of Relative Discrimination **E.H. Weber's Law** states that the JND is a *constant fraction* of the standard stimulus: $$\Delta I = k \cdot I$$ Dividing both sides by $I$: $$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k = \text{constant}$$ The ratio $k$ is called the **Weber fraction** (sometimes $\Delta I / I$ is itself called the JND). > [!NOTE] > **Fechner's integration of Weber's Law:** If Weber's fraction is constant, then sensation grows as the *logarithm* of stimulus magnitude. This produces **Fechner's Law**: > $$S = K \cdot \log\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ > where $S$ is the sensation magnitude and $K$ is a scaling constant. This is the theoretical basis of the **decibel scale**. --- ## 2.2 Discrimination in Three Auditory Dimensions ### (a) Loudness Discrimination (Intensity) At moderate listening levels, the Weber fraction is approximately **0.10**, meaning a 10 % change in sound intensity is just noticeable. At very low or very high intensities, the fraction increases (violation of Weber's Law at extremes). ### (b) Pitch Discrimination (Frequency) For pure tones, the JND in frequency depends on the centre frequency. Empirical rule: $$\Delta F \approx 0.003 \cdot F \quad (\text{around } 1000 \text{ Hz})$$ This means at 1000 Hz you can detect a change of about **3 Hz**, but at 100 Hz the JND is only ~0.6 Hz in proportional terms. ### (c) Duration Discrimination For tones between 100 ms and 2 s, the JND in duration is roughly: $$\Delta T \approx 0.10 \cdot T$$ --- ## 2.3 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) — The Practical Detection Metric In real environments, auditory signals must be detected against background noise. Detection depends on: $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = L_{\text{signal}} - L_{\text{noise}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Speech intelligibility requires roughly **SNR ≥ 6 dB** for reliable communication; warning signals in industry should be at least **10–15 dB** above ambient noise. --- ## 2.4 Absolute Identification — Miller's Law **George A. Miller (1956)** showed that absolute identification is limited by working memory: $$\text{Channel Capacity} \approx 7 \pm 2 \text{ categories}$$ * For **unidimensional** stimuli (e.g., tones varying *only* in pitch): about **3–4 categories**. * For **multidimensional** stimuli (pitch × loudness × duration × location): up to **7±2 categories**. Identification accuracy drops sharply when categories exceed this limit because confusions increase. --- ## 2.5 Signal Detection Theory (SDT) — Modern Extension Classical psychophysics cannot separate *sensitivity* from *bias*. **SDT** introduces two independent parameters: **Sensitivity index $d'$:** $$d' = z(H) - z(F)$$ **Response bias $\beta$:** $$\beta = \frac{f_{\text{noise}}}{f_{\text{signal}}}$$ where $H$ = hit rate, $F$ = false-alarm rate, $z$ = inverse standard-normal CDF, and $f$ denotes the probability density. > [!NOTE] > A high $d'$ = sensitive listener; a $\beta \neq 1$ indicates a systematic bias (cautious or reckless responder). --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---------|---------|----------------------| | 1 | Weber's Law (general) | $\Delta I / I = k$ | $k \approx 0.10$ for loudness | | 2 | Fechner's Law | $S = K \log(I/I_0)$ | Sensation ∝ log intensity | | 3 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log(I/I_0)$ dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | | 4 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log(p/p_0)$ dB | $p_0 = 20$ μPa | | 5 | Pitch JND | $\Delta F \approx 0.003\,F$ | ~3 Hz at 1 kHz | | 6 | Duration JND | $\Delta T \approx 0.10\,T$ | 10 % of duration | | 7 | SNR | $\text{SNR} = L_s - L_n$ | Speech ≥ 6 dB | | 8 | Miller's Law | Capacity $= 7 \pm 2$ | Absolute judgments | | 9 | Sensitivity (SDT) | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Higher = more sensitive | | 10 | Bias (SDT) | $\beta = f_n / f_s$ | $\beta = 1$ = unbiased | > [!WARNING] > When writing the Weber ratio inside a table or inline text, **never use the vertical bar `|`** as it breaks markdown. Use $\Delta I \big/ I$ or "ΔI/I" instead. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | **Aviation** | Distinct CVR warnings differ in **pitch, rhythm & location** to overcome absolute-identification limits. | | **Hospital ICU** | Alarm tones spaced ≥ 15 dB apart and harmonically distinct so nurses can identify them in noise. | | **Automotive** | Turn-signal "tick" is 10 dB above cabin noise — easily discriminable. | | **Industrial control** | Buzzers chosen so JND in loudness is exceeded by at least 3 dB so operators cannot confuse alerts. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Weber's Law to the Decibel Scale **Starting premise:** A just-noticeable change in intensity $\Delta I$ is a constant fraction of the original intensity $I$. $$\Delta I = k \cdot I$$ Treat this as a differential equation for the smallest detectable increment at intensity $I$: $$dI = k \cdot I$$ Separate variables: $$\frac{dI}{I} = k$$ Integrate both sides between an absolute threshold $I_0$ and the current stimulus $I$: $$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = \int_{0}^{S} k \cdot dS$$ The right-hand integral yields sensation $S$ directly (Fechner's postulate that equal increments in $\Delta I$ produce equal increments in sensation): $$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = k \cdot S$$ Simplify: $$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = k \cdot S$$ Convert natural log to log base 10 using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$2.303 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = k \cdot S$$ Rearrange to obtain **Fechner's Law**: $$S = \frac{2.303}{k} \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ Defining the constant $K = 2.303/k$ and choosing a reference intensity $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² gives the **Sound Intensity Level** in **decibels**: $$L_I \;[\text{dB}] = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ > **Logic of step-by-step conversion:** Weber's empirical law → differential form → separation of variables → integration against sensation scale → change of logarithm base → identification with the standard decibel definition used in acoustics. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — JND in Loudness > **Problem:** A 1000 Hz tone is presented at 60 dB SPL. Using Weber's fraction $k = 0.10$, find the JND in intensity (W/m²) and in dB. **Step 1.** Convert 60 dB SPL to absolute intensity: $$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{L/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{60/10}$$ $$I = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{6} = 10^{-6} \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 2.** Apply Weber's law with $k = 0.10$: $$\Delta I = k \cdot I = 0.10 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\Delta I = 10^{-7} \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 3.** Compute the JND in dB form: $$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I + \Delta I}{I}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1.10\,I}{I}\right)$$ $$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(1.10)$$ $$\Delta L \approx 10 \times 0.0414 = 0.414 \text{ dB}$$ > **Conclusion:** A 60 dB tone needs to increase by about **0.4 dB** to be just noticeably louder. This is the famous **1-dB rule-of-thumb** engineers use when designing audio gain stages. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Pitch JND > **Problem:** Find the JND in frequency for a 2000 Hz pure tone using $\Delta F \approx 0.003\,F$. **Step 1.** Substitute: $$\Delta F = 0.003 \times 2000 = 6 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2.** Express as a musical interval in **cents** (1200 cents = 1 octave): $$1 \text{ cent} = 2^{1/1200} - 1 \approx 0.000578$$ $$\text{Interval (cents)} = 1200 \log_2\left(\frac{F + \Delta F}{F}\right)$$ $$\text{Interval} = 1200 \log_2(1.003) \approx 1200 \times 0.00432 \approx 5.2 \text{ cents}$$ > **Conclusion:** Trained musicians can resolve pitch differences of ~5 cents — perfectly consistent with the Weber-fraction approach. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Absolute Identification Capacity > **Problem:** A designer wants to use auditory alerts differing only in **pitch** (unidimensional). How many distinct tones should she use? **Step 1.** Apply Miller's experimental finding for unidimensional auditory stimuli. > [!IMPORTANT] > Empirical studies (Pollack 1952, Miller 1956) show that for tones varying in **only one** attribute (e.g., pitch), absolute identification plateaus at about **3 to 4 categories**. **Step 2.** Compare with multidimensional coding. If each tone differs in *pitch + duration + location*: $$\text{Effective capacity} \approx 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27 \text{ distinguishable alerts}$$ > **Conclusion:** Unidimensional coding caps at ~4. To exceed this, designers must add redundant dimensions (e.g., temporal pattern, spatial origin) — a foundational principle of cockpit auditory alerting. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Signal Detection Theory Calculation > **Problem:** In a noise-monitoring task, an operator correctly detects 80 of 100 signals (hits) and falsely reports 10 of 100 noise trials (false alarms). Compute $d'$ and comment on sensitivity. **Step 1.** Compute hit rate and false-alarm rate: $$H = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80, \quad F = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10$$ **Step 2.** Convert to z-scores: $$z(H) = z(0.80) \approx 0.842, \quad z(F) = z(0.10) \approx -1.282$$ **Step 3.** Compute sensitivity: $$d' = 0.842 - (-1.282) = 2.124$$ > **Conclusion:** $d' \approx 2.1$ indicates a **highly sensitive** operator (well above the typical $d' = 1.0$ baseline). If the same operator scored $H = 0.40, F = 0.10$, then $d' = -0.253 - (-1.282) = 1.03$ — only moderate sensitivity. --- ## 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix | Question Type | Approach | Final Answer Format | |---------------|----------|---------------------| | Find JND | $\Delta I = kI$ | Numerical value in W/m² or dB | | Convert to dB | $L = 10\log(I/I_0)$ | dB SPL | | Find pitch JND | $\Delta F = 0.003F$ | Hz or cents | | Channel capacity | Miller's $7 \pm 2$ | Number of categories | | SDT sensitivity | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Dimensionless index | | Minimum alert level | SNR rule | dB above noise | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Psychophysical Threshold Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Auditory Stimulus Presented] --> B{Is there a
reference stimulus?} B -- No --> C[Absolute Threshold Test] B -- Yes --> D[Relative Discrimination Test] C --> E[Outcome: Heard / Not Heard] D --> F{JND exceeded?} F -- Yes --> G[Subject reports Difference] F -- No --> H[Subject reports No Difference] G --> I[Apply Weber Fraction k = 0.10] H --> I I --> J[Fechner's Law S = K log I by I0] J --> K[Convert to Decibel Scale] K --> L[Design Recommendation] L --> M[Auditory Signal Specification] ``` ## 4.2 Absolute Identification — Multidimensional Coding Strategy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Unidim[Unidimensional Coding] U1[Pitch Only] U2[Capacity approx 3 to 4 tones] end subgraph Multidim[Multidimensional Coding] M1[Pitch] M2[Duration] M3[Spatial Location] M4[Temporal Pattern] end Multidim --> C[Combined Capacity approx 27 plus categories] Unidim --> C C --> D[Engineered Alert System] D --> E1[Cockpit Warnings] D --> E2[ICU Alarms] D --> E3[Industrial Buzzers] ``` ## 4.3 Signal Detection Theory — Outcomes Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Stimulus] --> Q{Actual State} Q -- Signal Present --> SP[Signal Trial] Q -- Signal Absent --> NP[Noise Trial] SP --> R1{Response} R1 -- Yes --> Hit[Hit H] R1 -- No --> Miss[Miss] NP --> R2{Response} R2 -- Yes --> FA[False Alarm F] R2 -- No --> CR[Correct Rejection] Hit --> SDT[Compute d prime and beta] FA --> SDT SDT --> Out1[Sensitivity Index d prime] SDT --> Out2[Response Bias beta] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Auditory Display Design ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Source: Machine State] --> A2[Signal Encoder] A2 --> A3[Pitch Selection 500 to 3000 Hz] A2 --> A4[Loudness Set 15 dB above Noise] A2 --> A5[Duration Pattern 0.5 to 2 s] A3 --> A6[Auditory Display] A4 --> A6 A5 --> A6 A6 --> B1[Operator Ear] B1 --> B2[Cochlear Filtering] B2 --> B3[Auditory Nerve] B3 --> B4[Cortex: Absolute Identification] B3 --> B5[Cortex: Relative Discrimination] B4 --> C1[Recognition Memory 7 plus or minus 2] B5 --> C2[JND Comparison against Stored Template] C1 --> D[Operator Action] C2 --> D D --> E[Feedback to Machine] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a processing stage; arrows represent information flow. Node IDs (e.g., `A1`, `B2`) are alphanumeric and safe from Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define *Just Noticeable Difference (JND)*. State Weber's Law and give its auditory Weber fraction. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **Just Noticeable Difference (JND)** is the smallest change in a stimulus that an observer can detect 50 % of the time when a standard stimulus is present. **Weber's Law:** $$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k$$ where $\Delta I$ is the JND, $I$ is the standard stimulus intensity, and $k$ is the **Weber fraction**. For **loudness** at moderate levels, $k \approx 0.10$, i.e. a 10 % increase in intensity is just noticeable. --- ### Q2. What is Miller's Law? Why is it important for auditory alarm design? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Miller's Law** (1956) states that the number of absolute judgments a person can make about a unidimensional stimulus is limited to about **$7 \pm 2$ categories**. For purely auditory stimuli varying in one dimension (e.g., pitch alone), the limit is even lower — about **3 to 4**. **Importance for alarm design:** Designers cannot expect operators to identify more than 4–7 distinct alert tones from memory. If more alerts are required, they must use **redundant coding** (pitch + duration + spatial location) so that the effective capacity multiplies across dimensions. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain **Relative Discrimination** of auditory signals with reference to Weber's Law and Fechner's Law. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A 1000 Hz tone is presented at 50 dB SPL. Taking the Weber fraction as 0.10, calculate: *(i)* the absolute intensity $I$ in W/m², *(ii)* the JND in intensity $\Delta I$, *(iii)* the JND expressed in decibels. *(7 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` #### Model Solution **(a)** *Relative Discrimination* is the ability to detect a difference between two stimuli. It is governed by **Weber's Law**: $$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k$$ For auditory loudness, $k \approx 0.10$, meaning that a 10 % change in intensity is just detectable at moderate levels. At very low or very high intensities the fraction deviates, so Weber's Law is only strictly valid in the **middle range** of stimulation. Integrating Weber's differential form $\frac{dI}{I} = k \cdot dS$ from the absolute threshold $I_0$ to intensity $I$ gives **Fechner's Law**: $$S = K \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ This logarithmic relationship between physical intensity and perceived sensation is the foundation of the **decibel scale** used throughout acoustics. Practical implications include: * Equal increments in dB correspond to equal perceptual increments. * A 10 dB increase is perceived as a doubling of loudness (approximately). * Design of audio displays should use dB spacing ≥ 3 dB to ensure discriminable alerts. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i)** Absolute intensity at 50 dB SPL: $$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{L/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{50/10}$$ $$I = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{5} = 10^{-7} \text{ W/m}^2$$ **(ii)** JND in intensity: $$\Delta I = k \cdot I = 0.10 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$\Delta I = 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2$$ **(iii)** JND in dB: $$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I + \Delta I}{I}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.10)$$ $$\Delta L = 10 \times 0.04139 \approx 0.41 \text{ dB}$$ > **Conclusion:** A 50 dB tone must increase by about **0.41 dB** to be perceived as louder. This value is independent of the absolute level (within Weber's valid range) and is the basis of the "1 dB JND" rule of thumb. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** Discuss **Absolute Identification** of auditory signals. How does Miller's Law constrain the design of warning sounds? *(7 Marks)* **(b)** In a signal-detection experiment, an operator achieves 75 hits out of 100 signal trials and 15 false alarms out of 100 noise trials. Determine the sensitivity index $d'$ and the response bias $\beta$. Comment on the operator's performance. *(7 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` #### Model Solution **(a)** **Absolute Identification** is the ability to recognize and label a stimulus from memory without an external reference. Miller (1956) demonstrated that the absolute-judgment channel capacity is approximately **$7 \pm 2$** categories for multi-dimensional stimuli, but only **3 to 4** for purely unidimensional stimuli (e.g., pitch alone). **Constraints on warning-sound design:** * Designers cannot rely on operators distinguishing more than ~4 unidimensional tones. * If more alerts are required, redundant dimensions must be used — pitch, loudness, duration, rhythm, and spatial location. * The **effective capacity** multiplies across orthogonal dimensions (e.g., 3 pitches × 3 durations = 9 distinct alerts). * Auditory alerts must also be **above the absolute threshold** and **above the noise floor** (SNR ≥ 10–15 dB) for reliable detection. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1.** Compute hit and false-alarm rates: $$H = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75, \quad F = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15$$ **Step 2.** Convert to z-scores using standard normal tables: $$z(H) = z(0.75) \approx 0.674$$ $$z(F) = z(0.15) \approx -1.036$$ **Step 3.** Compute sensitivity index: $$d' = z(H) - z(F) = 0.674 - (-1.036)$$ $$d' = 1.71$$ **Step 4.** Compute response bias $\beta$ (likelihood ratio at criterion): $$\beta = \frac{f_n(x_c)}{f_s(x_c)}$$ Using the standard-normal PDF at the criterion $x_c = z(F) = -1.036$: $$f_n(x_c) = \phi(-1.036) \approx 0.226$$ For signals, the criterion is at distance $d'$ from the signal mean, so: $$x_c - d' = -1.036 - 1.71 = -2.746$$ $$f_s(x_c) = \phi(-2.746) \approx 0.0106$$ $$\beta = \frac{0.226}{0.0106} \approx 21.3$$ **Interpretation:** $d' = 1.71$ indicates **good sensitivity** (above the typical 1.0 baseline). The large $\beta \approx 21$ means the operator is using a **conservative criterion** — they require strong evidence before reporting a signal, which is appropriate for safety-critical monitoring tasks where false alarms are costly. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse absolute threshold with JND** — the first has no reference stimulus, the second does. > 2. **Don't write `|` inside a table** — it breaks the markdown column separator. > 3. **Don't drop units** — every W/m², dB, Hz, or cent must be explicitly stated. > 4. **For SDT, always compute both $d'$ AND $\beta$** — partial answers score zero on interpretation marks. > 5. **For Miller's Law, specify "unidimensional" vs "multidimensional"** — examiners specifically look for this distinction. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Absolute Threshold** = minimum detectable intensity **without** a reference. - **Difference Threshold (JND)** = minimum detectable change **with** a reference stimulus. - **Weber's Law**: $\Delta I / I = k$, with $k \approx 0.10$ for loudness. - **Fechner's Law**: $S = K \log(I / I_0)$, basis of the **decibel scale**. - **Pitch JND**: $\Delta F \approx 0.003\,F$ (~3 Hz at 1 kHz, ~5 cents musically). - **Duration JND**: $\Delta T \approx 0.10\,T$ for tones 0.1 s – 2 s. - **Reference values**: $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m², $p_0 = 20$ μPa, $L = 10\log(I/I_0)$. - **SNR rule**: speech ≥ 6 dB, alarms ≥ 10–15 dB above noise. - **Miller's Law**: $7 \pm 2$ categories (multi-dim), $3$–$4$ categories (unidimensional). - **Redundant coding** is mandatory when more than 4 alerts are needed. - **Signal Detection Theory**: separate **sensitivity $d'$** from **bias $\beta$** — use $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ and $\beta = f_n / f_s$. - **Engineering takeaway**: design auditory displays so that **detection, discrimination, and identification** are all independently satisfied with safety margins of 3 dB or more.

topics.title: Sound Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sound Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sound Localization** is the auditory system's perceptual ability to identify the spatial origin of a sound source in three-dimensional space—specifically its **azimuth** (horizontal angle), **elevation** (vertical angle), and **distance**—by processing acoustic cues arriving at the two ears. In human factors engineering, it is treated as a *sensorimotor transformation* where the Central Nervous System (CNS) decodes binaural and monaural acoustic variables into a spatial percept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT748 – Module 2):** Sound localization sits under the broader unit on *Auditory Perception and Displays*. It is a 2024 Scheme high-weightage topic because it directly influences the design of auditory warnings, cockpit voice alerts, 3D audio in control rooms, and human-machine interface safety.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a busy marketplace. Someone claps once. Instantly, your brain *points* an internal arrow toward the clapper—even with eyes closed. How?

The brain is essentially performing **triangulation using two receivers (your ears)**. Because your head is roughly **17–18 cm wide**, a sound from your right side reaches your **right ear earlier and louder** than your left ear. The brain compares:

- **Arrival Time** → "Which ear heard it first?"
- **Loudness** → "Which ear heard it louder?"
- **Tone Quality** → "How did the outer ear (pinna) filter it?"

This is identical to how a submarine uses two hydrophones to locate a ship, or how a stereo microphone array gives direction to recorded sound.

### 1.3 The Three Spatial Dimensions of Localization

| Spatial Dimension | Definition | Primary Acoustic Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Azimuth (Horizontal)** | Left–Right angle in the horizontal plane | ITD (low freq) + ILD (high freq) |
| **Elevation (Vertical)** | Up–Down angle from the horizontal | Pinna spectral notches & peaks |
| **Distance (Range)** | How far the source is | Overall loudness, spectral tilt, direct-to-reverberant ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The **Minimum Audible Angle (MAA)** for azimuth in the horizontal plane is approximately **1° to 3°** for broadband sounds in front of the listener. This is the human auditory system's *spatial resolution*—the engineering benchmark used when designing 3D audio systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Primary Binaural Cues

The human auditory system relies on **two complementary cues** for horizontal localization, formalized by Lord Rayleigh as the **Duplex Theory (1907)**.

#### A. Interaural Time Difference (ITD) — "The Stopwatch Cue"

- **Definition:** The difference in arrival time of the sound wave between the *nearer ear* and the *farther ear*.
- **Effective Range:** Dominant cue for frequencies **below ~1500 Hz** (where wavelength > head diameter).
- **Logic:** At low frequencies, the head casts no acoustic shadow; the wave simply bends (diffracts) around the head, but the *phase* of the sinusoid is preserved. The brain measures phase difference.
- **When source is directly to the side (90° azimuth):** ITD is maximum ≈ **0.63 ms** (for an average head radius of ~8.75 cm).
- **When source is directly ahead (0° azimuth):** ITD ≈ 0 ms (path lengths are equal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why phase, not delay, at low frequencies?** Because the human auditory system fires neural spikes locked to the waveform's phase (phase-locking). It cannot, however, resolve phase ambiguity when wavelength < twice the interaural distance (above ~1500 Hz), which is why ITD becomes ambiguous at high frequencies and the system switches to **ILD**.

#### B. Interaural Level Difference (ILD) — "The Volume Knob Cue"

- **Definition:** The difference in sound pressure level (SPL) between the two ears, also called **Interaural Intensity Difference (IID)**.
- **Effective Range:** Dominant cue for frequencies **above ~1500 Hz** (where wavelength < head diameter).
- **Logic:** At high frequencies, the head casts a real **acoustic shadow**. Sound cannot diffract around the head, so the far ear receives a significantly attenuated signal.
- **Magnitude:** ILD can exceed **20 dB** at high frequencies (3–5 kHz) for a 90° azimuth source.

### 2.2 The Monaural (Spectral) Cue — Pinna Filtering

- The **pinna** (visible outer ear) is asymmetric, with multiple ridges and cavities that selectively amplify or attenuate specific frequencies based on the *elevation* of the source.
- These produce **directionally-dependent spectral notches** (typically in the 6–10 kHz range) that the brain has *learned* to associate with specific vertical positions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cone of Confusion:** For any given pair of ITD and ILD values, there exists a *cone-shaped* locus of points in space that produces identical binaural cues. The pinna provides the additional spectral information needed to resolve the ambiguity on this cone.

### 2.3 Higher-Order Localization Phenomena

- **Precedence Effect (Haas Effect):** When two identical sounds arrive within **~5–35 ms**, the auditory system localizes the sound to the *first-arriving* source and suppresses the spatial perception of the echo. Critical for concert-hall acoustics and PA system design.
- **Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF):** The complete frequency-domain transfer function from a free-field sound source to the eardrum, encoding all binaural + pinna cues. Used in 3D audio rendering, VR, and aviation helmet design.
- **Binaural Unmasking:** A sound source can be heard more clearly against background noise if it is spatially separated from the masker—used in cockpit audio design (e.g., directional warnings).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) are used inside table cells.** All absolute-value and conditional notations use `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol | Meaning / Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum ITD (90° azimuth) | $\Delta t_{max}$ | $\Delta t_{max} \approx \dfrac{3a}{c}$ where $a$ is head radius, $c$ is speed of sound | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Speed of sound in air | $c$ | **343 m/s** at **20°C** | m/s |
| 3 | General ITD expression (azimuth $\theta$) | $\Delta t(\theta)$ | $\Delta t(\theta) = \dfrac{a}{c}\bigl(\theta + \sin\theta\bigr)$ (Woodworth formula) | s |
| 4 | ILD (high frequency approximation) | $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L \approx 20\log_{10}\!\bigl(\dfrac{p_{near}}{p_{far}}\bigr)$ | decibels (dB) |
| 5 | Wavelength cut-off for ITD dominance | $\lambda_{cutoff}$ | $\lambda \approx 2d$ where $d \approx 0.18$ m → $f \approx 950$ Hz (often cited as 1500 Hz) | Hz |
| 6 | Interaural distance (avg human) | $d$ | **0.17 to 0.18 m** | m |
| 7 | Minimum Audible Angle (front, broadband) | MAA | **1° to 3°** | degrees (°) |
| 8 | Precedence Effect window | $\Delta t_{prec}$ | **5 ms ≤ Δt ≤ 35 ms** → first source dominates localization | ms |
| 9 | Critical Bandwidth (related to ILD resolution) | $CB$ | Bark scale; relevant for frequency masking | Bark / Hz |
| 10 | Inverse-square law for distance cue | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$ | Intensity drops with square of distance; brain uses this for distance perception | W/m² |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation & ATC:** 3D-audio warning systems in cockpits (e.g., Honeywell's *AudioLogic*) use HRTF processing so that a "bank angle" alert appears to originate from the direction of the offending wing.
- **Automotive:** Directional auditory alerts (collision warnings, lane departure) improve driver reaction time by **~150 ms** over non-directional beeps.
- **Hearing Aids:** Binaural beamforming exploits ITD/ILD to spatially amplify the speaker directly in front of the user while attenuating side noise.
- **Virtual Reality (VR):** HRTF-based rendering produces immersive 3D soundscapes by simulating the natural ear-filtering cues.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Interaural Time Difference (ITD)

**Objective:** Prove that when a sound source is positioned at 90° azimuth (directly to the side), the ITD reaches its theoretical maximum value.

**Geometric Setup:**
- Consider the human head as a **rigid sphere of radius $a$**.
- Sound travels at speed $c$ through air.
- The listener is at the origin. The sound source is at angular azimuth $\theta$ measured from the median plane (the vertical plane directly in front of the listener).

#### Step 1 — Express the Path Length Difference

When sound arrives from azimuth $\theta$, it travels along two paths:
- To the **near ear**: path length $r_1$
- To the **far ear**: path length $r_2$

The extra distance the wave must travel to reach the far ear is the **path difference** $\Delta r$. Using the geometry of a sound wave approaching a sphere:

$$
\Delta r(\theta) = a\,(\theta + \sin\theta)
$$

> **Why?** The first term $a\theta$ accounts for the arc distance the wave must travel *around the head*. The second term $a\sin\theta$ accounts for the chord difference between the two ears.

#### Step 2 — Convert Distance Difference to Time Difference

Since time = distance / speed:

$$
\Delta t(\theta) = \dfrac{\Delta r(\theta)}{c} = \dfrac{a}{c}\,(\theta + \sin\theta)
$$

This is the **Woodworth formula** (1938) for ITD as a function of azimuth.

#### Step 3 — Evaluate at θ = 90° (π/2 radians)

At 90° azimuth:
- $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ rad
- $\sin\theta = 1$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta t_{max} = \dfrac{a}{c}\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} + 1\right)
$$

#### Step 4 — Plug in Realistic Human Head Values

Using standard anthropometric values:
- $a \approx 0.0875$ m (effective head radius ≈ 8.75 cm)
- $c = 343$ m/s at 20°C

$$
\Delta t_{max} = \dfrac{0.0875}{343}\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} + 1\right)
$$

Compute the bracketed term first:

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{2} + 1 \approx 1.5708 + 1.0000 = 2.5708
$$

Now compute the leading fraction:

$$
\dfrac{0.0875}{343} \approx 2.551 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s/rad}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta t_{max} \approx 2.551 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.5708 \approx 6.56 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta t_{max} \approx 0.656\,\text{ms} \approx 0.63\,\text{ms (commonly cited)}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The maximum time difference between the two ears is only **about two-thirds of a millisecond**. The brain must therefore have extraordinarily precise temporal processing—the auditory neurons fire in **phase-locked** synchrony with the stimulus waveform to detect this.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cue Selection by Frequency

| Frequency Range | Dominant Cue | Physical Mechanism | Brain Region (approx.) | Maximum Cue Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 20 – 200 Hz | ITD (phase) | Wave diffracts around head; phase preserved | Medial Superior Olive (MSO) | $\Delta t \le 0.63$ ms |
| 200 – 1500 Hz | ITD (group delay) | Envelope/syllable timing preserved | MSO | Same as above |
| 1500 – 5000 Hz | Transition zone | Wavelength ≈ head size | MSO + LSO | Mixed |
| > 5000 Hz | ILD + Pinna | Acoustic shadow + outer ear filtering | Lateral Superior Olive (LSO) + Cortex | $\Delta L$ up to ~20 dB |

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A sound originates at an azimuth of 60° to the right of a listener whose effective head radius is **9.0 cm**. Speed of sound is **343 m/s**.

**(a)** Compute the ITD using the Woodworth formula.

**(b)** Compute the corresponding path-length difference.

**(c)** Comment on whether ITD or ILD is the dominant cue if the source emits a pure tone at **300 Hz**.

#### Solution (a) — ITD Calculation

Given:
- $\theta = 60° = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ rad
- $a = 0.09$ m
- $c = 343$ m/s

$$
\Delta t = \dfrac{a}{c}\left(\theta + \sin\theta\right)
$$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$
\theta + \sin\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3} + \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)
$$

$$
= 1.0472 + 0.8660 = 1.9132\,\text{rad}
$$

Now compute the leading coefficient:

$$
\dfrac{a}{c} = \dfrac{0.09}{343} \approx 2.624 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s/rad}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta t = 2.624 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.9132 \approx 5.02 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta t \approx 0.502\,\text{ms}}
$$

#### Solution (b) — Path-Length Difference

$$
\Delta r = c \cdot \Delta t = 343 \times 5.02 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.1722\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta r \approx 17.22\,\text{cm}}
$$
