topics.title: Measures of Physiological Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measures of Physiological Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physiological Strain** is the quantifiable internal response (load) imposed on the human body's homeostatic systems — primarily the **cardiovascular**, **thermoregulatory**, **respiratory**, and **metabolic** systems — as a result of an external physical, thermal, or occupational stress.

In Human Factors Engineering, **strain** is distinguished from **stress** as follows:
- **Stress** = the external demand placed on the operator (e.g., load lifted, ambient temperature, work rate).
- **Strain** = the internal physiological cost (e.g., rise in heart rate, oxygen uptake, core temperature, sweat loss).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Examiner Phrasing):** "Physiological strain refers to the measurable deviation of bodily functions from their resting baseline, indicating the magnitude of work imposed on the human operator, and is used as an objective indicator of work intensity, fatigue, and thermal load."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engine Tachometer" of the Human Body

Imagine a human body as a **car engine**:
- **Stress** is the steepness of the hill the car is climbing (the demand).
- **Strain** is what the **RPM gauge** reads on the dashboard (the engine's response).
- Just as a car engine shows strain via RPM, oil temperature, and fuel consumption, the human body reveals strain through **heart rate, body temperature, oxygen consumption, and sweat rate**.
- If the RPM (strain) stays in the "green zone," the engine runs safely. Push it into the "red zone" for too long, and the engine overheats — for humans, this means **heat stroke, exhaustion, cardiovascular overload, or musculoskeletal injury**.

Thus, **measuring physiological strain is like installing a dashboard warning system on the operator** so that the ergonomist can redesign the task, tool, or environment before damage occurs.

### 1.3 Why Measure Physiological Strain? (KTU High-Yield Significance)

| Reason | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Workload Quantification** | Objective assessment of physical work intensity |
| **Fatigue Prediction** | Determines rest allowances and shift durations |
| **Safety Compliance** | Meets ISO 7243, ISO 8996, ISO 9886 thresholds |
| **Job Redesign** | Identifies over-stressed tasks in industry |
| **Health Surveillance** | Prevents heat illness, cardiac events in workers |
| **Performance Optimization** | Athletes, military, firefighters — strain monitoring |

### 1.4 Classification of Physiological Strain Measures (Big-Picture Map)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 syllabus groups the measures into **four major systems**:
> 1. **Cardiovascular Strain** — Heart Rate, Blood Pressure, HRR
> 2. **Metabolic Strain** — Oxygen Consumption, Energy Expenditure, RMR
> 3. **Thermoregulatory Strain** — Core & Skin Temperature, Sweat Rate
> 4. **Subjective/Integrative Strain** — RPE (Borg), PSI, SWV

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cardiovascular Measures of Strain

#### 2.1.1 Heart Rate (HR)
- Most widely used, non-invasive indicator of physical workload.
- Resting HR: **60–80 beats per minute (bpm)**.
- Working HR rises linearly with oxygen uptake up to ~**150 bpm**, then plateaus.
- **Maximum HR (HR_max) Estimation:** $HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age (years)}$
- Working HR is logged continuously using **telemetry chest straps** or **polar heart-rate monitors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A working HR exceeding **110 bpm** during a sustained 8-hour shift is considered indicative of moderate-to-heavy work in industrial ergonomics.

#### 2.1.2 Heart Rate Reserve (HRR) — Karvonen Formula
- The **percentage of cardiovascular capacity** being used.
- A more accurate indicator of strain than raw HR because it accounts for individual fitness.

$$HRR (\%) = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$$

- **Acceptable work limit (KTU reference):** Sustained work should not exceed **30–40 % HRR** for an 8-hour shift.
- **40–60 % HRR** is permissible for short-duration or moderate tasks.
- **> 60 % HRR** indicates heavy strain and risk of fatigue.

#### 2.1.3 Blood Pressure (BP)
- Systolic BP rises with dynamic work; diastolic remains stable or drops slightly.
- **Normal resting BP:** $\mathbf{120/80 \; mmHg}$.
- Working systolic BP should ideally stay **< 160 mmHg** for healthy adults.
- Measured via auscultation or automated oscillometric devices.

### 2.2 Metabolic Measures of Strain

#### 2.2.1 Oxygen Consumption (VO₂)
- Gold standard for aerobic strain.
- Units: **litres of O₂ per minute (L·min⁻¹)** or **mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹**.
- Resting VO₂ ≈ **0.25 L·min⁻¹ (3.5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹)** — the **1 MET** baseline.
- **Maximum VO₂ (VO₂max)** declines with age: $VO_{2max} \approx 3.5 + 0.2 \times (60 - \text{age})$ mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ (rough estimate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Benchmarks (Brouha's Classification of Work):**
> - Light work: VO₂ < 0.5 L·min⁻¹
> - Moderate work: 0.5 – 1.0 L·min⁻¹
> - Heavy work: 1.0 – 1.5 L·min⁻¹
> - Very heavy work: 1.5 – 2.0 L·min⁻¹
> - Extremely heavy: > 2.0 L·min⁻¹

#### 2.2.2 Energy Expenditure (EE)
- Derived from VO₂ using the caloric equivalent of oxygen.
- **Average caloric equivalent:** $1 \; L \; O_2 \approx 20.9 \; kJ \; (5.0 \; kcal)$.

$$EE \; (kJ/min) = VO_2 \; (L/min) \times 20.9$$

- The **net energy cost of a task** is:

$$EE_{net} = EE_{working} - EE_{resting}$$

#### 2.2.3 Relative Metabolic Rate (RMR) — ISO 8996
- Normalised indicator of metabolic strain.

$$RMR = \frac{M - M_0}{M_0}$$

Where:
- $M$ = working metabolic rate (W·m⁻² or W)
- $M_0$ = resting metabolic rate

| RMR | Class | Workload |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Rest | — |
| 0 – 1 | Light | Office, supervisory |
| 1 – 2 | Moderate | Assembly, light manual |
| 2 – 3 | Heavy | Construction, lifting |
| 3 – 4 | Very heavy | Mining, firefighting |
| > 4 | Extremely heavy | Untenable long-term |

### 2.3 Thermoregulatory Measures of Strain

#### 2.3.1 Core Temperature (T_core)
- Best single indicator of **heat strain**.
- Measured at **tympanic membrane, oesophagus, or rectum**.
- Resting T_core ≈ **37.0 ± 0.5 °C**.
- Upper safe limit: **38.0 °C** (sustained work); **38.5 °C** triggers work-stoppage protocols.
- Critical limit: **39.0–39.5 °C** (heat stroke risk).

#### 2.3.2 Skin Temperature (T_skin)
- Weighted mean of multiple body sites:

$$T_{skin} = 0.07 T_{forehead} + 0.20 T_{chest} + 0.20 T_{arm} + 0.20 T_{thigh} + 0.33 T_{leg}$$

- Useful for **cold strain** assessment (T_skin < 15 °C indicates severe cold strain).

#### 2.3.3 Mean Body Temperature (T̄_b)

$$\bar{T}_b = 0.67 \cdot T_{core} + 0.33 \cdot T_{skin}$$

#### 2.3.4 Sweat Rate (SR)
- Indirect index of heat strain; reflects evaporative cooling demand.
- Measured by **mass loss over time** corrected for fluid intake and urine.

$$SR \; (L/h) = \frac{\Delta M_{body} + M_{intake} - M_{urine}}{\Delta t}$$

- Sustained SR > **1.0 L/h** indicates heavy heat strain; > **1.5 L/h** is dangerous without rehydration.

### 2.4 Subjective & Integrative Measures

#### 2.4.1 Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) — Borg Scale
- **Borg 6–20 Scale:** correlates with HR (× 10 ≈ HR for moderate work).
- **Category-Ratio (CR-10) Scale:** used for localised muscular strain.
- Captures the **psychobiological** integration of all strain signals.

#### 2.4.2 Physiological Strain Index (PSI) — Moran (1998)
- Combines core temperature and heart rate into a single **0–10 scale**.

$$PSI = 5 \cdot \frac{T_{ret} - T_{r0}}{39.5 - T_{r0}} + 5 \cdot \frac{HR_t - HR_0}{180 - HR_0}$$

Where:
- $T_{r0}, HR_0$ = baseline (rest) values
- $T_{ret}, HR_t$ = current values
- **39.5 °C** and **180 bpm** are upper physiological safety limits

> [!NOTE]
> **PSI Interpretation (ISO 9886):**
> - PSI 0–2: No/Low strain
> - PSI 3–5: Moderate strain
> - PSI 6–7: High strain
> - PSI 8–10: Very high / dangerous

#### 2.4.3 Subjective Workload (SWV) & Fatigue Scales
- **NASA-TLX**, **SWAT**, **Samn-Perelli Fatigue Scale** — capture perceived strain.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Measure | Formula / Expression | Units | Normal / Safe Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HR_max | $220 - \text{Age}$ | bpm | Age-dependent |
| 2 | HRR (Karvonen) | $\frac{HR_w - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$ | % | $\leq 40\%$ (8 h) |
| 3 | Energy Expenditure | $VO_2 \times 20.9$ | kJ/min | Task-dependent |
| 4 | Relative Metabolic Rate | $\frac{M - M_0}{M_0}$ | — | $\leq 2.0$ (sustained) |
| 5 | Net EE | $EE_{work} - EE_{rest}$ | kJ/min | < 16 kJ/min (8 h) |
| 6 | Mean Skin Temp | $0.07T_{fh} + 0.20T_{ch} + 0.20T_{a} + 0.20T_{th} + 0.33T_{l}$ | °C | 30–34 °C |
| 7 | Mean Body Temp | $0.67T_{core} + 0.33T_{skin}$ | °C | < 37.5 °C |
| 8 | PSI | $5\frac{T_{ret} - T_{r0}}{39.5 - T_{r0}} + 5\frac{HR_t - HR_0}{180 - HR_0}$ | 0–10 | $\leq 5$ |
| 9 | Sweat Rate | $\frac{\Delta M_{body} + M_{in} - M_{urine}}{\Delta t}$ | L/h | < 1.0 L/h |
| 10 | O₂ Cost (Brouha) | VO₂ range by class | L/min | < 1.0 sustained |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineers' field rule:** If two of the three (HR, T_core, RPE) are simultaneously in the "red zone," **stop the task and redesign** the work cycle.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Mining & Foundries** — monitoring heat strain via PSI to schedule work-rest cycles per ISO 7243.
- **Defence / Firefighting** — wearable HR + T_core sensors for soldier health and PPE evaluation.
- **Automotive Assembly** — RPE and HRR to redesign hand-tool torquing tasks.
- **Sports & Rehabilitation** — VO₂max and HRR for training zone prescription.
- **Space Missions** — NASA uses HR, T_core, and sweat rate during EVA for crew safety.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heart Rate Reserve (Karvonen Formula)

**Given:** A 30-year-old worker has a resting heart rate of 72 bpm. During a 4-hour assembly task his heart rate stabilises at 138 bpm. Determine the HRR and comment on whether the task is sustainable for an 8-hour shift.

**Step 1 — Maximum HR from age:**

$$HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age} = 220 - 30 = 190 \; bpm$$

**Step 2 — Apply Karvonen formula:**

$$HRR = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$HRR = \frac{138 - 72}{190 - 72} \times 100$$

$$HRR = \frac{66}{118} \times 100 = 0.5593 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{HRR \approx 55.9\%}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (KTU Reference):**

> A sustained HRR of 55.9 % lies in the **moderate-to-heavy** band. KTU ergonomics guidelines state that an 8-hour shift should not exceed **30–40 % HRR**. Therefore, this task is **not sustainable** for a full shift without rest breaks or task redesign (e.g., job rotation, mechanical assist).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Expenditure from VO₂

**Given:** A construction labourer has a measured working VO₂ of 1.2 L/min and resting VO₂ of 0.25 L/min. Compute (a) total energy expenditure, (b) net energy cost, (c) classify the work (Brouha).

**Step 1 — Total EE:**

$$EE_{work} = 1.2 \times 20.9 = 25.08 \; kJ/min$$

**Step 2 — Resting EE:**

$$EE_{rest} = 0.25 \times 20.9 = 5.225 \; kJ/min$$

**Step 3 — Net EE:**

$$EE_{net} = 25.08 - 5.225 = 19.86 \; kJ/min \approx 19.9 \; kJ/min$$

**Step 4 — Brouha Classification:**
- VO₂ = 1.2 L/min lies in the range **1.0–1.5 L/min** → **Heavy work**.

**Step 5 — RMR Check:**

$$RMR = \frac{M - M_0}{M_0} = \frac{1.2 - 0.25}{0.25} = \frac{0.95}{0.25} = 3.8$$

- RMR = 3.8 lies in the **"Very heavy"** band (RMR 3–4). Borderline untenable for an 8-hour shift.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Skin & Mean Body Temperature

**Given (a foundry worker):**
- Forehead = 36.0 °C, Chest = 35.5 °C, Arm = 34.8 °C, Thigh = 34.5 °C, Leg = 33.2 °C
- Core (tympanic) T_core = 38.2 °C

**Step 1 — Weighted mean skin temperature:**

$$T_{skin} = 0.07(36.0) + 0.20(35.5) + 0.20(34.8) + 0.20(34.5) + 0.33(33.2)$$

$$T_{skin} = 2.52 + 7.10 + 6.96 + 6.90 + 10.956$$

$$\boxed{T_{skin} = 34.436 \; °C \approx 34.4 \; °C}$$

**Step 2 — Mean body temperature:**

$$\bar{T}_b = 0.67(38.2) + 0.33(34.4)$$

$$\bar{T}_b = 25.594 + 11.352 = 36.946 \; °C$$

$$\boxed{\bar{T}_b \approx 36.95 \; °C}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- T_core of **38.2 °C is in the moderate heat-strain zone** (above the 38.0 °C action limit). Recommended action: implement work-rest cycle (e.g., 50 min work / 10 min rest in shade with hydration).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Physiological Strain Index (PSI)

**Given:** A firefighter at rest has T_r0 = 37.0 °C and HR_0 = 75 bpm. After 20 minutes of structural firefighting, T_ret = 38.6 °C and HR_t = 168 bpm. Compute PSI.

**Step 1 — Temperature component:**

$$PSI_T = 5 \cdot \frac{38.6 - 37.0}{39.5 - 37.0} = 5 \cdot \frac{1.6}{2.5} = 5 \times 0.64 = 3.2$$

**Step 2 — Heart rate component:**

$$PSI_{HR} = 5 \cdot \frac{168 - 75}{180 - 75} = 5 \cdot \frac{93}{105} = 5 \times 0.8857 = 4.429$$

**Step 3 — Total PSI:**

$$PSI = PSI_T + PSI_{HR} = 3.2 + 4.429$$

$$\boxed{PSI \approx 7.63}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (ISO 9886 bands):**
- PSI 7.63 → **High strain** (band 6–7 = High).
- The firefighter should be **rotated out** for active recovery and cooling.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Sweat Rate Calculation

**Given:** Over a 2-hour foundry shift, a worker:
- Loses 3.4 kg body mass
- Drinks 1.2 L of water
- Urinates 0.2 L

**Step 1 — Net mass loss:**

$$\Delta M = 3.4 - 1.2 + 0.2 = 2.4 \; kg \; (2.4 \; L)$$

**Step 2 — Sweat rate:**

$$SR = \frac{2.4 \; L}{2 \; h} = 1.2 \; L/h$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- 1.2 L/h exceeds the 1.0 L/h "heavy heat strain" threshold.
- This worker requires **aggressive oral rehydration** (≈ 1.2 L/h replacement) and a **cool-down rest cycle** to avoid dehydration-induced cardiovascular strain.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Strain Measure Selection (KTU Decision Logic)

| Work Condition | Primary Strain Measure | Secondary Measure | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild assembly, low temp | HR, RPE | VO₂ | Cheap, non-invasive |
| Heavy manual lifting | HRR, RPE | VO₂, EMG | Cardio-muscular coupling |
| Hot environment (foundry, mines) | T_core, PSI | Sweat rate, HR | Heat stroke prevention |
| Cold environment (cold storage) | T_skin, T_core | HR, RPE | Frostbite, hypothermia risk |
| Long-duration, monotonous | RPE (Borg CR-10) | HRV, fatigue scales | Subjective load dominates |
| Protective clothing (PPE) | PSI, T_core | HR, sweat rate | Encapsulated heat dissipation |
| Firefighting / military | PSI, T_core | HR, sweat rate | Acute high-strain exposure |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
> **Define Physiological Strain. Distinguish between physiological stress and strain with a suitable industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Physiological strain is the internal, measurable response of the human body to an imposed external demand (stress), observed as deviations in heart rate, body temperature, oxygen consumption, etc.
- **Stress vs Strain (1.5 marks):**
  - **Stress** = external demand (e.g., load mass, ambient temperature, work rate).
  - **Strain** = internal response (e.g., rise in HR, VO₂, T_core).
- **Industrial example (0.5 mark):** A foundry worker exposed to 45 °C WBGT (stress) shows T_core rising to 38.2 °C and HR of 145 bpm (strain).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
> **List any THREE cardiovascular measures of physiological strain. Write the Karvonen formula for Heart Rate Reserve.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Three measures (1.5 marks):** Heart Rate (HR), Heart Rate Reserve (HRR), Blood Pressure (BP), HR Variability (HRV).
- **Karvonen formula (1.5 marks):**

$$HRR = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$$

where $HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Choose A or B*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the various **measures of physiological strain** used in human factors engineering. Classify them into cardiovascular, metabolic, thermoregulatory, and subjective categories. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A 28-year-old industrial worker has a resting heart rate of 70 bpm. During a 6-hour repetitive lifting task, his working heart rate stabilises at **145 bpm**. **(i)** Calculate the Heart Rate Reserve using the Karvonen formula. **(ii)** Comment on the sustainability of the task for an 8-hour shift. **(iii)** Suggest TWO engineering control measures. (7 marks)

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

**(a) Classification of Physiological Strain Measures (7 marks):**

**(i) Cardiovascular Strain (1.75 marks):**
- **Heart Rate (HR):** most common, non-invasive; rises linearly with VO₂ up to ~150 bpm.
- **Heart Rate Reserve (HRR):** percentage of cardio capacity utilised (Karvonen formula).
- **Blood Pressure (BP):** systolic rises with dynamic work; diastolic stable.
- **HR Variability (HRV):** time-domain indicator of autonomic fatigue.

**(ii) Metabolic Strain (1.75 marks):**
- **Oxygen Consumption (VO₂):** gold standard; measured via spirometry.
- **Energy Expenditure (EE):** $EE = VO_2 \times 20.9$ kJ/min.
- **Relative Metabolic Rate (RMR):** ISO 8996 normalisation; classes 0 to > 4.
- **Brouha's classification:** Light (< 0.5), Moderate (0.5–1.0), Heavy (1.0–1.5), Very heavy (1.5–2.0), Extreme (> 2.0 L/min).

**(iii) Thermoregulatory Strain (1.75 marks):**
- **Core Temperature (T_core):** tympanic, oesophageal, rectal; limit 38.0–38.5 °C.
- **Mean Skin Temperature (T_skin):** 5-site Hardy-DuBois weighted formula.
- **Mean Body Temperature (T̄_b):** 0.67 T_core + 0.33 T_skin.
- **Sweat Rate (SR):** mass-loss method; > 1.0 L/h is hazardous.

**(iv) Subjective & Integrative Strain (1.75 marks):**
- **RPE — Borg 6–20 / CR-10:** psychobiological self-rating.
- **Physiological Strain Index (PSI):** Moran (1998), 0–10 scale combining HR + T_core.
- **Subjective Workload (SWV):** NASA-TLX, Samn-Perelli Fatigue Scale.
- **Recovery HR:** drop in HR post-work as fatigue index.

---

**(b) Numerical & Design Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) HR_max (1 mark):**

$$HR_{max} = 220 - 28 = 192 \; bpm$$

**HRR (2 marks):**

$$HRR = \frac{145 - 70}{192 - 70} \times 100 = \frac{75}{122} \times 100 = 61.48\%$$

$$\boxed{HRR \approx 61.5\%}$$

**(ii) Sustainability Comment (2 marks):**
- KTU / ISO threshold for sustained 8-hour work is **< 40 % HRR** (some references cite 35 %).
- HRR of 61.5 % lies in the **"heavy strain"** band and is **NOT sustainable** for a full shift.
- The worker is at risk of cardiovascular fatigue, lower-back strain, and cumulative trauma disorders.
- Physiological recovery between cycles is insufficient.

**(iii) Engineering Control Measures (2 marks):**
- **Work-rest scheduling:** 50 min work / 10 min micro-break OR job rotation with lighter tasks (e.g., sorting).
- **Mechanical aids:** introduce a **pneumatic lift assist, conveyor, or exoskeleton** to reduce metabolic demand.
- **Environmental control:** ensure ventilation, hydration stations, and ambient WBGT < 26 °C.
- **Training:** teach proper lifting biomechanics to reduce peak HR spikes.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **Physiological Strain Index (PSI)** proposed by Moran et al. (1998). State its formula, identify the input variables, and explain its interpretation bands. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A steel-plant maintenance worker has the following strain data recorded before and after a 90-minute repair task in a hot zone:
>
> | Parameter | Rest (baseline) | After 90 min |
> |---|---|---|
> | Core Temperature (°C) | 37.0 | 38.5 |
> | Heart Rate (bpm) | 78 | 162 |
>
> Calculate the **Physiological Strain Index (PSI)** and interpret the result. Recommend the appropriate rest cycle according to ISO 9886. (7 marks)

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**(a) Physiological Strain Index — Moran et al. (1998) (7 marks):**

**Background (1.5 marks):**
- Developed by Moran, Shitzer, & Pandolf (1998) to provide a **single, normalised 0–10 score** combining cardiovascular and thermoregulatory strain — addressing the limitation of using HR or T_core alone.
- Adopted by **ISO 9886** as a recommended heat-strain assessment method.
- Validated for hot environments, protective clothing, and industrial work-rest cycles.

**Formula (2.5 marks):**

$$PSI = 5 \cdot \frac{T_{ret} - T_{r0}}{39.5 - T_{r0}} + 5 \cdot \frac{HR_t - HR_0}{180 - HR_0}$$

Where:
- $T_{r0}, HR_0$ = resting (baseline) values
- $T_{ret}, HR_t$ = current strain values
- 39.5 °C and 180 bpm are the upper physiological safety limits
- Equal weighting (5 each) given to T_core and HR

**Input Variables (1.5 marks):**
- T_core measured at tympanic membrane, oesophagus, or via ingestible telemetry pill
- HR via chest-strap or ECG

**Interpretation Bands (1.5 marks):**

| PSI | Strain Level | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 2 | No / Low | Continue |
| 3 – 5 | Moderate | Monitor, ensure hydration |
| 6 – 7 | High | Reduce workload, increase rest |
| 8 – 10 | Very High | Stop work, medical attention |

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify variables (1 mark):**
- $T_{r0} = 37.0 \; °C, \; T_{ret} = 38.5 \; °C$
- $HR_0 = 78 \; bpm, \; HR_t = 162 \; bpm$

**Step 2 — Temperature component (2 marks):**

$$PSI_T = 5 \cdot \frac{38.5 - 37.0}{39.5 - 37.0} = 5 \cdot \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 5 \cdot 0.6 = 3.0$$

**Step 3 — Heart rate component (2 marks):**

$$PSI_{HR} = 5 \cdot \frac{162 - 78}{180 - 78} = 5 \cdot \frac{84}{102} = 5 \cdot 0.8235 = 4.118$$

**Step 4 — Total PSI (1 mark):**

$$PSI = 3.0 + 4.118 = 7.118$$

$$\boxed{PSI \approx 7.12}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation & Recommendation (1 mark):**
- PSI ≈ 7.12 falls in the **"High strain" band (6–7)**.
- **Action:** Reduce workload, mandate rest cycle of at least **15–20 minutes** in a cooled rest area, ensure fluid + electrolyte replenishment, and re-assess PSI before resuming. If PSI continues to climb toward 8, **stop work**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the upper safety constants** (39.5 °C and 180 bpm) in the PSI formula — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using 220 – age** as the upper limit for HRR but then using **190** instead of (220 – age) in the Karvonen formula denominator.
> 3. **Mixing up RMR and Brouha** classes — RMR is dimensionless ratio; Brouha is absolute VO₂.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering-control recommendations** in numerical questions — the KTU rubric always awards 1–2 marks for the "remedial / design" portion.
> 5. **Not specifying measurement sites** for T_core (tympanic / oesophageal / rectal) — examiners expect this precision.
> 6. **In Part A**, writing only a definition without an example — deduct 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Strain ≠ Stress:** Stress is external demand; strain is the body's internal response.
- **Four measure families:** Cardiovascular, Metabolic, Thermoregulatory, Subjective/Integrative.
- **Heart Rate:** most common; HR_max = 220 – age.
- **HRR (Karvonen):** $HRR = \frac{HR_w - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$; safe limit ≤ 40 % for 8 h.
- **VO₂:** light < 0.5, heavy 1.0–1.5, extreme > 2.0 L/min (Brouha).
- **EE:** $EE = VO_2 \times 20.9$ kJ/min.
- **RMR (ISO 8996):** $RMR = \frac{M - M_0}{M_0}$; class 0 to > 4.
- **T_core limits:** rest ≈ 37 °C; action 38.0 °C; danger 39.0 °C.
- **Mean skin T:** 5-site Hardy-DuBois (0.07 fh + 0.20 ch + 0.20 arm + 0.20 thigh + 0.33 leg).
- **Mean body T:** 0.67 T_core + 0.33 T_skin.
- **Sweat rate:** $SR = \frac{\Delta M + M_{in} - M_{urine}}{\Delta t}$; hazardous > 1.0 L/h.
- **PSI (Moran):** $5 \cdot \frac{T_{ret} - T_{r0}}{39.5 - T_{r0}} + 5 \cdot \frac{HR_t - HR_0}{180 - HR_0}$; band 0–10.
- **RPE (Borg):** 6–20 scale correlates with HR×10; CR-10 for local muscular strain.
- **Standards to cite in answers:** ISO 7243 (heat), ISO 8996 (metabolic), ISO 9886 (strain assessment), NIOSH lifting equation.
- **Engineering controls** must be recommended in every numerical problem (rest cycle, job rotation, PPE redesign, environmental cooling, mechanical aids).
- **Always state measurement sites** for temperature and the device used (Polar strap, tympanic, ingestible pill).
- **KEEP UNITS**: bpm, °C, L/min, kJ/min, %, L/h — examiners deduct marks for missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Work Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Work Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Physical Work Load — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Work Load** is defined as the *aggregate physiological demand imposed on a human operator* while performing a given occupational task over a specified time period. It is quantified in terms of **energy expenditure (kcal/min or watts)**, **oxygen consumption (L/min)**, **cardiac cost (beats/min)**, and **relative metabolic rate (RMR)**.

In the KTU 2024 framework, Physical Work Load is treated as a measurable ergonomic variable that directly governs:
- Worker fatigue onset
- Musculoskeletal injury risk
- Productivity ceiling
- Acceptable shift-length limits

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Body as a Combustion Engine"

Imagine a human body as a finely tuned internal combustion engine. Just as a diesel engine consumes fuel (diesel) and produces mechanical work + heat, the body consumes **chemical energy from food (glucose, fatty acids)** and converts it to **mechanical work** plus **heat dissipation** through sweat and radiation.

| Engine Concept | Human Body Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Fuel Injector | Digestive system |
| Combustion Chamber | Mitochondria (cellular) |
| Fuel Consumption Rate | Oxygen Consumption (VO₂) |
| Exhaust Heat | Sweating / Radiative heat loss |
| RPM / Torque Load | Heart Rate (HR) / Muscle Tension |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (Industry Standard):** The human body operates at an efficiency of roughly **20–25%** when converting food energy into external mechanical work. The remaining 75–80% is dissipated as heat — this is why heavy physical labor makes us *hot and sweaty* rather than just *tired*.

## 1.3 Classification of Physical Work

Physical work is broadly classified into two fundamental categories — understanding this distinction is **critical** for KTU exam answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Work (Isometric):** Muscle contraction *without* joint movement. Energy is consumed continuously but no useful external work is produced. Example: holding a heavy box with arms extended. Highly fatiguing due to continuous pressure on blood vessels (occlusion of blood flow → oxygen debt).
>
> **Dynamic Work (Isotonic):** Muscle contraction *with* rhythmic joint movement. Blood circulation continues, oxygen is delivered, and lactate is flushed. Example: walking, cycling, lifting with motion. Far less fatiguing for equivalent energy output.

**Static Work is approximately 4× more fatiguing than dynamic work** for the same force output — this is a frequent KTU Part A question.

## 1.4 Components of Total Energy Expenditure

A worker's total daily energy cost comprises:

$$\text{Total Energy} = \text{BMR} + \text{Work Metabolism} + \text{Specific Dynamic Action (SDA)} + \text{Activity Adjustment}$$

- **BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate):** Energy for keeping the body alive at rest (~1.2 kcal/min for an average adult).
- **Work Metabolism:** Extra energy required for the task.
- **SDA:** Energy cost of digesting food (~6–10% of caloric intake).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3 | Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Metabolism Cascade (Step-by-Step Physiology)

When a person performs physical work, the body follows a deterministic energy supply hierarchy:

1. **Step 1 — ATP-PCr System (0–10 sec):** Phosphocreatine breakdown provides immediate ATP. No oxygen required (anaerobic).
2. **Step 2 — Anaerobic Glycolysis (10 sec – 2 min):** Glucose converted to lactate. Oxygen still not required; lactate accumulates.
3. **Step 3 — Aerobic Oxidation (>2 min, sustained work):** Glucose + fatty acids oxidized using inhaled **O₂**. This is the *steady-state* work zone.
4. **Step 4 — Oxygen Debt Recovery:** Post-work, the body consumes extra O₂ to clear lactate, restore PCr, and replenish oxygen stores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Sustained industrial work must keep the worker in the *aerobic zone (Step 3)*. If oxygen supply cannot meet demand, the worker shifts to anaerobic metabolism, accumulates lactate, and fatigue escalates rapidly.

## 2.2 Key Metrics Used in Ergonomic Workload Assessment

### 2.2.1 Oxygen Consumption (VO₂)
- **VO₂ max:** Maximum oxygen uptake capacity (typically 3–5 L/min for trained adults).
- **Working VO₂** should ideally be **< 50% of VO₂ max** for 8-hour shifts.
- **1 L of O₂ ≈ 5 kcal (4.985 kcal precisely, using 20.1 kJ/L).**

### 2.2.2 Heart Rate (HR)
- A linear relationship exists between HR and oxygen consumption up to ~140 bpm.
- **Max HR ≈ 220 − Age (years)** (Tanaka formula, more accurate than classical 220 − age).
- **Heart Rate Reserve (HRR):**
$$\text{HRR} = \text{HR}_{max} - \text{HR}_{rest}$$
- **Target Working HR** for an acceptable 8-hour workload:
$$\text{HR}_{work} \leq \text{HR}_{rest} + 0.4 \times \text{HRR}$$

### 2.2.3 Energy Cost
$$\text{Energy Cost (kcal/min)} = \frac{\text{Force (kgf)} \times \text{Distance (m)}}{\text{Efficiency} \times \text{Time (min)}}$$

### 2.2.4 Relative Metabolic Rate (RMR)
$$\text{RMR} = \frac{\text{Metabolic Rate during work} - \text{Resting Metabolic Rate}}{\text{Resting Metabolic Rate}}$$

RMR is dimensionless and used to compare workloads across workers of different body sizes.

### 2.2.5 Acceptable Work Load Classification (Based on ISO 8996 / KTU Reference)

| Workload Class | Energy Expenditure (kcal/min) | HR (beats/min) | RMR | Example Task |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Light | 2.5 – 5.0 | 75 – 100 | 0 – 1 | Desk work, inspection |
| Moderate | 5.0 – 7.5 | 100 – 125 | 1 – 2 | Walking with light load |
| Heavy | 7.5 – 10.0 | 125 – 150 | 2 – 4 | Manual lifting, forging |
| Very Heavy | 10.0 – 12.5 | 150 – 175 | 4 – 7 | Digging, sustained heavy labor |
| Extremely Heavy | $\geq$ 12.5 | $\geq$ 175 | $\geq$ 7 | Lumberjack work, coal mining |

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. Nearly every numerical in KTU Module 3 is solved from these.

| $\#$ | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Energy} = \text{VO}_2 \times 5 \text{ kcal/L}$ | VO₂ in L/min | Convert O₂ consumption to kcal |
| 2 | $\text{HR}_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}$ | bpm, years | Max safe heart rate |
| 3 | $\text{HRR} = \text{HR}_{max} - \text{HR}_{rest}$ | bpm | Working capacity reserve |
| 4 | $\text{Target HR} = \text{HR}_{rest} + 0.4(\text{HRR})$ | bpm | Acceptable 8-hr work HR |
| 5 | $\text{Net Cardiac Cost} = \text{HR}_{work} - \text{HR}_{rest}$ | beats/min | Workload indicator |
| 6 | $\text{Work} = F \times d$ | kgf·m | Mechanical work output |
| 7 | $\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | dimensionless | Human efficiency ≈ 20–25% |
| 8 | $\text{BMR (male)} = 66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H - 6.755A$ | W:kg, H:cm, A:yrs | kcal/day (Harris-Benedict) |
| 9 | $\text{BMR (female)} = 655.1 + 9.563W + 1.850H - 4.676A$ | W:kg, H:cm, A:yrs | kcal/day (Harris-Benedict) |
| 10 | $\text{Work-Rest Ratio} = \frac{t_{work}}{t_{rest}}$ | min/min | Fatigue management |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars used:** Absolute values are notated as $\vert x \vert$ written outside tables to prevent markdown table breakage. The table above uses parentheses, not pipes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Layout Design:** Designing assembly line tasks that keep workers in the *moderate* zone.
- **Occupational Safety:** Determining maximum shift durations for firefighters, miners, and soldiers.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Calibrating physiotherapy workloads.
- **Sports Ergonomics:** Designing training zones using HRR percentages.
- **Space & Aviation:** Sustained workloads in micro-gravity environments where VO₂ max is reduced ~15–25%.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3 | Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mandate:** KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy weight on numerical problem-solving. Below are fully worked problems with no skipped steps. Practice each one for at least 30 minutes.

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Energy Expenditure from Oxygen Consumption

**Question:** A worker cycles a bicycle ergometer and consumes oxygen at a steady rate of **1.8 L/min** for **20 minutes**. Calculate (a) the total energy expended in kcal, and (b) the energy expended in kilojoules. Take 1 L O₂ = 5 kcal.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a): Energy in kcal**

We are given that **1 litre of O₂ consumed ≈ 5 kcal** of energy released in the body. Therefore, energy expended per minute is:

$$\text{Energy per minute} = \text{VO}_2 \times 5 \text{ kcal/L} = 1.8 \times 5 = 9.0 \text{ kcal/min}$$

For a duration of 20 minutes, total energy is:

$$\text{Total Energy} = 9.0 \text{ kcal/min} \times 20 \text{ min} = 180 \text{ kcal}$$

**Part (b): Energy in kilojoules**

Using the conversion **1 kcal = 4.186 kJ**:

$$\text{Total Energy in kJ} = 180 \text{ kcal} \times 4.186 \text{ kJ/kcal}$$

$$\text{Total Energy in kJ} = 753.48 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** (a) **180 kcal**, (b) **≈ 753.48 kJ**. This falls in the *heavy work* category per ISO 8996.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Acceptable Heart Rate Calculation

**Question:** A 32-year-old male factory worker has a resting heart rate of **72 bpm**. Calculate (a) his maximum heart rate, (b) heart rate reserve, and (c) the upper limit of acceptable working heart rate for an 8-hour shift.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a): Maximum Heart Rate**

Using the Tanaka formula $\text{HR}_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}$:

$$\text{HR}_{max} = 220 - 32 = 188 \text{ bpm}$$

**Part (b): Heart Rate Reserve**

Subtracting resting HR from maximum HR:

$$\text{HRR} = \text{HR}_{max} - \text{HR}_{rest} = 188 - 72 = 116 \text{ bpm}$$

**Part (c): Acceptable Working HR (40% HRR criterion)**

Applying the industrial ergonomics formula:

$$\text{HR}_{work} = \text{HR}_{rest} + 0.4 \times \text{HRR}$$

$$\text{HR}_{work} = 72 + 0.4 \times 116 = 72 + 46.4 = 118.4 \text{ bpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** (a) **188 bpm**, (b) **116 bpm**, (c) **≈ 118 bpm**. If the worker's HR exceeds 118 bpm sustained, fatigue and risk of over-exertion rise sharply. Management must redesign the task.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Work, Energy, and Human Efficiency

**Question:** A labourer lifts a **25 kg load to a height of 1.5 m** in **2 seconds**, repeating this **20 times per minute**. Calculate (a) mechanical work per lift, (b) total mechanical work per minute, (c) total energy expended by body (assume efficiency 20%), and (d) classify the workload per ISO 8996.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a): Work per lift**

Work = Force × Distance. The force required to lift 25 kg vertically is equal to its weight (using g = 9.81 m/s²). However, in ergonomic units, weight is often expressed in kgf. We'll use 1 kgf = 9.81 N, but for body work calculations, the formula simplifies to:

$$W = m \times g \times h = 25 \times 9.81 \times 1.5$$

$$W = 367.875 \text{ J per lift}$$

**Part (b): Total mechanical work per minute**

Given 20 lifts per minute:

$$W_{total} = 367.875 \times 20 = 7357.5 \text{ J/min} = 7.3575 \text{ kJ/min}$$

**Part (c): Energy expended by the body**

Efficiency $\eta = 20\%$, so:

$$E_{body} = \frac{W_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{7.3575 \text{ kJ/min}}{0.20} = 36.79 \text{ kJ/min}$$

Converting to kcal/min (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ):

$$E_{body} = \frac{36.79}{4.186} \approx 8.79 \text{ kcal/min}$$

**Part (d): Workload Classification**

Comparing with the ISO 8996 table:

$$\boxed{8.79 \text{ kcal/min} \rightarrow \text{HEAVY WORK}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert kgf·m to joules. Remember: **1 kgf·m = 9.81 J**. Always use SI units in derivation problems unless the question explicitly demands CGS.

---

## 3.4 Problem 4 — BMR Calculation Using Harris-Benedict Equation

**Question:** Estimate the **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)** for a **45-year-old male worker** who is **170 cm tall** and weighs **70 kg**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Using the male Harris-Benedict equation:

$$\text{BMR} = 66.5 + (13.75 \times W) + (5.003 \times H) - (6.755 \times A)$$

Substituting W = 70, H = 170, A = 45:

$$\text{BMR} = 66.5 + (13.75 \times 70) + (5.003 \times 170) - (6.755 \times 45)$$

$$\text{BMR} = 66.5 + 962.5 + 850.51 - 303.975$$

$$\text{BMR} = 1575.535 \text{ kcal/day}$$

Converting to kcal/min:

$$\text{BMR per minute} = \frac{1575.535}{1440} \approx 1.094 \text{ kcal/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** **BMR ≈ 1575.5 kcal/day** (≈ 1.09 kcal/min). This is the energy cost of *staying alive* — the baseline from which work metabolism is added.

---

## 3.5 Problem 5 — Work-Rest Cycle Calculation

**Question:** A worker performs a task requiring **8.0 kcal/min**. His resting metabolic rate is **1.2 kcal/min**, and the maximum sustainable aerobic energy expenditure is **6.0 kcal/min**. Calculate the **work-rest ratio** that will keep the worker below the aerobic limit (assume recovery consumes O₂ at 1.5× resting rate during rest).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Net work metabolic cost per minute:**

$$\text{Net Work Cost} = 8.0 - 1.2 = 6.8 \text{ kcal/min above rest}$$

**Step 2 — Allowable net cost during work phase:**

$$\text{Allowable} = 6.0 - 1.2 = 4.8 \text{ kcal/min above rest}$$

**Step 3 — Recovery during rest:**

If during rest, recovery proceeds at 1.5× resting rate, the net O₂ debt cleared per minute of rest is:

$$\text{Recovery Rate} = 1.5 \times 1.2 - 1.2 = 0.6 \text{ kcal/min of debt cleared}$$

**Step 4 — Setting up balance (debt incurred = debt cleared):**

Let $t_w$ = work time, $t_r$ = rest time in a cycle.

$$\text{Debt incurred} = 6.8 \times t_w$$
$$\text{Debt cleared} = 0.6 \times t_r$$

Setting them equal:

$$6.8 \times t_w = 0.6 \times t_r \implies \frac{t_w}{t_r} = \frac{0.6}{6.8} = 0.0882$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The worker should work approximately **0.088 minutes for every 1 minute of rest** — i.e., roughly **5 min work : 57 min rest**. This is an extreme case indicating the task is *unsustainable* for 8-hour shifts and **must be redesigned**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3 | Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Supply Pathway in the Human Body

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Intake Carbohydrates Fats Proteins] --> B[Cellular Mitochondria]
    C[Inhaled O2 from Lungs] --> B
    B --> D{Aerobic Capacity Available?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Aerobic Oxidation Steady State]
    D -- No --> F[Anaerobic Glycolysis]
    E --> G[Mechanical Work Output plus Heat Loss]
    F --> H[Lactate Accumulation]
    H --> I[Oxygen Debt]
    I --> J[Recovery Phase Increased VO2]
    J --> G
```

## 4.2 Workload Classification Decision Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Identified] --> B[Measure VO2 and Heart Rate]
    B --> C{VO2 less than 0.5 L per min?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Light Work RMR less than 1]
    C -- No --> E{HR between 100 to 125 bpm?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Moderate Work RMR 1 to 2]
    E -- No --> G{HR between 125 to 150 bpm?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Heavy Work RMR 2 to 4]
    G -- No --> I{HR between 150 to 175 bpm?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Very Heavy Work RMR 4 to 7]
    I -- No --> K[Extremely Heavy Work RMR above 7]
    D --> L[Acceptable 8 hour shift]
    F --> L
    H --> M[Max 4 hour shift Rest cycles mandatory]
    J --> N[Max 2 hour shift Frequent microbreaks]
    K --> O[Task Redesign Required Mechanical Aids Essential]
```

## 4.3 Fatigue-Recovery Cycle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Work Shift] --> B[Phase 1 Aerobic Steady State]
    B --> C[Phase 2 Lactate Threshold Approaching]
    C --> D{Work Continued?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Phase 3 Oxygen Debt Accumulation]
    E --> F[Muscle Fatigue Failure Risk]
    D -- No --> G[Recovery Phase EPOC Elevated]
    G --> H[PCr Restoration Lactate Clearance]
    H --> I[Return to Baseline]
    I --> B
    F --> J[Compulsory Rest Stop Injury Risk]
    J --> G
```

## 4.4 Inputs to Physical Workload Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Worker Demographics Age Sex Weight Height] --> E[Workload Assessment]
    B[Task Parameters Force Duration Frequency Posture] --> E
    C[Environmental Factors Temperature Humidity Noise] --> E
    D[Measurement Tools HR Monitor Gas Analyzer Calorimeter] --> E
    E --> F[Output Energy Expenditure in kcal per min]
    E --> G[Output Heart Rate in bpm]
    E --> H[Output VO2 in L per min]
    E --> I[Output RMR Dimensionless]
    E --> J[Decision Acceptable or Redesign]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 3 | KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question 1: Define Physical Work Load and differentiate between static and dynamic work.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Physical work load is the physiological demand imposed on a worker during occupational tasks, quantified in terms of energy expenditure, oxygen consumption, and heart rate response.

| Parameter | Static Work | Dynamic Work |
|---|---|---|
| Muscle action | Isometric, no joint movement | Isotonic, rhythmic joint movement |
| Blood flow | Occluded, oxygen debt builds | Continuous, lactate flushed |
| Fatigue rate | Very high (4× faster) | Moderate |
| Example | Holding a heavy object overhead | Walking, lifting with motion |

> *(Full 3 marks requires the comparison table + example.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

### Question 2: Explain the concept of Oxygen Debt (EPOC).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Oxygen Debt**, now technically termed **Excess Post-exercise Oxygen Consumption (EPOC)**, is the additional volume of oxygen consumed *after* the cessation of physical work, beyond the resting oxygen requirement. It is used by the body to:

1. **Restore phosphocreatine (PCr) stores** in muscles.
2. **Oxidize accumulated lactic acid** back to glucose (Cori cycle).
3. **Re-oxygenate myoglobin and hemoglobin.**
4. **Elevate body temperature and respiration rates** to baseline.

A large oxygen debt indicates anaerobic metabolism during the task and predicts rapid fatigue onset.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**a)** Define the term *Physical Work Load*. Discuss in detail the various methods used to measure physical work load in industrial ergonomics. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A 40-year-old male worker weighing 75 kg and 175 cm tall operates a hand drill. His resting heart rate is 70 bpm and his working heart rate is 130 bpm. Calculate (i) maximum heart rate, (ii) heart rate reserve, (iii) net cardiac cost, and (iv) determine whether the task is acceptable for an 8-hour shift. Use Tanaka's formula. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Definition & Measurement Methods (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Physical work load is the quantitative measure of the physiological cost incurred by a worker in performing a given occupational task, expressed as energy expenditure, oxygen uptake, heart rate, or relative metabolic rate.

**Measurement Methods (5 marks):**

1. **Direct Calorimetry:** The worker is placed in an insulated chamber and the heat dissipated is measured directly. Highly accurate but impractical for industry.
2. **Indirect Calorimetry:** Oxygen consumption and CO₂ production are measured via a mouthpiece or face mask. The energy expenditure is computed as $E = \text{VO}_2 \times 5 \text{ kcal/L}$. The most widely used field method.
3. **Heart Rate Monitoring:** A linear relationship exists between heart rate (up to 140 bpm) and oxygen consumption. Wireless HR monitors are non-intrusive and ideal for field studies.
4. **Doubly Labeled Water (DLW) Method:** The subject drinks water containing isotopic ^2H and ^18O. The differential elimination rate measures total daily energy expenditure over 7–14 days.
5. **Task Analysis & Time Study:** Predicted energy costs are tabulated from standard reference tables (e.g., ISO 8996) based on task type.
6. **Subjective Scales:** Borg's RPE (Rating of Perceived Exertion) scale — 6 to 20 — correlates with HR and oxygen consumption.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** Age = 40, Weight = 75 kg, Height = 175 cm, HR_rest = 70 bpm, HR_work = 130 bpm.

**(i) Maximum Heart Rate (2 marks):**

Using Tanaka's formula:

$$\text{HR}_{max} = 220 - \text{Age} = 220 - 40 = 180 \text{ bpm}$$

**(ii) Heart Rate Reserve (2 marks):**

$$\text{HRR} = \text{HR}_{max} - \text{HR}_{rest} = 180 - 70 = 110 \text{ bpm}$$

**(iii) Net Cardiac Cost (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{Net Cardiac Cost} = \text{HR}_{work} - \text{HR}_{rest} = 130 - 70 = 60 \text{ bpm}$$

**(iv) Acceptability Check (1.5 marks):**

Acceptable working HR criterion (40% HRR rule):

$$\text{HR}_{acceptable} = \text{HR}_{rest} + 0.4 \times \text{HRR} = 70 + 0.4 \times 110 = 70 + 44 = 114 \text{ bpm}$$

Since the actual working HR (130 bpm) **exceeds** the acceptable 114 bpm:

> **Conclusion:** The task is **NOT acceptable** for a full 8-hour shift. Task redesign or work-rest cycling is mandatory. Recommended: introduce 10-minute rest breaks every 50 minutes of work.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the concept of *Relative Metabolic Rate (RMR)*. How is it used to classify workload in industries? Provide the classification table. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A worker performs a repetitive lifting task. Each lift raises a 30 kg mass through 1.2 m at a rate of 12 lifts per minute. The body operates at 20% efficiency. Determine the worker's energy expenditure rate in (i) watts, (ii) kcal/min, and (iii) classify the workload. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — RMR Concept (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Relative Metabolic Rate (RMR) is a dimensionless index that expresses the work metabolic rate as a multiple of the resting metabolic rate:

$$\text{RMR} = \frac{\text{Metabolic Rate}_{work} - \text{Metabolic Rate}_{rest}}{\text{Metabolic Rate}_{rest}}$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
RMR allows direct comparison of work intensity across workers of different body sizes, ages, and genders. A small worker and a large worker performing the same task will have different absolute kcal/min costs, but their RMR values will be comparable.

**Classification Table (4 marks):**

| Workload Class | Energy Expenditure (kcal/min) | RMR | HR (bpm) | Shift Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Light | 2.5 – 5.0 | 0 – 1 | 75 – 100 | Full 8 hours |
| Moderate | 5.0 – 7.5 | 1 – 2 | 100 – 125 | Full 8 hours with breaks |
| Heavy | 7.5 – 10.0 | 2 – 4 | 125 – 150 | Max 4 hours |
| Very Heavy | 10.0 – 12.5 | 4 – 7 | 150 – 175 | Max 2 hours |
| Extremely Heavy | $\geq$ 12.5 | $\geq$ 7 | $\geq$ 175 | Not sustainable; redesign |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** m = 30 kg, h = 1.2 m, rate = 12 lifts/min, η = 0.20.

**Step 1 — Work per lift (1.5 marks):**

$$W_{lift} = m \times g \times h = 30 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 = 353.16 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Work per minute (1.5 marks):**

$$W_{min} = 353.16 \times 12 = 4237.92 \text{ J/min} \approx 4.238 \text{ kJ/min}$$

**Step 3 — Body energy expenditure (1.5 marks):**

$$E_{body} = \frac{W_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{4.238}{0.20} = 21.19 \text{ kJ/min}$$

**(i) In Watts (1 mark):**

$$P = 21.19 \text{ kJ/min} = \frac{21190}{60} \approx 353.2 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) In kcal/min (1 mark):**

$$E_{body} = \frac{21.19 \text{ kJ/min}}{4.186 \text{ kJ/kcal}} \approx 5.06 \text{ kcal/min}$$

**(iii) Classification (0.5 mark):**

> **Moderate Work** (5.0 – 7.5 kcal/min range) — but on the lower end. Task is sustainable for 8 hours with planned microbreaks every 90 minutes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Many students write **75 kgf·m** instead of converting to **joules (× 9.81)**. Always show the SI conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing BMR with Total Daily Energy:** BMR is *resting*. Adding work metabolism gives *total* energy expenditure — KTU questions often ask specifically for one or the other.
> 3. **Static vs Dynamic Confusion:** Holding a load (static) consumes energy but produces *zero mechanical work*. Forgetting this leads to division by zero in efficiency calculations.
> 4. **Wrong Tanaka Formula:** Some students use the classical 220 − age. For workers above 40, this overestimates HR_max by 5–10 bpm. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly endorses Tanaka.
> 5. **RMR Calculation:** Forgetting to subtract resting metabolic rate yields incorrect "metabolic ratio" — must use *net* metabolic rate.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Physical work load = physiological cost of occupational tasks.
- ☐ **Static work** is ~4× more fatiguing than dynamic work for the same force.
- ☐ **1 L O₂ ≈ 5 kcal** (the most-used constant in Module 3).
- ☐ **Human efficiency ≈ 20–25%**; remainder dissipated as heat.
- ☐ **Tanaka HR_max = 220 − Age** (preferred over classical formula).
- ☐ **HRR = HR_max − HR_rest**; **40% HRR rule** governs 8-hr acceptability.
- ☐ **RMR** is dimensionless; allows cross-worker comparison.
- ☐ **Harris-Benedict:** Male BMR = 66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H − 6.755A.
- ☐ **EPOC (Oxygen Debt):** post-exercise O₂ consumption for recovery.
- ☐ **Workload classes:** Light / Moderate / Heavy / Very Heavy / Extreme.
- ☐ **Acceptable VO₂:** Should be < 50% VO₂_max for 8-hr shifts.
- ☐ **Borg RPE scale (6–20)** correlates with HR × 10.
- ☐ **Static work** causes blood vessel occlusion → anaerobic fatigue.
- ☐ **Work-Rest cycle** calculation ensures debt incurred = debt cleared.
- ☐ **Three energy systems:** ATP-PCr (10s), Anaerobic (2 min), Aerobic (>2 min).
- ☐ **Energy = Force × Distance** (work) and **Power = Work / Time**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Work Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Efficiency** is formally defined as the ratio of useful physical/mental work output produced by a human operator to the total metabolic energy expended in performing a given task, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{Work Efficiency } (\eta) = \frac{\text{Useful Work Output}}{\text{Total Energy Expended}} \times 100\%$$

In the context of **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)**, work efficiency transcends simple mechanical output. It is a **multidimensional performance metric** that quantifies how optimally a human operator's physical, cognitive, and physiological resources are converted into productive task accomplishment while maintaining health, safety, and well-being.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Body as a Combustion Engine**
> Think of a human worker exactly like a **car engine**:
> - **Fuel Input** = Food calories (kcal) consumed by the worker
> - **Useful Work** = Distance travelled (productive output)
> - **Wasted Energy** = Heat, exhaust, friction (sweating, panting, fatigue)
> - **Efficiency** = km per litre equivalent (productive work per calorie)

Just as engineers tune an engine for maximum km/litre, Human Factors specialists design **workstations, tools, schedules, and environments** to maximize the worker's km/calorie — getting maximum productivity without burning out the engine (worker).

### 1.3 Standard Physiological Constants & Metrics

The following **standard human physiological constants** govern work efficiency calculations:

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **1 kcal** | **4.186 kJ** (≈ 4.2 kJ) | kiloJoules |
| **1 Watt** | **0.143 kcal/min** | kcal/min |
| **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)** | **1.0 – 1.2 kcal/min** (average adult) | kcal/min |
| **Standard Daily Energy Output (Voit's Standard)** | **2400 – 3000 kcal/day** (women) / **3000 – 3600 kcal/day** (men) | kcal/day |
| **Maximum Oxygen Uptake (VO₂ max)** | **3.0 – 4.0 L/min** (trained athletes: up to 6 L/min) | L/min |
| **Resting Heart Rate** | **60 – 80 bpm** | beats per minute |
| **Maximum Heart Rate (HR max)** | **220 − Age** (in years) | bpm |
| **Sustained Work Heart Rate Limit** | **110 – 120 bpm** (8-hr shift safe) | bpm |

### 1.4 Classification of Human Work

Work efficiency must be analyzed differently for each category:

1. **Dynamic (Isotonic) Work** — Movement with muscular shortening (walking, lifting, hammering)
2. **Static (Isometric) Work** — Sustained muscular contraction without movement (holding a tool, posture maintenance)
3. **Mental/Cognitive Work** — Information processing, decision-making, vigilance tasks
4. **Combined Work** — Typical industrial tasks integrating physical and mental components

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Static work is far more fatiguing than dynamic work at the same energy cost because it occludes blood flow to contracted muscles, causing rapid accumulation of metabolic by-products (lactic acid).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deconstruction of Work Efficiency — The Operational Logic

The seemingly simple ratio hides **six interacting subsystems** that determine efficiency. Each must be analysed to design truly efficient workplaces.

#### Step 1 — Energy Input Subsystem
The worker draws energy from three sources:
- **Immediate ATP-PCr system** (0–10 sec) — phosphocreatine breakdown
- **Anaerobic glycolysis** (10 sec – 2 min) — produces lactic acid
- **Aerobic oxidation** (> 2 min) — sustainable, requires O₂

#### Step 2 — Work Conversion Subsystem
Oxygen is transported via the **cardiopulmonary system**:
$$\text{HR} \times \text{Stroke Volume} = \text{Cardiac Output} \;\; (\dot{Q})$$

#### Step 3 — Muscular Output Subsystem
Muscles convert chemical energy (ATP) into mechanical force, with efficiency limits imposed by fiber-type composition (Type I — slow oxidative; Type II — fast glycolytic).

#### Step 4 — Environmental Subsystem
Heat, humidity, noise, vibration, and lighting **modify both the worker's effort and perception of effort**.

#### Step 5 — Organizational Subsystem
Shift design, work-rest schedules, task variety, autonomy, and supervisory style affect sustained efficiency.

#### Step 6 — Cognitive-Affective Subsystem
Motivation, attention, boredom, mental fatigue, and emotional state modulate physiological capacity by ±20–30%.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Factor — KTU Examiner Logic

| Factor | Mechanism (Why it matters) |
|---|---|
| **Work-Rest Ratio** | Recovery of phosphocreatine stores and lactic acid clearance takes 3–5× longer than the work period itself |
| **Posture** | Poor posture increases static muscle work by 25–50% (Grandjean) |
| **Temperature** | Above 25°C, cardiac output diverts from muscles to skin → efficiency drops |
| **Skill Level** | Skilled workers reduce extraneous motions, improving mechanical efficiency |
| **Motivation** | Affects voluntary component of effort; can raise aerobic work capacity by 10–20% |

### 2.3 The Aerobic–Anaerobic Threshold — Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aerobic Threshold:** Below ~50% VO₂ max, work is purely aerobic — sustainable for 8 hours.
> **Anaerobic Threshold:** Above ~50% VO₂ max, lactic acid accumulates — work is no longer sustainable for full shifts.

The relationship between heart rate and oxygen consumption is approximately linear up to HR max, making **heart rate the most practical field indicator of work intensity**.

### 2.4 Work-Rest Cycle Formulas (KTU High-Yield)

The classical **Voit–Pettenkofer–Lehmann** framework gives KTU's standard formula set:

#### Lehmann's Rest Allowance Formula (for sustained 8-hr work)
$$\%R = \frac{(HR_{work} - HR_{rest})}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $\%R$ = **Rest allowance as percentage of shift time**
- $HR_{work}$ = Average working heart rate (bpm)
- $HR_{rest}$ = Resting heart rate (bpm)
- $HR_{max}$ = Maximum heart rate (220 − age) (bpm)

#### Pejtersen's Index (Energy Expenditure Estimation)
$$E = 0.5 \cdot (HR - 60) \;\; \text{kcal/min}$$

#### Sustained Work Limit (8-hr shift)
Recommended maximum aerobic work should keep heart rate **below 110–120 bpm** for 8-hour shifts.

### 2.5 Comprehensive KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = W_{out} / E_{in} \times 100$ | Work efficiency | % |
| 2 | $E = 0.5(HR-60)$ | Pejtersen's energy estimate | kcal/min |
| 3 | $\%R = \frac{HR_w - HR_r}{HR_{max}-HR_r}\times 100$ | Lehmann's rest allowance | % |
| 4 | $HR_{max} = 220 - Age$ | Max heart rate | bpm |
| 5 | $\dot{V}O_2\,max \approx 3.5 + 0.2 \cdot v$ | VO₂ for walking at speed $v$ | mL/kg/min |
| 6 | $T_{end} = a \cdot e^{-b \cdot L_{pct}}$ | Endurance vs % MVC | minutes |
| 7 | $E_{total} = BMR + E_{work} + E_{SDA}$ | Total daily energy | kcal/day |
| 8 | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{F \cdot d}{E} \times 100$ | Mechanical efficiency of lifting | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for KTU Exams:** Always state assumptions — atmospheric pressure, age, training state of subject, and measurement instrumentation (ergograph, Kymograph, or modern heart-rate monitor).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Work-efficiency principles are applied in:
- **Ergonomic workstation design** (automotive assembly lines — Toyota Production System)
- **Manual materials handling** (NIOSH lifting equation, recommended weight limit)
- **Shift scheduling** in aviation, healthcare, nuclear control rooms
- **Rehabilitation engineering** — designing jobs for workers with disabilities
- **Sports science & military training** — optimizing load/rest cycles
- **Software UX design** — cognitive load minimization to boost mental efficiency
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mechanical Efficiency of Human Lifting

> **Problem Context:** A worker of mass **75 kg** lifts a **20 kg** load through a vertical height of **1.5 m** in **3 seconds**. His metabolic energy expenditure during the lift is measured at **12 kcal/min**. Calculate his **mechanical work efficiency** for this task.

#### Step 1 — Compute Useful Mechanical Work Output
The work done against gravity is the load weight × vertical displacement.

$$W_{out} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$W_{out} = 20 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 1.5 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{out} = 294.3 \text{ J}$$

#### Step 2 — Convert to Standard Energy Unit (kcal)
Since 1 kcal = 4186 J:

$$W_{out} = \frac{294.3 \text{ J}}{4186 \text{ J/kcal}}$$

$$W_{out} = 0.0703 \text{ kcal}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Total Energy Expended in 3 seconds
Energy expenditure rate is 12 kcal/min. Convert to energy used in 3 seconds.

$$E_{in} = 12 \text{ kcal/min} \times \frac{3 \text{ s}}{60 \text{ s/min}}$$

$$E_{in} = 0.6 \text{ kcal}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Efficiency Formula

$$\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{0.0703 \text{ kcal}}{0.6 \text{ kcal}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta \approx 11.7\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Human mechanical efficiency for short, dynamic lifting is typically **10–20%**. For sustained cycling (smooth, rhythmic) it can rise to **22–25%** — the highest of any common human motion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lehmann's Rest Allowance Calculation

> **Problem Context:** A female assembly worker (age 35) is measured with the following data:
> - Resting HR = 72 bpm
> - Working HR = 130 bpm
> - Compute the **percentage rest allowance** required for an 8-hour shift.

#### Step 1 — Compute Maximum Heart Rate
Use the Fox formula (220 − Age):

$$HR_{max} = 220 - 35 = 185 \text{ bpm}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Lehmann's Formula
Substitute into:

$$\%R = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{130 - 72}{185 - 72} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{58}{113} \times 100$$

$$\%R \approx 51.3\%$$

#### Step 3 — Convert to Absolute Rest Time in an 8-hr Shift

$$T_{rest} = 0.513 \times 8 \text{ hr} \approx 4.1 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** An allowance of 51% rest is **unrealistically high** — this indicates the task is too strenuous. The job should be redesigned, or a 50/50 work/rest microcycle (e.g., 5 min work / 5 min rest) introduced. Sustainable industrial tasks should yield $\%R < 25\%$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparison of Static vs Dynamic Work Efficiency

> **Problem Context:** A worker holds a 5 kg tool (static work) for 2 minutes at 60% Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC). Oxygen consumption is 0.4 L/min. Compare with the same worker dynamically lifting the 5 kg tool at 12 cycles/min, where oxygen consumption is 1.2 L/min. Compute efficiency per unit of mechanical work.

#### Step 1 — Static Work Energy Cost
Time held = 2 min; O₂ cost = 0.4 L/min.
Energy of O₂ ≈ 5 kcal/L (physiological value).

$$E_{static} = 0.4 \times 5 \times 2 = 4.0 \text{ kcal}$$

#### Step 2 — Dynamic Work Energy Cost
Same duration: 2 min; O₂ cost = 1.2 L/min.

$$E_{dynamic} = 1.2 \times 5 \times 2 = 12.0 \text{ kcal}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Mechanical Output
Assuming a lift of 0.5 m at 12 cycles/min for 2 min = 24 lifts:

$$W_{mech} = 5 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 24 = 588.7 \text{ J}$$

#### Step 4 — Compare Efficiencies

$$\eta_{static} \approx 0\% \text{ (no displacement)}$$

$$\eta_{dynamic} = \frac{588.7 / 4186}{12} \times 100 \approx 1.17\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Despite static work costing **one-third** the energy, it produces **zero** useful displacement work → effectively 0% efficiency. This explains why **static (isometric) work is severely fatiguing** — high perceived effort, low output, and rapid onset of muscular pain (lactic acidosis).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Work-Rest Strategy

| Workload Intensity | HR (bpm) | % VO₂ max | Recommended Work:Rest | Cycle Duration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Light** | < 90 | < 25% | Continuous (no rest) | 8 hr |
| **Moderate** | 90–110 | 25–50% | 4:1 | 60 min |
| **Heavy** | 110–130 | 50–65% | 3:1 | 30 min |
| **Very Heavy** | 130–150 | 65–80% | 2:1 | 15 min |
| **Extremely Heavy** | 150–170 | 80–95% | 1:1 | 5–10 min |
| **Maximal** | > 170 | > 95% | 1:2 | < 2 min |

---

### 3.5 Endurance Time vs %MVC — The Rohmert Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rohmert's Law (1973):** Maximum endurance time decreases **exponentially** with increasing percentage of Maximum Voluntary Contraction. Static holding at > 50% MVC cannot be sustained beyond 1–2 minutes.

$$T_{end} \approx 10 \cdot e^{-0.024 \cdot (\%MVC - 15)} \;\; \text{(seconds)}$$

**Worked example:** Holding at 50% MVC:

$$T_{end} \approx 10 \cdot e^{-0.024 \times 35} \approx 4.3 \text{ s}$$

*(approximate — empirical curve; many sources give Rohmert's 60% MVC = 60 s, 100% MVC = 5–6 s)*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Work Efficiency System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Food Energy Intake] --> A1[Digestive System]
    A1 --> A2[Bloodstream Nutrient Pool]
    A2 --> A3[ATP Regeneration]
    A3 --> A4[Muscular System]
    A4 --> A5[Mechanical Work Output]
    A5 --> A6[Useful Productive Task]
    A6 --> A7[Work Efficiency Achieved]
    A4 -.-> B1[Heat Loss via Sweating]
    A4 -.-> B2[Respiratory CO2 Loss]
    A4 -.-> B3[Metabolic Waste Lactate]
    A1 -.-> B4[Basal Metabolism]
    C1[Task Design] --> A6
    C2[Workstation Layout] --> A6
    C3[Tools and Equipment] --> A4
    C4[Environmental Conditions] --> A1
    C5[Work Rest Schedule] --> A4
    C6[Training and Skill] --> A5
    D1[Noise] -.-> A4
    D2[Temperature] -.-> A4
    D3[Lighting] -.-> A6
    D4[Vibration] -.-> A4
```

### 4.2 Work-Rest Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Identify Task Type] --> S1{Static or Dynamic Work?}
    S1 -->|Static| S2[Determine percent MVC]
    S1 -->|Dynamic| S3[Measure Oxygen Consumption]
    S2 --> S4{percent MVC > 50?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Apply 1:2 Work Rest Ratio]
    S4 -->|No| S6[Apply 1:1 Work Rest Ratio]
    S3 --> S7[Compute percent VO2 max]
    S7 --> S8{percent VO2 max < 50?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Continuous Shift 8 hour]
    S8 -->|No| S10[Apply 3:1 or 2:1 Work Rest]
    S5 --> S11[Validate via Heart Rate Monitor]
    S6 --> S11
    S9 --> S11
    S10 --> S11
    S11 --> S12[Average HR less than 110 bpm?]
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Acceptable Job Design]
    S12 -->|No| S14[Redesign Task or Lower Load]
```

### 4.3 The Five Pillars of Work Efficiency (Pillars Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    WE[Work Efficiency Central Goal]
    P1[Pillar 1 Physiological Capacity]
    P2[Pillar 2 Task Design and Method]
    P3[Pillar 3 Environment and Conditions]
    P4[Pillar 4 Work Rest Scheduling]
    P5[Pillar 5 Human Factors Motivation and Training]
    P1 --> WE
    P2 --> WE
    P3 --> WE
    P4 --> WE
    P5 --> WE
    P1 --- P1a[VO2 max, BMR, HR, Strength]
    P2 --- P2a[Motion Economy, Posture, Tools]
    P3 --- P3a[Temp, Light, Noise, Vibration]
    P4 --- P4a[Microcycles, Shift Patterns, Recovery]
    P5 --- P5a[Skill, Learning, Morale, Incentives]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Safeguard Compliance Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are **purely alphanumeric** (A0, A1, S0, P1, etc.) — no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as node labels. No markdown formatting tags inside quoted labels. All labels are clean uppercase text.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define work efficiency. State the standard daily energy requirement (Voit's Standard) for an average Indian industrial worker.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Work efficiency is the ratio of useful work output to the total metabolic energy expended by a human operator, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Voit's Standard (1 mark for the value):**
- Light work: **2400 – 2800 kcal/day**
- Moderate work: **2800 – 3200 kcal/day**
- Heavy work: **3200 – 3600 kcal/day**

This standard accounts for BMR (~1500 kcal), occupational work (~500–1500 kcal), and Specific Dynamic Action of food (~10%).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any SIX factors affecting the work efficiency of an industrial worker.**

**Model Answer (3 marks = 6 factors × 0.5 each):**

1. **Physical factors** — strength, stamina, cardiovascular fitness (VO₂ max)
2. **Environmental factors** — temperature, humidity, lighting, noise, vibration
3. **Workstation design** — posture, reach, visibility, seat height
4. **Tools and equipment design** — grip, weight, vibration transmission
5. **Work-rest schedule** — microcycles, shift length, rest allowance
6. **Psychosocial factors** — motivation, job satisfaction, supervisory style
7. **Skill and training** — proficiency level, learning curve position

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Q3. (a)** A 28-year-old male worker on an assembly line shows the following measurements during a 4-hour shift:

- Resting heart rate: **70 bpm**
- Working heart rate: **128 bpm**
- Oxygen consumption during work: **1.2 L/min**
- Energy expenditure rate: **6.0 kcal/min**

Calculate:
(i) The percentage rest allowance required using Lehmann's formula. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The total energy expended in 4 hours of continuous work (in kcal). **(2 marks)**
(iii) The maximum sustainable heart rate limit for an 8-hour shift. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **static and dynamic muscular work** in terms of efficiency, fatigue onset, blood flow, and suitability for sustained industrial tasks. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute HR max**

$$HR_{max} = 220 - 28 = 192 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 2: Apply Lehmann's rest allowance formula**

$$\%R = \frac{128 - 70}{192 - 70} \times 100 = \frac{58}{122} \times 100 \approx 47.5\%$$

**Conclusion:** A 47.5% rest allowance means out of every 100 minutes, ~47 min must be rest. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3: Total energy in 4 hours**

$$E_{total} = 6.0 \text{ kcal/min} \times 240 \text{ min} = 1440 \text{ kcal}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Maximum sustainable HR for 8 hours**

Standard 8-hour sustained limit: **110–120 bpm** (cited from Grandjean, Kroemer, and KTU syllabus).
Hence this worker's HR of 128 bpm **exceeds the safe limit** for full-shift work. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Static Work | Dynamic Work |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical output** | Near zero (no displacement) | Substantial (force × distance) |
| **Mechanical efficiency** | ~0% | 15–25% |
| **Fatigue onset** | Very rapid (> 50% MVC = 1–2 min) | Slow and predictable |
| **Blood flow** | Occluded by contraction → lactate build-up | Phasic pumping → lactate cleared |
| **Cardiovascular strain** | High (BP rises sharply) | Moderate, matched to demand |
| **Oxygen demand** | Anaerobic (lactic) | Aerobic (sustained) |
| **Recovery time** | Long (3–5× work time) | Short (1–2× work time) |
| **Industrial suitability** | Poor for > 30% MVC | Excellent for cyclic tasks |

**Diagrammatic aid for board exam:** Draw a 2-column comparison table with arrows showing lactate accumulation in static work.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample QP, Module 3]**

**Q4. (a)** A worker lifts a **25 kg** load from floor to a platform **0.9 m** high repeatedly for 8 hours, completing **10 lifts per minute**. Energy expenditure is **8.5 kcal/min**. Calculate:
(i) The mechanical work done per lift. **(2 marks)**
(ii) The total useful work output in the entire 8-hour shift. **(2 marks)**
(iii) The total energy expended in the 8-hour shift. **(2 marks)**
(iv) The overall mechanical efficiency of the task. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** Explain **Lehmann's method of work-rest scheduling** and the **Rohmert Curve** for static work endurance. Discuss their significance in designing sustainable industrial jobs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Work per lift**

$$W_{lift} = m \cdot g \cdot h = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.9 = 220.7 \text{ J}$$

Converting to kcal:

$$W_{lift} = 220.7 / 4186 = 0.0527 \text{ kcal}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2: Total useful work in 8 hours**

Lifts per shift = 10 lifts/min × 60 min × 8 hr = 4800 lifts

$$W_{total} = 0.0527 \times 4800 = 253.0 \text{ kcal}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Total energy expended**

$$E_{total} = 8.5 \text{ kcal/min} \times 480 \text{ min} = 4080 \text{ kcal}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Overall efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{253.0}{4080} \times 100 \approx 6.2\%$$

**(1 mark)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world comment:** A 6.2% efficiency is low for lifting (typical 10–20%) — suggesting the worker is carrying significant extra body mass or has poor lifting technique. The job design should be reconsidered.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Lehmann's Method (3.5 marks):**
Lehmann (1962) proposed that the percentage of working time that must be allocated to rest can be calculated from heart-rate measurements:

$$\%R = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100$$

**Procedure:**
1. Measure resting HR (morning, seated, 10 min).
2. Measure working HR over representative period.
3. Apply formula.
4. Use the result to design work-rest microcycles (e.g., 50:10, 40:20).

**Significance:** Provides a **physiologically justified** rest allowance, replacing the older 10% rule of thumb.

**Rohmert Curve (3.5 marks):**
Rohmert (1973) established that for static (isometric) work, endurance time decreases exponentially with the percentage of MVC:

- **100% MVC** → ~5–6 seconds endurance
- **50% MVC** → ~1 minute
- **20% MVC** → ~5–10 minutes
- **< 15% MVC** → sustainable for hours

**Significance:** Shows that **static muscular effort must be minimised** in workstation design — use fixtures, jigs, or powered assistance wherever contraction exceeds 15% MVC.

**Board diagram tip:** Draw a graph with %MVC on X-axis (0–100), endurance time on Y-axis (log scale), showing the steep exponential drop.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students LOSE marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Forgetting to convert J → kcal (divide by 4186). Worth 1–2 marks lost.
> 2. **Wrong HR max formula** — Using **"200 − Age"** or **"210 − Age"** (both incorrect for KTU 2024). Use **220 − Age**.
> 3. **Confusing Static and Dynamic work** — Static work has zero mechanical output but high energy cost → 0% efficiency; do NOT calculate $\eta$ for static tasks.
> 4. **Missing the "%" sign** in Lehmann's answer — Always state "% rest allowance", not just a decimal.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — Always specify: "Assuming standard gravity 9.81 m/s²", "Assuming 1 kcal = 4186 J", "Assuming subject is healthy adult".
> 6. **In Q4, forgetting total lifts** — The 8-hour total is 4800 lifts, not just per-minute output.
> 7. **Answering only one choice in Part B** — KTU 2024 ESE mandates: Answer **any ONE FULL question** from each module. Reading both choices before deciding is essential.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Card — Work Efficiency**

- ☐ Work efficiency = Useful output ÷ Energy input × 100% (typically 10–25% for humans).
- ☐ **1 kcal = 4186 J = 4.186 kJ**; **1 W = 0.143 kcal/min**; **1 L O₂ ≈ 5 kcal**.
- ☐ **HR max = 220 − Age**; Sustained work limit = **110–120 bpm**.
- ☐ **Lehmann's %R = (HR_w − HR_r) / (HR_max − HR_r) × 100**.
- ☐ **Pejtersen's** energy estimate: **E (kcal/min) = 0.5 × (HR − 60)**.
- ☐ **Voit's Standard** daily energy: 2400–3600 kcal depending on work intensity.
- ☐ **Static work** = 0% mechanical efficiency despite high energy cost → most fatiguing.
- ☐ **Dynamic work** = 15–25% efficiency → most sustainable.
- ☐ **Rohmert Curve**: Endurance ↓ exponentially as %MVC ↑ above 15%.
- ☐ **Work-Rest** microcycles: 4:1 (light), 3:1 (moderate), 2:1 (heavy), 1:1 (very heavy).
- ☐ **Five Pillars**: Physiological Capacity, Task Design, Environment, Scheduling, Motivation.
- ☐ **Environmental limits**: Optimal temperature **20–24°C**, RH **40–60%**, illumination **300–500 lux** for general industrial work.
- ☐ **Aerobic threshold** ≈ **50% VO₂ max** — work above this is not sustainable for 8 hours.
- ☐ For KTU board exams: **Always draw a labelled diagram** of the work-rest cycle or the Rohmert Curve for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts.
- ☐ Mention the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** (1991) for manual materials handling — recommended weight limit **23 kg** under ideal conditions.
- ☐ Remember **Monotony and Boredom** reduce mental work efficiency — use job rotation, task variety, enriched job design.
- ☐ Cite at least one **named ergonomist** (Lehmann, Rohmert, Grandjean, Kroemer, NIOSH) for higher cognitive-level marks (KTU values CO₃ "Design/Development of Solutions").
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔋 SECTION 1: Energy Consumption — Definition, Scope & Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Consumption** in Human Factors Engineering (HFE) is defined as the **rate of metabolic energy expenditure** by the human body required to sustain physiological functions at rest and to perform physical work. It quantifies the *human cost of work* and is the cornerstone of **work physiology**, **occupational workload classification**, **fatigue analysis**, and **occupational health & safety** studies.

In the context of ergonomics, energy consumption is treated as the **physiological "fuel gauge"** of the human operator — analogous to how an electrical engineer monitors the wattage draw of a machine. It allows the human factors specialist to determine whether a given job design is **within human physiological limits** or whether it will cause premature fatigue, error, or injury.

## 1.2 Units of Measurement (Standard Metrics)

| Metric | Unit | Conversion Note |
|---|---|---|
| Energy | **kilocalorie (kcal)** | 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ = 4186 J |
| Energy | **joule (J)** | SI base unit of energy |
| Power (rate of energy use) | **kilocalorie per minute (kcal/min)** | Traditional ergonomics unit |
| Power | **watt (W)** | 1 W = 1 J/s = 0.01433 kcal/min |
| Oxygen uptake | **litres of O₂ per minute (L/min)** | Indirect calorimetry unit |
| Metabolic equivalent | **MET** | 1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min (resting) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants** (must be memorized for KTU exams):
> - **1 litre of O₂ consumed ≈ 5 kcal (≈ 20.9 kJ)** of energy released (mixed diet average).
> - **1 Watt = 0.01433 kcal/min** (or equivalently **1 kcal/min = 69.78 W**).
> - **1 MET = 1 kcal/kg/hour ≈ 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the human body as a **hybrid engine**:
- At **idle** (sleeping, reclining), the engine idles at its **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)** — burning just enough fuel to keep vital organs running (≈ 1 kcal/kg/hr).
- During **light cruising** (office typing, standing), the engine uses **2–3× the idle fuel**.
- During **heavy hauling** (digging, climbing stairs with load), the engine burns **6–10× the idle fuel**.
- Beyond a critical threshold, the engine **overheats** — analogous to anaerobic metabolism, lactic acid build-up, oxygen debt, and eventual exhaustion.

Just as a plant engineer would not assign a 5 HP motor to a 10 HP job, an ergonomist must not assign a sedentary worker to a job requiring 8 kcal/min of sustained output without rest allowances.

## 1.4 Components of Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE)

$$\text{TDEE} = \text{BMR} + \text{Activity Thermogenesis} + \text{Diet-Induced Thermogenesis}$$

where:
- **BMR** (Basal Metabolic Rate): Energy for involuntary life functions (~60–70 % of TDEE).
