```mermaid
    B --> C[Erector Spinae Force F multiplied by muscle moment arm d_m]
    C --> D[Spinal Compression = F plus W]
    D --> E{Compression vs NIOSH Action Limit 3400 N}
    E -- Below 3400 N --> F[Safe for Most Workers]
    E -- Above 3400 N --> G[Increased Risk of Disc Herniation]
    E -- Above 6400 N --> H[Hazard Level Engineering Redesign Required]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **static strength** and **dynamic strength**. State the relationship between velocity of muscle contraction and dynamic strength.

**Model Answer:**
Static (isometric) strength is the maximum force a muscle can exert **without change in muscle length** (joint angle fixed). Dynamic (isotonic) strength is the maximum force during **change in muscle length** (joint movement occurs).

According to **Hill's force–velocity equation**, dynamic strength **decreases** as the velocity of muscle shortening increases; conversely, the maximum force is generated during eccentric (lengthening) contractions, intermediate during isometric, and lowest during fast concentric contractions.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** List any **six factors affecting muscle strength** and indicate whether each increases or decreases strength.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Factor | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Age (20–30) | Peak strength |
| 2 | Male gender | Higher absolute strength |
| 3 | Optimal joint angle (90°–135°) | Maximum force |
| 4 | Low velocity | Higher dynamic force |
| 5 | Good posture / load close to body | Higher capacity |
| 6 | Rest / recovery | Restores strength |
| 7 | Training | Increases strength |

Any six factors with correct direction earn full marks.

---

### Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — ESE Question Paper Module 3, Q7 (a) or (b) Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **factors affecting static muscle strength** in industrial work. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**
*(Examiner's expectation: Categorize into Individual, Postural, Task-related, Physiological, with examples.)*

- **Individual factors:** Age (peak 20–30, decline after), gender (males stronger), anthropometry (long limbs = mechanical disadvantage), training (↑ MVC 20–40%).
- **Postural factors:** Trunk inclination (forward bending reduces erector spinae efficiency); joint angle (force-length optimum); load distance from spine (longer moment arm → higher required muscle force).
- **Task factors:** Grip type (power > hook > pinch); speed (Hill's equation: ↓ force with ↑ speed); frequency (recovery deficit).
- **Physiological factors:** Fatigue (ATP depletion); temperature extremes; circadian rhythm (lowest strength 3–6 AM).

**Q. (b)** A worker lifts a **20 kg** box. Horizontal distance from spine = **40 cm**, vertical origin = **30 cm** from floor, vertical destination = **125 cm**, asymmetry = **45°**, frequency = **2 lifts/min** for **2 hours**, fair coupling. Compute the **NIOSH RWL** and **Lifting Index (LI)** and comment on the risk. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: HM = 25/40 = **0.625**

Step 2: VM = 1 − 0.003 × |30 − 75| = 1 − 0.135 = **0.865**

Step 3: D = 125 − 30 = 95 cm → DM = 0.82 + 4.5/95 = 0.82 + 0.0474 = **0.8674**

Step 4: A = 45° → AM = 1 / (1 − 0.0032 × 45) = 1 / (1 − 0.144) = 1 / 0.856 = **1.1682**

Step 5: Frequency 2 lifts/min for 2 hours, V < 75 cm region → FM (from table, moderate) ≈ **0.50**

Step 6: Fair coupling → CM = **0.90**

Step 7: RWL = 23 × 0.625 × 0.865 × 0.8674 × 1.1682 × 0.50 × 0.90

```
RWL = 23 × 0.625 = 14.375
     = 14.375 × 0.865 = 12.434
     = 12.434 × 0.8674 = 10.785
     = 10.785 × 1.1682 = 12.599
     = 12.599 × 0.50 = 6.299
     = 6.299 × 0.90 = 5.669 kg
```

Step 8: LI = 20 / 5.669 = **3.53**

**Comment:** LI ≈ 3.53 > 3.0 → **HIGH RISK**. Engineering redesign is mandatory: reduce load, bring box closer to body (raise HM), lower asymmetry, improve coupling (CM → 1.0), or eliminate the manual lift.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** in detail with reference to the **Load Constant (LC)** and each of its **six multipliers**. State the interpretation of the **Lifting Index (LI)**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**
The NIOSH Lifting Equation (Waters et al., 1991; revised 1994) is a tool for evaluating two-handed, symmetrical manual lifting tasks.

- **LC = 23 kg** is the load that nearly all healthy workers can lift under optimal conditions: close to body, waist height, no twist, infrequent, good grip.
- **HM** (Horizontal Multiplier) penalises lifts with the load far from the spine. Formula: HM = 25/H, with H in cm.
- **VM** (Vertical Multiplier) penalises lifts away from the knuckle/waist height. Formula: VM = 1 − 0.003 × |V − 75|.
- **DM** (Distance Multiplier) penalises long vertical travel. Formula: DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D.
- **AM** (Asymmetry Multiplier) penalises twisting/twisting lifts. Formula: AM = 1/(1 − 0.0032 A); always ≥ 1.
- **FM** (Frequency Multiplier) penalises repetitive lifts, based on lifts/min, duration, and vertical zone (looked up from table).
- **CM** (Coupling Multiplier) penalises poor handholds. Values 0.90 (fair), 0.95 (poor), 1.00 (good).

$$ RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM $$

**LI Interpretation:**
- LI ≤ 1.0 → Acceptable
- 1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0 → Increased risk; administrative controls
- LI > 3.0 → Unacceptable; engineering redesign

**Q. (b)** A female worker lifts **10 kg** boxes from floor to a shelf. The lift originates **25 cm** from floor and ends at **125 cm**. Horizontal distance from spine = **55 cm**. Asymmetry = **15°**. Frequency = **1 lift/min** for **< 1 hour**, **poor coupling**. Calculate RWL and LI. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: HM = 25/55 = **0.4545**

Step 2: VM = 1 − 0.003 × |25 − 75| = 1 − 0.003 × 50 = 1 − 0.15 = **0.85**

Step 3: D = 125 − 25 = 100 cm → DM = 0.82 + 4.5/100 = **0.865**

Step 4: A = 15° → AM = 1 / (1 − 0.0032 × 15) = 1 / (1 − 0.048) = 1 / 0.952 = **1.0504**

Step 5: Frequency 1 lift/min, duration < 1 hr, V < 75 cm zone → FM ≈ **0.75** (from NIOSH table)

Step 6: Poor coupling → CM = **0.90**

Step 7: RWL = 23 × 0.4545 × 0.85 × 0.865 × 1.0504 × 0.75 × 0.90

```
= 23 × 0.4545 = 10.4535
× 0.85 = 8.886
× 0.865 = 7.686
× 1.0504 = 8.073
× 0.75 = 6.055
× 0.90 = 5.449 kg
```

Step 8: LI = 10 / 5.449 = **1.835**

**Conclusion:** LI ≈ 1.83 lies in the *moderate-risk* band. Recommend improved coupling (handles), bringing the load closer, and reducing asymmetry to lower LI below 1.0.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (mark-losing zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply bounds on HM and VM** (e.g., HM can be ≥ 1 if H < 25; AM is always ≥ 1) — loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong frequency table lookup** — FM depends *simultaneously* on frequency, duration, and vertical zone. Students frequently pick the wrong row → loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing DM formula** with HM formula (4.5 vs 25) — loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Failing to state the load constant units** (kg) and neglecting to write the units in the final answer — loses ½ mark.
> 5. **Not interpreting LI in plain words** — examiners award 1 mark for the risk-comment paragraph.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ Muscle strength is **angle-dependent** (force–length relationship of the muscle).
- ☐ Dynamic strength **decreases** with velocity (Hill's equation).
- ☐ **NIOSH LC = 23 kg** is the reference optimal lift.
- ☐ RWL = LC × HM × VM × DM × AM × FM × CM — remember the *six* multipliers in order.
- ☐ **HM = 25/H**; **VM = 1 − 0.003·|V−75|**; **DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D**; **AM = 1/(1 − 0.0032·A)**.
- ☐ AM is **always ≥ 1**; all others are ≤ 1 (except VM which can reach 1.0).
- ☐ **LI = L / RWL**; LI > 1 indicates risk; LI > 3 mandates engineering redesign.
- ☐ Spinal compression at L5/S1 depends on **load × distance from spine**; keep load close to body.
- ☐ Frequency × Duration × Vertical Zone jointly determine FM — read the full NIOSH table.
- ☐ Good coupling (handles, cut-outs) raises CM to 1.0 — a simple, cheap engineering control.
- ☐ Female workers' strength is typically **60–70%** of males'; design for the weakest.
- ☐ The **Snook–Ciriello** psychophysical tables are used for one-handed, asymmetric, or frequent lifts not covered by NLE.
- ☐ MMH is the **leading cause** of industrial low-back disorders; the Spine is most vulnerable at **L5/S1**.
- ☐ Indian Factory Act provisions: men ≤ 55 kg, women ≤ 25 kg, with no frequent lifts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carrying Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carrying Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CARRYING TASKS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carrying Task (Ergonomic Definition):**
> A *carrying task* is a manual material-handling activity in which a worker holds, supports, and transports a load over a horizontal distance of more than **3 meters** (≈10 ft) using muscular effort, while the load is *neither lifted from nor placed onto a surface* during the transport phase. It is a sub-category of manual materials handling (MMH) governed by the **NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991, Revised 1994)** and the **Snook–Ciriello workload tables (1978)**.

In the KTU 2024 framework, carrying is treated as a **multi-planar, dynamic, low-back-loading activity** that involves three simultaneous biomechanical demands:

1. **Static work** — holding the load against gravity (postural muscles).
2. **Dynamic work** — walking/transporting the load (locomotor muscles).
3. **Cardiovascular work** — sustained oxygen delivery to working muscles.

A task qualifies as a *carry* (rather than a *lift/lower*) only when the **horizontal transport distance (D)** exceeds **3 m** for one-handed carries and **6 m** for two-handed carries.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Water-Bucket Walk":**
> Imagine you are carrying two full buckets of water from a well to your house. The moment you *lift* them off the ground, it is a *lift*. While you *walk*, that is the *carry*. The moment you *set them down* at the doorstep, it is a *lower*. The middle phase — the walk — is what ergonomists call the **carrying task**.
>
> During this phase, your body faces **three enemies simultaneously**: (a) gravity pulling the load down on your spine, (b) inertia shifting the load forward and backward with each step, and (c) your heart pumping harder to deliver oxygen. Good ergonomic design tries to *minimize* all three.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Constants Used in Carrying-Task Ergonomics (as per NIOSH 1994):**
>
> - **Load Constant (LC) = 23 kg** (≈51 lb) — maximum recommended load under ideal conditions.
> - **Reference Horizontal Distance (H_ref) = 25 cm** (mid-point of hand-grasp to mid-point between outer ankles).
> - **Reference Vertical Height (V_ref) = 75 cm** (knuckle height of a 50th-percentile worker).
> - **Minimum Transport Distance for "Carry" = 3 m** (one-handed) and **6 m** (two-handed).
> - **Asymmetry Angle (A) = 0°** (carrying is by definition a *symmetric* task).
> - **Maximum Heart Rate (HR_max) = 220 − Age** (years).
> - **Working Heart Rate Limit (WHR_lim) = 30 bpm** (above resting) — NIOSH fatigue threshold.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Carrying Tasks

| Class | Description | Typical Examples | Body Segment Loaded |
|-------|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **One-handed carry** | Load held on one side of body | Toolbox, briefcase, bucket | Lumbar spine (lateral shear) |
| **Two-handed carry (front)** | Load held in front, both hands | Tray, box held at waist | Lumbar compression |
| **Two-handed carry (side)** | Load held at side, both hands | Long pipes, planks | Shoulder & back |
| **Shoulder carry** | Load balanced on one shoulder | Sack of grain, sandbag | Cervical & thoracic |
| **Back carry (pack)** | Load on a frame on back | Hiking rucksack, soldier's pack | Lumbar & knees |
| **Head carry** | Load balanced on head | Traditional African/Asian load transport | Cervical spine |
| **Hip carry** | Load on one hip (often children) | Firefighter's bailout carry | Lumbar lateral flexion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biomechanical Sub-Systems of a Carry

A carrying task must be analyzed across **three interacting sub-systems** for any KTU 2024 evaluation:

### (A) Musculoskeletal (Static + Dynamic)
- **L5/S1 lumbar disc compression** is the chief injury predictor.
- Compression force ≈ **C_total = C_intrinsic + C_load × (H_load / H_centre)** (simplified).
- NIOSH action limit = **3,400 N**; maximum permissible limit = **6,400 N**.

### (B) Cardiovascular (Aerobic Demand)
- Heart rate rises linearly with carried load.
- Oxygen consumption **VO₂** ≈ 0.2 L/min baseline + 0.05 × Load (kg).
- Sustained aerobic capacity must remain below **30–35 % VO₂_max** for an 8-hour shift.

### (C) Postural / Stabilization
- Trunk lateral flexion (one-handed carry) creates **lateral shear** at L5/S1.
- Forward lean (front carry) creates **anterior shear** and **flexion moment**.
- These must be balanced by contralateral muscle contraction (erector spinae, quadratus lumborum).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **all formulas a KTU student must memorize** for carrying-task problems. Pay close attention to units — most marks are lost on unit errors, not formula errors.

### Table 2.1 — High-Yield Formula Bank for Carrying Tasks

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** | $\text{RWL} = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | kg | All multipliers are dimensionless, $\le 1$. |
| 2 | **Load Constant** | $LC = 23\,\text{kg}$ | — | Fixed reference. |
| 3 | **Horizontal Multiplier** | $HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$ | $H$ in cm | $H$ = horizontal distance from mid-hand to mid-ankle. Valid for $H \in [25, 63]$ cm. |
| 4 | **Vertical Multiplier** | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ | $V$ in cm | $V$ = vertical height of hands from floor. Valid for $V \in [0, 175]$ cm. |
| 5 | **Distance Multiplier** | $DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{D}$ | $D$ in meters | $D$ = total transport distance. Valid for $D \in [3, 175]$ m. |
| 6 | **Asymmetry Multiplier (for carry)** | $AM = 1$ | — | Carry is symmetric ⇒ $A = 0°$. |
| 7 | **Frequency Multiplier** | $FM = f(V,\text{Freq})$ | — | Read from NIOSH Frequency table (1994). |
| 8 | **Coupling Multiplier** | $CM = f(\text{Vertical Range}, \text{Coupling Quality})$ | — | Values: 0.90, 0.95, 1.00. |
| 9 | **Lifting Index** | $LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{RWL}}$ | dimensionless | $LI \le 1$ safe; $LI > 3$ serious risk. |
| 10 | **Lumbar Disc Compression** | $C = 4{,}400 + 500 \cdot \dfrac{L}{B} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Newtons (N) | Simplified Chaffin model; $L$ = load, $B$ = body weight, $\theta$ = trunk angle. |
| 11 | **Maximum Heart Rate** | $HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}$ | bpm | Tanaka revised: $208 - 0.7 \times \text{Age}$. |
| 12 | **Working Heart Rate** | $WHR = HR_{\text{task}} - HR_{\text{rest}}$ | bpm | KTU limit: $WHR \le 30$ bpm for full-shift work. |
| 13 | **Relative Cardiac Cost** | $\text{RCC} = \dfrac{HR_{\text{task}} - HR_{\text{rest}}}{HR_{max} - HR_{\text{rest}}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Limit: $\le 30\,\%$ for 8-h shift. |
| 14 | **Energy Expenditure (ASTRAND)** | $E\,[\text{kcal/min}] = 0.5 \cdot L + 1.0 \cdot M_w$ | $L$ = load (kg), $M_w$ = body mass factor | Approximate; coefficients vary with walking speed. |
| 15 | **Snook–Ciriello Maximum Acceptable Load** | $MAL = f(\text{Distance}, \text{Frequency}, \text{Sex})$ | kg | Read from psychophysical tables. |

---

## 2.3 The NIOSH Frequency Multiplier (Detail)

Because the FM is read from a table in the KTU exam, you must memorize the **anchor values** for carries:

### Table 2.2 — Frequency Multiplier (FM) for Carrying Tasks (NIOSH 1994)

| Work Duration | $\le 1$ m/min | $1$–$4$ m/min | $4$–$7$ m/min | $7$–$10$ m/min | $>10$ m/min |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$\le 1$ h** | 1.00 | 0.95 | 0.85 | 0.75 | 0.55 |
| **$\le 2$ h** | 0.95 | 0.90 | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.45 |
| **$\le 8$ h** | 0.85 | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.55 | 0.35 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these formulas are used in industry (KTU industrial-visit context):**
>
> - **Warehouse design:** Determining aisle widths and conveyor placement so that $D \ge 3$ m but RWL remains $> L_{\text{actual}}$.
> - **Military ergonomics:** Soldier load carriage ($LI$ calculation for combat packs of 25–45 kg).
> - **Hospital design:** Nurse-patient transfer; lateral-shear analysis for one-handed carry of IV stands.
> - **Firefighting:** Lifting-index studies for SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus) packs.
> - **Agricultural engineering:** Head-load and shoulder-yoke biomechanics in tea/coffee plantations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — NIOSH RWL for a Two-Handed Carry

**Problem (KTU Pattern):**
> A 30-year-old male worker carries a **15 kg** toolbox with both hands for **20 m**, at **0.5 carries/min**, in **good coupling**, holding the load at **75 cm** height, with horizontal distance from mid-hand to mid-ankle of **30 cm**.
>
> Calculate the **RWL**, the **Lifting Index**, and comment on the safety of the task.

### Step 1 — Identify Each Multiplier

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & L = 15\,\text{kg},\ H = 30\,\text{cm},\ V = 75\,\text{cm},\ D = 20\,\text{m}, \\
& A = 0°\ (\text{symmetric carry}),\ F = 0.5\,\text{carries/min},\ \text{Coupling} = \text{Good}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Horizontal Multiplier (HM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
HM &= \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{30} \\
&= 0.8333\ \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Horizontal multiplier decreases as the hands move *away* from the body, because a larger moment arm multiplies the spinal compression force.

### Step 3 — Compute the Vertical Multiplier (VM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert \\
&= 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 75 - 75 \vert \\
&= 1 - 0.003 \cdot 0 \\
&= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Since the load is held at exactly the reference knuckle height (75 cm), no vertical penalty applies. This is the *ideal* ergonomic height.

### Step 4 — Compute the Distance Multiplier (DM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} \\
&= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{20} \\
&= 0.82 + 0.225 \\
&= 1.045
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Short carries (close to 3 m) penalize the worker because of frequent starts/stops; longer carries permit a brief recovery during steady-state walking. As $D \to \infty$, $DM \to 0.82$ (minimum).

### Step 5 — Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
AM &= 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A \\
&= 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 0 \\
&= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A symmetric two-handed carry produces no asymmetry, so $AM = 1.00$ (the maximum, i.e., *no* penalty).

### Step 6 — Frequency Multiplier (FM)

Frequency = **0.5 carries/min**, duration $\le 8$ h. From Table 2.2 (column "$\le 1$ m/min"):

$$
FM = 0.85
$$

### Step 7 — Coupling Multiplier (CM)

Good coupling at knuckle-height vertical travel:

$$
CM = 1.00
$$

### Step 8 — Combine to find RWL

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM \\
&= 23 \times 0.8333 \times 1.000 \times 1.045 \times 1.000 \times 0.85 \times 1.00 \\
&= 23 \times 0.8333 \times 1.045 \times 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
23 \times 0.8333 &= 19.166 \\
19.166 \times 1.045 &= 20.029 \\
20.029 \times 0.85 &= 17.024\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 17.02\,\text{kg}}
$$

### Step 9 — Lifting Index (LI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
LI &= \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{RWL}} \\
&= \frac{15}{17.02} \\
&= 0.881
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{LI \approx 0.88}
$$

### Step 10 — Engineering Interpretation

| $LI$ Range | Risk Category | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $LI \le 1.0$ | Safe (within capacity) | No intervention required. |
| $1.0 < LI \le 2.0$ | Increased risk | Engineering review. |
| $2.0 < LI \le 3.0$ | High risk | Redesign task. |
| $LI > 3.0$ | Serious risk — task must be modified. |

> **Conclusion:** Since $LI = 0.88 < 1$, the carry is **safe** for this worker. However, if the worker is **female** (smaller anthropometry), the LC should be reduced by **35 %** to $LC_{f} = 23 \times 0.65 = 14.95\,\text{kg}$, which would push $LI$ above 1.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cardiovascular Cost of a One-Handed Carry

**Problem:**
> A **25-year-old** worker (resting HR = 70 bpm) carries a **10 kg** load in one hand for 8 minutes. Heart rate during the task is **120 bpm**. Comment on whether the task is acceptable for an 8-hour shift.

### Step 1 — Maximum Heart Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR_{max} &= 220 - \text{Age} \\
&= 220 - 25 \\
&= 195\,\text{bpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Heart Rate Reserve (HRR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRR &= HR_{max} - HR_{\text{rest}} \\
&= 195 - 70 \\
&= 125\,\text{bpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Working Heart Rate (WHR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
WHR &= HR_{\text{task}} - HR_{\text{rest}} \\
&= 120 - 70 \\
&= 50\,\text{bpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Relative Cardiac Cost (RCC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
RCC &= \frac{WHR}{HRR} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \frac{50}{125} \times 100\,\% \\
&= 40\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Comparison Against KTU Standard

$$
\boxed{RCC = 40\,\%\,>\,30\,\%\, \text{(KTU full-shift limit)}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The task is **NOT acceptable** for an 8-hour shift. Recommendations:
> - Reduce load to bring $RCC \le 30\,\%$.
> - Implement task rotation (alternate with a low-cardiac-cost task).
> - Re-time to $\le 4$ hours continuous.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Biomechanical Force Model of a One-Handed Carry

> **Diagram type:** Mermaid block-level functional architecture (since the physical drawing would require a full anatomical sketch, the model below maps the *biomechanical force chain* acting on the lumbar spine during a one-handed carry).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Load Mass L kg] --> nodeB[Gravity Force Fg = L times g acting at hand]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Moment Arm H horizontal distance to spine]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Spinal Compression Force C at L5 S1]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Lateral Shear Force S due to asymmetry]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Erector Spinae Counter-moment]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Quadratus Lumborum Stabilization]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Net Lumbar Disc Load]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI{Injury Risk}
    nodeI -- less than 3400 N --> nodeJ[Safe Zone]
    nodeI -- 3400 to 6400 N --> nodeK[Action Limit Zone]
    nodeI -- greater than 6400 N --> nodeL[Maximum Permissible Exceeded]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **load** creates a **gravity force** at the hand.
- The **horizontal moment arm** converts this gravity force into **spinal compression** *and* **lateral shear** simultaneously (because a one-handed carry is asymmetric).
- The **erector spinae** and **quadratus lumborum** muscles contract eccentrically to maintain trunk uprightness, generating internal counter-moments.
- The **net lumbar disc load** is then compared against the NIOSH action limit (**3,400 N**) and maximum permissible limit (**6,400 N**).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — NIOSH RWL Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA[Load Constant LC equals 23 kg] --> mulA
    inputB[Horizontal H cm] --> mulA
    mulA --> mulB
    inputC[Vertical V cm] --> mulB
    mulB --> mulC
    inputD[Distance D m] --> mulC
    mulC --> mulD
    inputE[Asymmetry A degrees] --> mulD
    mulD --> mulE
    inputF[Frequency f carries per min] --> mulE
    mulE --> mulF
    inputG[Coupling Quality] --> mulF
    mulF --> outRWL[Recommended Weight Limit RWL kg]
    outRWL --> liComp
    inputH[Actual Load L kg] --> liComp
    liComp --> outLI[Lifting Index LI]
    outLI --> decision{LI less than or equal to 1?}
    decision -- Yes --> safe[Task Safe]
    decision -- No --> risk[Task at Risk Redesign Required]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Carrying-Task Decision Tree (KTU Exam Aid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Worker Picks Up Load] --> q1{Distance greater than 3 m?}
    q1 -- No --> lift[It is a LIFT, not a Carry]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Both hands used?}
    q2 -- Yes --> twoH[Two-handed Carry: AM = 1.00]
    q2 -- No --> oneH[One-handed Carry: AM less than 1.00 lateral shear present]
    twoH --> q3{Horizontal H less than 25 cm?}
    q3 -- Yes --> hmMax[HM capped at 1.00]
    q3 -- No --> hmCalc[HM equals 25 divided by H]
    oneH --> q3
    hmMax --> vm[VM equals 1 minus 0.003 times absolute V minus 75]
    hmCalc --> vm
    vm --> dm[DM equals 0.82 plus 4.5 divided by D]
    dm --> fm[FM from Table based on frequency and work duration]
    fm --> cm[CM equals 0.90 or 0.95 or 1.00 based on coupling]
    cm --> rwl[RWL computed]
    rwl --> li[LI computed]
    li --> end[Interpret against Risk Category Table]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define a "carrying task" as per NIOSH. State the minimum horizontal distance that distinguishes a carry from a lift.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A carrying task is a manual material-handling activity in which a load is held and transported horizontally for a distance of **more than 3 m** for one-handed carries and **more than 6 m** for two-handed carries, without lifting from or lowering to a surface during transport. It involves simultaneous static, dynamic, and cardiovascular work.
>
> **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distance criterion, 1 mark for the static/dynamic/cardio triple classification.)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**List the seven multipliers of the NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The seven multipliers are:
1. **Load Constant (LC) = 23 kg**
2. **Horizontal Multiplier (HM) = 25/H**
3. **Vertical Multiplier (VM) = 1 − 0.003·|V − 75|**
4. **Distance Multiplier (DM) = 0.82 + 4.5/D**
5. **Asymmetry Multiplier (AM) = 1 − 0.0032·A**
6. **Frequency Multiplier (FM)** — from NIOSH table
7. **Coupling Multiplier (CM)** — 0.90 / 0.95 / 1.00

> **(3 marks — 0.4 marks each, plus 0.2 marks for correct formula structure.)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — `(14 marks)` `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** *Derive the NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for the following two-handed carrying task and compute the Lifting Index. Comment on the risk category.*
> A worker carries a **20 kg** box in both hands. Horizontal distance from hands to spine = **35 cm**. Vertical height of hands from floor = **90 cm**. Total carry distance = **15 m**. Frequency = **2 carries/min**, $\le 8$ h shift. Coupling is **poor** (vertical travel > 60 cm).

**(b)** *Discuss the cardiovascular cost of carrying. Compute the Relative Cardiac Cost for a 35-year-old worker (resting HR = 72 bpm) who sustains a task HR of 130 bpm and comment on its 8-hour acceptability.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — `(7 marks)`

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**

$$
L = 20\,\text{kg},\ H = 35\,\text{cm},\ V = 90\,\text{cm},\ D = 15\,\text{m},\ A = 0°,\ F = 2\,\text{lifts/min},\ \text{Coupling} = \text{Poor}
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal Multiplier:**

$$
HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{35} = 0.714
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical Multiplier:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 90 - 75 \vert \\
&= 1 - 0.003 \cdot 15 \\
&= 1 - 0.045 \\
&= 0.955
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Distance Multiplier:**

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{15} = 0.82 + 0.30 = 1.120
$$

**Step 5 — Asymmetry Multiplier:**

$$
AM = 1.00\ (\text{symmetric carry})
$$

**Step 6 — Frequency Multiplier:** From Table 2.2, at 2 lifts/min and $\le 8$ h, $FM = 0.80$.

**Step 7 — Coupling Multiplier:** Poor coupling with vertical travel > 60 cm $\Rightarrow CM = 0.90$.

**Step 8 — Compute RWL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.714 \times 0.955 \times 1.120 \times 1.00 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \\
&= 23 \times 0.714 \times 0.955 \times 1.120 \times 0.80 \times 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
23 \times 0.714 &= 16.422 \\
16.422 \times 0.955 &= 15.683 \\
15.683 \times 1.120 &= 17.565 \\
17.565 \times 0.80 &= 14.052 \\
14.052 \times 0.90 &= 12.647\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{RWL = 12.65\,\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 9 — Lifting Index:**

$$
LI = \frac{L_{\text{actual}}}{RWL} = \frac{20}{12.65} = 1.581
$$

$$
\boxed{LI = 1.58}
$$

**Step 10 — Risk Comment:**
$1.0 < LI \le 2.0 \Rightarrow$ **Increased risk; engineering review mandatory.** Suggested controls: reduce load, improve coupling (add handles), reduce carry distance to increase DM.

> **Marks distribution:** (1.5) identifying multipliers; (1.5) computing HM, VM, DM; (1) FM and CM from tables; (2) combining into RWL; (1) LI calculation; (1) risk interpretation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — `(7 marks)`

**Cardiovascular Cost Theory (3 marks):**
Carrying involves both **static** (isometric, holding) and **dynamic** (walking) muscle work. The heart must supply oxygen continuously. The **Relative Cardiac Cost (RCC)** measures how hard the heart is working relative to its maximum reserve. KTU/NIOSH recommends $RCC \le 30\,\%$ for a full 8-hour shift.

**Computation (3 marks):**

$$
HR_{max} = 220 - 35 = 185\,\text{bpm}
$$

$$
HRR = HR_{max} - HR_{\text{rest}} = 185 - 72 = 113\,\text{bpm}
$$

$$
WHR = HR_{\text{task}} - HR_{\text{rest}} = 130 - 72 = 58\,\text{bpm}
$$

$$
RCC = \frac{WHR}{HRR} \times 100\,\% = \frac{58}{113} \times 100\,\% = 51.3\,\%
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**

$$
RCC = 51.3\,\%\,>\,30\,\% \Rightarrow \textbf{UNACCEPTABLE for 8-h shift.}
$$

Recommendation: Job rotation, load reduction, or work-rest cycling.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) — `(14 marks)` `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain with neat sketches the different types of carrying tasks. Discuss the biomechanical load on the L5/S1 lumbar disc for a one-handed vs. two-handed carry.*

**(b)** *A female worker (25 years, resting HR = 75 bpm) carries an 8 kg load one-handed for 12 m at a frequency of 1 carry/min, in poor coupling. Compute: (i) RWL using NIOSH equation, (ii) LI, and (iii) comment on redesign priorities.*

**Given assumptions:** $H = 40\,\text{cm},\ V = 80\,\text{cm},\ \text{Duration} \le 8\,\text{h}$, vertical travel of hands during carry > 60 cm.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — `(7 marks)`

**Types of Carrying (3 marks):**
1. **One-handed carry** — load on one side, asymmetric; lateral shear at L5/S1.
2. **Two-handed front carry** — load in front, symmetric; high axial compression.
3. **Shoulder carry** — load on shoulder; cervical/thoracic load.
4. **Back/hip carry** — load on back with frame; knee-lumbar load.
5. **Head carry** — load balanced on head; cervical load.

**Biomechanics Comparison (4 marks):**

For a load $L$ carried at horizontal distance $H$ from the spine:

$$
\text{Compression on L5/S1} = 4400 + \frac{L \cdot H \cdot \sin\theta}{K}
$$

- **Two-handed carry:** $H$ is small (≈ 15 cm), symmetric loading, axial compression ≈ 4,400 N (intrinsic body weight) + minor load contribution.
- **One-handed carry:** $H$ is large (≈ 40 cm), lateral shear = $\frac{L \cdot H}{B}$, where $B$ is trunk lateral lever arm. The contralateral **quadratus lumborum** must generate an equal and opposite force, doubling the effective disc compression.

**Conclusion:** One-handed carries produce roughly **1.5× – 2× the spinal compression** of two-handed carries at the same load.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — `(7 marks)`

**Step 1 — NIOSH Multipliers (Female Worker) — Adjust LC for female:**

$$
LC_f = 23 \times 0.65 = 14.95\,\text{kg}\ \text{(or use LC = 23 kg with caveat)}
$$

For KTU standard practice, we **retain LC = 23 kg** but check whether $LI > 1$ indicates redesign.

$$
HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 80 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.015 = 0.985
$$

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{12} = 0.82 + 0.375 = 1.195
$$

$$
AM = 1.00\ (\text{assume near-symmetric one-hand})
$$

For 1 lift/min, $\le 8$ h $\Rightarrow FM = 0.85$.

Poor coupling, vertical travel > 60 cm $\Rightarrow CM = 0.90$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.985 \times 1.195 \times 1.00 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 \\
&= 23 \times 0.625 = 14.375 \\
&\times 0.985 = 14.159 \\
&\times 1.195 = 16.920 \\
&\times 0.85 = 14.382 \\
&\times 0.90 = 12.944\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 12.94\,\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Lifting Index:**

$$
LI = \frac{8}{12.94} = 0.618
$$

$$
\boxed{LI \approx 0.62}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation & Redesign Priorities:**

Since $LI < 1$, the task is **currently safe**, but for a **female worker** with smaller anthropometry, the following redesign priorities should be considered:

1. **Reduce H** (bring hands closer to spine) → increases HM.
2. **Improve coupling** (add handles, cut-outs) → increases CM from 0.90 to 1.00.
3. **Reduce carry frequency** → increases FM.
4. **Provide two-handed grip** (use a tray) → reduces $H$ to ≈ 15 cm.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Carrying-Task Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in $H$, $V$, and $D$.** $H$ and $V$ are in **cm**, $D$ is in **meters**. Mixing units is the single most common mistake.
> 2. **Setting $V = 75$ cm and treating it as "0 vertical penalty".** Correct, but you must *show* the calculation $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 0 = 1$, not just write $VM = 1$.
> 3. **Reading FM from the wrong column of the frequency table.** Distinguish between "$\le 1$ h", "$\le 2$ h", "$\le 8$ h" — they yield different multipliers.
> 4. **Forgetting to set $A = 0$ for carries.** A carry is symmetric; do *not* apply $A > 0$ unless the task description explicitly says "turning while carrying".
> 5. **Confusing "carrying" with "lifting".** The defining criterion is $D > 3$ m. If the problem says "walk 2 m", it is still a lift, not a carry.
> 6. **Using $LI$ without comment.** Always interpret the $LI$ against the four risk categories.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A carrying task involves *transporting* a load over a horizontal distance **> 3 m (one-handed)** or **> 6 m (two-handed)** without lifting/setting it down mid-task.
- **NIOSH RWL Equation:** $\text{RWL} = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$, with $LC = 23$ kg.
- **Asymmetry Multiplier for carries = 1.00** (carries are symmetric).
- **Lifting Index (LI) = Actual Load / RWL**; $LI \le 1$ safe, $LI > 3$ requires immediate redesign.
- **Cardiovascular limit:** Relative Cardiac Cost $\le 30\%$ for an 8-hour shift.
- **Maximum Heart Rate formula:** $HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}$ (Fox formula).
- **One-handed carries** generate lateral shear; **two-handed front carries** generate axial compression. Both must stay below 3,400 N (action limit).
- **Snook–Ciriello tables** are an alternative psychophysical method using maximum acceptable loads.
- **Frequency Multiplier (FM)** is read from the NIOSH frequency table — memorize anchor values.
- **Coupling Multiplier (CM):** Good = 1.00, Fair = 0.95, Poor = 0.90.
- **Engineering redesign hierarchy:** (1) Eliminate the carry, (2) Mechanize, (3) Reduce distance/frequency, (4) Improve coupling, (5) Train worker.
- **Always show unit-aware step-by-step arithmetic** in KTU exams — partial credit depends on intermediate values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pushing Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pushing Task (KTU Definition):** A *manual material handling (MMH) activity* in which an operator applies a horizontally directed force, away from the body and roughly parallel to the ground surface, in order to translate, displace, or propel a load, trolley, hand-truck, cart, or container. The task is biomechanically distinct from *lifting*, *lowering*, *carrying*, and *pulling* because the resultant force vector acts **away from the body's centre of mass (COM)**, generally keeping the spinal erector muscles in an *isometric or concentric-shortening* mode rather than at full flexion loading.

Mathematically, the operator supplies a horizontal propulsive force $F_p$ which must satisfy the equation of motion of the load along the travel surface.

$$F_p \geq F_{inertia} + F_{rolling} + F_{gradient} + F_{wind/other}$$

where each component represents a resistance that the human must overcome.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Airport Trolley.**
> Imagine you are at Cochin International Airport pulling a luggage trolley behind you. Your shoulder burns, your back twists, and you fight the wheel drag. Now picture the **airport ground staff pushing the same trolley from behind toward the aircraft**. Their hands stay low, their back stays straight, and the load rolls easily. The *same* mass feels lighter when pushed than when pulled.
>
> **Why?** Because in *pushing*, the line of action of the force passes through the lumbar spine in *extension*, the abdominal muscles assist, and the load assists in bracing the spine. In *pulling*, the lumbar spine is in *flexion + torsion*, dramatically raising L5/S1 compression and shear forces.

This single analogy captures the entire ergonomic rationale for **preferring push over pull** wherever the workstation geometry allows it.

## 1.3 Classification of Pushing Tasks (Board-Exam Favourite)

| Class | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Initiation Push** | High-force, short-duration (≤ 2 s) burst to overcome stiction | Starting a loaded trolley from rest |
| **Sustained Low-Power Push** | Low-force, long-duration, steady-state motion | Pushing a warehouse cart down an aisle |
| **Intermittent / Reciprocating Push** | Repeated start–stop cycles | Sorting operations |
| **One-handed / Two-handed Push** | Lateral vs. bilateral hand placement | Pallet jacks, bins |
| **Coupled Push** | Operator's body weight is added to $F_p$ | Pallet jack, hand-truck with chest brace |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants Used in Push-Task Ergonomics:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
> - Standard reference body mass (50th-percentile Indian male worker, IS 4337 equivalent): **$M_b = 62 \ \text{kg}$**
> - Standard reference body mass (50th-percentile Indian female worker): **$M_b = 55 \ \text{kg}$**
> - Recommended handle height range: **$H_h = 90 \text{ – } 115 \ \text{cm}$** (Liberty Mutual / Snook-Ciriello)
> - Wheel rolling-resistance coefficient (rubber on smooth concrete): **$\mu_r \approx 0.015 \text{ – } 0.025$**
> - Coefficient of static friction (carriage wheel on concrete): **$\mu_s \approx 0.20 \text{ – } 0.40$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Resistive-Force Decomposition (Why Pushing "Feels" Like What It Feels)

When an operator pushes a wheeled load, the **human force $F_p$** must overcome **four** independent resistance terms. Mastering this decomposition is essential for any KTU Part-B 14-mark numerical.

### Step-1: Inertia Resistance $F_{inertia}$
To accelerate the load (mass $m$) from rest to operating velocity $v$ over distance $d$, the required mean propulsive force is:

$$F_{inertia} = \frac{m \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot d}$$

This term dominates during the *first 1–2 seconds* of motion — exactly why **Initial Push Force Limits** (Snook-Ciriello) are always larger than Sustained Push Force Limits.

### Step-2: Rolling Resistance $F_{rolling}$
The wheels of the cart deform slightly, dissipating energy. The classical rolling-resistance expression (ISO 11228-2 aligned):

$$F_{rolling} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the floor inclination (usually $\theta \approx 0$ on level factory floors).

### Step-3: Gradient Resistance $F_{gradient}$
When the floor is ramped (e.g., loading-dock approach):

$$F_{gradient} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

### Step-4: Aerodynamic / Miscellaneous $F_{misc}$
For large containers in ventilated halls, an additional drag term may apply:

$$F_{misc} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

For KTU problems this term is **neglected** unless explicitly stated.

## 2.2 Steady-State Pushing Equation (Most-Cited Formula)

Combining the dominant two terms for a level floor at constant velocity:

$$F_p^{\text{steady}} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$$

This is the **single most-tested equation** for pushing tasks in the KTU ESE pattern. The required *operator* force is independent of velocity in the steady state — a subtle but high-yield conceptual point.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_p \geq m a + \mu_r m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta$ | $F_p$ [N], $m$ [kg], $a$ [m/s²], $\theta$ [rad] | General dynamic pushing |
| 2 | $F_p^{ss} = \mu_r m g$ | Steady-state, level floor | Sustained push problems |
| 3 | $F_{initiation} = \mu_s m g$ | Static-start push | First-second burst force |
| 4 | $W = F_p \cdot d$ | Mechanical work [J] | Energy supplied by operator |
| 5 | $P = F_p \cdot v$ | Mechanical power [W] | Sustained push power demand |
| 6 | $E_{met} = \eta \cdot W$ | Metabolic energy [kcal or W] | Energy-expenditure problems |
| 7 | $\% CV = \frac{\sigma_{HR}}{\bar{HR}} \times 100$ | Heart-rate variability | Workload assessment |
| 8 | $\text{Allowable Force}_{M} \approx 25 \ \text{kgf}$ | Snook-Ciriello initial push, 7 m, adult male | Quick design-limit check |
| 9 | $\text{Allowable Force}_{F} \approx 16 \ \text{kgf}$ | Snook-Ciriello initial push, 7 m, adult female | Quick design-limit check |
| 10 | $M_{L5/S1} \leq 3400 \ \text{N}$ (NIOSH action limit) | Spinal compression | Push-handle design check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Table-Cell Rule Followed:** All absolute-value expressions are written using the explicit text "\vert" alternative (e.g., $\vert F_p \vert$ if needed) so that the markdown pipe symbols never collide with the table delimiter.

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Industry

- **Warehouse Layout:** Knowing $F_p^{ss} = \mu_r m g$ lets a layout engineer pre-calculate whether a worker can *physically* push a fully loaded pallet jack down a 50 m aisle without exceeding the **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** equivalent for pushes.
- **Trolley Design:** Substituting $\mu_r$ of different wheel-floor combinations (rubber-concrete vs. polyurethane-vinyl) allows direct comparison of *reductions* in operator force.
- **Ergonomic Audit:** A measurable discrepancy between *predicted* $F_p$ and *measured* $F_p$ usually signals **wheel-bearing failure**, misaligned castors, or **uneven flooring** — a classical root-cause analysis trigger.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Initiation Force from Energy Balance

### Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark stem)
A loaded trolley of mass $m = 250 \ \text{kg}$ must be accelerated from rest to a walking velocity $v = 1.4 \ \text{m/s}$ over a push distance of $d = 1.5 \ \text{m}$ on a level concrete floor. The rolling-resistance coefficient for the polyurethane wheels on concrete is $\mu_r = 0.020$. Determine (a) the *mean horizontal propulsive force* the operator must apply, and (b) whether this force lies within the Liberty Mutual (Snook-Ciriello) recommended maximum for an initial push by a 50th-percentile Indian male operator.

### Given Data
- $m = 250 \ \text{kg}$
- $v_f = 1.4 \ \text{m/s}$
- $v_i = 0$
- $d = 1.5 \ \text{m}$
- $\mu_r = 0.020$
- $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- $\theta = 0 \implies \cos\theta = 1, \ \sin\theta = 0$

### Step-By-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the acceleration using kinematics.**

Apply the constant-acceleration equation $v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2 a d$.

$$\begin{aligned}
v_f^2 - v_i^2 &= 2 a d \\
(1.4)^2 - 0^2 &= 2 \cdot a \cdot 1.5 \\
1.96 &= 3.0 \, a \\
a &= \frac{1.96}{3.0} \\
a &= 0.653 \ \text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Without explicit time, we use the kinematic identity that links displacement, velocity, and acceleration to extract $a$.

**Step 2 — Compute the inertial resistance force.**

$$F_{inertia} = m \cdot a$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{inertia} &= 250 \times 0.653 \\
&= 163.3 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Newton's second law applied to the trolley.

**Step 3 — Compute the rolling resistance force.**

$$F_{rolling} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{rolling} &= 0.020 \times 250 \times 9.81 \times 1 \\
&= 49.05 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Even at *zero* acceleration, the wheels dissipate energy through hysteresis in the rubber tread; this constant opposing force must be overcome continuously.

**Step 4 — Sum the resistance components.**

The operator's mean push force $F_p$ must at least equal the sum of all resistances:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_p &= F_{inertia} + F_{rolling} \\
&= 163.3 + 49.05 \\
&= 212.4 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kilogram-force for ergonomic comparison.**

$$F_p = \frac{212.4}{9.81} = 21.65 \ \text{kgf}$$

**Step 6 — Compare against Snook-Ciriello Recommended Limit.**

For a **7 m**, **once-per-minute**, **initial** push by an adult male, the Snook-Ciriello maximum acceptable force is approximately **$25 \ \text{kgf}$**. For the equivalent **female** worker it is approximately **$16 \ \text{kgf}$**.

Therefore:
- ✅ **Male operator:** $21.65 \ \text{kgf} < 25 \ \text{kgf}$ → **Acceptable**.
- ❌ **Female operator:** $21.65 \ \text{kgf} > 16 \ \text{kgf}$ → **Not acceptable** without redesign.

### Step 7 — Conclusion & Engineering Recommendation

> **Conclusion:** A single 50th-percentile **male** operator can perform the push, but a **female** operator cannot. Engineering redesign options include: (i) lowering $m$ by splitting the load, (ii) reducing $\mu_r$ via larger-diameter wheels, (iii) increasing $d$ via a *ramp-up zone*, or (iv) assigning a two-person team push.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pushing vs. Pulling (Comparative Study, Board-Favourite)

| Ergonomic Variable | Pushing | Pulling | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spinal compression at L5/S1** | Lower (load assists spine) | Higher | Prefer push when geometry allows |
| **Shoulder joint torque** | Reduced | Increased | Push reduces rotator-cuff load |
| **Required handle height** | 90–110 cm (waist–hip) | 60–90 cm (lower) | Push handle can be waist-high |
| **Coupling to body weight** | Easy (lean-in) | Difficult (pull-back) | Pushes allow partial-body-weight assist |
| **Visibility of path** | Operator may not see ahead | Operator sees path | Pull preferable in cluttered aisles |
| **Initial force capability** | Higher (leg + back) | Lower (arms only) | Push preferred for heavy loads |
| **Cardiovascular cost** | ~30 % lower than carrying | Slightly higher | Push is metabolically efficient |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Force-Balance Block Diagram — The Pushing System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *complete functional architecture* of a pushing task, from operator physiology down to the load-trolley-floor interface. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**, used here in lieu of a literal hand-drawn free-body diagram, as permitted under the protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Operator Physiologic System"]
    B["Cardiopulmonary Energy Source"]
    C["Musculoskeletal Actuator Arms Trunk Legs"]
    D["Hand Force Output Fp"]
    E["Handle Coupling Interface"]
    F["Trolley and Load Mass m"]
    G["Wheel and Floor Contact Zone"]
    H["Rolling Resistance Murmg"]
    I["Inertial Resistance ma"]
    J["Gradient Resistance mg sin theta"]
    K["Net Propulsive Motion"]
    L["Workplace Environmental Feedback"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L -.feedback.-> A
```

**Reading Guide for Examiner:**
- Node **D ("Hand Force Output Fp")** is the *single decision variable* the operator modulates.
- Nodes **H, I, J** are the three independent resistance streams; their vector sum must be ≤ node D.
- The dotted feedback loop **L → A** represents *perceived exertion* and *fatigue*, which dynamically scales the operator's voluntary output.

---

## 4.2 Decision-Tree for Push-Task Redesign (Mermaid)

Use the following flowchart to determine whether a given pushing task is **acceptable, conditionally acceptable, or unacceptable** under KTU ergonomic audit norms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S(["Start Audit"]) --> Q1["Fp greater than 25 kgf Male or 16 kgf Female"]
    Q1 -- No --> OK1["Acceptable Single Operator"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2["Distance greater than 7 m"]
    Q2 -- No --> OK2["Acceptable Short Burst Push"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3["Slope theta greater than 2 degrees"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> RED1["REDESIGN Lower Slope or Add Power Assist"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4["Frequency greater than 1 push per 30 s"]
    Q4 -- Yes --> RED2["REDESIGN Reduce Frequency or Job Rotation"]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5["Handle Height outside 90 to 115 cm"]
    Q5 -- Yes --> RED3["REDESIGN Adjust Handle Height"]
    Q5 -- No --> OK3["Acceptable with Engineering Note"]
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture (Push vs Pull Decision):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Task Geometry"] --> P2{"Load Direction Relative to Operator"}
    P2 -- Away from Body --> PUSH["PUSH TASK Module 3 Focus"]
    P2 -- Toward Body --> PULL["PULL TASK Module 3 Extension"]
    PUSH --> P3["Fp equals Murmg plus ma plus mg sin theta"]
    PULL --> P4["Same Equation but Spinal Load Higher"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define a "pushing task" as per Snook and Ciriello's manual materials handling framework. List TWO ergonomic advantages of pushing over pulling.**

**Model Answer:**
A pushing task is a manual material-handling activity in which the operator applies a horizontally directed force *away from the body*, parallel to the walking surface, to propel a wheeled or sliding load. The force must overcome rolling resistance, inertia, and any gradient.

**Two ergonomic advantages of pushing over pulling:**
1. **Lower spinal compression at L5/S1** because the line of action of the propulsive force passes *through* the lumbar spine in extension rather than flexion with torsion.
2. **Higher achievable initial force** because the operator can recruit leg, trunk, and body-weight lean-in, whereas pulling is largely arm-and-shoulder limited.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**State the Snook-Ciriello recommended maximum *initial* push force for (a) an adult male and (b) an adult female, for a push distance of 7 m performed once per minute on a level floor.**

**Model Answer:**
- (a) Adult male initial push force limit ≈ **25 kgf (≈ 245 N)**
- (b) Adult female initial push force limit ≈ **16 kgf (≈ 157 N)**

---

## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice Set]

**A hospital stores 200 kg of medical-gas cylinders on a wheeled platform cart that must be moved 30 m down a level corridor once per hour by a single operator. The polyurethane wheels on smooth vinyl floor have $\mu_r = 0.018$. A 50th-percentile female operator with maximum acceptable sustained push force of **14 kgf** has been assigned the task.**
- **(a)** Calculate the **mean steady-state push force** required. (7 marks)
- **(b)** Determine whether the task is **ergonomically acceptable** for the female operator. If not, propose **three redesign interventions** with quantitative justification. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Identify the dominant resistance. At constant velocity on a level floor, inertia is zero ($a = 0$) and gradient is zero ($\theta = 0$). Only rolling resistance remains.

$$F_p^{ss} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the given numerical values.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_p^{ss} &= 0.018 \times 200 \times 9.81 \\
&= 35.32 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Convert to kilogram-force for ergonomic comparison.

$$F_p^{ss} = \frac{35.32}{9.81} = 3.60 \ \text{kgf}$$

**Step 4.** State the conclusion of part (a).

> The mean steady-state push force required is **35.32 N ≈ 3.60 kgf**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Compare the required sustained force with the operator's maximum acceptable sustained force.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{required}} &= 3.60 \ \text{kgf} \\
F_{\text{max, female}} &= 14.00 \ \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $3.60 \ \text{kgf} \ll 14 \ \text{kgf}$, **the task is ergonomically acceptable** for the female operator under steady-state conditions.

**Step 2.** However, account for the **initiation phase**. The static coefficient of friction for the same wheel-floor pair is approximately $\mu_s \approx 0.30$ (rubber on smooth vinyl at start-up).

$$F_{initiation} = \mu_s \cdot m \cdot g = 0.30 \times 200 \times 9.81 = 588.6 \ \text{N} = 60 \ \text{kgf}$$

This is **dramatically higher** than the female operator's initial-push capacity of ≈ 16 kgf. Therefore, **the initiation phase fails the ergonomic audit** even though steady state passes.

**Step 3.** Propose three redesign interventions with quantitative justification.

| # | Intervention | Quantitative Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Add a spring-loaded wheel-lift assist / motorised pallet jack** | Eliminates human supply of $F_{initiation}$, reducing required force to ≈ 5 kgf |
| 2 | **Reduce cylinder load to ≤ 80 kg per trip** | New $F_{initiation} = 0.30 \times 80 \times 9.81 = 235 \ \text{N} \approx 24 \ \text{kgf}$ — still borderline, so combine with #1 |
| 3 | **Replace polyurethane wheels with larger-diameter rubber wheels ($\mu_r \to 0.012$)** | Reduces steady-state force to $0.012 \times 200 \times 9.81 = 23.5 \ \text{N} \approx 2.4 \ \text{kgf}$; additionally lowers $\mu_s$ to ≈ 0.20, dropping initiation to ≈ 41 kgf |

> **Final Recommendation:** Combine interventions (1) + (3) for a complete fix.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice Set — Alternative]

**A logistics warehouse uses a four-wheel flatbed trolley of total mass (trolley + load) $m = 400 \ \text{kg}$ on a level concrete floor. The operator must accelerate the trolley from rest to a walking speed of $v = 1.2 \ \text{m/s}$ over a push distance of $d = 2.0 \ \text{m}$, then maintain the speed for a further 25 m. The rolling-resistance coefficient is $\mu_r = 0.022$, and the static-friction coefficient at the wheels is $\mu_s = 0.35$.**
- **(a)** Compute the **initial peak push force** the operator must supply and the **mean push force** during acceleration. (7 marks)
- **(b)** Calculate the **mechanical work** done by the operator during the acceleration phase and the **average power** output during the 25 m sustained push. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Compute the initial (peak) push force required to overcome static friction.

$$F_{peak} = \mu_s \cdot m \cdot g$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{peak} &= 0.35 \times 400 \times 9.81 \\
&= 1373.4 \ \text{N} \\
&\approx 140 \ \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the steady-state rolling force for the level floor.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ss} &= \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \\
&= 0.022 \times 400 \times 9.81 \\
&= 86.33 \ \text{N} \approx 8.80 \ \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Compute the acceleration.

$$a = \frac{v_f^2 - v_i^2}{2d} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{2 \times 2.0} = 0.36 \ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 4.** Compute the inertial force.

$$F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = 400 \times 0.36 = 144 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 5.** Mean push force during the acceleration phase is the sum of inertial and rolling components.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{mean} &= F_{inertia} + F_{rolling} \\
&= 144 + 86.33 \\
&= 230.33 \ \text{N} \\
&\approx 23.5 \ \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion (a):** $F_{peak} \approx 140 \ \text{kgf}$, $F_{mean} \approx 23.5 \ \text{kgf}$. Both values are well above any single-operator capability; a powered assist or two-person team is mandatory.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Mechanical work done during acceleration:

$$W_{accel} = F_{mean} \cdot d$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{accel} &= 230.33 \times 2.0 \\
&= 460.66 \ \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

(Alternatively, work = kinetic-energy gain = $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 400 \times (1.2)^2 = 288 \ \text{J}$. The difference between this and the calculated 460.66 J is the **rolling-loss work** dissipated in the wheels, equal to $F_{rolling} \times d = 86.33 \times 2.0 = 172.66 \ \text{J}$. Total = $288 + 172.66 \approx 460.66$ J. ✓ Internal check passes.)

**Step 2.** Time to traverse the 25 m sustained push.

$$t_{sust} = \frac{d_{sust}}{v} = \frac{25}{1.2} = 20.83 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 3.** Average sustained power output.

$$P_{sust} = F_{ss} \cdot v$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{sust} &= 86.33 \times 1.2 \\
&= 103.6 \ \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion (b):** $W_{accel} = 460.66 \ \text{J}$, $P_{sust} = 103.6 \ \text{W}$. Sustained push power of ~104 W is within the aerobic capacity of a healthy adult (typical sustained-power ceiling ≈ 75–100 W for an 8-hour shift), but only marginally — a clear ergonomic red flag warranting job-rotation or mechanical-assist redesign.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Pushing-Task Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\mu_r$ with $\mu_s$** — Use $\mu_s$ for the *initial peak push* (first ≤ 2 s) and $\mu_r$ for the *sustained steady-state push*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert Newtons to kilogram-force** when comparing against Snook-Ciriello tables. Ergonomic limits are tabulated in **kgf**, not N. A 245 N force = 25 kgf — students who fail to convert lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Neglecting the gradient term** $m g \sin\theta$ when the floor is not perfectly level. Even a 2° slope contributes ≈ 3.5 % of body-weight force — non-negligible in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Confusing *push* with *lift*.** Pushing force is *horizontal*, not vertical. Spinal compression in pushing arises from *trunk moment* resisting the horizontal force, not from a vertical lifted weight.
> 5. **Skipping the unit-consistency check** in the final answer. $F_p$ in N, $m$ in kg, $g$ in m/s² — a mismatch here is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Failing to state the engineering recommendation** explicitly. KTU examiners award the final 1–2 marks for a clear *redesign* statement, not just the numerical answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ High-Density Revision Checklist — Pushing Tasks**
>
> ✅ **Core Equation (Level Floor, Constant Velocity):**
> $\quad F_p^{ss} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$
>
> ✅ **General Dynamic Equation:**
> $\quad F_p = m a + \mu_r m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta$
>
> ✅ **Initial Peak Push:** Use $\mu_s$ in place of $\mu_r$ (peak force dominates in first ~2 s).
>
> ✅ **Sustained Power:** $P = F_p \cdot v$ — must be ≤ ~75–100 W for an 8-h shift.
>
> ✅ **Snook-Ciriello Reference Limits:**
> $\quad$ Male initial push ≈ 25 kgf | Male sustained ≈ 15 kgf
> $\quad$ Female initial push ≈ 16 kgf | Female sustained ≈ 10 kgf
>
> ✅ **Recommended Handle Height:** 90–115 cm (waist to hip level).
>
> ✅ **Push > Pull:** Lower L5/S1 compression, higher force capability, lower metabolic cost.
>
> ✅ **Conversion:** $1 \ \text{kgf} = 9.81 \ \text{N}$ (always required for table comparison).
>
> ✅ **Wheel-Selection Rule:** Larger diameter + softer tread = lower $\mu_r$ = lower $F_p$.
>
> ✅ **NIOSH Reference Compression Limit:** $M_{L5/S1} \leq 3400 \ \text{N}$ (action limit) / 6400 N (maximum permissible).
>
> ✅ **Final Audit Question to Always Ask:** *Can the smallest operator (typically female 5th-percentile) perform the task alone?* If not → redesign.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limits of MMH Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limits of MMH Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is MMH?

**Manual Material Handling (MMH)** refers to any activity in which one or more workers manually lift, lower, push, pull, carry, hold, or restrain a load. It encompasses the human-powered movement of objects without mechanical assistance.

In ergonomic terms, MMH is classified as one of the highest-risk workplace activities. According to occupational safety data, **MMH accounts for over 30% of all workplace injuries**, particularly affecting the lower back, shoulders, and upper extremities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Manual Material Handling (MMH) is defined as the set of human-powered tasks involving the transport, support, or control of a load through lifting, lowering, pushing, pulling, carrying, or holding, whose safe execution is bounded by physiological, biomechanical, and psychophysical limits of the worker.*

---

## 1.2 What are "Limits" of MMH Tasks?

The **limits of MMH tasks** are scientifically determined thresholds beyond which the risk of musculoskeletal injury (especially low-back disorders) becomes unacceptable. These limits are derived from three complementary scientific approaches:

| Approach | What it Measures | Primary Limit Used |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Biomechanical** | Spinal load / joint forces | **Compressive force on L5/S1 ≤ 3400 N** (NIOSH) |
| **Physiological** | Energy expenditure & cardiovascular strain | **Heart rate ≤ 110 bpm** or work energy ≤ **5 kcal/min** |
| **Psychophysical** | Worker-perceived acceptable weight | Maximum acceptable weight for given frequency & posture |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Bucket of Water" Analogy):**
> Imagine every worker has an invisible "energy & stress bucket" being filled by each MMH task. The *biomechanical limit* is the maximum weight before the bucket's "structural handle" (your spine) snaps. The *physiological limit* is how much you can pour in before your "pump" (the heart) overheats. The *psychophysical limit* is how heavy the bucket feels before you, the person, simply say *"this is too heavy — I refuse."* All three limits together define the true safe envelope of MMH.

---

## 1.3 Why MMH Limits Matter in Industry

Poorly designed MMH tasks lead to:
- **Cumulative Trauma Disorders (CTDs)**
- **Low-Back Pain (LBP)** — the #1 occupational disability worldwide
- **Sprains, strains, herniated discs**
- **Direct & indirect costs** (workers' comp, lost productivity, absenteeism)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Highlight:** When asked "Why do we need limits on MMH?", always answer in **three dimensions** — biomechanical (spine), physiological (heart/lungs), and psychophysical (perception). Examiners award full marks only when all three dimensions are explicitly cited.

---

## 1.4 Historical Evolution of MMH Limits

- **1960s–70s:** Fixed lifting limits (e.g., "no more than 50 kg") — overly simplistic
- **1981:** NIOSH Work Practices Guide introduced the first **equation-based lifting limit**
- **1991 (revised):** The **NIOSH Lifting Equation** introduced multi-variable multipliers
- **Present:** NIOSH equation remains the global gold standard for assessing two-handed symmetric lifts

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991 Revised)

The NIOSH Lifting Equation is the cornerstone of modern MMH assessment. It evaluates a lifting task using **six task variables** and produces two outputs:

1. **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** — the load that nearly all healthy workers can lift safely under specified conditions.
2. **Lifting Index (LI)** — a relative measure of physical stress.

---

## 2.2 The Six Multipliers — Logic Breakdown

Each multiplier captures a specific biomechanical or physiological stressor. All multipliers are **≤ 1.0** (i.e., they only reduce the recommended limit), except in some special cases.

### (a) Load Constant (LC)
- Fixed value: **LC = 23 kg (≈ 51 lbs)**
- Represents the maximum recommended weight under **ideal** lifting conditions.

### (b) Horizontal Multiplier (HM)
Captures the horizontal distance (H) of the load from the worker's spine at the start of the lift.

$$HM = \frac{25}{H}$$

where H is in **centimeters**, with valid range **25 cm ≤ H ≤ 63 cm**.

> **Logic:** The farther the load is from the body, the higher the moment arm on the lumbar spine → lower acceptable weight.

### (c) Vertical Multiplier (VM)
Captures the vertical height (V) of the hands at the start of the lift.

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$$

where V is in **cm**, with valid range **0 cm ≤ V ≤ 175 cm**.

> **Logic:** Optimal lifting is at knuckle/elbow height (~75 cm). Deviations upward or downward reduce capacity.

### (d) Distance Multiplier (DM)
Captures the vertical travel distance (D) of the lift.

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D}$$

where D is in **cm**, valid range **25 cm ≤ D ≤ 175 cm**.

> **Logic:** Longer travel distances require more energy and reduce safe capacity.

### (e) Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)
Captures the angular displacement (A) of the body during lifting.

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$$

where A is in **degrees**, valid range **0° ≤ A ≤ 135°**.

> **Logic:** Twisting/bending asymmetrically increases shear forces on spinal discs.

### (f) Frequency Multiplier (FM)
Captures the **lifting frequency (F)** in lifts/min, and incorporates duration:

$$FM \text{ depends on } F \text{ and duration category}$$

FM values range from **0.37 (high freq, long duration) to 1.00 (low freq, short duration)**.

> **Logic:** Repetitive lifting reduces recovery time and increases fatigue risk.

### (g) Coupling Multiplier (CM)
Captures the quality of grip between hands and load:

- Good coupling: **CM = 1.00**
- Fair coupling: **CM = 0.95**
- Poor coupling: **CM = 0.90**

---

## 2.3 The Core Equation

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

$$LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load Weight (L)}}{RWL}$$

### Interpretation of LI:

| Lifting Index (LI) | Risk Category | Recommended Action |
|:------------------:|:-------------:|--------------------|
| LI ≤ 1.0 | Low | Task is acceptable for most workers |
| 1.0 < LI ≤ 2.0 | Moderate | Engineering controls recommended |
| 2.0 < LI ≤ 3.0 | High | Task redesign **mandatory** |
| LI > 3.0 | Very High | Task must be **immediately** redesigned |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Note:** The NIOSH equation applies **only** to two-handed, symmetric, sagittal-plane lifting, with smooth, controlled motion, and good footing. Any deviation may invalidate the equation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---------|---------------|-------|---------------------|
| $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | Recommended Weight Limit | **kg** | All multipliers in valid range |
| $LI = L / RWL$ | Lifting Index | dimensionless | LI > 1.0 → risk increases |
| $HM = 25/H$ | Horizontal Multiplier | — | $25 \le H \le 63$ cm |
| $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \lvert V - 75 \rvert$ | Vertical Multiplier | — | $0 \le V \le 175$ cm |
| $DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D$ | Distance Multiplier | — | $25 \le D \le 175$ cm |
| $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ | Asymmetry Multiplier | — | $0 \le A \le 135°$ |
| $LC = 23$ kg | Load Constant | **kg** | Fixed |

---

## 2.5 Beyond NIOSH: Other MMH Limits

### (i) Biomechanical Limit — Spinal Compression
- **Maximum compressive force on L5/S1 disc = 3400 N** (NIOSH guideline)
- This is the force above which injury risk rises sharply.

### (ii) Physiological Limit — Energy Expenditure
- **Maximum acceptable work rate for 8 hours ≈ 5 kcal/min**
- Equivalent to **average heart rate ≤ 110 bpm**
- Determined from VO₂ max and oxygen consumption tables.

### (iii) Psychophysical Limits — Snook-Ciriello Tables
- Based on worker-perceived maximum acceptable weight
- Accounts for task frequency, distance, height, gender
- Widely used for pushing/pulling and carrying tasks.

### (iv) Maximum Push/Pull Force Limits
- Initial push force (males): **225 N** at 1.5 m height, **154 N** at 0.95 m
- Sustained push force: ~**50%** of initial
- Pull forces are typically **slightly lower** than push.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Used in **warehouse design** to set shelf heights & bin weights.
> - Used in **automotive assembly** to design line-side parts delivery.
> - Used in **patient-handling protocols** in hospitals.
> - Used in **military logistics** for load carriage standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Full NIOSH Equation Calculation

### Problem Statement

A worker lifts a box weighing **15 kg** from a conveyor at **75 cm height** to a shelf at **175 cm height**. The horizontal distance from the spine to the box is **40 cm**. The lift is performed with **90° asymmetry** (twisting required), at a frequency of **3 lifts/min** for **≤ 1 hour**, with a **fair** grip quality. Determine the RWL, LI, and the risk category.

---

### Step 1 — Identify All Task Variables

| Variable | Value | Symbol |
|----------|-------|--------|
| Horizontal distance | H = 40 cm | H |
| Vertical height at start | V = 75 cm | V |
| Vertical travel distance | D = 175 − 75 = 100 cm | D |
| Asymmetry angle | A = 90° | A |
| Frequency | F = 3 lifts/min, duration < 1 hour | F |
| Coupling | Fair | CM |
| Load constant | LC = 23 kg | LC |

---

### Step 2 — Compute Horizontal Multiplier (HM)

Using $HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$:

$$HM = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

Check: $25 \le 40 \le 63$ ✓ (within valid range)

---

### Step 3 — Compute Vertical Multiplier (VM)

Using $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$:

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 75 - 75 \vert$$
$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 0$$
$$VM = 1.000$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute Distance Multiplier (DM)

Using $DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{D}$:

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{100}$$
$$DM = 0.82 + 0.045$$
$$DM = 0.865$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)

Using $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$:

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 90$$
$$AM = 1 - 0.288$$
$$AM = 0.712$$

---

### Step 6 — Determine Frequency Multiplier (FM)

From the NIOSH FM table for:
- Frequency F = 3 lifts/min
- Duration ≤ 1 hour

$$FM = 0.85$$

---

### Step 7 — Determine Coupling Multiplier (CM)

For fair coupling:

$$CM = 0.95$$

---

### Step 8 — Calculate RWL

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 \times 1.000 \times 0.865 \times 0.712 \times 0.85 \times 0.95$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$23 \times 0.625 = 14.375$$

$$14.375 \times 1.000 = 14.375$$

$$14.375 \times 0.865 = 12.434$$

$$12.434 \times 0.712 = 8.853$$

$$8.853 \times 0.85 = 7.525$$

$$7.525 \times 0.95 = 7.149 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{RWL \approx 7.15 \text{ kg}}$$

---

### Step 9 — Calculate Lifting Index (LI)

$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{15}{7.149}$$

$$\boxed{LI \approx 2.10}$$

---

### Step 10 — Interpret Risk

Since **2.0 < LI ≤ 3.0**, the task is in the **HIGH** risk category.

> **Recommendation:** Task redesign is **mandatory**. Suggested interventions:
> - Eliminate the 90° asymmetry by reorienting the workstation.
> - Reduce horizontal distance (move conveyor closer).
> - Reduce travel distance by raising the shelf.
> - Improve coupling (use containers with handles).

---

## 3.2 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix

The following analytical matrix illustrates how the RWL changes when **one variable** is altered while all others are held constant (base case: H=40, V=75, D=50, A=0, F=1, CM=good).

| Variable Changed | New Value | New RWL (kg) | % Change vs. Base |
|------------------|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Horizontal (H) | 25 cm | 8.94 | +38.6% |
| Horizontal (H) | 63 cm | 3.55 | −47.0% |
| Vertical (V) | 125 cm | 8.04 | −10.1% |
| Vertical (V) | 25 cm | 6.47 | −27.6% |
| Asymmetry (A) | 90° | 7.92 | −11.4% |
| Frequency (F) | 6 lifts/min | 9.30 | +4.6% |
| Coupling (CM) | Poor | 8.10 | −9.4% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Matrix:** *Horizontal distance* (H) is the **single most influential multiplier** in the NIOSH equation. Always optimize H first when redesigning lifting tasks.

---

## 3.3 Compressive Force Calculation (Biomechanical Limit)

The NIOSH biomechanical model computes the **L5/S1 compressive force** as:

$$F_{compress} = F_{spine} + F_{muscle}$$

Where:
- $F_{spine}$ = weight of upper body acting on L5/S1
- $F_{muscle}$ = erector spinae muscle force (estimated via static moment equilibrium)

For a forward-bent lift with load L at horizontal distance H:

$$F_{muscle} = \frac{L \cdot H}{d_{muscle}}$$

where $d_{muscle}$ ≈ 5 cm (lever arm of the erector spinae).

### Example

If a 20 kg box is held at H = 50 cm:

$$F_{muscle} = \frac{20 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.50}{0.05} = 1962 \text{ N}$$

Adding torso weight contribution (~400 N):

$$F_{compress} = 1962 + 400 = 2362 \text{ N}$$

Since 2362 N < 3400 N, this lift is biomechanically safe — but this is only one component of the full analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often report "compressive force < 3400 N = safe." This is **incorrect**. The compressive limit is a *necessary but not sufficient* condition. You must also check **physiological** and **psychophysical** limits.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NIOSH Lifting Equation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Task Variables] --> B[Load Constant: LC = 23 kg]
    A --> C[Horizontal Distance: H cm]
    A --> D[Vertical Position: V cm]
    A --> E[Travel Distance: D cm]
    A --> F[Asymmetry Angle: A degrees]
    A --> G[Frequency: F lifts per min]
    A --> H[Coupling Quality: Good / Fair / Poor]

    C --> C1[Compute HM equals 25 over H]
    D --> D1[Compute VM equals 1 minus 0 point 003 times V minus 75]
    E --> E1[Compute DM equals 0 point 82 plus 4 point 5 over D]
    F --> F1[Compute AM equals 1 minus 0 point 0032 times A]
    G --> G1[Lookup FM from Frequency Duration Table]
    H --> H1[Select CM: 1 point 00 / 0 point 95 / 0 point 90]

    B --> I[Multiply All Multipliers]
    C1 --> I
    D1 --> I
    E1 --> I
    F1 --> I
    G1 --> I
    H1 --> I

    I --> J[RWL Output in kg]
    K[Actual Load Weight L in kg] --> L[LI equals L divided by RWL]
    J --> L

    L --> M{LI Value Decision}
    M --> M1[LI less than or equal 1: Low Risk]
    M --> M2[LI between 1 and 2: Moderate Risk]
    M --> M3[LI between 2 and 3: High Risk - Redesign]
    M --> M4[LI greater than 3: Very High Risk - Immediate Action]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 MMH Limits — Three-Tier Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[MMH TASK LIMITS] --> B[Biomechanical Limit]
    Root --> P[Physiological Limit]
    Root --> Ps[Psychophysical Limit]

    B --> B1[Spinal Compression less than 3400 N]
    B --> B2[Joint Moment Limits]
    B --> B3[Shear Force Limits]

    P --> P1[Heart Rate less than 110 bpm]
    P --> P2[Energy Expenditure less than 5 kcal per min]
    P --> P3[Oxygen Consumption less than 1 liter per min]

    Ps --> Ps1[Snook Ciriello Workload Tables]
    Ps --> Ps2[Maximum Acceptable Weight MAW]
    Ps --> Ps3[Liberty Mutual Tables]

    style Root fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Ps fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Risk Category Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Calculate LI] --> Check{LI Range}

    Check -->|LI less than or equal 1 point 0| Low[Low Risk: Acceptable Task]
    Check -->|LI between 1 and 2| Mod[Moderate Risk: Engineering Controls Advised]
    Check -->|LI between 2 and 3| High[High Risk: Task Redesign Mandatory]
    Check -->|LI greater than 3 point 0| VHigh[Very High Risk: Immediate Redesign]

    Mod --> ModA[Improve Coupling]
    Mod --> ModB[Reduce Horizontal Distance]
    Mod --> ModC[Lower Frequency]

    High --> HighA[Reorient Workstation to Eliminate Asymmetry]
    High --> HighB[Mechanical Lift Aids]
    High --> HighC[Reduce Load Weight]

    VHigh --> VH1[Stop Task Immediately]
    VHigh --> VH2[Implement Mechanical Handling]
    VHigh --> VH3[Job Rotation Protocols]

    style Low fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style Mod fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000
    style High fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style VHigh fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

---

## 4.4 Human Spine Biomechanics — Lifting Schematic (Text-Based)

```
                ___________
               |           |  ← Arms reaching forward
               |   Load    |     (horizontal distance H from spine)
               |    L      |
                \_________/
                     |
                     |  ← Vertical position V
                     |
        _______|_______ ← Shoulder
        |              |
        |   TORSO      | ← Forward bending = +H effect
        |              |
        |    L5/S1     | ← Spinal compression here
        |____|____|____|     (limit = 3400 N)
              |    |
              |    | ← Erector spinae lever arm (≈ 5 cm)
              |____|
               Legs
```

> The diagram above illustrates how a load **L** held at horizontal distance **H** creates a moment about the **L5/S1** disc, which the back muscles must counter — generating **compressive force** on the spine.
