## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define body movement in the context of human factors engineering. Classify them based on the three anatomical planes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A body movement is the displacement of a body segment about a joint, defined by an axis of rotation, a plane of motion, and a measurable angular range.
- **Sagittal plane (1 mark):** Flexion, Extension, Dorsiflexion, Plantarflexion.
- **Frontal plane (0.5 mark):** Abduction, Adduction, Lateral Flexion, Inversion, Eversion.
- **Transverse plane (0.5 mark):** Rotation (medial/lateral), Pronation, Supination.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between pronation and supination. Why is supination considered the strongest grip position?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Pronation (1 mark):** Forearm rotation such that the palm faces downward/posteriorly; ROM 0°–90°.
- **Supination (1 mark):** Forearm rotation such that the palm faces upward/anteriorly; ROM 0°–90°.
- **Why supination is strongest (1 mark):** In supination, the **biceps brachii** acts as a powerful elbow flexor *and* supinator, providing maximum mechanical advantage. This is why ergonomic screwdriver handles are designed to bias the forearm into a **neutral–supinated** position to reduce wrist strain and maximize torque output.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> *(Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE from Q-A and Q-B)*

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify the different types of body movements in human factors engineering. Explain the plane of motion, axis of rotation, and primary joints for any six of them with diagrams. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The classification of body movements in human factors engineering is based on the **anatomical plane** in which the motion occurs and the **axis** about which rotation takes place. The three cardinal planes are sagittal, frontal (coronal), and transverse. Six important movements are explained below:

**1. Flexion (Sagittal plane, frontal axis):**
A movement that decreases the angle between two articulating bones, generally in the anterior direction. Primary joints involved are the shoulder (0°–180°), elbow (0°–150°), hip (0°–120°), and knee (0°–135°). In ergonomics, sustained trunk flexion beyond 20° is a high-risk posture for lower-back pain.

**2. Extension (Sagittal plane, frontal axis):**
A movement that increases the angle between two articulating bones, returning from a flexed position. It occurs at the same joints as flexion but in the opposite direction. Hyperextension (beyond neutral) should generally be avoided as it increases compressive loads on spinal facet joints.

**3. Abduction (Frontal plane, sagittal axis):**
A movement of a body part away from the midline. The shoulder has the largest range (0°–180°), followed by the hip (0°–45°). It defines the **lateral work envelope** in workstation design; controls placed beyond 40° abduction are difficult to operate.

**4. Adduction (Frontal plane, sagittal axis):**
The reverse of abduction — movement of a body part toward or past the midline. Typical ROM is 0°–50° past midline. Relevant in cross-body reach tasks such as keyboard-to-mouse transitions.

**5. Pronation (Transverse plane, vertical axis):**
Forearm rotation turning the palm downward/posteriorly. ROM is 0°–90°. This is the **highest-risk wrist posture** for carpal tunnel syndrome when combined with high-grip force, hence power tools are designed to allow **neutral or supinated** wrist positions.

**6. Supination (Transverse plane, vertical axis):**
The reverse of pronation — palm rotates upward/anteriorly. ROM is 0°–90°. Supinated grip is the **strongest human grip position** because the biceps brachii assists in flexion, increasing the mechanical advantage of the forearm flexors.

| Movement | Plane | Axis | Primary Joint | Typical ROM (deg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flexion | Sagittal | Frontal | Shoulder, Elbow, Hip, Knee | 0–180 |
| Extension | Sagittal | Frontal | Same | 0–50 |
| Abduction | Frontal | Sagittal | Shoulder, Hip | 0–180 |
| Adduction | Frontal | Sagittal | Shoulder, Hip | 0–50 past mid |
| Pronation | Transverse | Vertical | Forearm (radioulnar) | 0–90 |
| Supination | Transverse | Vertical | Forearm (radioulnar) | 0–90 |

(Diagrams: refer to Figures in standard KTU prescribed text.)

**(b) Discuss the ergonomic significance of body movements in the design of workplaces and tools. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The ergonomic significance of body movements in workplace and tool design is rooted in the principle of **fitting the task to the human (anthropometric and biomechanical fit)** rather than forcing the human to adapt to the task.

**1. Work Envelope Definition:**
The **normal working zone** is bounded by an arm's reach with the shoulder flexed 0°–25° in the sagittal plane, while the **maximum reach zone** is bounded by 0°–90° flexion. Primary controls (frequently used) must lie within the normal zone, while secondary or emergency controls may be placed in the maximum zone. This is the basis of the **Niebel–Freivalds zonal theory** taught in KTU.

**2. Reduction of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs):**
Repeated movements such as **wrist flexion + ulnar deviation + high grip force** ("the dangerous triad") lead to **carpal tunnel syndrome**. By analyzing body movements, ergonomists redesign tasks to keep the wrist in a **neutral posture** (0°–15° flexion, 0°–10° deviation), reducing injury rates by 30–60% (as per **NIOSH cumulative trauma studies**).

**3. Tool Handle Design:**
The **power grip** requires the hand to close around a cylindrical handle. Anthropometric data show the **mean hand breadth** is **87 mm (men) / 78 mm (women)**. The optimal **handle diameter** is therefore **30–50 mm** — outside this range, grip strength drops sharply. Movement analysis confirms that **supinated grip orientation** reduces forearm muscle strain by **up to 25%** compared to a fully pronated grip.

**4. Manual Lifting Tasks:**
The **Niosh Lifting Equation** uses trunk flexion angle, knee flexion angle, and reach distance to compute the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)**. Movement analysis shows that lifting with a **stooped posture (lumbar flexion > 20°)** reduces RWL by **40%** compared to a **squat lift (knee flexion > 90°)**.

**5. Job Rotation and Rest Cycles:**
Movement analysis identifies muscle groups that are statically loaded (e.g., sustained shoulder elevation). **Rohmert's fatigue curve** shows that for static holds, endurance time **T = 1.38 × (Load/MVC)⁻¹·⁴⁵ minutes**. Design recommendations: alternate tasks to use different muscle groups, and provide **micro-breaks of 30–60 seconds** per 20 minutes of repetitive work.

**6. Workspace Layout and Reach Zones:**
Using shoulder **abduction ROM** (0°–180°), the lateral reach envelope is set at **~ 60° abduction** for sustained work and **~ 90° for occasional reach**. Controls placed outside this zone cause **shoulder abduction–external rotation strain**, a common complaint in overhead assembly workstations.

**Conclusion:** A scientific understanding of body movements is the cornerstone of ergonomic design, enabling workplaces that are safer, more productive, and compliant with the **Factories Act 1948 (India)** and the **OSH Code 2020** standards adopted in KTU 2024 scheme assessments.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the movements of the human shoulder joint. List the typical range of motion (in degrees) for each movement and indicate the corresponding plane and axis. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **shoulder (glenohumeral) joint** is a **ball-and-socket joint** with the greatest range of motion of any joint in the human body. It allows movements in all three cardinal planes plus combinations.

**1. Flexion / Extension (Sagittal plane, Frontal axis):**
- Flexion: 0°–180° (lifting the arm forward and upward).
- Extension: 0°–50° (moving the arm backward).
- *Ergonomic note:* Continuous flexion > 60° without support leads to **supraspinatus tendonitis**.

**2. Abduction / Adduction (Frontal plane, Sagittal axis):**
- Abduction: 0°–180° (raising the arm sideways).
- Adduction: 0°–50° past midline (lowering toward and across the body).
- *Ergonomic note:* The "danger zone" for shoulder abduction is **60°–120°**, where the rotator cuff tendons are most impinged.

**3. Medial (Internal) Rotation / Lateral (External) Rotation (Transverse plane, Vertical axis):**
- Medial rotation: 0°–90° (arm at side, forearm turns inward).
- Lateral rotation: 0°–90° (arm at side, forearm turns outward).
- *Ergonomic note:* Medial rotation is required for **screw-driving** tasks; lateral rotation is required for **key-turning** tasks.

**4. Horizontal Abduction / Adduction (Transverse plane, Vertical axis, after 90° abduction):**
- Horizontal abduction: 0°–130° (arm moves backward in horizontal plane).
- Horizontal adduction: 0°–40° (arm moves across the chest).
- *Ergonomic note:* Important in defining **rear-reach** in cockpit design.

**5. Circumduction (Composite of all three planes):**
- 360° conical loop at full ROM.
- *Ergonomic note:* Defines the **3-D reachable workspace** in front of the operator.

| Movement | Plane | Axis | ROM (deg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flexion | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–180 |
| Extension | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–50 |
| Abduction | Frontal | Sagittal | 0–180 |
| Adduction | Frontal | Sagittal | 0–50 past mid |
| Medial Rotation | Transverse | Vertical | 0–90 |
| Lateral Rotation | Transverse | Vertical | 0–90 |
| Horizontal Abd | Transverse | Vertical | 0–130 |
| Horizontal Add | Transverse | Vertical | 0–40 |
| Circumduction | Composite | Composite | 360° |

**(b) A worker lifts a 10 kg load from the floor to shoulder height. Identify the body movements at the ankle, knee, hip, lumbar spine, shoulder, and elbow. For each, state the plane, axis, and a typical ROM. Use this analysis to recommend an ergonomic lifting technique. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Joint | Movement | Plane | Axis | Typical ROM Used (deg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ankle | Dorsiflexion → Plantarflexion | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–50 |
| Knee | Flexion → Extension | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–135 |
| Hip | Flexion → Extension | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–120 |
| Lumbar Spine | Flexion (controlled) | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–20 (safe) |
| Shoulder | Flexion | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–90 |
| Elbow | Flexion (to grip load) | Sagittal | Frontal | 0–90 |

**Analytical Reasoning:**

A **squat lift** uses **ankle dorsiflexion, knee flexion, and hip flexion** as the primary lower-limb movements, while keeping the **lumbar spine near neutral (0°–20° flexion)**. This redistributes the load to the powerful **quadriceps and gluteal muscles**, sparing the lumbar discs.

In contrast, a **stoop lift** (legs straight, back bent) forces the **lumbar flexion to 45°–60°**, dramatically increasing the **compressive and shear forces** on the L5–S1 disc. The NIOSH compressive force limit is **3,400 N**; stoop lifting of 10 kg can exceed this limit by **30–50%**, leading to high risk of herniated disc.

**Ergonomic Recommendations:**

1. **Use a squat or semi-squat lift** (knee flexed 90°–135°, hip flexed 45°–60°, lumbar spine < 20° flexion).
2. **Keep the load close to the body** (≤ 25 cm from the lumbar spine) to minimize moment arm.
3. **Avoid trunk rotation** during the lift — pivot the feet instead.
4. **Use the legs as the primary lifting muscle group** (gluteus maximus, quadriceps).
5. **For repetitive lifts**, follow the **Niosh recommended weight limit (RWL) of 23 kg** under ideal conditions and adjust downward by **multipliers** for awkward postures.
6. **Maintain neutral wrist and shoulder postures** to avoid secondary CTDs.

**Conclusion:** Movement-based analysis confirms that **squat lifting** with a **neutral spine** is biomechanically optimal, reducing the lumbar disc compression force by approximately **40–60%** compared to a stoop lift.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "axis" and "plane":** The *plane* is the surface in which motion occurs (e.g., sagittal); the *axis* is the line about which rotation happens (e.g., frontal axis). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for each swap.
>
> 2. **Missing the neutral 0° reference:** Always state that all joint angles are measured from the **anatomical position**, not from a flexed starting point.
>
> 3. **Quoting ROM without the unit "degrees":** Writing "shoulder flexion 180" is incomplete — write **"0°–180°"**.
>
> 4. **Confusing dorsiflexion with extension:** Dorsiflexion is a *foot* movement (toes up), not ankle extension. Examiners specifically test this.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the ergonomic application:** A purely anatomical answer (no workplace relevance) will cap marks at **60%** of the allotted. Always link each movement to a **workstation, tool, or task** example.
>
> 6. **Mislabeling pronation/supination:** Pronation = palm down (posterior); supination = palm up (anterior). The **mnemonic**: "**SUP**ination = **SUP**ply (hold soup in palm)".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Body Movements in Human Factors**
>
> - [x] All joint angles are measured from the **anatomical position** (0° reference).
> - [x] Three cardinal planes: **Sagittal, Frontal (Coronal), Transverse**.
> - [x] Three cardinal axes: **Frontal, Sagittal, Vertical**.
> - [x] **Flexion decreases** joint angle; **Extension increases** it.
> - [x] **Abduction = away from midline; Adduction = toward midline.**
> - [x] **Pronation = palm down; Supination = palm up** (supination = strongest grip).
> - [x] **Dorsiflexion = toes up; Plantarflexion = toes down.**
> - [x] **Inversion = sole in; Eversion = sole out.**
> - [x] **Circumduction** = 360° circular movement (combined).
> - [x] **Opposition** is unique to the **thumb** (CMC joint).
> - [x] **Trunk flexion > 20°** in lifting = high LBP risk.
> - [x] **Shoulder abduction 60°–120°** = rotator cuff impingement zone.
> - [x] **Pronated grip + high force + repetition** = carpal tunnel syndrome triad.
> - [x] **Normal work zone = 0°–25° shoulder flexion; Maximum zone = 0°–90° flexion.**
> - [x] **Handle diameter 30–50 mm** = optimal for power grip.
> - [x] **Squat lift > stoop lift** for spinal loading reduction.
> - [x] **Angular velocity ω = Δθ/Δt** (rad/s); **Linear velocity v = ω × L**.
> - [x] **Static torque τ = W × L** at the joint.
> - [x] **Rohmert's fatigue curve** governs static-hold endurance.
> - [x] **NIOSH RWL = 23 kg** under ideal conditions, adjusted by **6 multipliers**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classes of Motor Movements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classes of Motor Movements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Human Factors Engineering (Ergonomics)**, the term **"Classes of Motor Movements"** refers to the systematic taxonomy used to categorize human skeletal-muscular actions based on their **kinematic structure**, **degree of feedback dependency**, **temporal continuity**, and **neuromuscular control characteristics**. These classifications allow ergonomic analysts to predict task performance, design workplaces, allocate control-display ratios, and optimize the human-machine interface.

The two principal classification frameworks taught under the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

### A. Classification Based on Movement Structure (Welford / Schmidt Taxonomy)

| Class | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Discrete Movements** | Have a clearly identifiable start and finish point; involve one primary agonist burst. |
| **Serial Movements** | A concatenated sequence of discrete sub-movements performed in a fixed order. |
| **Continuous Movements** | Have no distinct beginning or end; spatial and temporal features are co-varied. |

### B. Classification Based on Control Loop (Adams / Pew Taxonomy)

| Class | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Open-Loop (Ballistic) Movements** | Pre-programmed actions completed before feedback can influence the outcome. Duration typically **< 200 ms**. |
| **Closed-Loop (Servo) Movements** | Movements that use sensory feedback during execution to correct deviations. Duration typically **> 250 ms**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 expects students to differentiate between *discrete, serial, and continuous* motor actions and to relate them to **open-loop vs. closed-loop** neuromuscular control, which directly impacts workstation design (e.g., choice of joysticks, push-buttons, foot-pedals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Reference:** The taxonomy draws heavily on the works of **A.T. Welford (1968)** and **J.A. Adams (1971)** on the structure of human skill.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **musician playing a piano**:

- **Discrete Movement** = A single key-press with a sharp attack and release. You can clearly tell *when* it started and *when* it ended.
- **Serial Movement** = Playing a fixed musical scale (Do-Re-Mi...). Each note is a discrete movement, but they are chained together in a predetermined order, like typing a word.
- **Continuous Movement** = Drawing a smooth violin bow across the string. There is no clear "start note" or "end note" — the movement flows seamlessly, just like steering a car around a long curve.

Now imagine the **same musician in two contrasting moods**:

- **Open-Loop (Ballistic)** = A snappy, reflexive drum hit. The drummer commits fully to the action *before* hearing whether it landed on the beat — there is **no time** for feedback during the strike.
- **Closed-Loop (Servo)** = Slowly placing a fingertip on a single piano key in a quiet studio. The performer watches and listens to the descending finger and can **continuously correct** its trajectory until contact.

This analogy captures the essence of human motor control — actions are shaped by whether the nervous system **plans ahead** (feedforward) or **corrects on-the-fly** (feedback).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Reaction Time (RT)** for simple movements ≈ **180–250 ms**.
- **Movement Time (MT)** in Fitts' Law: $MT = a + b \cdot ID$
- **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits: $ID = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{2A}{W} \right)$
- **Neuromuscular Delay** ≈ **30–50 ms** (minimum latency for feedback use).
- **Open-Loop Threshold**: Movements shorter than **200 ms** are typically ballistic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class I — Discrete Movements (Single-Degree Bursts)

A discrete movement is characterized by a **single, identifiable motor action** with an unmistakable initiation and termination. Examples include:
- Pressing a light switch
- Pulling a fire alarm lever
- Tapping a touch-screen icon
- Trigger-pull of a firearm

### Operational Logic

The neuro-motor system executes a **tri-phasic EMG pattern**:
1. **First Agonist Burst (AG1)** — Accelerates the limb toward the target.
2. **Antagonist Burst (AG2)** — Decelerates the limb to prevent overshoot.
3. **Clamp Phase** — Holds the limb steady at the target using co-contraction.

The presence of **visual proprioceptive feedback** is *not* required for short bursts, but it is essential for accuracy when the movement duration exceeds ~250 ms.

## 2.2 Class II — Serial Movements (Chained Discrete Actions)

A serial movement is a **temporal concatenation of two or more discrete sub-movements**. The hallmark feature is **order**: the actions must occur in a pre-specified sequence.

### Sub-categorization

- **Homogeneous Serial Movement** — All components are identical (e.g., tapping the same key repeatedly while reading).
- **Heterogeneous Serial Movement** — Components differ (e.g., typing the word "ergonomics" — each letter is a distinct movement).

### Performance Law for Serial Movements

Total Time for a Serial Task (Hick's Law + Fitts' Law combined):

$$
T_{serial} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( a + b \cdot \log_2 \left( \frac{2A_i}{W_i} + 1 \right) \right)
$$

This is critical when designing **keyboard layouts**, **control sequences in aircraft cockpits**, and **assembly-line workstations**.

## 2.3 Class III — Continuous Movements (Co-Varying Trajectories)

Continuous movements lack a defined start and end. The performer must **simultaneously regulate spatial position and temporal velocity**.

Examples:
- Steering a car along a winding road
- Tracking a moving radar blip with a joystick
- Drawing a freehand circle
- Singing a sustained vowel

### Control Theory Framework

Continuous movements are best modeled as a **closed-loop pursuit tracking system** with three controllers:
1. **Predictive (Feedforward) Controller** — Uses learned models of the target.
2. **Compensatory (Feedback) Controller** — Corrects residual error.
3. **Pursuit (Preview) Controller** — Updates based on target motion.

## 2.4 Class IV — Open-Loop (Ballistic) Movements

These are **pre-programmed**, all-or-nothing actions. Once initiated, they cannot be modified. The central nervous system (CNS) sends the entire motor program to the muscles *before* the movement begins.

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| Duration | **< 200 ms** |
| Feedback Used | None during execution |
| Advantage | Speed, reflex-like response |
| Disadvantage | Cannot correct mid-flight errors |
| Typical Use | Emergency stop buttons, reflex tapping |

## 2.5 Class V — Closed-Loop (Servo) Movements

These movements **rely on sensory feedback** (vision, proprioception, auditory) to compare the actual limb position against the desired target.

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| Duration | **> 250 ms** |
| Feedback Used | Continuous (visual, tactile, proprioceptive) |
| Advantage | High accuracy, self-correcting |
| Disadvantage | Slower due to processing delay (~30–50 ms per loop) |
| Typical Use | Precision assembly, surgery, calligraphy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A worker's task-design should **minimize the need for closed-loop precision** by widening tolerances, providing larger displays, and using detent mechanisms — this lowers fatigue and error rates.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ | Fitts' Law — Movement Time as a function of Index of Difficulty | $MT$ in ms, $ID$ in bits |
| 2 | $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$ | Index of Difficulty (Standard Form) | bits |
| 3 | $ID = \log_2(A/W + 1)$ | Index of Difficulty (Shannon Form) | bits |
| 4 | $T = a + b \cdot H$ | Hick-Hyman Law — Choice Reaction Time vs. Information | $T$ in ms, $H$ in bits |
| 5 | $H = \log_2(N)$ | Information content of N equally-likely choices | bits |
| 6 | $v_{max} \approx 1/3 \cdot v_0$ | Peak velocity ≈ 1/3 of initial velocity (Brooks' rule) | m/s |
| 7 | $T_{serial} = \sum T_i$ | Total time of a serial task = sum of sub-movement times | ms |
| 8 | **200 ms rule** | Movements below this duration are open-loop | ms |
| 9 | **250 ms rule** | Movements above this duration use closed-loop control | ms |
| 10 | $R = \log_2 (1 + P/C)$ | Rate of information transmission (motor channel capacity) | bits/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive the design of **Fitts' Law-compliant interfaces** (e.g., larger touch-targets on smartphones for safer emergency access, optimal button sizes on medical infusion pumps, and tailored control-display ratios in fighter-jet cockpits).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Threshold

The 200 ms threshold is not arbitrary — it is derived from the **minimum feedback latency** of the human nervous system.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the minimum reaction time to a stimulus.**

For a simple stimulus-response pair, the reaction time is empirically observed to be approximately:
$$
RT_{min} \approx 180 \text{ to } 200 \text{ ms}
$$

This includes sensory transduction, central processing, and motor output.

**Step 2 — Identify the neuromuscular delay.**

Once a corrective motor command is generated, it requires additional time for the muscle to produce measurable force:
$$
t_{NMD} \approx 30 \text{ to } 50 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the minimum useful feedback window.**

For feedback to influence the *outcome* of a movement, the corrective force must arrive before the movement terminates:
$$
T_{min,feedback} = RT_{min} + t_{NMD}
$$

$$
T_{min,feedback} \approx 180 + 50 = 230 \text{ ms} \approx 200 \text{ to } 250 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the binary classification rule.**

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
&\text{If } T_{movement} < 200 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Open-Loop (Ballistic)} \\
&\text{If } T_{movement} > 250 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Closed-Loop (Servo)} \\
&\text{If } 200 \leq T_{movement} \leq 250 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Transitional (Mixed)}
\end{aligned}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Board questions frequently ask students to "identify the type of control" for a given task. Use the threshold to justify your answer numerically.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fitts' Law (Analytical Form)

**Step 1 — Define the fundamental relationship.**

Fitts postulated that the *time* to move to a target is proportional to its *information content* (or difficulty):

$$
MT = f(ID)
$$

where $ID$ is the **Index of Difficulty**, measured in **bits**.

**Step 2 — Define Index of Difficulty.**

By analogy with Shannon's channel capacity theorem:

$$
ID = \log_2 \left( \frac{2A}{W} \right)
$$

where:
- $A$ = **Amplitude** (distance from start to target center)
- $W$ = **Width** (target tolerance zone)
- The factor of **2** accounts for movement initiation noise.

**Step 3 — Linearize for empirical use.**

Experimental data (Fitts & Peterson, 1954) showed that MT vs. ID is essentially linear:

$$
\boxed{MT = a + b \cdot ID = a + b \cdot \log_2 \left( \frac{2A}{W} \right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Shannon correction (MacKenzie, 1989).**

For low-precision cases where $A < W$, the original formula yields *negative* ID. MacKenzie proposed:

$$
ID = \log_2 \left( \frac{A}{W} + 1 \right)
$$

This is the form used in **HCI** (Human-Computer Interaction) and KTU board answers.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Board Question)

**Problem:** A factory operator must reach a control button placed **400 mm** away. The button has a diameter of **40 mm**. Using Fitts' Law, calculate the Index of Difficulty (using the Shannon form) and estimate the movement time, given $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit.

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

$$
A = 400 \text{ mm}, \quad W = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad a = 50 \text{ ms}, \quad b = 150 \text{ ms/bit}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{A}{W} = \frac{400}{40} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Index of Difficulty.**

$$
ID = \log_2 \left( \frac{A}{W} + 1 \right) = \log_2 (10 + 1) = \log_2 (11)
$$

Using $\log_2 (11) = \dfrac{\ln 11}{\ln 2}$:

$$
ID = \frac{2.3979}{0.6931} \approx 3.46 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Movement Time.**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 50 + (150 \times 3.46)
$$

$$
MT = 50 + 519 = 569 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Classify the movement.**

Since $MT = 569 \text{ ms} > 250 \text{ ms}$, the movement is **Closed-Loop (Servo)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The operator requires nearly 0.6 seconds to safely reach and press the button. Designers should place the most-used controls closer (lower $A$) and make them physically larger (higher $W$) to reduce cycle time and fatigue.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Discrete | Serial | Continuous | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start/End Defined | Yes | Sub-units Yes | No | Yes | Yes |
| Typical Duration | 100–500 ms | > 1 s | Indefinite | < 200 ms | > 250 ms |
| Feedback Use | Optional | Per subunit | Continuous | None | Heavy |
| Speed | Fast–Medium | Medium | Variable | Very Fast | Slower |
| Accuracy Demand | High | Medium | Low–Medium | Low | High |
| Example | Button press | Typing | Steering | Reflex blink | Surgery |
| Control Theory | Single servo | Multi-servo | Pursuit tracker | Feedforward | Feedback |
| Learning Stage | Cognitive→Associative | Associative→Autonomous | Autonomous | Reflex | Skill |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the three structural classes of motor movements. Give one industrial example for each.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The three structural classes of motor movements are:

1. **Discrete Movements:** Actions with a clearly defined start and end. *Example:* Pressing an emergency stop button on a conveyor belt.
2. **Serial Movements:** A sequential chain of discrete sub-movements performed in a fixed order. *Example:* Typing a password on an industrial HMI panel.
3. **Continuous Movements:** Smooth, sustained actions lacking a distinct beginning or termination. *Example:* A crane operator steering a load along a curved path.

**Why these classes matter:** They determine the **type of control system** (open-loop vs. closed-loop) a designer must accommodate. Discrete tasks favor push-buttons; continuous tasks favor joysticks.

### Question 2: Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop motor control. State the threshold duration for each.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Open-Loop (Ballistic) | Closed-Loop (Servo) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback Use | None during execution | Continuous sensory feedback |
| Duration | **< 200 ms** | **> 250 ms** |
| Programming | Pre-set motor program | Real-time correction |
| Accuracy | Lower (no mid-flight correction) | Higher (error-corrected) |
| Example | A blink reflex | A surgeon suturing a wound |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write "open-loop uses no feedback" — but feedback is *generated*; it simply arrives **after** the action completes. Frame it as "feedback is too late to influence the outcome."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Module Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 3 — Internal Choice**
> *Answer ANY ONE full question from Module 3 (typically Q.9 or Q.10 of the ESE paper).*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify motor movements into three structural categories. For each, describe the **tri-phasic EMG pattern** (where applicable) and provide one **human-machine interface** example from a real industrial setting. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Adams Classification** of motor movements. Discuss how the **200 ms / 250 ms threshold** is derived from the minimum reaction time and neuromuscular delay. Compute the movement time for a task with $A = 300$ mm and $W = 25$ mm using Fitts' Law (Shannon form), with $a = 60$ ms, $b = 130$ ms/bit, and classify the resulting movement. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) — 7 Marks**

The three structural classes are:

1. **Discrete Movements (3 marks):** These involve a single identifiable action. The EMG pattern consists of:
   - **AG1 (Agonist Burst):** Accelerates the limb.
   - **AG2 (Antagonist Burst):** Decelerates the limb.
   - **Clamp Phase:** Co-contraction to hold position.
   *Industrial example:* A CNC operator pressing the *Cycle Start* button.

2. **Serial Movements (2 marks):** A chain of discrete sub-actions in fixed order.
   *Industrial example:* A keyboard operator entering a 6-digit shift code on a control panel.

3. **Continuous Movements (2 marks):** No discrete start/end.
   *Industrial example:* A radar operator tracking an aircraft on a PPI display using a joystick.

---

**(b) — 7 Marks**

**Adams Classification (3 marks):**

Adams (1971) divided motor movements based on their **dependence on sensory feedback**:

- **Open-Loop (Ballistic):** Movements with duration < 200 ms where feedback cannot intervene.
- **Closed-Loop (Servo):** Movements > 250 ms that integrate visual, proprioceptive, and tactile feedback to minimize error.

**Threshold Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
T_{min,feedback} = RT_{min} + t_{NMD} \approx 180 + 50 \approx 230 \text{ ms}
$$

For feedback to be useful, the movement must exceed this threshold. Empirically, this gives the **200/250 ms cut-off**.

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given $A = 300$ mm, $W = 25$ mm, $a = 60$ ms, $b = 130$ ms/bit:

$$
\frac{A}{W} = \frac{300}{25} = 12
$$

$$
ID = \log_2(12 + 1) = \log_2(13) = \frac{\ln 13}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.565}{0.693} \approx 3.70 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
MT = 60 + (130 \times 3.70) = 60 + 481 = 541 \text{ ms}
$$

Since $MT = 541 \text{ ms} > 250 \text{ ms}$, the movement is **Closed-Loop (Servo)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop servo control model** of a human motor movement. Label all key components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A quality-control worker uses a computer mouse to click on an icon located **120 mm** away. The icon's click-target is **15 mm** wide. Using Fitts' Law (Shannon form), calculate: (i) the Index of Difficulty, (ii) the Movement Time (with $a = 45$ ms, $b = 110$ ms/bit), and (iii) determine whether the movement will be open-loop or closed-loop. Justify your answer using the **Adams threshold**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) — 7 Marks**

**Closed-Loop Servo Control Model:**

Refer to **Section 4.2** (Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Servo Control Architecture).

Key components (5 marks):

1. **Reference Signal:** The desired target location (e.g., icon coordinates).
2. **Comparator (Error Detector):** Computes the difference between reference and actual position.
3. **Controller (CNS):** Generates motor commands based on the error signal.
4. **Effector (Muscle-Limb System):** Executes the motor command.
5. **Feedback Pathways:** Visual, proprioceptive, and tactile channels returning actual position to the comparator.
6. **Loop Delay:** Approximately **200–250 ms** for the entire cycle.

**Operation (2 marks):** The comparator continuously subtracts actual from desired position. Any non-zero error triggers a corrective motor command. This cycle repeats until the error is within the target's tolerance zone.

---

**(b) — 7 Marks**

Given: $A = 120$ mm, $W = 15$ mm, $a = 45$ ms, $b = 110$ ms/bit.

**(i) Index of Difficulty (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{A}{W} = \frac{120}{15} = 8
$$

$$
ID = \log_2(8 + 1) = \log_2(9) = \frac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.197}{0.693} \approx 3.17 \text{ bits}
$$

**(ii) Movement Time (2 marks):**

$$
MT = 45 + (110 \times 3.17) = 45 + 348.7 \approx 393.7 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Classification (3 marks):**

Since $MT = 393.7 \text{ ms} > 250 \text{ ms}$, the movement falls into the **Closed-Loop (Servo)** category as per the **Adams threshold**.

**Justification:** The 393.7 ms duration is more than sufficient for the central nervous system to receive visual feedback, process it (~180 ms RT), and send a corrective motor command (~50 ms NMD). Therefore, the worker can correct any mid-flight deviation, ensuring high accuracy when clicking the icon.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Classes of Motor Movements" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the 200 ms and 250 ms thresholds.** The 200 ms is the *upper* bound for ballistic (open-loop); the 250 ms is the *lower* bound for servo (closed-loop). Marks are deducted for stating them as identical.
> 2. **Using the Standard Fitts' formula when negative IDs are possible.** Always use the **Shannon form** $ID = \log_2(A/W + 1)$ unless the problem explicitly demands the standard form and guarantees $A \geq W/2$.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** KTU expects *bits* for ID, *ms* for MT, and *mm* (or consistent SI units) for $A$ and $W$.
> 4. **Mixing up serial and continuous movements.** *Serial = chain of discrete units.* *Continuous = smoothly co-varied trajectory without discrete subunits.* Many students confuse these.
> 5. **Omitting the justification for open/closed-loop classification.** You must explicitly compare your computed $MT$ to the 200/250 ms threshold — do not leave it implied.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classes of Motor Movements**

- **Three Structural Classes:** Discrete, Serial, Continuous (Welford / Schmidt).
- **Two Control Classes:** Open-Loop (Ballistic) and Closed-Loop (Servo) (Adams).
- **Discrete Movement:** Defined start + end → Single tri-phasic EMG burst.
- **Serial Movement:** Sequence of discrete sub-actions → Order matters.
- **Continuous Movement:** No defined start/end → Co-varied speed and position.
- **Open-Loop Threshold:** $MT < 200 \text{ ms}$ → No feedback during execution.
- **Closed-Loop Threshold:** $MT > 250 \text{ ms}$ → Feedback-driven correction.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(A/W + 1)$ (Shannon form, KTU-preferred).
- **Index of Difficulty:** Measured in **bits** — higher bits → harder task → longer MT.
- **Hick-Hyman Law:** Choice reaction time grows with $\log_2(N)$ alternatives.
- **Neuromuscular Delay:** ~30–50 ms — the reason for the 200/250 ms threshold.
- **Industrial Application:** Larger buttons, shorter distances, and detent mechanisms reduce ID → faster, less fatiguing operations.
- **Continuous Tracking** = Pursuit or Compensatory control — common in radar, driving, and process-control rooms.
- **Serial Tasks** = Total time is the *sum* of sub-task times (no overlap benefit unless automatized).
- **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw a block diagram for closed-loop control — it's worth full marks on the 7-mark sub-question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed of Movements - Reaction Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speed of Movements - Reaction Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Speed of Movement — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed of Movement** is defined as the rate at which a human operator can execute a discrete, serial, or continuous motor action, measured in terms of **Movement Time (MT)** or its reciprocal **Movement Rate (MR)**. In ergonomics, it is fundamentally studied through the **Speed–Accuracy Trade-off**, formalized by Fitts' Law.

In human factors engineering, the "speed of movement" is not merely about how fast a limb can travel. It encompasses the *information-processing load*, *motor-control planning*, and *neuromuscular execution latency* associated with performing a task. The concept is intrinsically linked to **reaction time**, because every voluntary movement is preceded by an information-processing sequence that decides *when* and *how* to move.

### 1.2 Reaction Time — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reaction Time (RT)** is the temporal interval between the onset of a *stimulus* and the initiation of the *overt muscular response* to that stimulus. Mathematically:
>
> $$\text{Reaction Time (RT)} = t_{\text{response}} - t_{\text{stimulus}}$$
>
> The units are typically expressed in **milliseconds (ms)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Traffic Light Analogy" 🚦**
>
> Imagine you are driving and waiting at a red traffic light. The moment the light turns green:
> - Your **eyes** register the change (stimulus detection).
> - Your **brain** decides "I can now go" (decision).
> - Your **leg** lifts from the brake and presses the accelerator (motor response).
>
> The tiny gap between the light turning green and your foot actually moving the pedal is **reaction time**. Now imagine doing this when the light could turn green, yellow, or red — you'd hesitate longer because your brain must *choose* the correct response. This delay is **choice reaction time**. The faster and more accurate you need to move (smaller steering wheel turns, finer controls), the slower you move — this is the **speed–accuracy trade-off** formalized by **Fitts' Law**.

### 1.4 Standard Physiological Constants (Baseline Values)

| Sense Modality | Typical Simple Reaction Time | Reference |
|----------------|------------------------------|-----------|
| Visual | **~200–250 ms** | Woodworth (1938) |
| Auditory | **~140–170 ms** | Welford (1968) |
| Tactile (touch) | **~130–155 ms** | Sanders & McCormick |
| Olfactory (smell) | **~300–500 ms** | Empirical average |

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Human Factors Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Significance: This topic directly informs the design of:
> - Emergency-stop systems and braking distances
> - Cockpit / control-room alarm response layouts
> - CNC machine interface response times
> - Workplace safety protocols where reaction latency determines accident probability

A human operator's **reaction time** is often the **dominant bottleneck** in any closed-loop man–machine system. Designers must allocate time budgets that exceed the slowest plausible RT under fatigue, stress, or environmental noise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stages of Information Processing (Pre-Movement Sequence)

Before any voluntary movement occurs, the human nervous system executes a four-stage pipeline:

1. **Stimulus Onset** — A physical event (light, sound, touch) activates a sensory receptor.
2. **Sensory Encoding** — The receptor transduces energy into neural signals; the brain registers the stimulus type and intensity.
3. **Decision/Response Selection** — The brain selects the appropriate response from a set of possibilities. This stage obeys **Hick's Law**.
4. **Motor Execution** — Neural commands travel via the corticospinal tract to the muscles, initiating movement. This stage obeys **Fitts' Law**.

### 2.2 Classification of Reaction Time (Welford's Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welford (1968)** classified reaction time into four hierarchical levels based on cognitive complexity:

| Level | Type | Description | Typical Duration |
|-------|------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Simple Reaction Time (SRT)** | One stimulus → One fixed response | ~200 ms |
| 2 | **Recognition Reaction Time** | Multiple stimuli → Response only to a designated target | ~250 ms |
| 3 | **Discrimination Reaction Time** | Multiple stimuli → Response to *any* relevant signal | ~300 ms |
| 4 | **Choice Reaction Time (CRT)** | Multiple stimuli → Different responses for each | ~350–500 ms |

### 2.3 Classification of Movement Speed

| Movement Type | Description | Example |
|---------------|-------------|---------|
| **Discrete** | Single, distinct act with definite start/end | Pressing a button |
| **Serial** | Sequence of discrete acts linked in order | Typing a word |
| **Continuous** | Smooth, uninterrupted action requiring ongoing control | Steering a car |
| **Cyclic** | Repetitive continuous movement | Pedalling a cycle |
| **Static (Postural)** | Holding a posture against load | Gripping a tool |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Exam (ESE)**

| # | Law / Equation | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fitts' Law (MT form)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | MT = Movement Time (s); D = Distance to target (m); W = Target width (m); a, b = empirical constants | Control knob design, button sizing |
| 2 | **Fitts' Law (Rate form)** | $IP = \dfrac{ID}{MT} = \dfrac{1}{b}$ | IP = Index of Performance (bits/s); ID = Index of Difficulty (bits) | Compare device usability |
| 3 | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | D in m; W in m; ID in bits | Quantify task complexity |
| 4 | **Hick's Law** | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$ | RT = Reaction Time (ms); N = Number of equally probable choices; a, b = constants | Number of menu items, alarm count |
| 5 | **Hyman Enhancement** | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ | Variant ignoring empty choice | Simpler approximation |
| 6 | **Simple RT constant** | $RT_{\text{simple}} \approx 200\,\text{ms}$ (visual) | Fixed reference value | Baseline safety design |
| 7 | **Speed–Accuracy Function** | $\text{Accuracy} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\text{Speed}}}$ | Empirical relationship | Safety-critical operations |
| 8 | **Crossman's MT relation** | $MT = a + b \cdot K$ where $K = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | Same as Fitts'; b ≈ 0.1 s/bit (typical) | Predictive ergonomics |

### 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hick's Law has a logarithm?**
>
> Information theory (Shannon, 1948) shows that the information content of choosing among N equally probable alternatives is $\log_2(N)$ bits. Human decision time scales with this information content because each bit requires a fixed mental processing "quantum" (~60–150 ms). Thus, doubling the choices adds only ONE bit of decision time — not a doubling of reaction time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Fitts' Law has a logarithm?**
>
> The amplitude (D) and tolerance (W) define the *motor information* required to land on a target. $\log_2(2D/W)$ quantifies this as bits of spatial information. Movement duration is the time to transmit these bits through the motor channel, whose bandwidth is approximately **10–12 bits/second** for skilled operators.

### 2.6 Factors Affecting Reaction Time and Movement Speed

**A. Stimulus-Related Factors:**
- Intensity (brighter stimulus → faster RT, up to a saturation point)
- Modality (auditory < visual < tactile < olfactory in speed)
- Spatial location (foreperiod effect)
- Signal-to-noise ratio

**B. Operator-Related Factors:**
- Age (RT peaks at ~20–30 years; declines thereafter)
- Fatigue and sleep deprivation
- Practice and training (skill acquisition)
- Attention and arousal (Yerkes–Dodson inverted-U)
- Handedness (dominant hand ~5–10% faster)

**C. Environmental Factors:**
- Ambient temperature (extremes slow RT)
- Vibration and noise
- Lighting conditions
- Drug/alcohol effects

**D. Task-Related Factors:**
- Number of stimulus-response alternatives (Hick's)
- Target size and distance (Fitts')
- Compatibility (stimulus-response mapping)
- Foreperiod variability

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hick's Law (Choice Reaction Time)

We begin from the **information-theoretic premise** that selecting among $N$ equally probable alternatives carries an information load of $H$ bits:

$$
H = \log_2(N)
$$

The empirical postulate (Hick, 1952; Hyman, 1953) is that the **average reaction time is proportional to the information transmitted**:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot H
$$

Substituting $H = \log_2(N)$:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

**Step 1 — Enhancement by Hyman:** Hyman observed that when N = 0 (no stimulus), RT is undefined. To preserve mathematical symmetry, the modified form uses $(N+1)$:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)
$$

**Step 2 — Solvability for constants:** For a known RT at $N_1$ and $N_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_1 &= a + b \cdot \log_2(N_1+1) \\
RT_2 &= a + b \cdot \log_2(N_2+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the two equations eliminates $a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_2 - RT_1 &= b \cdot \left[\log_2(N_2+1) - \log_2(N_1+1)\right] \\
b &= \dfrac{RT_2 - RT_1}{\log_2(N_2+1) - \log_2(N_1+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
a = RT_1 - b \cdot \log_2(N_1+1)
$$

**Worked Example:**
Suppose $N_1 = 1$ gives $RT_1 = 250$ ms and $N_2 = 7$ gives $RT_2 = 400$ ms.

**Step 3 — Compute $b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \dfrac{400 - 250}{\log_2(8) - \log_2(2)} \\
b &= \dfrac{150}{3 - 1} \\
b &= \dfrac{150}{2} \\
b &= 75 \text{ ms/bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= 250 - 75 \cdot \log_2(2) \\
a &= 250 - 75 \cdot 1 \\
a &= 175 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with $N = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT &= 175 + 75 \cdot \log_2(4) \\
RT &= 175 + 75 \cdot 2 \\
RT &= 175 + 150 \\
RT &= 325 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Fitts' Law (Movement Time)

**Starting principle:** The motor system transmits spatial information at a finite rate (bandwidth-limited channel).

**Step 1 — Define Index of Difficulty (ID):**

The *motor information* required to hit a target of width $W$ located at distance $D$ is:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right) \quad \text{(bits)}
$$

> The factor of 2 in the numerator accounts for the approach uncertainty (from either direction toward a one-dimensional target).

**Step 2 — Define Index of Performance (IP):**

$$
IP = \dfrac{ID}{MT} \quad \text{(bits/s)}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume linear proportionality:**

Empirically, MT is a linear function of ID:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

Substituting:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for constants via linear regression on $(ID, MT)$ data pairs.**

Given $(D_1, W_1) \Rightarrow (ID_1, MT_1)$ and $(D_2, W_2) \Rightarrow (ID_2, MT_2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_1 &= a + b \cdot ID_1 \\
MT_2 &= a + b \cdot ID_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \dfrac{MT_2 - MT_1}{ID_2 - ID_1} \\
a &= MT_1 - b \cdot ID_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example (Crossman-style):**
An operator moves a stylus to a target. Two trials:
- Trial 1: $D = 0.20$ m, $W = 0.02$ m, $MT = 0.50$ s.
- Trial 2: $D = 0.40$ m, $W = 0.04$ m, $MT = 0.60$ s.

**Step 5 — Compute ID for Trial 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_1 &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 0.20}{0.02}\right) \\
ID_1 &= \log_2(20) \\
ID_1 &\approx 4.322 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute ID for Trial 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_2 &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 0.40}{0.04}\right) \\
ID_2 &= \log_2(20) \\
ID_2 &\approx 4.322 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Identical ID by coincidence → we need a different data set. Let us adjust Trial 2: $W = 0.01$ m.

**Step 7 — Recompute Trial 2 with $W = 0.01$ m, $MT = 0.80$ s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_2 &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 0.40}{0.01}\right) \\
ID_2 &= \log_2(80) \\
ID_2 &\approx 6.322 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \dfrac{0.80 - 0.50}{6.322 - 4.322} \\
b &= \dfrac{0.30}{2.000} \\
b &= 0.150 \text{ s/bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= 0.50 - 0.150 \times 4.322 \\
a &= 0.50 - 0.6483 \\
a &\approx -0.148 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Fitts' Law for this operator:**

$$
\boxed{MT = -0.148 + 0.150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hick vs. Fitts

| Parameter | Hick's Law | Fitts' Law |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Domain | Cognitive (decision) | Motor (execution) |
| Independent variable | $N$ (number of choices) | $D$ (distance), $W$ (target width) |
| Information measured | Bits of *choice* information | Bits of *spatial* information |
| Typical slope $b$ | 60–150 ms/bit | 0.05–0.30 s/bit |
| Typical intercept $a$ | 100–250 ms | -0.20 to +0.20 s |
| Real-world analogy | Selecting a menu item | Clicking an icon precisely |
| Critical for | Alarm panels, menu design | Touchscreens, control knobs |

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Reaction Time. Distinguish between Simple Reaction Time (SRT) and Choice Reaction Time (CRT).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reaction Time (RT)** is the time interval between the onset of a stimulus and the initiation of an overt motor response to that stimulus, expressed in milliseconds (ms) (1 mark).
>
> **Simple Reaction Time (SRT)** occurs when there is only *one* stimulus and *one* corresponding response. The subject knows in advance what response to make (e.g., pressing a button when a light appears). SRT is approximately **200 ms** for a visual stimulus (1 mark).
>
> **Choice Reaction Time (CRT)** occurs when there are *multiple* possible stimuli, each requiring a *different* response. The subject must identify the stimulus first, then select the correct response (e.g., pressing one of three buttons depending on which of three lights appears). CRT is longer — typically **300–500 ms** — because of the additional decision-making stage described by **Hick's Law** (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Fitts' Law. What does the Index of Performance (IP) represent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Fitts' Law** states that the time required to move a pointer (or limb) to a target is a linear function of the *Index of Difficulty (ID)* of the movement:
>
> $$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$$
>
> where $D$ is the distance to the target and $W$ is the target width (1 mark).
>
> The **Index of Performance (IP)** is defined as the ratio of ID to MT and represents the *information throughput* of the motor system, expressed in **bits per second**:
>
> $$IP = \dfrac{ID}{MT} = \dfrac{1}{b}$$
>
> It indicates how efficiently the human can transmit spatial-motor information (1 mark).
>
> A higher IP means a more efficient motor channel — useful for benchmarking different input devices (mouse, touchscreen, joystick) (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) Explain the different stages of human information processing that precede a motor response. How does Hick's Law model the decision stage? Solve a problem in which a CRT experiment yields RT = 220 ms for N = 1 and RT = 420 ms for N = 7. Determine the constants $a$ and $b$ in Hick's equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stages of Information Processing (3 marks):**

The human response to a stimulus passes through four sequential stages:

1. **Stimulus Onset** — A physical event activates sensory receptors (eyes, ears, skin).
2. **Sensory Encoding** — The receptor converts physical energy into neural impulses; the signal travels via afferent nerves to the brain (≈ 30–50 ms latency).
3. **Perceptual Identification & Decision** — The brain compares the stimulus against internal templates and selects the appropriate response. Duration obeys **Hick's Law**.
4. **Motor Execution** — The chosen response is executed via the corticospinal tract and muscles. Duration obeys **Fitts' Law**.

**Hick's Law (2 marks):**

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)
$$

The logarithmic form arises because each additional choice contributes only **one bit** of information ($\log_2$ basis), and each bit requires a fixed mental-processing quantum.

**Problem Solution (2 marks):**

Given: $N_1 = 1,\; RT_1 = 220$ ms and $N_2 = 7,\; RT_2 = 420$ ms.

**Step 1 — Set up two equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
220 &= a + b \cdot \log_2(1+1) = a + b \cdot \log_2(2) = a + b \cdot 1 \\
420 &= a + b \cdot \log_2(7+1) = a + b \cdot \log_2(8) = a + b \cdot 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract to eliminate $a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
420 - 220 &= b \cdot (3 - 1) \\
200 &= 2b \\
b &= 100 \text{ ms/bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to find $a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
220 &= a + 100 \cdot 1 \\
a &= 220 - 100 = 120 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Hick's equation:**

$$
\boxed{RT = 120 + 100 \cdot \log_2(N+1) \text{ ms}}
$$

**(b) Define Speed-Accuracy Trade-off. With a neat sketch, derive Fitts' Law. A factory worker must move a tool between two posts: $D = 0.5$ m apart with target width $W = 0.025$ m. If $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.12$ s/bit, calculate the movement time. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Speed–Accuracy Trade-off (2 marks):**

The **Speed–Accuracy Trade-off** states that as the speed of a movement increases, the accuracy decreases, and vice versa. It is mathematically embodied in Fitts' Law, which links movement duration to the spatial precision demanded.

**Derivation of Fitts' Law (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Index of Difficulty (ID).** The information required to hit a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ is:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right) \text{ bits}
$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the bidirectional approach to a one-dimensional target.

**Step 2 — Assume linear proportionality** between MT and ID (verified empirically):

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and write Fitts' Law:**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define Index of Performance:**

$$
IP = \dfrac{ID}{MT} = \dfrac{1}{b} \quad \text{(bits/s)}
$$

A sketch would show MT (y-axis) plotted against ID (x-axis) as a straight line with slope $b$ and intercept $a$.

**Numerical Problem (2 marks):**

Given: $D = 0.5$ m, $W = 0.025$ m, $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.12$ s/bit.

**Step 1 — Compute ID:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 0.5}{0.025}\right) \\
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{1.0}{0.025}\right) \\
ID &= \log_2(40) \\
ID &\approx 5.322 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= 0.05 + 0.12 \times 5.322 \\
MT &= 0.05 + 0.6386 \\
MT &\approx 0.689 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{MT \approx 0.69 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a) Explain Welford's classification of reaction time. List at least four factors affecting reaction time and explain how each factor influences it. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Welford's Classification (4 marks):**

Welford (1968) categorized reaction time into four hierarchical levels of increasing complexity:

1. **Simple Reaction Time (SRT):** A single stimulus requires a single fixed response. The subject has full advance knowledge of what is required. Typical duration ≈ **200 ms** (visual).

2. **Recognition Reaction Time:** Multiple stimuli are presented, but the subject responds *only* to a pre-designated "target" stimulus. This adds a *recognition* stage. Typical duration ≈ **250 ms**.

3. **Discrimination Reaction Time:** Multiple stimuli are presented, and the subject must respond to *any* stimulus that meets a criterion (e.g., any red light, regardless of position). Duration ≈ **300 ms**.

4. **Choice Reaction Time (CRT):** Each of multiple stimuli is mapped to a *different* response. The subject must identify the stimulus *and* select the corresponding response. Duration ≈ **350–500 ms**, depending on number of choices (Hick's Law applies).

**Factors Affecting Reaction Time (3 marks — list any four):**

| Factor | Influence |
|--------|-----------|
| **Stimulus Intensity** | Brighter/louder stimuli reduce RT up to a saturation point (Hick's Law on signal detection). |
| **Foreperiod (warning interval)** | A variable or very short foreperiod slows RT (psychological refractoriness). |
| **Practice / Training** | Extensive practice can reduce RT by **20–40%** through motor-program automation. |
| **Fatigue & Sleep Deprivation** | Increase RT by **15–50%** and degrade decision accuracy. |
| **Age** | RT peaks around age 20–30, then gradually declines. |
| **Stimulus Modality** | Auditory < Tactile < Visual < Olfactory in increasing RT. |
| **Stimulus–Response Compatibility** | Mismatched S–R mappings (e.g., left button for right light) can double RT. |

---

**(b) A driver must respond to one of 5 possible brake-light patterns. Using Hick's Law with $a = 180$ ms and $b = 90$ ms/bit, calculate the choice reaction time. If the vehicle is travelling at 60 km/h, how far does it travel during this reaction time? Comment on the safety implication. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute CRT using Hick's Law (2 marks):**

Given: $N = 5$, $a = 180$ ms, $b = 90$ ms/bit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT &= a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1) \\
RT &= 180 + 90 \cdot \log_2(5+1) \\
RT &= 180 + 90 \cdot \log_2(6) \\
RT &= 180 + 90 \cdot 2.585 \\
RT &= 180 + 232.65 \\
RT &\approx 412.65 \text{ ms} \approx 0.413 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert speed to m/s (1 mark):**

$$
v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute stopping distance due to RT alone (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{RT} &= v \times RT \\
d_{RT} &= 16.67 \times 0.413 \\
d_{RT} &\approx 6.88 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Safety Implication (2 marks):**

> During the **0.413 s** reaction window, the vehicle travels **~6.88 m** before any braking is even initiated. Adding typical braking distance (≈ 15–20 m on dry asphalt), the *total stopping distance* is **~22–27 m**. This emphasizes why KTU-emphasized ergonomic design recommends:
> - Reducing the *number of choices* on instrument panels (Hick's Law)
> - Increasing *stimulus intensity* (brighter brake lights)
> - Improving *stimulus–response compatibility* (consistent color coding)
> - Allowing ≥ **1.5 seconds** of *preview time* for the driver

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Hick's Law with Fitts' Law:** Hick's Law applies to the *decision* stage (number of choices); Fitts' Law applies to the *motor* stage (distance and target size). Writing the wrong law in the wrong context → **−2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $(N+1)$ Hyman form:** Some texts use $\log_2(N)$; others use $\log_2(N+1)$. KTU examiners accept both **only if explicitly stated**. Mixing the two within one problem → **−1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Unit errors in Fitts' Law calculations:** Forgetting to convert $D$ and $W$ to the *same units* before computing $ID$ → **−1 to −2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps:** Examiners award marks for each derivation step, not just the final answer. Skipping "$\log_2(8) = 3$" or "$2D/W$ substitution" loses partial credit.
>
> 5. **Missing units in final answer:** Always write ms for RT, s for MT, bits for ID, and bits/s for IP.
>
> 6. **Confusing $a$ and $b$:** In Fitts' Law, $b$ (slope) has units of seconds/bit; $a$ (intercept) has units of seconds. Stating units wrong → **−1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Reaction Time (RT)** = time from stimulus onset to response initiation; typically **200 ms** for visual SRT.
- ✅ **Welford's 4 Levels:** SRT → Recognition → Discrimination → Choice RT.
- ✅ **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$; **decision** stage.
- ✅ **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2D/W)$; **motor** stage.
- ✅ **Index of Difficulty (ID)** = $\log_2(2D/W)$ — measured in **bits**.
- ✅ **Index of Performance (IP)** = $ID/MT$ — measured in **bits/second** (typical: 4–12 bits/s).
- ✅ **Speed–Accuracy Trade-off:** Faster movement = larger ID → more error.
- ✅ **Simple RT order:** Auditory (~150 ms) < Visual (~200 ms) < Olfactory (~400 ms).
- ✅ **Slope of Hick's Law** typically **60–150 ms/bit**.
- ✅ **Slope of Fitts' Law** typically **0.05–0.30 s/bit**.
- ✅ **Practice** can reduce RT/MT by **20–40%**.
- ✅ **Engineering design rule:** Keep the **number of choices low** and the **target size large** to minimize operator response time.
- ✅ **Total task time** = RT (Hick) + MT (Fitts) + feedback delay.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Movement Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Movement Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Movement Time (MT)** is the *standardized, predetermined time required by a qualified human operator* to execute a **single, fundamental, elemental body motion** under specified working conditions, expressed in recognized time units (TMU, seconds, or minutes).

In the framework of **Work Measurement** and **Methods Engineering**, movement time is NOT a measured (stopwatch) value; it is a **predetermined, engineered value** derived from analytical motion study systems. It forms the atomic building block of **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** such as **MTM (Methods-Time Measurement)**, **MOST (Maynard Operation Sequence Technique)**, and **Basic Motion Timestudy (BMT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standardized):** *"Movement time is the time consumed in performing a basic human motion, determined through predetermined time systems, used to establish standard times for industrial operations."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are at home and want to **pick up a cup of tea from the table** ☕.

- Your brain first *reaches* toward the cup (Reach).
- Your fingers *grasp* the handle (Grasp).
- You *move* the cup toward your mouth (Move).
- You *position* it for drinking (Position).
- You *release* it back on the table (Release).

Each of these sub-actions is a **basic movement**. Now, suppose an industrial engineer wants to *predict* — without a stopwatch — exactly how long this entire sequence should take for a trained worker. That prediction, broken down motion-by-motion, is the **Movement Time**.

> **Analogy:** Think of Movement Time like the "atomic clock" of human motion. Just as chemists know the exact mass of a hydrogen atom (1.008 u), ergonomists know the *standard time* for a 6-inch reach (≈ 5.5 TMU). You just look up the value — no measurement needed.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

| Metric | Symbol | Value | Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Measurement Unit** | **TMU** | **1 TMU = 0.00001 hour = 0.036 seconds = 1/100 minute** | Standard unit in MTM-1 system |
| Second | s | 1 s = 27.8 TMU | SI practical unit |
| Minute | min | 1 min = 1666.67 TMU | Higher-level planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TMU and not seconds directly?** TMU was chosen because most basic motion times fall in the 1–25 range when expressed in TMU, avoiding decimals and making manual calculation easier. The conversion factor **1 TMU = 0.036 s** must be memorized for KTU problems.

### 1.4 Fundamental Premises Underlying Movement Time

1. **Human motion is decomposable** into a finite set of *basic* or *elemental* motions.
2. A **qualified, trained operator** working at a **normal pace** under **standard conditions** will execute these motions in **deterministic, predictable times**.
3. Movement times are **independent of operator skill** once trained (skill affects *method*, not *basic time*).
4. Summing the times of all basic motions in an operation gives the **Normal Time (NT)** of that operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS) — The Taxonomy

A **PMTS** is a work measurement technique in which times for basic human motions are established in advance (predetermined) from films, video analysis, or laboratory studies, then applied to estimate standard times for new work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Major PMTS Systems (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **MTM-1 (Methods-Time Measurement)** — 1950s, most widely used, 22 basic motions in TMU.
> 2. **MTM-2** — Simplified version of MTM-1 for high-frequency repetitive work.
> 3. **MOST (Maynard Operation Sequence Technique)** — 1960s, uses 3 basic sequences: **A** (Activity), **B** (Body), **S** (Subject).
> 4. **Basic Motion Timestudy (BMT)** — Work Factor system predecessor.
> 5. **Work Factor System** — Quick, simplified, uses tables keyed to weight & distance.
> 6. **Dimensional Motion Times (DMT)** — More recent, computer-friendly.

### 2.2 MTM-1: The 22 Basic Motions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTM-1 Basic Motions (Memorize for KTU):**
> 
> **Hand/Arm Motions (Arm Motions):**
> 1. **Reach (R)** — Move hand empty to an object/location.
> 2. **Move (M)** — Move hand with an object to a destination.
> 3. **Turn (T)** — Rotate hand/wrist/forearm about its long axis.
> 4. **Crank (C)** — Crank-type circular motion of hand.
> 
> **Finger Motions (Hand Motions):**
> 5. **Grasp (G)** — Closing fingers around an object.
> 6. **Position (P)** — Aligning, orienting, engaging an object.
> 7. **Release (RL)** — Letting go of an object.
> 8. **Disengage (D)** — Breaking contact between two objects.
> 9. **Apply Pressure (AP)** — Pressing an object against a surface.
> 10. **Regrasp (R1)** — Successive grasps.
> 
> **Combined Motions:**
> 11. **Move with Press (MP)** — Move with simultaneous pressure.
> 
> **Eye/Foot/Leg Motions:**
> 12. **Eye Travel (ET)** — Eye movement between points.
> 13. **Eye Focus (EF)** — Eye fixation on a point.
> 14. **Foot Motion (FM)** — Foot movement (pedal).
> 15. **Leg Motion (LM)** — Leg movement.
> 16. **Bend, Stoop, Kneel, etc.** — Posture change motions.
> 17. **Arise** — Return to standing.
> 18. **Sit, Stand, Walk, Turn Body** — Body movements.
> 
> **Cranial:**
> 19. **Head/Aye motion** — Not standard in MTM-1 typically.
> 
> **Concurrent (Tandem) Motions (Made-for-Up):**
> 20–22. Various **Tandem (T)** or **Simultaneous (S)** motion allowances.

### 2.3 Reach (R) — Distance-Based Time Tables

The Reach motion time depends on the **distance of reach** and the **case type**:

| Case | Description | TMU Formula / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **R-A** | Reach to object in **fixed location** (jig, fixture) | $TMU = 2.0$ (constant up to 2 inches) |
| **R-B** | Reach to object in **approximate/indefinite location** | $TMU = 2.0 + 1.5 \times (D-2)$ for $D > 2$ inches |
| **R-C** | Reach to object in **crowded/jumbled location** (to grasp small object) | $TMU = 2.0 + 1.5 \times D$ (longer) |

> For **R-B**, the standard distance-based table is:

| Distance D (inches) | R-B (TMU) | R-C (TMU) |
|---|---|---|
| Up to 2 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| 2 – 3 | 3.5 | 5.0 |
| 3 – 4 | 4.5 | 6.5 |
| 4 – 6 | 5.5 | 8.5 |
| 6 – 8 | 7.5 | 10.5 |
| 8 – 12 | 10.5 | 14.0 |
| 12 – 16 | 13.5 | 17.0 |
| 16 – 20 | 16.5 | 20.5 |
| 20 – 24 | 19.5 | 24.0 |
| 24 – 28 | 22.5 | 27.5 |
| 28 – 32 | 25.5 | 31.0 |

### 2.4 Move (M) — Distance + Weight + Case-Based

The Move motion time depends on **distance, weight, and case**:

| Case | Description |
|---|---|
| **M-A** | Move object to **other hand or fixed stop** (mechanical guidance) |
| **M-B** | Move object to **approximate/indefinite location** (most common) |
| **M-C** | Move object to **precise/accurate location** (with positioning) |

**Base Move (M-B) Times for distance only (weight $\leq 2$ lb):**

| Distance D (inches) | M-B (TMU) |
|---|---|
| Up to 2 | 2.0 |
| 2 – 3 | 3.5 |
| 3 – 4 | 4.5 |
| 4 – 6 | 5.5 |
| 6 – 8 | 7.5 |
| 8 – 12 | 10.5 |
| 12 – 16 | 13.5 |
| 16 – 20 | 16.5 |
| 20 – 24 | 19.5 |

> For $D > 24$ inches, a **constant velocity model** is used:
> $$MT_{extra} = 1.0 \times (D - 24) \text{ TMU per inch}$$

### 2.5 Weight Allowance (Move with W)

When moving an object > 2 lb, a **weight allowance** is added to the base Move time:

$$MT_{total} = MT_{base} + MT_{weight}$$

where the weight allowance is tabulated as:

| Weight Range (lb) | Additional TMU (per move) |
|---|---|
| 2 – 6 | 1.0 |
| 6 – 10 | 1.5 |
| 10 – 16 | 2.0 |
| 16 – 22 | 2.5 |
| 22 – 30 | 3.0 |
| 30 – 38 | 3.5 |
| 38 – 46 | 4.0 |
| 46 – 54 | 4.5 |
| 54 – 62 | 5.0 |

> For weights > 62 lb: $MT_{weight} = 5.0 + 0.5 \times (W - 62)/8$ (in steps).

### 2.6 Other Basic Motions — High-Yield Summary

| Motion | TMU | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Grasp (G-1A)** Simple grasp | 2.0 | Easiest, smallest object |
| **Grasp (G-1B)** Regrasp/Transfer | 2.0 | |
| **Grasp (G-1C)** Object $\leq 1$ inch diameter | 3.5 | |
| **Grasp (G-1D)** Object 1/4 to 1/2 inch | 7.0 | |
| **Grasp (G-1E)** Tiny object (pin, wire) | 16.0 | |
| **Grasp (G-2)** Wrap-around grasp | 5.0 | |
| **Grasp (G-3)** Cylindrical grasp | 5.0 | |
| **Position (P-1)** Symmetrical, no press | 5.0 | |
| **Position (P-2)** Non-symmetrical, no press | 7.0 | |
| **Position (P-3)** Difficult/notable | 11.0 | |
| **Position (P-4)** Semi-symmetrical | 14.0 | |
| **Position (P-5)** With light pressure | 21.0 | |
| **Release (RL-1)** Normal release | 2.0 | |
| **Release (RL-2)** Contact release | 2.0 | |
| **Disengage (D-1)** Loosening object | 4.0 | |
| **Disengage (D-2)** Forceful disengage | 7.0 | |
| **Apply Pressure (AP-1)** Light | 10.5 | |
| **Apply Pressure (AP-2)** Heavy | 16.0 | |
| **Turn (T)** | $1.5 \times \text{weight factor} \times \text{angle}$ | Per turn |
| **Crank (C)** | $0.85 \times \text{turns}$ | Per revolution segment |
| **Eye Travel (ET)** | 7.3 TMU (max 30°, min 20°) | Per fixation |
| **Eye Focus (EF)** | 7.3 TMU | Per focus |
| **Foot Motion (FM)** | 8.5 TMU | Simple foot motion |
| **Leg Motion (LM)** | 1.5 TMU/inch + constant | Per leg motion |
| **Bend** | 29.0 TMU | 30°–60° from vertical |
| **Stoop** | 36.0 TMU | 60°–90° from vertical |
| **Kneel (one knee)** | 31.0 TMU | |
| **Kneel (both knees)** | 49.0 TMU | |
| **Sit** | 34.0 TMU | |
| **Arise (from bent)** | 28.0 TMU | |
| **Arise (from stoop)** | 34.0 TMU | |
| **Walk (per step)** | 5.3 TMU (per pace, 1 pace = 30 inches) | |
| **Walk (obstructed)** | 6.0 TMU/pace | |

### 2.7 Fitts' Law — Distance-Difficulty Relationship

For **targeted/repetitive aiming movements**, the movement time is related to the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**:

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID$$

$$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$$

where:
- $D$ = **distance** to target (movement amplitude)
- $W$ = **width** of target (tolerance)
- $a, b$ = empirically derived constants ($a \approx 50$–$200$ ms, $b \approx 100$–$150$ ms/bit for hand movements)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fitts' Law in Industry:** Used in HCI, control panel design (button size vs. distance), and inspection tasks. *Wider targets and shorter distances = faster movements.*

### 2.8 MOST System — High-Yield Quick Reference

MOST uses **3 sequences × 5 parameters** (very efficient):

| Sequence | Sub-codes | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **A** (Activity) | A0, A1, A2, A3, A4, A5, A6 | Action distance + body motion (e.g., A0 = 0 steps, A1 = 1 step …) |
| **B** (Body) | B0, B1, B2, B3, B4, B5 | Lateral body motion (side, step) |
| **S** (Subject) | G, A, P, R, I, L, X | Object motion (Get, move Away, Put, Return, Inquire, Log) |

A typical MOST index: $A_1 B_0 G_1 A_1 P_1$ = 10 + 1 + 10 + 10 + 10 = 41 TMU (rough example).

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| TMU to seconds | $t_s = 0.036 \times TMU$ | s | Conversion in problems |
| TMU to minutes | $t_{min} = TMU / 100$ | min | Conversion |
| TMU to hours | $t_h = TMU / 100{,}000$ | hr | Standard time calc |
| Reach (R-B) | $TMU = f(D)$ | TMU | Look up table |
| Move (M-B) | $TMU = f(D) + f(W)$ | TMU | Distance + weight |
| Position | $TMU = f(\text{cases P-1 to P-5})$ | TMU | Per assembly |
| Fitts' Law | $MT = a + b \log_2(2D/W)$ | ms or s | HCI / control design |
| Walk time | $TMU = 5.3 \times N_{steps}$ | TMU | Per pace (30 in) |
| Standard time | $ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$ | min | Final output |
| Normal time | $NT = \sum TMU_i$ | min | Sum of all motions |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Conversion:** Always convert TMU → hours ($/100{,}000$) before multiplying by number of cycles and adding allowances.
