## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Movement Time to Standard Time

> **Given:** An operator performs a sequence of motions in one cycle of an operation.
> **To find:** The **Standard Time (ST)** of the operation.

#### Step 1: Decompose the operation into basic motions

For example, operation: *"Pick up a 5 lb bolt from a bin and place it in a fixture."*

Basic motions:
1. **Reach to bolt in bin (R-C, 12 inches):** Look up table → **14.0 TMU**
2. **Grasp bolt (G-1B, cylindrical 5 lb):** → **5.0 TMU**
3. **Move bolt to fixture (M-B, 8 inches, 5 lb):** → **7.5 + 1.0 = 8.5 TMU**
4. **Position bolt in fixture (P-2):** → **7.0 TMU**
5. **Release bolt (RL-1):** → **2.0 TMU**
6. **Reach back to bin (R-A, 8 inches):** → **7.5 TMU**

#### Step 2: Sum the TMU values to get Normal Cycle Time (in TMU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_{TMU} &= 14.0 + 5.0 + 8.5 + 7.0 + 2.0 + 7.5 \\
&= 44.0 \text{ TMU}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** We add all the per-motion TMU values linearly because in MTM-1, motions are assumed to be **sequential**, not overlapping (unless *Tandem* notation is used).

#### Step 3: Convert TMU to minutes

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_{min} &= \frac{NT_{TMU}}{100} \\
&= \frac{44.0}{100} \\
&= 0.44 \text{ min per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute the time for the required number of cycles

For a daily production of $N = 400$ cycles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_{daily} &= 0.44 \times 400 \\
&= 176 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Add Personal, Fatigue, and Delay Allowances (AF)

Assume Allowances Factor $AF = 15\% = 0.15$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT_{daily} \times (1 + AF) \\
&= 176 \times (1 + 0.15) \\
&= 176 \times 1.15 \\
&= 202.4 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The Standard Time includes *allowances* for the operator's rest, personal time, and unavoidable delays (e.g., tool breakage, supervisor talk, fatigue recovery). The **Normal Time** is what the worker does *at normal pace*; **Standard Time** = Normal Time × (1 + Allowance Factor).

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Fitts' Law Application

> **Problem:** A control knob is 15 cm away from the operator's hand. The target width (tolerance) is 1.0 cm. If $a = 100$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit, find the expected movement time.

#### Step 1: Compute Index of Difficulty

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) \\
&= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \times 15}{1.0}\right) \\
&= \log_2(30) \\
&= \frac{\ln 30}{\ln 2} \\
&\approx 4.907 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Compute Movement Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
&= 100 + 150 \times 4.907 \\
&= 100 + 736.05 \\
&= 836.05 \text{ ms} \\
&\approx 0.836 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The operator will take approximately **0.84 seconds** to move to and acquire the 1-cm target from 15 cm away.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Weight-Corrected Move Time

> **Problem:** An operator moves a 25 lb object 18 inches to an approximate location. Compute the M-B move time with weight allowance.

#### Step 1: Base M-B Time for 18 inches (look up table)

$$
MT_{base} = 16.5 \text{ TMU}
$$

#### Step 2: Weight allowance for 25 lb (falls in 22–30 lb range)

$$
MT_{weight} = 3.0 \text{ TMU}
$$

#### Step 3: Total Move Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_{total} &= MT_{base} + MT_{weight} \\
&= 16.5 + 3.0 \\
&= 19.5 \text{ TMU}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right PMTS

| Criterion | MTM-1 | MOST | Work Factor | BMT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ease of use** | Moderate | Easy (fastest) | Easy | Moderate |
| **Accuracy** | Highest | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Training time** | 1–2 weeks | 2–3 days | 2–3 days | 1 week |
| **Best for** | High-precision / assembly | Quick estimates, planning | Mixed | Historical use |
| **Number of basic motions** | 22 | 3 sequences | 14 | 16 |
| **Industry adoption** | High | Very high (automotive) | Moderate | Low |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams, default to MTM-1 unless stated otherwise.**

### 3.5 Variables Affecting Movement Time

1. **Distance of movement** — Directly proportional (linear or log-linear).
2. **Object weight** — Adds weight allowance to Move time.
3. **Object shape & size** — Affects Grasp and Position time.
4. **Symmetry of placement** — Symmetric (P-1) vs. asymmetric (P-2/P-3).
5. **Tactile feedback / blindness** — Adds $+$ allowances.
6. **Posture** — Sitting/standing changes base times.
7. **Operator fatigue** — Long-cycle fatigue is compensated by allowances, not by changing basic time.
8. **Environmental conditions** — Heat, cold, vibration, lighting (Lumen/m²) can change pace.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Define Movement Time. What is a Time Measurement Unit (TMU) and what is its value in seconds?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Movement Time (1 mark):** Movement time is the standardized, predetermined time required by a qualified, trained operator working at normal pace to perform a single, basic, elemental human motion, as established in a Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS) such as MTM.

**TMU — Definition and value (2 marks):** A Time Measurement Unit (TMU) is the standard unit of time in the MTM system.

$$1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hour} = 0.036 \text{ seconds} = \frac{1}{100} \text{ minute}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion to remember:**
> $$1 \text{ second} = 27.8 \text{ TMU}, \quad 1 \text{ minute} = 1666.67 \text{ TMU}, \quad 1 \text{ hour} = 100{,}000 \text{ TMU}$$

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **List any six basic motions used in the MTM-1 system. State the TMU value for a simple grasp (G-1A) and a normal release (RL-1).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Six basic MTM-1 motions (1.5 marks):**
1. Reach (R)
2. Move (M)
3. Grasp (G)
4. Position (P)
5. Release (RL)
6. Disengage (D)

*(Other valid: Turn T, Apply Pressure AP, Eye Travel ET, Eye Focus EF, Crank C, Foot Motion FM, Leg Motion LM, Bend, Stoop, Sit, Arise, Walk)*

**TMU values (1.5 marks):**
- Simple Grasp **G-1A** = **2.0 TMU**
- Normal Release **RL-1** = **2.0 TMU**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Note:** Two alternative 14-mark questions are provided. Either is valid per KTU ESE pattern (typically: Answer ONE 14-mark OR two 7-mark sub-questions from a choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

> **(a)** Explain the MTM-1 system in detail. List the basic hand/arm motions and describe the factors that affect the **Move (M)** time. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An operator performs the following motions in one cycle of an operation: **R-C (10 in) = 12 TMU**, **G-1B = 5 TMU**, **M-B (6 in, 8 lb) = 7.5 + 1.5 = 9 TMU**, **P-2 = 7 TMU**, **RL-1 = 2 TMU**, **R-A (back 6 in) = 5.5 TMU**. If 500 cycles are produced per day and the allowance factor is 20%, determine the **Standard Time** in minutes per day. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MTM-1 System Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Methods-Time Measurement (MTM-1)** system, developed by **H. C. Zippin** and refined by **Maynard, Stegemerten, and Schwab** in the 1940s–50s, is a **Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS)** that establishes standard times for **basic human motions** in advance from laboratory analysis of films of trained operators.

**Key features:**
- **22 basic motions** are defined (hand/arm, finger, body, leg, eye).
- Times are expressed in **TMU (Time Measurement Units)**.
- **1 TMU = 0.00001 hour = 0.036 seconds**.
- The system assumes a **qualified, trained operator** at **normal pace**.
- Times are **predetermined** — no stopwatch needed for new operations.

**Basic Hand/Arm Motions (2 marks):**
1. **Reach (R)** — Move empty hand toward an object.
2. **Move (M)** — Move hand *carrying an object* to a destination.
3. **Turn (T)** — Rotate hand/wrist about the long axis.
4. **Crank (C)** — Continuous circular motion (e.g., handwheel).

**Factors affecting Move (M) time (3 marks):**
1. **Distance moved** — Directly proportional (tabulated 2 → 24+ inches).
2. **Weight of object** — Weight allowance added beyond 2 lb.
3. **Case type** — M-A (fixed stop), M-B (indefinite location), M-C (precise location with positioning).
4. **Object size and grip difficulty** — Affects coupled Grasp + Move.
5. **Tactile/visual conditions** — Blind moves add allowance.
6. **Symmetry of placement** — Asymmetric placement increases time.

**(b) Standard Time Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Sum all TMU values (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_{TMU} &= 12 + 5 + 9 + 7 + 2 + 5.5 \\
&= 40.5 \text{ TMU/cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to minutes per cycle (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_{min/cycle} &= \frac{40.5}{100} \\
&= 0.405 \text{ min/cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Normal Time per day (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_{day} &= 0.405 \times 500 \\
&= 202.5 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Add allowance factor AF = 20% = 0.20 (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT_{day} \times (1 + AF) \\
&= 202.5 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
&= 202.5 \times 1.20 \\
&= 243.0 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final answer with unit (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{ST = 243 \text{ min/day} = 4.05 \text{ hours/day}}
$$

**Step 6: Conversion to seconds (1 mark):**

$$
ST = 243 \times 60 = 14{,}580 \text{ s/day}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **(a)** What is **Fitts' Law**? Derive the Index of Difficulty (ID) and explain how it is applied in workplace design. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A worker must reach for a 1-inch wide switch located 8 inches from the resting hand position. Using Fitts' Law with $a = 50$ ms and $b = 120$ ms/bit, calculate the **expected movement time**. If the switch width is doubled to 2 inches (same distance), what is the percentage reduction in movement time? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fitts' Law Explanation (7 marks):**

**Fitts' Law (2 marks):** Proposed by **Paul Fitts (1954)**, it is a predictive model of human movement that mathematically relates the **time to acquire a target** to the **distance** of the movement and the **width (tolerance)** of the target.

**Mathematical form (2 marks):**

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID$$

**Derivation of Index of Difficulty (ID) (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) \\
\text{where } D &= \text{ distance to target} \\
W &= \text{ target width tolerance}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\log_2$ arises from **Shannon's information theory** analogy: the information required to identify a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ scales logarithmically with the ratio $2D/W$.

**Application in workplace design (1.5 marks):**
- **Control panel button sizing** — Wider, more prominent buttons reduce MT.
- **Inspection tasks** — Larger tolerances reduce inspection time.
- **HCI / touchscreen design** — Icons and menus sized for fastest acquisition.
- **Assembly line parts bins** — Larger openings reduce grasp time.
- **Driver cockpit controls** — Frequently used controls placed closer and larger.

**(b) Fitts' Law Numerical (7 marks):**

**Case 1: Switch width = 1 inch, distance = 8 inches (3 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute ID:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_1 &= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \times 8}{1}\right) \\
&= \log_2(16) \\
&= 4.0 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute MT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_1 &= 50 + 120 \times 4.0 \\
&= 50 + 480 \\
&= 530 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: Switch width = 2 inches, distance = 8 inches (3 marks):**

**Step 3: Compute ID:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_2 &= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \times 8}{2}\right) \\
&= \log_2(8) \\
&= 3.0 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute MT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_2 &= 50 + 120 \times 3.0 \\
&= 50 + 360 \\
&= 410 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Percentage reduction (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ reduction} &= \frac{MT_1 - MT_2}{MT_1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{530 - 410}{530} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{120}{530} \times 100 \\
&\approx 22.64\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Doubling the switch width reduces movement time by approximately **22.6%**, demonstrating that ergonomic enlargement of targets yields significant productivity and accuracy gains.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Writing $MT = 530$ without specifying ms vs. s. Always state the unit.
> 2. **Inverting the Fitts' Law argument:** Some students write $ID = \log_2(W/2D)$ — this is **wrong**; it must be $2D/W$.
> 3. **Adding TMU values incorrectly:** Don't add weight and distance in the same column — use the **base + weight** structure.
> 4. **Forgetting the allowance factor:** Standard Time = NT × (1 + AF). Many students forget AF and write $ST = NT$.
> 5. **Confusing R-A, R-B, R-C:** Each has different table values. A reach to a **fixed stop** is R-A; **approximate** location is R-B; **jumbled/crowded** is R-C (slowest).
> 6. **Not converting TMU to minutes:** 1 TMU = 1/100 minute. If the answer is in TMU, it's incomplete.
> 7. **Wrong log base:** Some use $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ in Fitts' Law. Always use $\log_2$ (binary logarithm).
> 8. **Confusing MTM with MOST:** Don't describe MOST sequences as MTM basic motions — they are different systems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Movement Time:**

- **Definition:** Movement Time = time for a **single basic human motion** as predetermined in a PMTS (not measured by stopwatch).
- **Unit:** **1 TMU = 0.036 s = 1/100 min = 0.00001 hour**.
- **MTM-1 System** has **22 basic motions**, tabulated in TMU.
- **Reach (R)** depends on **distance (D) and case (A/B/C)**. R-C is the slowest.
- **Move (M)** depends on **distance + weight + case**. Weight > 2 lb adds a **weight allowance**.
- **Grasp (G)**: G-1A (simplest) = **2 TMU**; G-1E (tiny) = **16 TMU**.
- **Position (P)**: P-1 (symmetric, 5 TMU) to P-5 (heavy press, 21 TMU).
- **Release (RL-1)** = **2 TMU**; **Disengage (D-1)** = **4 TMU**; **D-2** = **7 TMU**.
- **Apply Pressure (AP-1)** = **10.5 TMU**; **AP-2** = **16 TMU**.
- **Eye Travel (ET)** = **7.3 TMU**; **Eye Focus (EF)** = **7.3 TMU**.
- **Foot Motion (FM)** = **8.5 TMU**; **Walk** = **5.3 TMU per pace (30 in)**.
- **Bend** = **29 TMU**; **Stoop** = **36 TMU**; **Kneel (1)** = **31 TMU**; **Sit** = **34 TMU**.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2D/W)$. **Logarithm base = 2 (binary)**. Wider/shorter = faster.
- **MOST System:** 3 sequences (A, B, S). Faster analysis than MTM-1, but less precise.
- **Standard Time** = **Normal Time** × **(1 + Allowance Factor)**.
- **Allowance Factor (AF)** typically 15%–25% for personal, fatigue, and contingency.
- **PMTS Advantages:** No stopwatch needed; consistent standards; method-improving tool; fair to operator.
- **PMTS Limitations:** High training cost; assumes qualified operator; doesn't account for environmental extremes.
- **Key Conversion Triad:** TMU → minutes (÷100) → hours (÷60) → cycles × ST/day.
- **Engineering Use:** Work measurement, cost estimation, incentive wage systems, layout planning, ergonomic design.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 / MOVEMENT TIME NOTES**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme NEP-2020 Outcomes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy of Movements. Human Control of Systems - Compatibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accuracy of Movements. Human Control of Systems - Compatibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Accuracy of Movements

**Accuracy of movement** refers to the capability of a human operator to position, direct, and reach a target precisely with a limb (typically the hand) using a manual control. It is one of the most fundamental measures of motor performance in human–machine interaction and is directly linked to the design of displays, controls, and workstations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Acc. 3.1):** *Accuracy of Movement is the degree of conformity of a controlled movement (output) to the intended movement pattern specified by a display or task goal, quantitatively expressed as the deviation between the target position and the achieved position.*

### Sub-Divisions of Accuracy (Welford's Classification)

| Type | Definition | Typical Behaviour |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Absolute Accuracy (A)** | Ability to reach a target *without* any external reference or prior knowledge | Lowest accuracy — large terminal scatter |
| **Relative Accuracy (Reproductive, B)** | Ability to *return* to a previously set position from a different starting point | Highest accuracy (~ 2× Absolute) |
| **Speed of Movement** | Rate at which a limb traverses between two positions | Inversely related to accuracy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Empirical Result (Welford, 1968):** Relative accuracy is approximately **twice** as good as absolute accuracy. This is why *dials with a fixed reference (e.g., “zero” mark)* produce far fewer operator errors than *dials without any reference.*

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Dart-Thrower's Dilemma"

Imagine throwing darts at a board in a dimly lit pub:
- **Absolute accuracy** = You walk up blindfolded and try to hit the bullseye on your **first** throw.
- **Relative accuracy** = You throw once (anywhere), *see* where the dart landed, and then try to **return** to that exact spot.
- **Why does relative accuracy win?** Because your brain now has a **closed-loop feedback** (the memory of the previous throw), whereas the absolute case is an **open-loop ballistic move**.

The same logic governs every manual control in a cockpit, on a CNC console, or in a surgical robot.

---

## 1.2 Human Control of Systems

A human performing any physical task — steering a car, flying an aircraft, or tracking a moving radar target — is acting as a **closed-loop servo-mechanism** whose output is muscle actuation and whose input is a perceived error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Human Control of Systems is the process by which an operator perceives display information (stimulus), cognitively interprets it, and executes muscular responses to minimise the discrepancy between the desired state and the actual state of a machine.*

### Two Fundamental Modes of Control

| Mode | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Open-Loop (Ballistic / Pre-programmed)** | Movement is initiated using a *stored motor program*; **no feedback** is used during execution. Very fast, fixed-duration. | Swatting a mosquito, hammering a nail |
| **Closed-Loop (Feedback-based)** | Movement is *continuously corrected* using sensory feedback (vision, proprioception). Slower, accurate. | Steering a car along a winding road |

> [!NOTE]
> Most real human tasks are **dual-mode**: the initial phase is **open-loop ballistic** (≈ first 100–200 ms), after which feedback correction converts it into a **closed-loop** corrective phase.

### Types of Tracking Displays (Sub-module 3.2)

1. **Compensatory Tracking** — A *single* display shows only the **error** (difference between target and controlled element). The operator's job is to drive the error to zero.
2. **Pursuit Tracking** — Display shows both the **target** and the **controlled element** separately; the operator must match the marker to the target.
3. **Combined Tracking** — Both target and error are shown; operator has full information.

Pursuit tracking is *easier* (less cognitive load) and yields lower error than compensatory tracking.

---

## 1.3 Compatibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Compatibility is the degree of correspondence between the spatial arrangement, direction of motion, or conceptual meaning of a stimulus (display) and the required response (control). High compatibility produces fast, accurate responses; low compatibility produces systematic errors.*

The principle was formalised by **Ahramowitz & Laughery (1966)** and later expanded by **Wickens et al. (2013)** within the **Multiple Resource Theory**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Sticky Door Knob"

You push a door expecting it to open outward, but it actually opens inward — you instinctively push when you should pull. The push/pull mapping of your hand did **not** match the door's mechanics. That mismatch is **incompatibility**. Now imagine every door in a building having the *same* push/pull convention — your body learns it once, and every subsequent entry becomes automatic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Human factors rule of thumb:** *“If the response follows naturally from the stimulus, the operator does not need to think — the muscle moves by itself.”*

### The Three Classical Categories of Compatibility

1. **Stimulus–Response (S-R) Compatibility** — Spatial location/direction of stimulus matches response.
2. **Movement Compatibility** — Direction of limb movement matches direction of displayed symbol change (e.g., moving a knob *up* increases the value shown).
3. **Conceptual / Cultural Compatibility** — Conventional symbol–meaning pairings (e.g., red = stop, green = go, “−” = decrease).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Speed–Accuracy Trade-off (Woodworth, 1899)

Woodworth's classical law states:

> *As movement speed increases, accuracy decreases — the two are inversely related.*

This is the foundation for the modern formulation in Section 3.

### Fitts' Law (The Mathematical Quantification)

In **1954**, Paul Fitts quantified the speed–accuracy relationship. It is the single most cited equation in human factors engineering.

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID$$

Where:
- **MT** = Movement Time (seconds)
- **ID** = Index of Difficulty (bits)
- **a, b** = Empirical constants (a = reaction/initial time, b = information-processing rate per bit)
- **IP** = Index of Performance = $ID / MT$ (bits/second)

### Two Equivalent Forms of ID

| Form | Equation | Best Use |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Shannon Formulation** | $ID = \log_2\!\big(2A/W\big)$ | Standard textbook (Fitts 1954) |
| **Welford Formulation** | $ID = \log_2\!\big(A/W + 1\big)$ | Removes edge artefacts for $A \to 0$ |

Where:
- $A$ = **Amplitude** (distance from start to target centre)
- $W$ = **Width** (target tolerance / size)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** *Fitts' Law has been experimentally verified for pointing devices (mouse, stylus, touchpad, VR controllers) and even for surgeon-tool movement in robotic surgery. It allows designers to predict the time a user will need to acquire a target on a touchscreen before the device is built.*

---

## 2.2 The Two Components of Human Movement (Schmidt's Schema Theory)

Any aimed movement has two phases:

| Phase | Duration | Feedback Used | Description |
|-------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Primary (Ballistic)** | First ~ 100–200 ms | None — pre-programmed | Subconscious; based on a *recall schema* of past movements |
| **Secondary (Corrective)** | After ~ 200 ms | Continuous visual/proprioceptive | Conscious adjustment; uses feedback loop |

> The accuracy of the *primary phase* depends entirely on the operator's **motor schema** (learned map of stimulus → response). The *secondary phase* improves accuracy but at the cost of **movement time**.

---

## 2.3 The Human as a Closed-Loop Controller — Transfer Function

In manual control theory, the human operator is modelled as a **quasi-linear controller** with an approximate transfer function:

$$H(s) = \frac{K \cdot e^{-\tau s}(T_L s + 1)}{(T_I s + 1)(T_N s + 1)}$$

Where:
- $K$ = Operator gain
- $\tau$ = **Reaction-time delay** (≈ 0.2 s)
- $T_L$ = Lead time constant (anticipatory action)
- $T_I$ = Lag time constant (low-pass filtering)
- $T_N$ = Neuromuscular time constant (≈ 0.1 s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** When the human is the inner-loop controller of an unstable system (e.g., a fighter jet), the designer must account for the **delay $\tau$**, or the closed-loop system will oscillate catastrophically. This is the central problem of *pilot-induced oscillation (PIO)*.

---

## 2.4 Tracking — Compensatory vs Pursuit

| Parameter | Compensatory | Pursuit | Combined |
|-----------|--------------|---------|----------|
| Information shown | Error only | Target + Marker | Both target & error |
| Operator behaviour | "Drive-to-zero" | "Match-to-target" | Hybrid |
| Cognitive load | High | Low | Medium |
| RMS Error (typical) | Highest | Lowest | Medium |
| Use-case | ILS landing, simple radio dials | Radar tracking, gunnery | Advanced HUDs |

---

## 2.5 Compatibility — Deeper Taxonomy

### 2.5.1 Stimulus–Response (S-R) Compatibility

- **Spatial Compatibility:** Stimulus location *predicts* response location.
  - *Example:* A red warning light on the *left* engine must be acknowledged with the *left* hand.
- **Response-Set Compatibility:** Mixed within-block mappings produce interference (Stroop-like effects).

### 2.5.2 Movement (Directional) Compatibility

Direction of hand/limb movement must match direction of change shown on the display.

| Display movement | Expected control movement | Compatibility |
|------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Bar grows upward | Knob rotated **clockwise** *or* slider pushed **forward/up** | High |
| Number on digital display increases | Control moves to the **right** or **up** | High |

### 2.5.3 Ahramowitz & Laughery's Three Design Principles (1966)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Principle of Expected Direction** — Controls should operate in the direction the operator *expects*.
> 2. **Principle of Common Association** — Controls should resemble the function they control (e.g., a steering wheel *turns* like the wheels).
> 3. **Principle of Spatial Coding** — Controls should be located and shaped for easy discrimination.

### 2.5.4 Cultural (Conventional) Compatibility

| Symbol / Code | Convention | Application |
|---------------|------------|-------------|
| 🔴 Red | Danger / Stop | Brake pedals, emergency stop |
| 🟢 Green | Safe / Go | Start buttons, ready indicators |
| **↑ / ↑** | Increase | Volume, throttle, brightness |
| **“−”** | Decrease | Volume, brightness |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Index of Difficulty (Shannon)** | $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$ | bits | Original Fitts formulation |
| 2 | **Index of Difficulty (Welford)** | $ID = \log_2(A/W + 1)$ | bits | Recommended modern form |
| 3 | **Fitts' Movement Time** | $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ | seconds | Linear in ID |
| 4 | **Index of Performance** | $IP = ID / MT$ | bits/sec | Higher = better motor control |
| 5 | **Throughput (effective IP)** | $TP = \dfrac{\log_2(A/W + 1)}{MT}$ | bits/sec | Used in ISO 9241-9 |
| 6 | **Speed-Accuracy Trade-off** | $Error \propto e^{-k \cdot v}$ | dimensionless | Empirical; k = constant |
| 7 | **Reaction Delay (Human)** | $\tau \approx 0.2$ s | seconds | Visual stimulus |
| 8 | **Relative vs Absolute Accuracy** | $B \approx 2A$ | mm | Empirical Welford ratio |
| 9 | **Human TF (McRuer)** | $H(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-\tau s}(T_L s + 1)}{(T_I s + 1)(T_N s + 1)}$ | — | Quasi-linear model |
| 10 | **Compatibility Score** | $C = \dfrac{t_{\text{incompatible}} - t_{\text{compatible}}}{t_{\text{compatible}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Lower is better |

> All quantities use natural positive values; $A, W, MT > 0$. The $\log_2$ uses standard binary logarithm. Where absolute value is required, write $\lvert x \rvert$ using **\lvert** syntax, *not* pipe characters, to avoid breaking markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fitts' Law from Information Theory

Fitts reasoned that any aimed movement is essentially a **transmission of information** from the operator's *intention* (target) to the *muscle actuation* (end-effector). Using Shannon's channel capacity framework:

### Step 1 — Define the "Signal" and "Noise"

The target has a **centre** (signal) and an **acceptable tolerance** of width $W$ (noise band). The operator must resolve which "bin" out of the range the target occupies.

$$\text{Number of distinguishable targets} = \frac{A}{W}$$

*(Amplitude divided by tolerance gives the discrete "steps" of position.)*

### Step 2 — Convert to Information Units (Bits)

By Shannon's definition of information:

$$\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{A}{W}\right) \quad &\text{(Fitts' original 1954 formulation)}
\end{aligned}$$

However, this fails at the edge case $A \to 0$ (giving $ID \to -\infty$). Fitts introduced the factor of 2:

$$\begin{aligned}
ID_{\text{Shannon}} &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \\
&= \log_2(2) + \log_2\!\left(\frac{A}{W}\right) \\
&= 1 + \log_2\!\left(\frac{A}{W}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Welford's Improved Formulation

To prevent the singularity at $A = 0$, Welford substituted:

$$\begin{aligned}
ID_{\text{Welford}} &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) \\
&= \log_2\!\left(\frac{A + W}{W}\right) \\
&= \log_2(A + W) - \log_2(W)
\end{aligned}$$

This form yields $ID = 0$ when $A = 0$ (perfectly consistent) and is the modern ISO standard.

### Step 4 — Movement Time Equation

Empirically, MT is observed to be linearly related to ID:

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID$$

where $a$ is the intercept (reaction/initial time) and $b$ is the slope (the *time per bit* of information processed).

### Step 5 — Index of Performance

Rearranging for operator throughput:

$$\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{1}{b + a/ID}
\end{aligned}$$

For large $A$ (so $ID$ dominates), $IP \to 1/b$ bits/sec — the **maximum throughput** of the operator's motor channel.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Fitts' Law

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** An operator must move a mouse cursor from one button (width $W = 20$ mm) to another located $A = 160$ mm away. For this device, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 200$ ms/bit. Find the movement time and the index of performance.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute ID (Shannon form)} \quad ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \\
&= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 160}{20}\right) \\
&= \log_2(16) \\
&= 4.0 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Compute MT} \quad MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
&= 50 \text{ ms} + 200 \text{ ms/bit} \times 4.0 \text{ bits} \\
&= 50 + 800 \\
&= 850 \text{ ms} \\
&= 0.85 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Compute IP} \quad IP &= \frac{ID}{MT} \\
&= \frac{4.0 \text{ bits}}{0.85 \text{ s}} \\
&= 4.71 \text{ bits/s}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** A typical desktop mouse yields $IP \approx 4 \text{–} 7$ bits/sec; touch screens are slightly lower (2–4 bits/sec). Our cursor falls comfortably in the desktop range.

---

## 3.3 Derivations for Speed–Accuracy Trade-off

### Empirical Model (Schmidt, Zehl & Carter, 1980)

Schmidt's team proposed a power-relation between velocity and accuracy:

$$W = k \cdot v^{p}$$

Where:
- $W$ = target width (accuracy tolerance)
- $v$ = peak velocity of movement
- $k, p$ = empirical constants ($p \approx -0.5$ to $-0.8$ for adults)

### Linearised Form (Log-transform)

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln W &= \ln k + p \cdot \ln v \\
\Rightarrow W &= k \cdot v^{p}
\end{aligned}$$

For $p = -0.5$, halving the velocity increases accuracy tolerance by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Compatibility Assessment

Let the response time under compatible and incompatible mappings be $t_c$ and $t_i$ respectively. The **compatibility cost** is:

$$C = \frac{t_i - t_c}{t_c} \times 100\%$$

| Compatibility Type | Typical Cost $C$ | Example |
|--------------------|------------------|---------|
| High spatial (aligned) | 0–5 % | Stick forward → nose down |
| Moderate (rotated 90°) | 20–40 % | Stick right → rudder right |
| Inverted (180°) | 50–80 % | Stick back → nose down (reversed) |
| Cultural mismatch | 10–30 % | Red button = start (unfamiliar user) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical design rule:** If $C > 30\%$, the mapping is *unacceptable* and must be redesigned (relabel, recode, or reposition).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Compatibility Cost

> An experimental task has compatible mean reaction time $t_c = 350$ ms and incompatible $t_i = 580$ ms. Compute the compatibility cost.

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{580 - 350}{350} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{230}{350} \times 100\% \\
&= 65.71\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Verdict:** Cost is *far* above the 30 % threshold → the mapping is unacceptable.

---

## 3.6 The Open-Loop / Closed-Loop Transition — Mathematics

Let the motor command be $u(t)$, the actual hand position be $x(t)$, and the target be $x_T$. The error is $e(t) = x_T - x(t)$.

### Open-loop phase ($0 \le t \le t_1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
u(t) &= f_{\text{schema}}(x_T) \quad &\text{(no feedback)} \\
x(t) &= \int_0^t u(\tau)\, d\tau \quad &\text{(integration)} \\
e(t_1) &= x_T - x(t_1) \quad &\text{(terminal error)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Closed-loop phase ($t > t_1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
u(t) &= K_p \cdot e(t) + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} \quad &\text{(PD controller)} \\
\text{Steady-state error} \quad e_{ss} &\to 0 \quad &\text{as } t \to \infty
\end{aligned}$$

This *hybrid* model explains why **skilled** operators (surgeons, pilots) have very small terminal error — their **schema** is precisely tuned; novices need prolonged feedback correction and never quite close the loop in time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Human as a Closed-Loop Tracking Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Generator] -->|Desired Position xT| B[Display]
    B -->|Visual Stimulus| C[Human Sensory Stage]
    C -->|Perception| D[Human Cognitive Stage]
    D -->|Motor Command u| E[Human Muscular Stage]
    E -->|Limb Motion x| F[Controlled Machine]
    F -->|Output Position| G[Error Comparator]
    G -->|Error Signal e| B
    H[Feedback - delay tau] --> C
```

> **Reading the loop:** The human *senses* the display, *cognitively* computes the corrective command, and *muscularly* moves the control. The new output is compared against the target; the residual *error* drives the next correction. The total sensory-motor delay $\tau \approx 200$ ms limits the bandwidth.

---

## 4.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OLC["Open-Loop Ballistic Control"]
        O1[Stimulus] --> O2[Schema Lookup]
        O2 --> O3[Pre-programmed Motor Command]
        O3 --> O4[Muscle Actuation]
        O4 --> O5[Fixed-Duration Movement]
    end
    subgraph CLC["Closed-Loop Feedback Control"]
        C1[Stimulus] --> C2[Error Perception]
        C2 --> C3[Re-compute Command]
        C3 --> C4[Muscle Actuation]
        C4 --> C5[Movement Update]
        C5 --> C6[Re-compare to Target]
        C6 --> C2
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Compatibility Mapping Hierarchy (Wickens / Ahramowitz)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Root - Compatibility] --> S[Spatial S-R Compatibility]
    R --> M[Movement Compatibility]
    R --> C[Conceptual / Cultural Compatibility]
    S --> S1[Stimulus location predicts response location]
    M --> M1[Direction of limb motion matches display change]
    M --> M2[Clockwise knob - pointer moves right]
    C --> C1[Red - Stop]
    C --> C2[Green - Go]
    C --> C3[Up arrow - Increase]
    C --> C4[Down arrow - Decrease]
```

---

## 4.4 Tracking Display Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COMP["Compensatory - Single Display"]
        CMP1[Target dot stays fixed at center] --> CMP2[Operator drives error needle to zero]
    end
    subgraph PUR["Pursuit - Two Markers"]
        PUR1[Target moves across screen] --> PUR2[Marker shows controlled element]
        PUR2 --> PUR3[Operator aligns marker to target]
    end
    subgraph COMB["Combined"]
        CMB1[Target shown] --> CMB2[Error shown separately]
        CMB2 --> CMB3[Operator uses both cues]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Speed–Accuracy Trade-off (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Intends Target Position] --> B{Distance A and Tolerance W}
    B --> C[Compute ID bits]
    C --> D[Lookup Fitts Slope b]
    D --> E[MT equals a + b times ID]
    E --> F{High MT implies accuracy UP or DOWN}
    F -->|Yes| G[Speed DOWN accuracy UP]
    F -->|Speed UP| H[accuracy DOWN - Fitts Tradeoff]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define *accuracy of movement* and distinguish between *absolute* and *relative* accuracy.

**Model Answer:**

*Accuracy of movement* is the degree of closeness between the intended target position and the actual end-position reached by the operator's limb or control. It is the most fundamental metric of manual-control performance.

**Absolute accuracy** (Type A) is the ability to reach a target **without any external reference** — the operator has no prior knowledge of the correct position. This is essentially an *open-loop* ballistic task.

**Relative accuracy** (Type B) is the ability to **return** to a target whose position has been previously set and is therefore *known*. This is essentially a *closed-loop* task using stored feedback.

Empirically (Welford), **relative accuracy is approximately twice as good as absolute accuracy**. Therefore, displays with fixed reference marks (e.g., a zero index on a dial) consistently outperform displays without such marks. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State **Ahramowitz & Laughery's** three principles of control–display compatibility.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Principle of Expected Direction:** Controls should operate in the direction the operator *expects* the system to respond.
2. **Principle of Common Association:** Controls should operate in a manner *resembling* the function they control (e.g., a steering wheel rotates like the wheels).
3. **Principle of Spatial Coding:** Controls should be selected, shaped, and located for *ease of discrimination* — distinct shapes for distinct functions. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 3 Pattern]**

#### (a) **Explain Fitts' Law in detail. Define Index of Difficulty (ID) and Index of Performance (IP), and discuss the engineering significance of the law in the design of pointing devices and graphical user interfaces. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Statement of the Law:**
Fitts' Law (1954) quantifies the relationship between the *time* required to move to a target and the *difficulty* of the target acquisition. It is mathematically stated as:

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID$$

where $MT$ is movement time, $a$ is the reaction/initial time, $b$ is the slope (time per bit), and $ID$ is the **Index of Difficulty** measured in bits.

**2. Index of Difficulty:**
Two formulations exist:

- **Shannon form:** $ID = \log_2(2A / W)$
- **Welford form:** $ID = \log_2(A/W + 1)$

where $A$ = amplitude (distance to target) and $W$ = tolerance (target width).

**3. Index of Performance:**
The reciprocal-of-slope measure of motor throughput:

$$IP = \frac{ID}{MT} \;\; \text{(bits per second)}$$

This is a measure of how efficiently the operator transmits information via motor commands.

**4. Significance in Engineering:**

- **Predicts user-interface performance:** Designers of touch-screens, VR controllers, and computer mice can compute acquisition time *before* building the device.
- **ISO 9241-9 Standard:** Uses Fitts' IP as the *benchmark* for non-keyboard input devices.
- **Design heuristic:** *Increase target size $W$* and *reduce distance $A$* to lower ID and speed up interactions.
- **Comparative metric:** Modern touch screens give $IP \approx 2\text{–}4$ bits/s; trackballs $\approx 2\text{–}3$ bits/s; head-controlled pointers $\approx 1.5\text{–}2.5$ bits/s.

**5. Worked Example (Included for completeness):**
For $A = 200$ mm, $W = 50$ mm, $a = 80$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit:

$$ID = \log_2(200/50 + 1) = \log_2(5) \approx 2.32 \text{ bits}$$
$$MT = 80 + 150 \times 2.32 = 428 \text{ ms}$$
$$IP = 2.32 / 0.428 = 5.42 \text{ bits/s}$$

**(7 marks — distributed: 2 law statement, 2 ID/IP definitions, 3 engineering significance & example)**

#### (b) **Discuss the two types of tracking displays — *compensatory* and *pursuit* — with reference to their information content, operator workload, and typical applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Compensatory Tracking:**
- The display shows **only the error** between the target and the controlled element (e.g., a centred needle showing deviation).
- The operator's task is to *drive the error to zero* by actuating the control.
- **Cognitive load is high** because the operator must internally maintain a model of the target motion.
- **Application:** Instrument Landing System (ILS) indicators, simple altimeters, basic radio dials.

**2. Pursuit Tracking:**
- The display shows both the **target** and the **controlled element** as separate markers.
- The operator's task is to *match the marker to the target* by tracking.
- **Cognitive load is lower** because the operator can perceive the *error* directly by sighting along both markers.
- **Application:** Radar tracking, air-to-air gunnery reticles, modern HUDs.

**3. Combined Tracking:**
- Both target and error are displayed simultaneously (e.g., a flight-director system).
- Yields the lowest RMS tracking error and is used in advanced fly-by-wire cockpits.

**4. Comparative Analysis:**

| Feature | Compensatory | Pursuit |
|---------|--------------|---------|
| Information shown | Error only | Target + element |
| Operator workload | High | Low |
| RMS Error | High | Low |
| Learning time | Long | Short |
| Use-case | ILS, basic gauges | Radar, HUD |

**5. Engineering Insight:**
Fitts' Law also applies: in pursuit tracking, the *effective target width* perceived by the operator is *larger* than in compensatory, so the ID is lower and performance is superior.

**(7 marks — distributed: 2 each for compensatory and pursuit, 1 comparison table, 2 application + insight)**

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (Internal Option for KTU Students)**

#### (a) **What is *compatibility* in human factors? Classify the various types of compatibility and explain with two examples each how they influence operator performance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
*Compatibility* is the degree of correspondence between the spatial arrangement, direction of motion, or conceptual meaning of a **stimulus** (display) and the required **response** (control). High compatibility reduces reaction time and error rate.

**Classification:**

1. **Stimulus–Response (S-R) Compatibility**
   - *Spatial:* The location of the stimulus predicts the location of the response.
   - *Example 1:* A red warning lamp on the *left engine* is paired with the *left* hand acknowledgement switch.
   - *Example 2:* In an assembly line, the screw on the *left* side of the chassis is tightened using the *left-hand* tool — left-to-left mapping.

2. **Movement (Directional) Compatibility**
   - *Example 1:* In a cockpit, pushing the control stick *forward* causes the aircraft's nose to *pitch down* — the limb motion and the displayed response match.
   - *Example 2:* A radio's volume knob turns *clockwise* to *increase* volume — the upward visual trend on the dial matches the clockwise hand motion.

3. **Conceptual / Cultural Compatibility**
   - *Example 1:* Red = Stop, Green = Go (universally accepted traffic-light convention).
   - *Example 2:* The symbol “−” intuitively maps to *decrease*; “+” maps to *increase*.

**Influence on Performance:**
- Reaction time is **shortest** for highly compatible mappings and can increase by **50–80 %** for incompatible ones (Stroop-type interference).
- Error rate is **lowest** for compatible mappings; *systematic* errors (always in the same wrong direction) characterise incompatible mappings.
- Fatigue is *higher* with incompatible mappings because of sustained cognitive effort.

**(7 marks — distributed: 1 definition, 4 classification with 2 examples each compressed, 2 performance influence)**

#### (b) **Explain the human operator as a closed-loop controller using a block diagram. Discuss the importance of *reaction-time delay* and *neuromuscular lag* in the stability of the controlled system. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Human-as-Controller Block Diagram:**

```
TARGET ──► DISPLAY ──► SENSORY (vision/proprioception)
                            │
                       COGNITIVE DECISION (delay τ)
                            │
                       NEUROMUSCULAR SYSTEM (lag TN)
                            │
                       MUSCLE ACTUATION ──► CONTROLLED ELEMENT
                                                       │
                                                  COMPARATOR ◄── FEEDBACK
```

- **Sensory block:** Receives visual/proprioceptive input; perceptual delay ≈ 50 ms.
- **Cognitive block:** Decision-making; introduces the dominant delay $\tau \approx 200$ ms.
- **Neuromuscular block:** Convert neural command to muscle force; lag $T_N \approx 100$ ms.
- **Comparator:** Implicit comparison inside the CNS.

**2. Transfer-Function Model:**

$$H(s) = \frac{K \cdot e^{-\tau s}(T_L s + 1)}{(T_I s + 1)(T_N s + 1)}$$

- $K$ = gain, $\tau$ = reaction delay, $T_L$ = lead, $T_I$ = lag, $T_N$ = neuromuscular lag.

**3. Importance of Reaction-Time Delay $\tau$:**
- The pure delay $e^{-\tau s}$ introduces a *phase lag* proportional to $\omega \tau$ where $\omega$ is the system frequency.
- At high frequencies, $\tau$ dominates and *destabilises* the closed loop.
- **Pilot-Induced Oscillation (PIO):** A classic example is when the pilot over-corrects due to the delay, producing growing oscillations (e.g., the YF-22 prototype, Airbus A320 early flight tests).

**4. Importance of Neuromuscular Lag $T_N$:**
- $T_N \approx 0.1$ s acts as a low-pass filter — the muscle cannot follow control inputs above ≈ 1.6 Hz ($= 1/2\pi T_N$).
- Beyond this bandwidth, the operator behaves as a *passive element* and cannot stabilise the system.

**5. Engineering Consequence:**
- Designers must ensure the *controlled element bandwidth* is well below $1/\tau$ and $1/T_N$ — typically < 0.5 Hz for manual control loops.
- Adaptive autopilots assist the pilot precisely because the human bandwidth is limited.

**(7 marks — distributed: 2 block diagram & model, 2 each for $\tau$ and $T_N$)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing absolute with relative accuracy** — absolute is *first-time*, relative is *return*; relative ≈ 2× absolute. Don't mix them up in worked examples.
> 2. **Mixing $A$ and $W$ in Fitts' Law** — $A$ is **distance to target**, $W$ is **target width/tolerance**, *not* the screen width.
> 3. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$** — Fitts' law is in **binary bits**, not decimal digits. $ID = \log_2$ always.
> 4. **Forgetting units of $MT$** — be consistent (seconds vs milliseconds); the IP will be bits/sec *only* if MT is in seconds.
> 5. **Calling the human a "perfect" controller** — never ignore the $\tau = 200$ ms delay; it is *the* defining limitation.
> 6. **Listing types of compatibility without examples** — KTU examiners always want *engineering examples* (aircraft stick, traffic lights, mouse cursor). Generic lists without examples get 0.5–1 mark deduction per item.
> 7. **Writing the wrong direction of compatibility cost** — *incompatible* time must be the *numerator* for cost > 0. Reversing the formula is a 1-mark penalty.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Accuracy types:** Absolute < Relative. **Relative ≈ 2 × Absolute** (Welford).
- **Speed-Accuracy trade-off:** As speed ↑, accuracy ↓. Quantified by **Fitts' Law**.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot ID$; **ID in bits** ($\log_2$); **IP in bits/sec**.
- **Shannon ID:** $\log_2(2A/W)$ — original. **Welford ID:** $\log_2(A/W + 1)$ — preferred.
- **Two-phase movement:** Open-loop *primary* (≈ 100–200 ms, ballistic) → Closed-loop *secondary* (feedback-corrected).
- **Control modes:** Open-loop = no feedback (fast). Closed-loop = feedback (accurate).
- **Tracking types:** *Compensatory* (error only, hard) < *Pursuit* (target + marker, easy) < *Combined*.
- **Human transfer function:** $H(s) = K e^{-\tau s}(T_L s + 1) / [(T_I s + 1)(T_N s + 1)]$.
- **Reaction-time delay:** $\tau \approx 200$ ms — the bottleneck of manual control.
- **Neuromuscular lag:** $T_N \approx 100$ ms — limits manual bandwidth to ≈ 1.6 Hz.
- **Compatibility (S-R):** Stimulus location ↔ Response location.
- **Movement compatibility:** Limb motion direction ↔ Display change direction (clockwise ↔ increase).
- **Cultural compatibility:** Red = Stop, Green = Go; “+” = Increase, “−” = Decrease.
- **Ahramowitz & Laughery's principles:** Expected direction, Common association, Spatial coding.
- **Compatibility cost formula:** $C = (t_i - t_c) / t_c \times 100\%$. **Threshold: $C > 30\%$ = unacceptable.**
- **Design rule:** Increase target size $W$ and reduce distance $A$ → lower $ID$ → faster $MT$.
- **Fitts in ISO:** ISO 9241-9 uses Fitts' IP to benchmark input devices.
- **Pilot-Induced Oscillation:** Caused by excessive $\tau$ in unstable aircraft loops.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spatial Compatibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT748 - HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spatial Compatibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spatial Compatibility** is a fundamental principle in Human Factors Engineering that describes the degree of correspondence between the **spatial arrangement of controls** and the **spatial arrangement of the displays (or system responses)** they manipulate. It is one of the seven principles of display-control compatibility originally enumerated by **Paul M. Fitts (1951)** in his seminal work *"Human Engineering for an Effective Air Navigation and Traffic Control System"* (Ohio State University).

In precise KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, spatial compatibility is defined as:

> The match between the **geometric/locational layout** of input devices (controls) and the corresponding output devices (displays), such that the user's natural spatial mapping produces the intended system response without requiring conscious translation.

The two primary sub-classifications are:

| Sub-Type | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Spatial Layout Compatibility** | The physical position of the control on the panel matches the position of its associated display element. |
| **Movement (Directional) Compatibility** | The direction of control motion corresponds to the direction of the resulting display/response movement. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car. When you turn the **steering wheel to the right**, the car moves to the right. When you push the **accelerator pedal forward**, the car moves forward. This "natural-feeling" mapping is **spatial compatibility** at work.

Now imagine a bizarre alternative world:
- Turning the steering wheel **right** makes the car turn **left** (low compatibility)
- Pressing the **brake** makes the car go **faster** (low movement compatibility)

In such a world, every driver would crash within seconds. The reason real-world interfaces feel intuitive is because designers exploit **spatial compatibility** to align human spatial expectations with mechanical reality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Spatial compatibility is the **single most heavily tested** topic from Module 3 under the KTU 2024 scheme. Students must master both the theoretical taxonomy (Fitts' classification) and real-world engineering examples (cockpits, vehicles, industrial consoles).

---

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The key measurable parameter associated with spatial compatibility is **Response Time (RT)** and **Error Rate**, typically expressed in:

- **Milliseconds (ms)** for reaction time
- **Percentage (%)** for error frequency
- **Bits/second** for information throughput (derived from Fitts' Law)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fitts' Law Connection:** Compatibility is a multiplicative factor in the index of difficulty. Higher spatial compatibility **reduces effective index of difficulty**, thereby improving throughput and reducing errors per Shannon's formulation $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$.

---

## 1.4 Why Spatial Compatibility Matters — The Underlying Psychology

Humans possess powerful **spatial-cognitive mapping** abilities developed through:
1. **Early motor learning** (reaching, grasping, pointing in 3D space)
2. **Cultural and population stereotypes** (e.g., "up" = "more", "right" = "forward in time")
3. **Repeated practice** with conventional interfaces

When the control-display mapping violates these expectations, the user must engage **conscious, effortful translation**, which:
- Increases reaction time
- Increases error rate
- Increases cognitive load (mental workload)
- Causes fatigue during prolonged operation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fitts' Compatibility Classification (Detailed Taxonomy)

Paul Fitts, in collaboration with **Bingham** and **Deininger**, identified **seven categories** of compatibility between displays and controls. Spatial compatibility is the **most heavily weighted** of these:

### Category 1: Spatial (Locational) Compatibility
- **Definition:** The control is located in the same relative position as the display it operates.
- **Example:** In a multi-engine aircraft cockpit, the throttle for Engine 1 (left-most engine) is positioned on the **far left** of the throttle quadrant, Engine 2 throttle next to it, and so on.

### Category 2: Movement (Directional) Compatibility
- **Definition:** The direction of control movement matches the direction of the display element or system response.
- **Example:** Moving a joystick **forward** causes a cursor on the screen to move **upward** (or toward the user, depending on convention).

### Category 3: Population Stereotype Compatibility
- **Definition:** The control-response relationship aligns with what the majority of a population has been culturally trained to expect.
- **Example:** Turning a **light switch UP** to turn a light ON — this is a learned population stereotype, not a physical necessity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Spatial layout** compatibility = position-to-position mapping.
> - **Movement** compatibility = direction-of-motion mapping.
> - **Population stereotype** compatibility = culturally-learned expectation.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identify the Display Set**
Determine all display elements that require user manipulation (e.g., dials, gauges, screens, cursors).

**Step 2 — Identify the Control Set**
Determine all available input devices (e.g., knobs, switches, joysticks, levers).

**Step 3 — Establish the Mapping Function**
Define a one-to-one (or one-to-many) mapping function $f: C \to D$ where each control $c_i \in C$ is associated with one or more displays $d_i \in D$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Spatial Mapping**
Verify that the geometric layout of $C$ mirrors the geometric layout of $D$.

**Step 5 — Evaluate Movement Mapping**
Verify that the kinematic direction of $C$ (linear, rotary, multi-axis) corresponds to the visual direction of $D$.

**Step 6 — Cross-Check Population Stereotypes**
Verify that the mapping aligns with the expectations of the trained operator population.

**Step 7 — Measure Performance**
Quantify using $RT$, $Error\%$, and Fitts' throughput $TP$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fitts' Index of Difficulty | $ID = \log_2(2A / W)$ | bits | Quantifies task difficulty |
| Movement Time (Fitts' Law) | $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ | seconds | Predicts task completion time |
| Throughput | $TP = ID / MT$ | bits/sec | Measures human-machine efficiency |
| Compatibility-Adjusted ID | $ID_{eff} = k_c \cdot ID$ | bits | $k_c < 1$ for high compatibility |
| Reaction Time (Hick's Law) | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | seconds | Time to choose among $n$ options |
| Information Transfer Rate | $ITR = \log_2(n) / RT$ | bits/sec | Channel capacity of operator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Cell Rule Note:** Absolute-value notation uses `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity, per the KTU 2024 strict formatting protocol.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Aviation Cockpits:** Throttle quadrant, flight stick, rudder pedals — all designed with strict spatial compatibility.
2. **Automotive Industry:** Steering wheel rotation direction = wheel direction; gear lever H-pattern mirrors transmission gear positions.
3. **Industrial Control Rooms:** Valve handles on a panel match the spatial arrangement of pipeline junctions on a P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram).
4. **Medical Devices:** Surgical robot master controls spatially correspond to the slave end-effector's orientation.
5. **Consumer Electronics:** TV remote volume-up button is on the upper side (population stereotype).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Compatibility-Adjusted Fitts' Law

We begin with Fitts' original Shannon formulation. The standard form assumes **perfect compatibility** (i.e., the user does not need to translate between control and display). When spatial compatibility is **imperfect**, an additional translation cost is introduced.

**Step 1 — Standard Fitts' Law**

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID$$

where $ID = \log_2(2A / W)$.

**Step 2 — Introduce Compatibility Coefficient $k_c$**

Define $k_c \in [0.5, 2.0]$ as the **compatibility multiplier**:
- $k_c = 1.0$ → neutral compatibility
- $k_c < 1.0$ → high spatial compatibility (reduces effective difficulty)
- $k_c > 1.0$ → low spatial compatibility (increases effective difficulty)

**Step 3 — Compute Effective Index of Difficulty**

$$ID_{eff} = k_c \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Movement Time Equation**

$$MT_{eff} = a + b \cdot k_c \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute Throughput Under Compatibility**

$$TP_{eff} = \frac{ID_{eff}}{MT_{eff}} = \frac{k_c \cdot \log_2(2A/W)}{a + b \cdot k_c \cdot \log_2(2A/W)}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation Example**

Suppose $A = 100$ mm, $W = 10$ mm, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit, and $k_c = 0.8$ (high compatibility).

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2(2 \cdot 100 / 10) \\
&= \log_2(20) \\
&= 4.322 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_{eff} &= 0.8 \cdot 4.322 \\
&= 3.458 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_{eff} &= 50 + 100 \cdot 3.458 \\
&= 50 + 345.8 \\
&= 395.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP_{eff} &= 3.458 / 0.3958 \\
&= 8.736 \text{ bits/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

Without compatibility ($k_c = 1.0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= 50 + 100 \cdot 4.322 \\
&= 482.2 \text{ ms} \\
TP &= 4.322 / 0.4822 \\
&= 8.963 \text{ bits/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** While raw throughput is similar, the high-compatibility case yields a **faster absolute movement time (395.8 ms vs 482.2 ms)**, demonstrating the practical benefit of spatial compatibility in time-critical tasks.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Compatibility Levels Across Common Interfaces

| Interface Type | Spatial Layout | Movement Direction | Population Stereotype | Overall Compatibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aircraft Yoke (Roll) | High | High | High | **Excellent** |
| Automobile Steering | N/A (single) | High | High | **Excellent** |
| Industrial Lever-Indicator | Variable | Variable | Moderate | **Application-Dependent** |
| Touchscreen Drag-and-Drop | High | High | High | **Excellent** |
| Inverted Aircraft Yoke | High | Inverted | Low (for trained pilots) | **Poor for novices** |
| Keyboard Arrow Keys | High | High | High | **Excellent** |
| Gaming Controller (FPS) | High | High | High | **Excellent** |
| Multi-tab Software UI | Moderate | Variable | Low | **Moderate** |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Performance Matrix (Compatibility vs. Performance)

| Compatibility Level | Typical RT Increase | Typical Error Rate | Operator Workload |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High** | Baseline | < 2% | Low |
| **Moderate** | +15–30% | 2–8% | Moderate |
| **Low** | +50–100% | 8–25% | High |
| **Reversed (Inverted)** | +100–300% | 15–40% | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spatial Compatibility — Conceptual Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Operator Perception Layer] --> B[Display Set D]
    B --> C{Spatial Mapping Function f}
    C --> D[Control Set C Layout]
    C --> E[Control Movement Direction]
    C --> F[Population Stereotype Check]
    D --> G{Compatibility Evaluation}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[High Compatibility Output]
    G --> I[Moderate Compatibility Output]
    G --> J[Low Compatibility Output]
    H --> K[Low RT - Low Error - High Throughput]
    I --> L[Moderate RT - Moderate Error]
    J --> M[High RT - High Error - Operator Fatigue]
```

## 4.2 Fitts' Seven Categories of Compatibility (Spatial Subsystem Highlighted)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Spatial Locational Compatibility]:::hl
    P2[Movement Directional Compatibility]:::hl
    P3[Population Stereotype Compatibility]
    P4[Conceptual Compatibility]
    P5[Mode of Control Compatibility]
    P6[Control-Response Compatibility]
    P7[Display-Response Compatibility]
    P1 --> Out1[Position-to-Position Mapping]
    P2 --> Out2[Direction-to-Direction Mapping]
    P3 --> Out3[Cultural Expectation Mapping]
    P4 --> Out4[Symbolic Meaning Mapping]
    P5 --> Out5[Discrete vs Continuous Mapping]
    P6 --> Out6[Effort-to-Outcome Mapping]
    P7 --> Out7[Visual Feedback Mapping]
    classDef hl fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Aircraft Cockpit Throttle Quadrant — Spatial Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DisplaysPanel[Engine Display Panel - Spatial Order L to R]
        D1[E1 Indicator]
        D2[E2 Indicator]
        D3[E3 Indicator]
        D4[E4 Indicator]
    end
    subgraph ThrottleQuadrant[Throttle Quadrant - Spatial Order L to R]
        T1[T1 Throttle]
        T2[T2 Throttle]
        T3[T3 Throttle]
        T4[T4 Throttle]
    end
    D1 --- T1
    D2 --- T2
    D3 --- T3
    D4 --- T4
    T1 --> PUSH1[Push T1 Forward]
    T2 --> PUSH2[Push T2 Forward]
    T3 --> PUSH3[Push T3 Forward]
    T4 --> PUSH4[Push T4 Forward]
    PUSH1 --> R1[E1 Thrust Increase]
    PUSH2 --> R2[E2 Thrust Increase]
    PUSH3 --> R3[E3 Thrust Increase]
    PUSH4 --> R4[E4 Thrust Increase]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Designing for Spatial Compatibility

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([New Interface Design Task]) --> Q1{Is the display layout fixed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Mirror control layout to display layout]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Choose optimal display layout first]
    Q2 --> Q4{Can movement direction match display response?}
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Assign movements intuitively]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Use population stereotypes as fallback]
    Q5 --> Q7[Test with target user population]
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 --> Q8{Error rate below 5%?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Done([Approved Interface])
    Q8 -- No --> Q9[Redesign mapping - reduce k_c]
    Q9 --> Q7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define spatial compatibility. Differentiate between spatial layout compatibility and movement (directional) compatibility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Spatial compatibility is the degree of correspondence between the **spatial arrangement of controls** and the **spatial arrangement of the displays** they manipulate, such that the operator's spatial intuition produces the intended system response.
- **Spatial Layout Compatibility** concerns the *positional* mapping: the control occupies the same relative location on the panel as the display it operates (e.g., left throttle ↔ left engine gauge).
- **Movement (Directional) Compatibility** concerns the *directional* mapping: the direction in which a control is moved matches the direction in which the display element or system response moves (e.g., joystick forward ↔ cursor up).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differentiation)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a population stereotype in the context of spatial compatibility? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A population stereotype is a **culturally or experientially learned expectation** shared by the majority of a user population regarding which control movement should produce which display response. It is not based on physical necessity but on **collective training and convention**.
- **Example 1:** Turning a light switch **UP** turns the light ON.
- **Example 2:** Moving a mouse **forward** (away from the user) causes the on-screen cursor to move **upward**.

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail Fitts' classification of display-control compatibility. Highlight the role of spatial and movement compatibility within this taxonomy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A radar operator must select one of 16 targets using a trackball. The targets are arranged in a 4×4 grid on a 200 mm × 200 mm display. Each target icon is 20 mm wide. The trackball-to-cursor mapping is **non-spatial** (cursor moves horizontally regardless of trackball orientation). Estimate the movement time using Fitts' Law, assuming $a = 80$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit, and a compatibility coefficient $k_c = 1.4$.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Fitts' Classification of Display-Control Compatibility (1951):**

Paul Fitts, working with Bingham and Deininger, identified **seven categories** of compatibility between displays and controls. These categories determine how easily and accurately an operator can translate intent into action:

1. **Spatial (Locational) Compatibility** [1 mark]
   - The control is positioned in the same relative location as the display.
   - *Example:* Multi-throttle aircraft quadrant mirrors engine layout.

2. **Movement (Directional) Compatibility** [1 mark]
   - Direction of control motion matches direction of display response.
   - *Example:* Push steering yoke forward → nose pitches down.

3. **Population Stereotype Compatibility** [1 mark]
   - Mapping aligns with culturally-learned expectations.
   - *Example:* Volume slider up = louder.

4. **Conceptual Compatibility** [1 mark]
   - The control's symbolic meaning matches its function.
   - *Example:* Red button = stop/emergency.

5. **Mode of Control Compatibility** [1 mark]
   - Discrete controls for discrete functions; continuous for continuous.
   - *Example:* Toggle switch for on/off; rotary knob for variable intensity.

6. **Control-Response Compatibility** [1 mark]
   - Effort level matches the magnitude of response expected.

7. **Display-Response Compatibility** [1 mark]
   - Visual feedback format aligns with the response.

**Role of Spatial & Movement Compatibility (Synthesis):**
Spatial and movement compatibility are the **two most critical categories** because they engage the operator's pre-conscious spatial cognition. Violations force **explicit cognitive translation**, dramatically increasing reaction time and error rate. In time-critical domains (aviation, surgery, military), even small compatibility violations can be fatal.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters** [0.5 marks]
- $A = 200$ mm (display amplitude)
- $W = 20$ mm (target width)
- $a = 80$ ms
- $b = 150$ ms/bit
- $k_c = 1.4$ (low compatibility)

**Step 2 — Compute Standard Index of Difficulty** [1 mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \\
&= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \cdot 200}{20}\right) \\
&= \log_2(20) \\
&= 4.322 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Compatibility Coefficient** [1 mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_{eff} &= k_c \cdot ID \\
&= 1.4 \cdot 4.322 \\
&= 6.051 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Movement Time** [1.5 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_{eff} &= a + b \cdot ID_{eff} \\
&= 80 + 150 \cdot 6.051 \\
&= 80 + 907.6 \\
&= 987.6 \text{ ms} \\
&\approx 988 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation** [2 marks]

The movement time of approximately **988 ms** is significantly longer than it would be under high compatibility ($k_c = 1.0$):

$$
MT_{k_c=1} = 80 + 150 \cdot 4.322 = 728.3 \text{ ms}
$$

This represents a **35.6% increase** in movement time due to the non-spatial trackball mapping. In a radar intercept scenario involving multiple incoming targets, this delay is operationally significant.

**Recommendation:** Replace the non-spatial trackball with a **spatial touchscreen** ($k_c \approx 0.7$) to reduce effective ID and movement time, thereby improving operator throughput during high-tempo engagements.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat examples, explain the three sub-types of spatial compatibility. Discuss why violating spatial compatibility is hazardous in aviation cockpit design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An industrial control panel has 8 valves arranged linearly over a 1.6 m pipe. Each valve handle is 5 cm wide. A poorly-trained operator must close the correct valve under time pressure. The spatial layout compatibility is low ($k_c = 1.6$). Compute the effective movement time and suggest two design improvements.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**The Three Sub-Types of Spatial Compatibility:**

1. **Spatial Layout (Locational) Compatibility** [2 marks]
   - The control's position on the panel mirrors the display/response element's position.
   - *Example:* In a four-engine aircraft, Throttle 1 (leftmost in cockpit) controls Engine 1 (leftmost on wing).

2. **Movement (Directional) Compatibility** [2 marks]
   - The kinematic direction of control motion matches the visual/physical direction of the response.
   - *Example:* Pulling the control yoke *backward* raises the aircraft's nose *upward*.

3. **Population Stereotype Compatibility** [2 marks]
   - The mapping aligns with shared cultural expectations of the operator population.
   - *Example:* Operators expect "clockwise" rotation of a knob to *increase* a value (volume, brightness).

**Why Violation is Hazardous in Aviation:** [1 mark]
Aviation cockpits are high-workload, time-critical environments. Pilots operate under **g-forces**, **vibration**, **fatigue**, and **time pressure**. Any spatial-compatibility violation forces the pilot into conscious translation, costing precious milliseconds during takeoff, landing, or combat maneuvers. History shows that incompatibility-induced errors have contributed to multiple aviation incidents, making spatial compatibility a **non-negotiable design criterion** in cockpit ergonomics.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters** [0.5 marks]
- $A = 1600$ mm (total amplitude)
- $W = 50$ mm (valve handle width)
- Assume $a = 60$ ms, $b = 120$ ms/bit
- $k_c = 1.6$

**Step 2 — Compute Standard ID** [1.5 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \cdot 1600}{50}\right) \\
&= \log_2(64) \\
&= 6.0 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Compatibility Coefficient** [1 mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_{eff} &= 1.6 \cdot 6.0 \\
&= 9.6 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Effective Movement Time** [1.5 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_{eff} &= 60 + 120 \cdot 9.6 \\
&= 60 + 1152 \\
&= 1212 \text{ ms} \\
&\approx 1.21 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with High-Compatibility Baseline** [1 mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT_{k_c=1} &= 60 + 120 \cdot 6.0 = 780 \text{ ms} \\
\Delta MT &= 1212 - 780 = 432 \text{ ms (55.4% increase)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Two Design Improvements** [1.5 marks]

1. **Re-arrange valve handles to mirror the spatial layout of the pipeline** (visible through a transparent pipe schematic). This reduces $k_c$ from 1.6 toward 0.8.
2. **Color-code each valve handle** with the corresponding pipeline section color, leveraging **population stereotype compatibility** for faster visual identification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Spatial Compatibility Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating spatial layout with movement compatibility** — they are distinct sub-types. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if students use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting population stereotype as a separate category** — many students treat it as a sub-case of movement compatibility. It is NOT. It is a parallel category.
> 3. **Omitting numerical substitution steps in Part B derivations** — KTU 2024 scheme requires **every algebraic step** shown explicitly. Skipping steps costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong base for logarithm** — Fitts' Law uses base-2 logarithm yielding **bits**. Using base-10 or natural log is a frequent error worth 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Not citing Fitts (1951)** — Examiners award bonus credit for proper attribution. Always cite "Fitts' Compatibility Principle, 1951."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Spatial compatibility** = spatial correspondence between controls and displays (Fitts, 1951).
- [x] **Three sub-types:** Spatial Layout, Movement Directional, Population Stereotype.
- [x] **High compatibility** → low RT, low error, low workload.
- [x] **Low compatibility** → high RT, high error, high workload.
- [x] **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2A/W)$
- [x] **Compatibility-adjusted ID:** $ID_{eff} = k_c \cdot ID$
- [x] **$k_c < 1$** = high compatibility; **$k_c > 1$** = low compatibility.
- [x] **Aviation cockpits** are the canonical high-compatibility design case.
- [x] **Population stereotypes** are cultural, not physical.
- [x] **Cite Fitts (1951)** and **Bingham** for full credit.
- [x] **Always use $\log_2$** in Fitts' Law derivations.
- [x] **Show all numerical steps** in Part B answers.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** throttle quadrants, steering wheels, light switches, mouse cursors, trackballs.

### 🔑 Mnemonic for the Three Sub-Types: **"S-M-P"**

| Letter | Sub-Type | Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Spatial Layout | **Position** |
| **M** | Movement Directional | **Path** |
| **P** | Population Stereotype | **People** |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
