The shoulder-grip reach is measured from the **wall** (backrest reference) to the **grip center**. The fingertip can reach an additional hand-breadth beyond the grip:

$$R_{f,\text{tip}} = S_{r,5} + B_{h,5}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the *grip* allowance to get *functional* reach.**
The functional reach is the distance to a point the *fingertip* can touch, not the deepest point a *cylindrical grip* can enter. We subtract the hand-breadth (we don't need a grip; we just need fingertip contact) and a safety margin:

$$R_{f} = (S_{r,5} + B_{h,5}) - 2 B_{h,5} - 2 S_{a}$$

$$R_{f} = S_{r,5} - B_{h,5} - 2 S_{a}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$R_{f} = 700 - 75 - 2(25) = 700 - 75 - 50$$

$$R_{f} = 575 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
Any primary control must lie within **575 mm of the backrest reference plane**. Secondary controls may lie within 575–700 mm (i.e., reachable by trunk flexion but with postural cost). Controls beyond 700 mm are functionally inaccessible without leaving the seat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 5th percentile?** The minimum-spaced operator (smallest reach) defines the maximum control distance. KTU 2024 scheme expects you to use *5th percentile for reach* and *95th percentile for clearance* — these are the two cardinal design rules of accommodation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Push-Button Spacing from Finger Anatomy

**Step 1 — Define the failure mode.** Two push-buttons are *accidentally co-actuatable* when a single finger press on one cannot be made without also depressing the other.

**Step 2 — Express the geometry.** Let button 1 be at $x = 0$ and button 2 at $x = S_c$ (center-to-center). The finger contact area is a circle of diameter $b_f$ (the functional finger breadth, including glove if used). For the press on button 1 to miss button 2, the closest edge of the finger must lie beyond the closest edge of button 2:

$$\frac{b_f}{2} + \frac{d_b}{2} \leq S_c - \frac{d_b}{2} - \frac{d_b}{2}$$

Wait — that is wrong. Let me redo this with clearer logic.

The finger center is at the **button 1 center** (we are pressing button 1). The button 2 lies at $S_c$ to the right. The finger is a circle of diameter $b_f$, so it extends from $-\frac{b_f}{2}$ to $+\frac{b_f}{2}$ relative to its center. The right edge of button 1 is at $+\frac{d_b}{2}$. The left edge of button 2 is at $S_c - \frac{d_b}{2}$. For button 1 to be pressed without the finger overlapping button 2, we need:

$$+\frac{b_f}{2} \leq S_c - \frac{d_b}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $S_c$.**

$$S_c \geq \frac{d_b}{2} + \frac{b_f}{2}$$

$$S_c \geq \frac{d_b + b_f}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Re-check with a more conservative model.** The standard HFE model (e.g., Woodson, *Human Factors Design Handbook*) uses a slightly different formulation. The most cited one is:

$$S_{c,\min} = d_b + \frac{b_f}{2}$$

This assumes the button is pressed at its center (diameter $d_b$) and the finger can be displaced half a finger-breadth off-center before the other button is touched. For a 12 mm push-button and 19 mm finger:

$$S_{c,\min} = 12 + \frac{19}{2} = 12 + 9.5 = 21.5 \text{ mm}$$

Rounded up, the **25 mm center-spacing** rule emerges — the canonical value you should quote in a KTU exam.

**Step 5 — Knob spacing derivation.** For two rotary knobs of diameter $d_k$ each, the failure mode is the *hand wrapping one knob touches the next knob*. Hand breadth $B_h \approx 100$ mm at the 5th percentile. The hand envelops a circle of diameter $B_h$ around the knob center. Two such circles must not intersect:

$$S_{c,\min} = d_k + B_h$$

For a 50 mm knob: $S_{c,\min} = 50 + 100 = 150$ mm. This is the upper bound; the canonical HFE table gives **100–150 mm** for large rotary controls, depending on whether simultaneous two-hand operation is expected.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Work-Surface Height Range for 5%–95% Accommodation

**Step 1 — State the design intent.** The work surface must accommodate at least the 5th-percentile female to the 95th-percentile male.

**Step 2 — Identify the governing dimension.** Standing elbow height (acromion-to-elbow with arm hanging, then raised 90°):
- 5th percentile female (U.S. data, used as KTU default): $E_{h,5} = 950$ mm
- 50th percentile: $E_{h,50} = 1050$ mm
- 95th percentile male: $E_{h,95} = 1150$ mm

**Step 3 — Apply the work-surface offset.** Standard ergonomic rule: $H_{ws} = E_h - 75$ mm (work surface lies 75 mm below the elbow to permit a 100°–110° elbow flexion at the working posture).

$$H_{ws,\,5} = 950 - 75 = 875 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{ws,\,95} = 1150 - 75 = 1075 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required adjustability.**

$$\Delta H_{ws} = H_{ws,\,95} - H_{ws,\,5} = 1075 - 875 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Recommend an adjustment range.** A standard crank- or motor-adjustable workstation offers **$\pm 100$ mm of adjustment** around a mid-point, giving 200 mm of range. This is the **minimum** to span 5th–95th percentile users in a single workforce.

$$H_{ws,\,\text{adjustable}} = H_{ws,\,50} \pm 100 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{ws,\,\text{adjustable}} = 975 \text{ mm} \pm 100 \text{ mm} = 875 \text{ to } 1075 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students quote the *static* 50th-percentile dimension (e.g., 1050 mm standing) without computing the **range**. Examiners deduct marks for forgetting the adjustability requirement.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The "Golden Triangle" Reach Area

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.** The operator is seated. The two shoulders are at horizontal positions $\pm \frac{B_{s,5}}{2}$ relative to the body's mid-line, where $B_{s,5} = 360$ mm is the 5th-percentile biacromial breadth.

**Step 2 — Identify the reach boundary.** The forward reach of each hand is the shoulder-grip reach minus a body-breadth allowance for trunk rotation:

$$R_{\text{left}} = S_{r,5} - \frac{B_{s,5}}{2} - B_{h,5}$$

$$R_{\text{left}} = 700 - 180 - 75 = 445 \text{ mm (forward reach of left hand from left shoulder)}$$

**Step 3 — Translate to a panel.** Laying out the panel coordinate system with origin at the body's mid-line, the left hand can reach from $-180$ mm to $+(445 - 180) = +265$ mm. By symmetry, the right hand can reach from $-265$ mm to $+180$ mm. The **overlap region** is from $-180$ mm to $+180$ mm — a band 360 mm wide.

**Step 4 — State the design rule.** The most-frequently-used controls should lie in the overlap region (the "golden triangle" with the operator's sternum at its apex), and they should be **centerline-symmetric** for tasks that are not hand-specific.

**Step 5 — Compute the visual comfort line.** The principal work area should lie at a downward gaze angle of 20°–30° below the horizontal visual axis. For a 95th-percentile eye height of 1200 mm (seated) and a viewing distance of 500 mm:

$$H_{\text{primary work}} = H_{\text{eye,95}} - D_v \tan(25^{\circ})$$

$$H_{\text{primary work}} = 1200 - 500 \times 0.466 = 1200 - 233 = 967 \text{ mm (above floor)}$$

For a seated operator with seat-pan at 450 mm, this is $967 - 450 = 517$ mm above the seat pan — the canonical position of a primary display.

## 4.4 Functional Block Annotation — Why This Architecture

The Mermaid diagrams above are not aesthetic — they encode the **decision process** the KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark workplace design answer:

- **Diagram 1** shows the *branching logic* of how an ergonomist chooses a control's spacing rule from its actuation class. A student answer that lists *only* the numerical rules (e.g., "25 mm for buttons") without the **decision tree** loses half the process marks.
- **Diagram 2** shows the *iteration* between anthropometric data and prototype validation. A "design-and-forget" answer loses marks for failing to mention user trials.
- **Diagram 3** shows the **categorization** of controls and the *layered* application of rules (spacing first, then frequency, then final position).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "functional reach" and explain its role in control spacing design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Functional reach $R_f$ is the maximum horizontal distance from a defined body reference (usually the backrest plane) to which an operator can extend a hand to actuate a control while maintaining a neutral, low-fatigue posture. For a seated operator, it is computed as the 5th-percentile shoulder-grip reach minus the hand breadth and a safety/clothing allowance:
$$R_f = S_{r,5} - B_{h,5} - 2 S_a \approx 575 \text{ mm}$$
In control spacing design, $R_f$ sets the *outer boundary* of the primary control zone: any control placed beyond this distance becomes inaccessible to the smallest operator and must either be relocated or designated for secondary (infrequent) use with a reach posture that includes trunk flexion.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the "golden triangle" in workplace design, and which anthropometric variables define it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *golden triangle* is the region of the work surface bounded by the operator's two shoulders (at the biacromial breadth) and the forward reach limit. It is defined by three anthropometric variables: (i) **biacromial breadth** $B_s$ (≈ 360 mm at 5th percentile, sets the lateral width of the triangle), (ii) **shoulder-grip reach** $S_r$ (≈ 700 mm at 5th percentile, sets the depth), and (iii) **functional reach** $R_f$ (derived from $S_r$ and hand breadth, sets the usable boundary inside the triangle). Primary, high-frequency controls are placed inside this triangle; secondary controls are placed outside it but within an extended reach envelope.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module 4 Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, ESE Pattern]`
**(a) [7 marks]** A control panel for a CNC machining centre must accommodate 12 push-buttons (12 mm cap diameter), 4 rotary selector knobs (40 mm knob diameter), and 2 toggle switches (15 mm lever width). The intended operator population has 5th-percentile finger breadth of 19 mm, hand breadth of 100 mm, and a safety allowance of 25 mm. Determine the **minimum center-to-center spacing** for each control class and sketch the panel layout logic.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the same CNC workstation, compute the required **adjustable range** of the work-surface height if the 5th-percentile standing elbow height is 950 mm and the 95th-percentile is 1150 mm. Comment on whether a single fixed work-surface height can be used.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Push-buttons (12 mm cap, finger-actuated).**
Apply the anti-actuation rule:
$$S_{c,\min} = d_b + \frac{b_f}{2} = 12 + \frac{19}{2} = 12 + 9.5 = 21.5 \text{ mm} \approx 25 \text{ mm (canonical HFE value)}$$
**Minimum center pitch for push-buttons = 25 mm.**

**Step 2 — Rotary knobs (40 mm, hand-actuated).**
Apply the hand-breadth rule:
$$S_{c,\min} = d_k + B_{h,5} = 40 + 100 = 140 \text{ mm} \approx 150 \text{ mm (canonical)}$$
**Minimum center pitch for rotary knobs = 150 mm.**

**Step 3 — Toggle switches (15 mm lever, finger-actuated).**
Lever sweep ≈ 30° each side; finger must be able to grip the lever without touching the adjacent switch. Effective handle diameter ≈ 25 mm (lever tip + thumb pad). Apply the lever rule:
$$S_{c,\min} = d_{lever} + 2 \cdot b_f = 25 + 2(19) = 63 \text{ mm} \approx 75 \text{ mm (canonical)}$$
**Minimum center pitch for toggles = 75 mm.**

**Step 4 — Panel layout logic (block diagram answer):**
- Group the 12 push-buttons in a 3×4 matrix, pitch 25 mm, total footprint ≈ 75 mm × 100 mm.
- Place the 4 rotary knobs in a horizontal row at the top of the panel, pitch 150 mm, total width ≈ 600 mm.
- Place the 2 toggle switches on the lower-left corner, pitch 75 mm, separated by ≥ 100 mm from the push-button matrix (no class mixing inside the 25 mm exclusion zone).
- Reserve a **visual field** of at least 30 arc-min separation between any two same-class controls.

| Control Class | Count | Min Pitch (mm) | Row/Col Layout | Total Footprint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Push-buttons | 12 | 25 | 3×4 | 75×100 mm |
| Rotary knobs | 4 | 150 | 1×4 | 600×50 mm |
| Toggles | 2 | 75 | 1×2 | 75×25 mm |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the standing-elbow rule for the 5th and 95th percentile users.**
$$H_{ws} = E_h - 75 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{ws,5} = 950 - 75 = 875 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{ws,95} = 1150 - 75 = 1075 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required range.**
$$\Delta H_{ws} = 1075 - 875 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Select the adjustment range.** A standard motorized sit-stand work surface offers ±100 mm of adjustment around the 50th-percentile midpoint of 975 mm, yielding the full 875–1075 mm range. The required range is therefore **±100 mm minimum**.

**Step 4 — Comment on a fixed work surface.** A *single fixed* work surface at the 50th-percentile height (975 mm) would leave the 5th-percentile operator working with the elbow 75 mm *below* the surface (a forward-rolled shoulder posture leading to trapezius fatigue within 30 min) and the 95th-percentile operator working with the elbow 75 mm *above* the surface (shoulder elevation and abduction, again causing fatigue). A fixed height is therefore **inadvisable** for a workforce spanning 5th–95th percentile; **adjustability is mandatory** under KTU/ANSI/ISO workplace standards.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, ESE Pattern — Alternative Choice]`
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain, with a labelled sketch, the **three governing constraints** of control spacing. How are these reconciled in a single panel layout?

**(b) [7 marks]** For a seated workplace with a 5th-percentile shoulder-grip reach of 700 mm, 5th-percentile hand breadth of 75 mm, and 95th-percentile thigh depth of 250 mm, compute the **functional reach** and the **leg-room depth**. Justify the choice of percentiles for each calculation.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The three governing constraints of control spacing are:

1. **Minimum-spacing (anti-accidental-actuation) constraint.** Controls must be far enough apart that the operator's actuating digit cannot co-actuate an adjacent control. Quantitatively:
$$S_{c,\min} = d_{\text{control}} + (\text{digit breadth rule})$$
Examples: 25 mm for index-finger buttons, 75 mm for toggles, 100–150 mm for rotary knobs, 150 mm for foot pedals.

2. **Maximum-spacing (reachability) constraint.** Controls must lie within the operator's functional reach. Quantitatively:
$$R_f = S_{r,5} - B_{h,5} - 2S_a$$
For the seated operator: $R_f \approx 575$ mm. Controls beyond this distance are inaccessible.

3. **Visual-discrimination constraint.** Adjacent same-class controls must subtend at least 30 minutes of visual arc to be distinguishable at a glance.
$$S_{vis} \geq 2 D_v \tan(0.25^{\circ}) \approx 0.00873 D_v$$

**Reconciliation logic:**
- Apply the *anti-actuation* minimum first (this is almost always the largest).
- Then check *visual discrimination* (often satisfied automatically, but verify for high-density panels).
- Then verify that the chosen positions lie within the *reach envelope*.
- If any constraint is violated, re-locate, re-group by function, or split into sub-panels.
- Apply *importance weighting*: the most-frequently-used and most-critical controls are placed at the **optimum point** of the golden triangle (centre, near reach maximum).
- Sketch (textual): show the panel as concentric zones — innermost is the *primary* zone (within 300 mm of sternum), next is the *secondary* zone (300–500 mm), and outermost is the *tertiary* zone (500–575 mm) for emergency-only controls.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Functional reach.** Use the 5th-percentile shoulder-grip reach because we want to design for the *smallest* user (so the largest population can reach):
$$R_f = S_{r,5} - B_{h,5} - 2 S_a = 700 - 75 - 50 = 575 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Leg-room depth.** Use the 95th-percentile thigh depth because we want to *clear* the largest user (so no user is cramped):
$$D_{leg} = T_{d,95} + S_{k,95} + M_c$$
For the 95th-percentile seated dimensions (U.S. civil data): $T_{d,95} = 250$ mm, $S_{k,95} = 600$ mm, $M_c = 25$ mm.
$$D_{leg} = 250 + 600 + 25 = 875 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Justification of percentile choice.**
- For *reach*, the smallest user defines the *limit*: if she can reach, the larger users can also reach. Hence the **5th percentile**.
- For *clearance*, the largest user defines the *limit*: if he fits, the smaller users also fit. Hence the **95th percentile**.

This is the KTU 2024 Module 4 **cardinal rule of accommodation**: 5th percentile for *capability* dimensions, 95th percentile for *envelope/clearance* dimensions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Quoting only one percentile.** Many answers use the 50th percentile for *everything*. KTU examiners explicitly test whether you know that *reach* uses 5th and *clearance* uses 95th. **Lose 2–3 marks if you get this wrong.**
> 2. **Confusing edge-to-edge and center-to-center spacing.** If the question gives a button diameter and asks for "spacing," state both and the conversion.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety/clothing allowance** $S_a$ in the reach formula. This is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Listing workplace design dimensions without the adjustability range.** A 975 mm work surface is *incomplete*; you must also state the ±100 mm range and *why* it is required.
> 5. **Skipping the iteration with user trials.** A workplace design that goes straight from "compute dimensions" to "production layout" loses the process marks for missing the 5pct/50pct/95pct user-trial validation step.
> 6. **Using $|$ inside markdown tables.** Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid table-rendering errors in your answer script.
> 7. **Omitting the "why" behind each rule.** KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) rewards *reasoning*, not just rule-recall. A bare table of dimensions without justification will earn at most 60% of the marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Control spacing has three constraints**: anti-accidental-actuation (minimum), reachability (maximum), and visual discrimination.
- **Anti-actuation minimum** is set by the *breadth of the actuating digit*: 19 mm finger → 25 mm buttons; 100 mm hand → 150 mm knobs; 100 mm boot → 150 mm pedals.
- **Center-to-center spacing** = edge-to-edge spacing + control diameter. Always convert between the two.
- **Functional reach** (seated, 5th percentile) ≈ **575 mm**. Controls beyond this are functionally inaccessible.
- **Golden triangle** is bounded by the biacromial breadth and the forward reach. Primary controls go inside; secondary go in the extended envelope; emergency-only go at the extreme.
- **Cardinal rule of accommodation**: 5th percentile for *capability* (reach, strength), 95th percentile for *envelope* (clearance, leg-room).
- **Work-surface height** = elbow height − 75 mm. Required **adjustability range** ≈ ±100 mm to span 5th–95th percentile.
- **Seated popliteal clearance** (knee height under desk) ≈ 425 mm; **leg-room depth** ≈ 875 mm.
- **Maximum push force** at fingertip ≈ 20 N sustained, 60 N transient. **Maximum rotary torque** ≈ 2.5 N·m sustained, 7 N·m transient.
- **Visual pitch** between same-class controls must subtend ≥ 30 arc-min.
- **Frequency-importance rule**: most-used and most-critical controls go at the *optimum* of the reach envelope, not the minimum spacing.
- **Workplace design is iterative**: anthropometric data → design dimensions → prototype → user trials (5pct, 50pct, 95pct) → iterate.
- **Standards to remember**: ANSI/HFES 100, ISO 9241, MIL-STD-1472, NFPA 79, IS 1860 (India), ADA-AG (accessibility).
- **Always state units in mm** in KTU exam answers; do not mix inches and millimetres.
- **Always justify the percentile choice** — this is the single highest-leverage sentence in any 14-mark workplace design answer.
